LESSON 1: Lesson 193
Source: network-intercept-vtt

All right, so in this module, we're going to apply the concept of architectures into an entire enterprise. And if we remember what the word architecture itself means, it means a whole bunch of components that work together to allow us to use the architecture for purposes for which it was intended. And so if we take an entire architecture and break it down into components, when we want to secure the entire architecture, we're going to do the same thing. So we're going to take different components and break it down and protect each one. That's really what an architecture is. It's a whole bunch of components. Now if we add the word security to it, security means that each of the components that make up the entire architecture are protected as much as we need, usually based on value. Now if we apply this to an entire company, That's where the word enterprise comes in. An enterprise is the entire company itself, and it's also made up of different components. So when you look at entire company, an enterprise, It's going to be made up of people, processes, technologies and network and data, for example. And so if you want to protect an entire company, The way that we do it is we break it down into the major components, protect each one based on value of each, and that's what we call a enterprise security architecture. So the same concepts that we talked about before in how we protect any architecture, whether it's really small or whether it's a large enterprise, a company that may be global, for example. We protect it the same way by breaking it down into components and that's what we refer to as an enterprise security architecture. Now protecting companies is nothing new. We've been doing that for decades and so the industries have come up with frameworks to facilitate all of this and help companies achieve enterprise security architecture. And as far as how many there exists, there are really three significant ones that the industry has come up with. And what you see on the screen here are the three major enterprise security architectures that we've come up with. And they're listed sort of in a chronological order. And so if you look at the history of enterprise security architectures, the very first one that was developed is called Zachman. And then that was followed by something called Sabsa. And the latest one, which seems to be the one that companies prefer to use because of what companies look like today, is actually called TOGAF. But when you look at the way that these are structured, They really look very similar because they do exactly the same thing that we've been talking about in how you protect architectures. So if you remember the concept of breaking things down into specific components and then protecting each of the components, it's exactly what these enterprise security architectures do. So if you look at the very first one here which was developed in the early 70s, it's a way to take the entire company, break it down into components that are represented here by each of the levels or layers that you see and so the idea is you look at each of the components and you ask the same questions for example in what security needs to be able to do in order to help achieve security for each of the different layers or components so the idea is you ask the same questions and of all of the different components for example but if you look at different layers of what any company looks like they're going to give you different answers to those questions. So for example, if you ask, you know, upper management, what do they want security to be able to do for them? they're going to give you different answers than if you ask that same question to, for example, the operations people or users in specific departments, for example. And so this is a way to capture in a two-dimensional perspective the answers to what security needs to be able to address at different levels of the organization so that we can capture everything in this two-dimensional table format and then analyze that information to be able to address the security requirements. The problem with Zachman is that It was developed for companies in the way that companies looked back in the 70s. And back then, companies looked much different than they do today. And so the industry had to take this and adapt it to what companies look like today. And that's what really SABSA provides for us. It's a different view of the components that really make up what companies look like today. so that again we can ask questions related to security at different levels of the organization to capture relevant information that can then be analyzed through the SABSA process to be able to design and implement security requirements at different levels of the organization. and the latest one is actually called TOGAF which obviously stands for the Open Group Architecture Framework and even though it looks a little bit different than the previous two that we just showed you It's really the same concept where you see the word requirements in the middle here, but all of the outside pieces are each of the components that really represent value to the organization itself. And so again, it's a way for us to understand the security requirements based on value for each of the components that makes up the entire enterprise or company and understand those from a goals and objectives perspective so that we can design ultimately security that's built into every major component of an enterprise and address security for the same. In other words, whether you're using a small architecture like a laptop, or a really large organization that is global, the way that we approach security is exactly the same way. And that is to break it down into individual components that represent value to the organization and not only understand the security requirements but be able to design and implement those in the best way possible which means obviously the most efficient and cost-effective way possible to be able to implement security. These are three significant enterprise security architecture models that allow companies to achieve enterprise security architecture.

Okay, in this module, we're going to expand the role of a policy. We've already talked about how a policy provides the foundation necessary for effective security to exist in any organization. And that's true of asset security, specifically a data classification policy. So typically in most organizations you will have a asset classification policy that talks about the importance of protecting assets and the value that they represent to the organization and that of course includes The accountabilities, the responsibilities that go along with that. The owner will always be accountable, not only for protecting their assets, but also in owning, for example, the asset classification policy that really dictates how we protect assets within the organization itself. Now, when you look at any policy, including a data classification policy, you know that a policy is simply a corporate law that communicates management's intentions in how it applies to the organization. In other words, it tells us what is what is not acceptable. and who is accountable and responsible for protecting assets. The owners are always going to be accountable. Everyone else, basically custodians, will be responsible. But a policy communicates what's important. The way that we implement the policy is through the elements that obviously are below that, that we've talked about certainly in domain number one. But obviously the way that we implement policies are through standards, procedures, baselines and also guidelines and a combination of all of those. And as you'll remember standards are specific hardware and software products typically and solutions that can again implement the policy itself. Procedures are step-by-step requirements that need to be followed. And baselines are typically configurations, parameters, for example, that we apply in different technologies that are also mandatory. And guidelines, as the word implies, simply means suggestions. You should or you should not. And when you put all of these elements together, standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines. They are really the security controls that we apply in this case in protecting, for example, data and allows the organization to achieve the requirements of the data classification policy that owners are ultimately responsible for. And where security comes in is obviously in supporting not only the policy itself so that everyone is aware of it, but also in how we use the standards, the procedures, the baselines and the guidelines and how everybody should be educated and being able to use the right products and so on. Now in any policy, we need to obviously understand the requirements of what that policy addresses, which is obviously the value of the assets themselves, which really dictate how we protect that important asset in the end. And so the owners are always accountable. for the contents of the policy itself. And so what adds value to the asset and how we therefore protect it is dictated by a number of elements that you see here up on your screen. And that includes things like laws and regulations because that's what dictates how a company would achieve its compliance. So that's obviously something that would add value to the asset and would require us to protect it to that level. Does the asset have anything to do with privacy requirements which would also add value? Is there a cost of creation for example or using the asset that also adds value? So what you're seeing here on your screen are some of the elements that owners are in the best position to be able to understand because these are all elements that add value to the asset which obviously dictates how we ultimately protect it. The owner is always in the best position to understand all of those factors, those elements such as the ones that you see here that add value and therefore dictate how the asset needs to be protected as it goes through its life cycle. And so what you see here on the next slide is typically what we refer to as the asset life cycle, even though here it says information life cycle. We're using information as an example of an asset that companies would want to protect. But again, information is only one example of a valuable asset, but all assets will go through a life cycle. from the time that we create it or acquire it to the time that at some point in the future we're going to destroy or decommission that asset. the idea here is if we keep track of the asset life cycle it may actually increase or decrease in value and so your asset classification systems that are there to protect your assets need to be able to support an increase in classification or a decrease in classification. And again, it's the owner that is in the best position to be able to understand when something increases in value or decreases in value as it goes through its life cycle but your asset classification system needs to be able to support that so that again what we do is we if the asset increases in value it means that we need more protection mechanisms we need more security to be able to be applied to that asset. If it decreases in value then we have to drop its classification level to something lower which means that again lower security controls will be required in order to protect at always the value of the asset. So the whole bottom line here is that when you implement an asset classification system that supports protecting assets we need to be able to support an increase in classification or a decrease in classification as that asset goes through its life cycle represented here. on your slide as you see and it doesn't matter whether that asset lasts for just a few months or you may have some really long retention periods. The idea is that whatever level it's at that is representing the value is how we protect it. And baselines will dictate what those minimum levels of security are that we need to apply to assets as they go through their asset life cycle, which again could be long. in some cases related to different companies that operate in different industries and may have different compliance requirements, for example.

LESSON 6: Lesson 198
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

All right, we've been talking about the importance of applications and how they need to be secured. And one of the most important environments where we rely on applications to be able to give functionality is the database environment. Now, if you look at the history of databases, you know they've been around for decades and what they really allow us to do is store very sensitive valuable information that ultimately drives business decisions I mean that's why companies store information in databases. Now it is applications that allow us to interface with ultimately that data that is stored in the databases themselves. So when you think about how we protect any architecture, and we've talked about this many times before, The way that we protect architectures is by breaking them down into components, protecting each of the components. So when you look at a database architecture it's usually made up of different components such as the ones that you see here on the slide here. Obviously you've got hardware and on top of that you've got an operating system. And it is software or an application that really allows people to be able to interface with the database and its contents ultimately. And of course the whole important thing that we have to do in any environment here is to protect the data itself. but how we interface with the data ultimately is provided through the application itself. That means that the application has to have really good security. Now before we can talk about the security that's built into applications, we have to look at how database environments have really evolved over the years. And so ultimately what they look like today are typically tables. Tables that will represent information in two dimensional tables. In fact, in some cases, We don't even call them databases anymore, we call them tables and what we're talking about here is a technology called relational database management systems. The database management system is really the set of applications that allow users to interface with the contents of the database so that we can report and query and analyze to be able to drive decisions themselves. But what you see on the screen here is a representation of what relational databases actually look like. Two dimensional tables that allow us to structure data very easily But the reason why it's called relational is because we have the ability to be able to link and relate different tables together. And the advantage of that of course is to be able to analyze more information together. So if you put information together, we are able to use analysis tools to be able to look for trends and relationships and correlations and patterns that become evident because that information is together in how we link them for example and so the advancements that we've made for example in relation is that we use also different terminology to be able to relate to certain things that we used to call them before like for example they were called databases We try and not use the word database anymore. We actually call them tables or relations. And in fact, instead of calling them fields, in some cases in this technology, we call them attributes. and the records for example are typically referred to as tuples or tuples and the intersection of tuples and attributes is what we call for example a field. This is the terminology that we typically use in relational technology. Now the advantage of being able to use relational is where that name relational comes from. We can relate tables together but the only way that we can do this is to actually repeat information from one table into the other. And so the way that this typically works and we've got a pretty good example here that you see up on your screen. If we have a student table there is something called a primary key that is actually represented for example as the first field The student ID here is referred to as the primary key. Now that information has to be repeated into another table somewhere else. in order to allow that relation or that join to actually exist. So the primary key that exists in one table that is actually repeated in another table is referred to as the foreign key in that other table. Now there are some very important integrity rules. that would allow this to be done properly, because if you don't address those integrity rules, then obviously this link, this relation, is not something that's gonna happen properly. And so the two very important integrity rules that we need to remember are, first of all, entity integrity. And what that means is that for every primary key value that we have, for example in the student table, that value has to be valid. In other words, if you look at how it's structured right now it is always five characters but they are always numeric characters and of course if we added one more record into that student table you know the primary key in that record would have to be something that is unique. So through data validation we would ensure that we have entity integrity. That's what means having for example a valid primary key in that student table. the foreign key which is really the primary key that is repeated in the other table also has to be valid and if it is valid it allows the second integrity rule that we have to remember and that's called referential integrity. So referential integrity means that for every foreign key value, for example, that exists in the registration table, there's a valid reference back into the student table so that the two can actually be joined. linked and so the security that's built into the application would first of all ensure entity integrity every primary key is valid and referential integrity which means that for every foreign key that we have there's a valid link back into another table as a primary key for example. So that's what those two really important integrity rules mean. Now in any database environment, a database is only valuable if we update, for example, the information in the database to make it more factual, more relevant, more timely, more current. And so if you think about what databases look today, we have to allow a whole bunch of users to be able to update, for example, the records in that database at the same time. So what also becomes very important to address from a security perspective in any database environment are what we call concurrency controls, or some people refer to them as lock controls. And what this means is that we have to prevent, for example, two people from being able to make a change to the same database element at the same time because if we did obviously that would affect the integrity of the database contents itself and so we have to address what's called concurrency controls or law controls and what that means is applying what we call the ACID test which is what you see here up on your screen. Obviously the word ACID comes from the first letter of each of these requirements that need to be applied to any database that allows updating in real time for example. So we need to understand each one of these. The A is for atomicity and what that means is that either all changes take effect or none do. In other words, when you apply a transaction to a database, it typically requires a number of steps for us to go through before we can complete that transaction successfully. If your steps don't complete successfully, all of them, then the ones that you did complete successfully would have to be backed out. It would be atomicity controls within the application that would ensure that. And this is the one that we typically see a lot, especially online. You know, if you're making, for example, an update to a database, let's say you're booking a seat on a flight. and you're going through the process of booking that seat on a flight through your airline's website, for example, there's probably a number of steps that you have to go through before you complete that. Let's say you get to the point where you need to pay for it with your credit card. and your phone rings and you start talking on the phone, your conversation lasts, let's say 10 minutes, and then you hang up and you try and resume the transaction where you left it. The system is probably going to tell you that you've timed out. And so you need to start back at the beginning again. So all of those steps that you had completed up until that point where you got to the point where you were paying for it and you got that phone call would have to be backed out. It is atomicity controls that would ensure that. The second rule is consistency and simply what that means is that once you apply a transaction successfully to the database, that transaction that you've applied does not affect the integrity of the database before it looked like before you applied your transaction. In other words, by you booking a seat on a flight and doing it properly did not affect anyone else that also had seats on that same flight. In other words, your change does not adversely affect the integrity of the database before it looked like you made your change. The next one is isolation. And that simply means that the results of your transaction is not actually visible to any other user of the database. until you complete your transaction successfully. So nobody knows for example that you're booking the last business class seat because if they did they might try and get ahead of you. So everything that you're doing is isolated from any other user of the database until you complete that transaction successfully. It is isolation controls that would ensure that. And the last one, durability, I think pretty intuitive as far as what that means. If you apply a transaction successfully, that transaction lasts. It's durable. There's no way for it to disappear. when you show up to the airport to get on your flight there actually is a seat for you on that flight and so that's durability and when you put all of these together It means that the application that controls access to the database ensures that each of these requirements that are part of what we call the asset test are addressed properly and properly for us to ensure the integrity of the data within the database itself.

LESSON 7: Lesson 199
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Let's talk about a really big problem that we have in security. And that is that in companies, we have an almost unlimited number of assets. We have people, we have data, we have buildings, we have systems, we have all of these very, very valuable assets. And the big problem that we have is that there is a very, very limited amount of funds of people's time to protect those assets. So we have this big challenge. We have to figure out how do we effectively protect this vast number of assets with a very limited budget. And the number of people that we have that can actually protect our systems is very limited. So how do we do this? How do we most effectively figure out how we protect our systems? What controls do we put in place where? What are the best controls to choose? What's the process we use to figure this out? And the process is risk management. So let's go through the risk management process. Let's understand how do we identify our most valuable assets? How do we analyze the risks associated with those assets? And how do we treat those risks? So let's look at the first part of this process, which is evaluation. How do we go about identifying our most valuable assets? There's two primary methods that we can use for ranking what are our most valuable assets, quantitative and qualitative. Quantitative means we're specifically using quantitative values to identify our most valuable assets. So something might be worth a million dollars versus something that's worth a thousand dollars or ten trillion dollars. Quantitative analysis is using monetary values to rank our most valuable assets. But it's often very difficult to put an exact dollar value on something. Like how much exactly is an organization's reputation worth? Or how much exactly is a particular system worth or a particular piece of data? It's often very difficult to quantify this in exact dollar values. So the other method that we use most of the time, in fact, is what's known as qualitative analysis. And qualitative is a relative ranking system. We say one thing is high versus another thing that's medium or low. And so the first step of risk management is to identify our most valuable assets and rank those assets from most valuable down to least valuable. The next step in risk management is then to think about what are the risks associated with each of those valuable assets. And when we think about the risk analysis process, there's four major pieces that we look at. There's first of all, the threat. We don't have a risk unless we have a threat. We then have to think about, is there an associated vulnerability? We then look at, if that threat exercised that vulnerability, what would be the impact to the organization? And then the final piece that we look at in risk analysis is, what's the probability? How likely, how often, what's the probability of a particular threat exercising a particular vulnerability and the resultant impact. How often is that going to occur? Once we've identified all four of those pieces, we can then start to understand all the risks associated with an asset. And it's a one to N relationship. And by that, I mean that for each asset, you're likely to have a number of different risks associated with each asset. And so then the final step in the risk analysis piece is to actually rank the risks from the most concerning to the least concerning. And how do we go about ranking those risks? Well, it's actually exactly the same two methods that we used for ranking the assets, quantitative and qualitative. And later on, we're going to get into a lot more detail on that specific form of quantitative risk analysis known as the ALE calculation. The next module will talk about that. So what's the final step in risk management? The final step is treatment. So treatment is the process where we treat the risks. We somehow try and deal with the risks. And there's four major ways that we can do that. The first is to avoid. Second is transfer. And when we say transfer, you should associate that with one word, and that is insurance. And then where we spend the vast majority of our time is mitigation. This is where we implement mitigating controls to try and reduce the risk down to an acceptable level. And the final treatment method is risk acceptance. This is where we accept the risk that remains.

============================================================
LESSON 8: Lesson 200
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Thank you. As part of the risk management process, we talked about how there was three major stages to risk management, value, risk, and treatment. Let's dig into treatment in a little more detail. Let's talk about how we go about treating risk. And as we talked about just briefly before, there's four major ways that we can treat risk. avoid, transfer, mitigate, and accept. So let's look at each of these in a little more detail, and I have to apologize for my horrible PowerPoint drawing skills here, but this diagram is meant to show a diving board with two different levels, and a pool of water that you're somehow physically supposed to be able to land in even though the diving board doesn't line up. So just ignore my terrible drawing skills and assume that somehow this would physically work. But let's use this diving board as a method of thinking about the four different treatment methods. And the first method means that we avoid the risk. So what does risk avoidance mean? When we say risk avoidance, what are we truly doing here? Well, risk avoidance is you don't do whatever the risky thing is, you avoid the risk. So if moving to the cloud is super risky, the way that you would avoid that risk is, not move to the cloud. But there's a really big downside to risk avoidance, and that is opportunity cost. If you avoid a risk, you avoid the upside of what that risk might have gotten you. And there's a lot of advantages to moving to the cloud as an example. And so if you entirely avoid the cloud, you avoid all of those potential advantages. And so that's a big downside with risk avoidance. And you can see this in real life. If an organization is too risk adverse, if they don't take any risks, what's inevitably going to happen to that organization? they will cease to exist. They won't continue on in the marketplace anymore. You always have to take a measured degree of risk. So risk avoidance is good for the really, really sketchy, scary things, but not good for everything. So what's the next way that we can treat risk? Well, oh, and by the way, how would we depict risk avoidance using the diver board? Go the other way. Don't jump. That's how you avoid the risk. Now, what's the next way that we can treat risk? Transfer. And using the diving board analogy here, transfer the risk would maybe you get someone else to jump. I don't know. See if they make it. But you should actually associate transference with one word. And that word is insurance. The way that we transfer the risk to someone else is to buy insurance. But keep in mind two very important words that we've already talked about. Accountability and risk. responsibility, because it's very important to understand that if an organization, for instance, has the risk in that they have to protect important data because they're the owners of that data, can they transfer the accountability for the protection of that data? And the answer is emphatically, absolutely no. They can transfer the responsibility but not the accountability. Now, what's the next way that we can treat risk? And this is frankly where we spend the vast majority of our time in security, and that is mitigation. And fundamentally what mitigation means is we put in place controls that try and reduce the risk down to an acceptable level. But can we ever reduce the risk down to zero? The answer is no. We can never find the perfect set of controls that will reduce risk to zero. So that's where I'll introduce a new term here. When you've reduced the risk as much as you can, there will always be what is known as some residual risk left over. And so using the diving board analogy here, how would we try and mitigate or reduce the risk? Well, we could try jumping off the lower diving board and maybe that would somehow survive that. Now, what's the final way that we can treat risk? Acceptance. Acceptance simply means we accept the risk. And there's a few places that we would do this. So I just talked about mitigation and how we always have some residual risk left over. That risk is typically accepted. We might also decide to accept risk if the cost of the controls exceeds the cost of the risk occurring. In other words, the controls are not cost justified. In that case, we might just decide to accept the risk. Now, the final really important thing to think about with risk acceptance is who should be accepting the risk. And the answer is the owner. The owner is always the one that should be accepting the risk for a particular asset. Why the owner? Well, because they best understand exactly what that asset is, how valuable it is, how big of a deal would it be to the organization if that risk occurred. They're best positioned to understand all that. and therefore they're best positioned to accept the risk. And using the final little diving board analogy here, here's how we could accept risk. Just jump from the top. So that's risk treatment. Thank you.

============================================================
LESSON 9: Lesson 201
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We spent a bunch of time talking about all the different types of controls that we have and all the different categories of controls and functional and assurance aspects of controls. So how do we go about deciding how much control, how many controls to put in place, how much security to put in place? And fundamentally, this comes down to a decision, a very simple decision, and it's basically how much security is cost justified. It's always a balance between the cost of protecting an asset against the level of acceptable risk, which fundamentally is about every control needs to be cost justified. It has to help the organization achieve its goals and objectives. And importantly, the control can't cost more than the actual risk that you're trying to cover. So what are some of the decisions, the determining factors that we use in actually selecting a particular control that we're gonna use? Here are some of the major pieces that we might look at. We might look at exactly how well does this control align to the business goals and objectives? How does this control actually help the business achieve its goals and objectives? Is this control cost effective? Does this control provide both the functional and assurance aspects that every control should provide? The final piece that we want to look at for every control is, is it a complete control? Does it provide a combination of both preventive, detective, and corrective aspects? So these are the major pieces that we would look at when determining what controls we want in place. Once we have controls actually put in place, we want to make sure those controls are actually working correctly. And that's where we start to talk about metrics and measuring and making sure that these controls are working correctly. And when it comes to defining risk metrics, There's a lot of different things that we could measure. And so what it really boils down to with measurement is actually figuring out what metrics we can measure, but more importantly, who's our audience? Who are we actually presenting these metrics to? And what do they actually need to know from these metrics to make good business decisions? That's where I love the term metrics that matter. You want to pick metrics that actually make sense to the audience. So that's a big part of measuring effectiveness of controls is picking the right metrics and reporting them in the right way to the right people. Another really important aspect of risk management is this concept of continuous improvement. And by that, I mean it's a very long, arduous process to go through the entire risk analysis process, the risk management process, to think about our most valuable assets, to identify all the risks associated with those assets, and then figure out all the treatment. It can sometimes takes months, even years to go through that entire process. And when an organization's done, it's not like they get to just kind of be like, done finally with that. I'm out. Never doing that again. No, I mean, it's a continuous process. You have to constantly refresh this risk management process. Why? Well, it's because the environment is constantly changing. There's constantly new threats out there. There's new vulnerabilities appearing within the environment. The number of the assets that we have that we need to protect are constantly being added and removed. So the whole risk management landscape is constantly changing. And so we have to have a model of constantly refreshing our risk management process. What's the perfect timing for how often you should redo your risk management, redo the process? There is no one precise timing, date, anything like that. The perfect answer is as often as necessary. What does that mean? Well, whenever there is a significant new threat, a new vulnerability, a new asset in the environment, that should trigger the risk management process for those pieces. And so when we talk about continuously updating, this is what it really means. We're triggering the update of our risk management process by significant changes in our environment. Thank you.

LESSON 12: Lesson 204
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Let's talk about malware. What is malware? Let's start with that simple definition. Malware is software that's been intentionally written to somehow harm your system, do something nefarious. Now, we often misuse some of the terminology around this. We tend to call everything a virus, for instance. But a virus is actually a particular type of malware. So what I'm gonna do next in this next few minutes is talk about all the different types of malware that are out there. And I'm gonna give you very specific, very concise definitions for each of the different types of malware. because it's not uncommon on the exam that you'll get questions about the different types of malware. And so it's important for you to understand exactly what they look like, what do they do, and One other caveat I'll give to this as well, and that is that malware out in the wild often exhibits multiple different aspects or different types of malware that we're going to talk about here. That'll become obvious as we talk about it. So let's start with the most commonly referred to piece of malware, a virus. What is the defining characteristic of a virus? It's actually a piece of malware that needs to be triggered by a user. That's what we call a virus. It's a piece of malware that somehow has to be triggered by the user. Which implies something really important. What's one of the best ways of preventing a virus outbreak on a system? It's actually user training and awareness so they know not to double click on particular types of files and open things up. That's the best way of preventing a virus. Next, let's look at worms. Worms are particularly dangerous because worms are self-propagating. They can spread on their own. How? Well, they spread through some known vulnerability that the malware writer has found. And so they write this of the way to exploit this vulnerability into their worm. And then that worm can self-propagate, can spread on its own. And that's why when you've heard of major malware outbreaks in the past, things like SQL Slammer, the reason they were able to spread so incredibly quickly to millions of systems in a matter of hours is because they were a worm. They were self-propagating. Next, let's look at companions. Companions malware is not actually doing anything nefarious on its own. Instead, a companion is just a helper. It's helping out the major piece of malware in some way. A perfect example of a companion is something like a packer that You know, helps condense down the malware code and then de-pack it, un-condense it later on. So that's a companion. Doesn't do anything evil on its own. Next, let's look at macros. When you hear the word macro, you probably think of the macros that you build into say Excel documents to automate certain tasks. That's a perfect example of what a macro virus is or a macro malware. A macro is code that we've written into another application. And the Microsoft products where you can use VBScript to write macros is a perfect example of macro malware. You can actually use VBScript to write malicious code into a macro that you then add into something like an Excel document. Which is why when you open up an Excel document, something like that, in Word, you get such dire warnings. Are you really sure you want to open this? Because often, or sometimes, opening an Excel document can actually get one of these macros to run automatically and do something harmful to your system. Next, let's look at multipartite malware. The name actually does a pretty good job of explaining this, multipartite. That word multi in there, It means more than one. And that's what a multi-partite piece of malware means. It means it's spreading in more than one different way. So a perfect example of a multi-partite piece of malware is actually Stuxnet. If you've ever read into this a little bit or heard about it, Stuxnet was actually a piece of malware that was designed to be installed and run in an Iranian nuclear refinement facility where they were creating plutonium. In order to create plutonium, you have to spin centrifuges at really, really high speed, like 40,000 RPM. This equipment is obviously very sensitive. So someone, the US, wrote a piece of malware to run on this Iranian facility. And what it was doing was actually spinning these centrifuges just a little bit too fast to cause them to fail. And even more deviously, it was actually hiding what it was doing from the operators of this facility. Their systems, their screens, their dashboards were telling them everything's working perfectly, and yet their equipment was failing. Now, as you can imagine, this was a very well-protected facility. It was certainly not connected to the internet. So how did this malware actually get into this facility? And Stuxnet was running in real facilities. So how did it get in there? Well, it's suspected that the first way that it got in was on a USB stick. So the first way that Stuxnet infected a system was through a USB vulnerability. And then, once it was on the first system, it then spread across the network. So it spread in multiple different ways. First through a USB vulnerability, and then through a network vulnerability. Multi-partite. Next, polymorphic. When you hear those words polymorphic or that word polymorphic, it's actually composed of two different words, poly and morphic. Poly means many, morphic means to change. And that's a really good definition of what polymorphic malware is. It's constantly changing, morphing. Why? To evade detection. Most anti-malware systems are signature based. They look for a particular set of characteristics, a certain file name, file size, variable names within the malware. It looks for a specific set of characteristics. And if a piece of malware is constantly morphing, changing itself, it's to evade detection. So that's polymorphic. Next, let's look at Trojans. So you've probably heard, if you know your Greek mythology, the Trojan horse. It looked like a very lovely thing that you would want to bring inside your defenses, but it was actually filled with... something you didn't want, something evil. And that's a perfect example of a Trojan. A Trojan is something that looks very innocent, a file that you would want to install, but it's actually filled with malware. So that's a Trojan. Next, botnets. All right, there's a few pieces that we need to know about botnets. First of all, let's understand the concept of what is a botnet. Well, a botnet is this concept that an attacker finds a vulnerability that they can exploit that allows them to take over and install some software on a victim system. and they install a little bot or they make that system a zombie. And what they're doing is they're essentially allowing the attacker gets the ability to remotely control the system, to get that system to send out spam email, to do cryptocurrency mining, to do all sorts of nefarious things. And now the idea of a bot net is that we don't just have one zombie that we can remotely command and control. We have hundreds, thousands, maybe millions. And when you connect all of these systems together, you create a giant bot net that allows the attacker to remotely control potentially millions of systems. and this is frankly how the vast majority of spam gets sent on the internet is by botnets and they're used nowadays for lots of cryptocurrency mining things like that in fact you can even get botnet as a service if you look on the dark web there's some excellent services out there for botnets as a service that you can buy Next, let's look at boot sector infectors. Okay, what's a boot sector? Well, on a hard drive, you have a little bit of memory space right at the start of the hard drive that's reserved for the firmware, for the code that's meant to help that hard drive spin up for the low-level control of the hard drive. Now, this boot sector is never meant to be accessed by the operating system, by any other code, except for when the hard drive first starts up and for the normal operation of the hard drive. Now, if a piece of malware were to install itself in the boot sector, When would that malware run when the system first starts up? And the answer is, right at the very boot up of that system, this malware would be able to run then. And which of the system's security controls would be in place? none, the operating system wouldn't be running, the anti-malware system wouldn't be running. So boot sector infectors, they're able to install themselves into the boot sector of a hard drive, which makes them very difficult to detect, and it allows them to run well before any of the other security controls would even place on a system. Next, let's look at hoaxes, pranks. Hoaxes and pranks are not actually software. They're social engineering. It's where you use techniques to get someone to do something either funny or maybe malicious to their system through social engineering techniques. So pranks are just for fun, like flipping someone's screen upside down or something like that. And hoaxes are where you actually get someone to do something that's potentially bad for their system. I'll give you a great example. You can tell your parents this. If their system is starting to run a little bit slowly, just tell them to go to the command prompt. You'll have to teach them how to do that. and then get them to type in C colon star dot star, or sorry, delete C colon star dot star. So what's that going to do? Significantly increase the speed of their system. No, don't do that. It's a very bad idea. But that's a perfect example of a hoax. Next, let's talk about logic bombs. A logic bomb is a piece of malware that when a certain set of logic is met, the bomb will go off. So I'll give you a great example of this. This is a true story. So there was a very disgruntled system administrator. He worked at a small company. He'd been there for many, many years. He had control, access to, Everything. Now, over time, he was starting to get a little disgruntled. You know, these bastards, they don't pay me enough money. And so he decided to write a logic bomb. This logic bomb asked one very simple question every morning. It asked, am I still an employee? And one morning, the HR system said, No. And what did his logic bomb do? It salted the earth. This guy had access to everything, so it deleted all the SharePoint sites, all the databases, all the backups. It destroyed everything. So that's a perfect example of a logic bomb, and also why you shouldn't do it, because he's now in jail. Next, let's look at stealth malware. Stealth malware is a piece of malware that's been specifically designed to actively disguise itself, to hide, to be stealth, to not show itself on the system. Next, let's look at ransomware. I'm sure everyone has heard about ransomware now. It's in the news all the time. Ransomware is this concept of how can attackers monetize breaking into your system? The way that they do it is they'll encrypt your data or your systems, or in some other way, make your systems and data unavailable, and then they'll require some sort of bribe, some sort of ransom to unlock your systems. That's ransomware. And it's becoming incredibly pervasive nowadays because frankly, it's good money for the attackers. This is a very good revenue stream for them. Next, let's look at root kits. All right, this is very sophisticated and stealthy malware. So we've talked about the system kernel. The system kernel is the heart of the operating system. It's what controls all the low-level functionality. It's the software that controls the entire system. A rootkit is a piece of malware that's able to infect that system kernel to, in a sense, control that system kernel to give the rootkit privileged access. And with that privileged access, the rootkit can basically do whatever it wants. And one very important thing that rootkits will do is they'll disguise or hide themselves. And because they can control the operating system, if you do a search, for instance, if the anti-malware system does a scan through the hard drive to look for the rootkit, the rootkit will just tell the operating system not to display any of the files that are the rootkit, which makes rootkits very difficult to detect and remove from systems. Very dangerous malware. Next, let's look at data diddlers. And I gotta start this off by saying I didn't make these names up, okay? So what's a data diddler? A data diddler makes very small changes to your data over a long period of time. Why? to avoid detection. So if you make very, very small changes over a long period of time, it'll probably evade detection. Again, I'm not making these names up. There's one specific type of Data Diddler. It's known as a salami attack, and it's specific to financial systems. So how do you slice salami? Very thinly. That's the idea of a salami attack. The idea is when you're performing financial transactions, calculating interest, things like that, there's always some rounding that has to be done. And in a salami attack, you take off fractions of a penny from those financial calculations and you store those fractions of a penny in another account somewhere. And it's not very much money. It's a very, very small amount of money. But over a long period of time, it might equal a lot of money. Go and watch Office Space if you want to learn more about that one. There you go, that's the different types of malware. Now there's one final very important concept that we have to talk about related to malware, and that is this concept of zero day. So what is zero day? Well, a zero day is any of the types of malware that we've just talked about, It's when is it first released? When is it first known about? And day zero, the first day that it's released into the wild is known as a zero-day piece of malware. And the reason this is particularly dangerous, zero days, is because none of us know what these things are. We don't know what they can do. We don't know how to detect them. And so zero day malware, first time it's ever seen in the wild, very dangerous, can have a huge impact on systems and therefore very valuable pieces of malware. So there you go, that's all the pieces of malware that you need to know about.

LESSON 20: Lesson 212

============================================================

Most organizations nowadays have a huge number of different service providers that they use for all sorts of different services across the organization. And companies need to be able to understand, are their service providers actually doing what they're supposed to be doing? Are they actually providing the services that they're supposed to be providing? And are those services secure? In this section, we're going to dig a little bit into how companies can gain some level of assurance that their service providers are actually doing what they're supposed to be doing. Now, one way that a company could do this is they could conduct their own audit. So let's talk about some different audit approaches, and this doesn't necessarily specifically relate to service providers. So let's first talk about internal audits. An internal audit is an audit conducted by a company using its own auditors on its own systems. So it's entirely internal to the organization. That's an internal audit. An external audit is where an organization sends their auditors out to audit a service provider. So a good example of this would be if a customer was using Amazon Web Services, AWS, as their cloud provider, they would send their auditors to AWS to audit the AWS environment. That would be known as an external audit. And that's actually a really good example of why we have a third type of audit known as third party audits. So let's reflect for a moment. How many customers does Amazon have? millions. And if Amazon even let a small percentage, a quarter of 1% of their customers in to audit their environment, they would be overrun by auditors. And so what Amazon does in this case is they hire an external auditor, they hire a third party auditor, sorry, they hire a third party auditor to come in and audit their environment for them. This is known as third party audit. And the reason we call it third party audit is because there are three parties involved. There's the customer, the service provider and the independent auditor. And the way this arrangement typically works is that a service provider will hire an independent auditor to come in and audit their environment, produce a report, which can then be handed out to all the service providers' customers. And the reason that this is very popular nowadays for an organization to get assurance is that just imagine you're a company and you go to your service provider and you ask them, how secure are your controls? How secure are the services that you're providing to me? What are they going to say to you? I mean, it would obviously be in their best interest to say, oh yeah, our services are perfect, don't worry at all, best ever. But as a consumer, are you truly gonna believe them? And I suggest you probably shouldn't. And so that's why we use third party audit. And that's why it's so consistently used nowadays. It's not the company, the service provider itself that's auditing their services. It's an independent auditor, a third party that's doing it. Now, the most common type of third-party audit report nowadays is the SOC 2 report. So let's dig into SOC 2 reports and understand what they look like and how they fit together and what they provide to the consumer. Now, before I start explaining SOC 2 reports, I think it's useful to understand who is the organization that created SOC 2 reports. And the reason that I think this is useful is there's actually three types of SOC reports and then two subtypes, and it can all get rather confusing. So by giving you this bit of context, I think it'll help you to remember what these different SOC reports are. So what is the organization behind the SSAE 18 standard? In the US, it's the AICPA. And the AICPA is made up of financial auditors. So what do you think a SOC 1 report focuses on? financial reporting related issues. And to be honest, as a security professional, we don't really care about SOC 1 reports. Now SOC 2 reports, they focus on what are known as the five trust principles. And these five trust principles are known as security, availability, confidentiality, processing integrity, and privacy. Now, as security professionals, these are absolutely the things that we care about. So SOC 2 reports, very useful to security professionals. Now, a SOC 2 report actually contains a fair amount of fairly confidential information. So organizations, service providers don't want to just hand out SOC 2 reports to just anyone. And so that was actually why the SOC 3 standard was created. A SOC 3 report is a sanitized, summarized version of a SOC 2 report. And to be totally honest, as a security professional, we don't really care about SOC 3 reports because they're basically just a marketing tool. They don't tell us any of the information that we're truly interested in knowing to get the assurance that we need that the processes are working properly at our service provider. Now, just to make this slightly more confusing, there's actually two types of SOC 1 and SOC 2 reports. So there's a SOC 1, type 1, type 2, and a SOC 2, type 1, type 2. So what is this type 1, type 2 thing? It's actually fairly simple. here's what a type 1 report covers it covers design at a point in time what does that mean well design means the auditor will come in and look to see if the controls are designed properly it's basically just a paper-based exercise they're looking at the policies the procedures things like that to see if the control is designed at a point in time, so on like the Monday when they come in and conduct their audit. Does that tell you if the control is actually operating effectively? Not in the least. So type one just covers design at a point in time and frankly, not that helpful for us as security professionals. But a type two report covers everything a type one report covers. It covers design, but it also covers operating effectiveness, not just at a point in time, but over a period of time. typically a year and here's what a type 2 audit looks like the auditor in order to test that the control is both designed and operating effectively will typically come in and let's use change management as an example the auditor will come in and first see if the change management process has been designed appropriately So they'll first look to see if there's a proper policy and procedures and standards and so forth, if the control is designed properly. And then they'll test to see if it's operating effectively by looking at the entire population of changes that have occurred in, say, a year. and then taking a sampling of those changes and testing each of those changes to make sure that it followed all the policies, all the procedures, to make sure that the right initiation for the impact assessment for the change was done, that the right approvals were in place, that the right testing was done, that the right back-out plans were created, that the right regression testing was done, and all this was documented. If all that was done properly, then the control is usually found to be operating effectively. So in summary, as security professionals, what do we want? We want SOC 2 reports because they cover the five trust principles, and we want a SOC 2 type 2 because it covers design and operating effectiveness. One final, but very important slide here. And that is the different roles that are typically involved in an audit. This is probably very useful information for you to remember. So here's a quick summary of what are some of the major roles that you'd have involved in your compliance function and what would they do? So at the highest level, you have your senior management. And what are they doing? They're providing the right tone from the top. They're supporting the compliance function, making sure that it's well-funded, that it has the support of senior management, etc. Below them, you're going to have your audit committee. Your audit committee is made up of stakeholders from around the organization that can provide input on the compliance function, making sure it's looking at the right things, is aware of the right regulations, understands the right parts of the business, and so forth. Below that, your security officer is going to advise from a security perspective on what the audit should cover from a security perspective, how the results should be interpreted. You'll also have your compliance manager. Your compliance manager is specifically responsible for making sure the compliance function is created, staffed, conducting audits, running properly. And then you have your internal auditors and your external auditors. Your internal auditors are an organization's own auditors auditing the organization's own processes. And external auditors are auditors that are brought in. So this would be where a company would hire an audit firm to come in and audit their controls. Therefore, those are external auditors. And the reason that an organization would typically bring in external auditors is because they want an independent, unbiased view on things. So those are some of the major roles that you should remember related to the compliance function.

One of the most important aspects of security management is what we call governance and specifically we're going to be talking about security governance and how it should align with corporate governance, but before we can get into how we align the two, let's define the word governance itself. And so if we write this, we have the word governance. Now, what does this mean? In fact, the word itself, if you spit it up, comes from the verb to govern. And what does that mean? Obviously, we elect officials, for example, as part of government. When you go and vote for someone, We're voting for a government to ultimately represent us. The whole purpose of that government and those people that make up government is to govern the country, for example, in such a way so that it gains in value and it gains in the value of the assets that are part of that country like for example its people. And so effective governance is always focused on increasing value like we've been talking about this entire course. And so in a corporate environment, we have the same thing. We have people that are going to initiate and activate and drive what the organization does in order for it to govern the proper way to increase in value. And so when we look at who is accountable for that, it's the board of directors. The board of directors are there to govern or to at least direct how governance is done. within the organization so that ultimately the organization itself increases in value. Now the board of directors are typically not in the best position to oversee what the actual company does. and so they appoint a CEO that becomes directly accountable for what we call corporate governance. So corporate governance are all of those activities, initiatives, undertakings that the organization does in order to really increase its value and the value of its assets. So what does a company do for example as part of corporate governance? puts out new products, it acquires other companies, it improves how it does certain processes and functions within the organization, it strikes relationships with other companies, for example, all focused on increasing the value of the organization itself. Now what security does is called security governance. And so what are these? These are all of the activities, the initiatives, the undertakings that the security function drives and supports within the organization. And so what security does is called security governance. It's also called security management, which is the focus of this entire domain. And so what security does should be aligned and focused on corporate governance activities. So if you think about all of those things that the organization does, that are ultimately there to allow it to achieve its goals and objectives, that are always focused on increasing the value of the organization itself. Why wouldn't security be aligned with that? and contributing towards that and focusing and aligned with all of those activities that are focused on increasing value. I mean value is obviously a very important word in any organization. And it doesn't matter what industry you're in, what your goals and objectives are, any organization is always gonna be focused on this and increasing it. And so the CEO is directly accountable for driving corporate governance that ultimately is focused on increasing the value of the organization. Security has to be aligned with that. Why would any organization want what security does to go away from that or to take away from that? And so if you think about how organizations will establish and assign budgets and resources to the security function, You want that to return governance, corporate governance, so that we're focused on increasing value. The only way that this works is to align the two. But the two can only be aligned top down. And that is, you cannot just establish, for example, budgets and resources to security and hope that security does the right things that will ultimately focus on corporate governance. Security professionals aren't really focused on all of those activities that the corporate governance and those that are accountable for it are focused on. So the alignment has to be top-down. And that means that those that are accountable for corporate governance, the CEO, the board of directors, and other high-level executives, need to drive what security does in its activities and initiatives and undertakings. It cannot work the other way around. The two need to be aligned, but it has to be a top down. And this is where companies fail and security functions fail. When this alignment does not exist, but it's not driven top down. It has to be driven top down. And the way that we ensure this is by involving those people that are focused on corporate governance activities to drive what security does. And so that usually requires the establishment of a corporate governance committee, security governance committee that drives what security does. And what they do is they meet on a regular basis and they will prioritize and initiate and justify, for example, all of those initiatives that the security function is going to undertake that are ultimately going to be aligned with corporate governance activities. It's the only way that can work. And based on the importance of what security does today through its security management, it has to be established this way it has to be aligned this way and we're going to show you a slide that really shows the alignment and how important it is in how we do this in most organizations today so the answer to the question that you see up on the board is how do we align security with what's really important to the organization itself is top down, but it has to involve those people that are ultimately accountable for meeting the goals and objectives of the organization itself. And so what you see on your slide here is that those that are accountable, including the CEO, need to become accountable for what security does or does not do. And the only way that that can happen is for them to be directly involved and have that conviction that is required. in order for security to do the things that it needs in order to allow the organization to increase in value. What you see on this slide is sort of a structure that a lot of organizations try and get to to ensure that alignment that is top-down focused on goals and objectives of the organization itself. What you see at the top is the most important. and it's called Corporate Governance and Executive Oversight. And maybe the word oversight is not important enough to use there. It should probably say something like conviction. But what it means is upper management and the executive understands and believes very strongly the importance of security contributing towards the goals and objectives of the organization and because of that they end up being not only involved but also committed and convicted about how important it is that security contributes to those goals and objectives. for example compliance there is no way that compliance can be achieved unless the compliance requirements are actually driven top down as we've said before and then everything else that you see in that slide is what's required but the bottom line is that everything has to be top down like for example the next piece that you see there is security organization and what that means is where is actually security function placed in the organization and who does it report to. And because it's directly related to and contributing towards goals and objectives, the security function should report to the CEO and not, for example, the CIO who's only focused on protecting information. That doesn't work anymore. It has to be related to and contributing towards what the CEO is accountable for and then when you get to the bottom for example here you've got security solutions like identity management and physical security and network security and some of the other pieces that are part of what we call a security program. But look how we got to those. We got to those top down, based on commitment, involvement, conviction. from upper management because they understand and believe the importance of security and how it needs to contribute towards all of those things that equate to value of the organization, which we call corporate governance initiatives. But what security does needs to ultimately be aligned with those. But the only way that that alignment works is top down by involving those that are accountable for goals and objectives of the organization.
