We're still in domain number one, which as you know is all about security management, how we manage the security requirements of the organization and how we should always be aligned and contributing towards goals and objectives. In this module we're going to talk about how security controls should apply to people and what this is referred to as in the exam outline from ISE squared is personnel security policy. So these are policies and controls that apply to people. And if you think about where we have to apply security controls to people, it's at different times or parts of your employment, for example. From an organizational perspective, when you hire people or before you hire people, you need to do some things related to security like background checks, for example, or verify educational records. But then once that person has been employed and hired, for example, there are security controls that need to be applied to that person as they're doing their job throughout employment. And then of course at some point in the future, When the employment agreement is terminated for whatever reason, whether the person leaves on their own behalf or retires or goes to work for someone else, There needs to be certain things that need to get done from a security perspective, like removing user access and enforcing non-disclosure agreements, for example. And so those are the types of security controls that we need to think about in this section. There's also one more part of this that applies to people. and that is organizations today rely on a lot of third-party services like for example cloud providers or you could have services that are being provided by third parties so you may have to apply security policies to those third parties as well and what we want to remember about that is that the only way that you can achieve security and get them to conform for example to your security policies is through contracts agreements non-disclosure agreements and service level agreements and so on so that's really a summary of this entire module here within the security management domain. So if you start at candidate screening and at the time that you hire people, for example, One thing that you might want to do is give them job descriptions that include specific security capabilities or responsibilities that they need to take care of. If someone has something in their job description that is specifically related to security, they're going to take that seriously because they know that they're being measured on something that is in their job description. So if we've got the luxury of a job description, usually is a good thing from a security perspective. And before you hire people, obviously see here how you need to check things like employment history and their educational background. Verify that they have the skills that they say they have so that they can perform the job. Maybe in some cases you want to be able to do criminal checks and reference checks as well. Now these are all examples of security controls that obviously get done before you hire or at the time of hiring. And as we've said all along, what we need to remember about any type of security control is that they should always be cost effective. And so whether you do all of these or not is typically based on what that person is going to be doing for the organization. and the value that it represents. You always want security controls to be cost-effective which at a minimum means that the cost of the control itself is less than The value that you're trying to protect. That is always a really important goal to achieve in security. If a security control is not cost effective, we don't implement it. It's as simple as that because in that case you are not contributing to adding value to the organization and in fact you are taking away from it. Now during your employment with an organization, this slide shows some of the types of security controls that, again, organizations may implement that relate to people as part of their employment. So we've got acceptable use policies, non-disclosure agreements for certain people that are working with very sensitive information. You may impose some non-compete agreements on people, for example. And something that should apply to everyone at all levels of the organization is a code of ethics or a code of conduct that typically companies will impose on everyone. and so on. So these are examples of controls that are applied during your employment and in fact this slide continues on with that theme. Other examples like for example job rotation and some companies call this rotation of duties but it simply means that you take someone out of a job and you move someone else into it. And from a security perspective, that can be very effective. There are some industries, for example, like the financial industries banks may implement something like this and if you think about what kind of a control this provides if you move someone out of a job and you place someone into it It can be a preventive type of a control because if I know that someone is moving into my job, it may prevent me from doing something that is unethical or against policy, for example. And if someone is moving into my job, it can also be a detective type of a control because if someone moves into my job, they would be able to detect that I am doing something that is unethical or against policy or even illegal. So it can also be a detective type of a control. And finally, job rotation can also be a corrective type of a control. Because if somebody moves into my job and notices that I was doing something against policy, obviously they're going to fix it. So something like job rotation is one of those controls that we said is very effective. And we've said all along in this course, and you should always remember, that a good control or a good complete control is one that can prevent, detect, and correct. And something like job rotation is a perfect example of that. Other examples of controls that could be applied during your employment as a person that works within the organization, separation of duties. And that's a favorite one for a lot of industries and a lot of organizations. Some companies also call it segregation of duties. And it simply means that we separate a task so that it cannot be completed by one individual alone. It needs to involve more than one individual. and from a security perspective that's obviously very good because it can prevent fraud and it forces collusion doesn't it because if you want to do something that is illegal or against policy you're going to have to involve someone else and that in itself can be preventive or a really good deterrent but it can also be more detective and corrective as well so these are examples of really good controls and we've already talked about how how we implement access controls in the organization should always focus on need to know least privilege concepts And I think we know exactly what that means. We only give you what you absolutely need to know to perform your job function. And we only give you the least privilege that you require with that. So those are good controls that should always be applied. And finally, mandatory vacations. Again, not a control that you would see typically implemented in all organizations, but forcing people to take vacations at certain times of the year. would force that person to be replaced for that time that they're away on vacation. So it can be a control just like job rotation and therefore provide preventive, detective and corrective capabilities. are again good examples of controls. Now one thing that we need to remember however is that as we've said multiple times, controls always need to be cost effective. So whether you implement those controls throughout the entire organization, or whether you only do it where the value is such that it warrants those controls, is a decision that obviously the organization needs to make. But here's a perfect example. Separation of duties should not be implemented in all areas of the organization. You would only implement that where it makes sense from a cost effectiveness perspective. So any of these controls, as well as any other that we talk about in this class, should always be cost effective, meaning it costs less than the value that you're trying to protect. It's always value that drives security in any organization, not the risk, because the risk is something that can ultimately impact value. So value is what drives security. The risk is something that can impact that value, which is important to understand as well. But it's value that drives security. And so where the value is such, that's where we implement any type of control. Now these ones is just a summary here that you see on this slide of some of the controls. Anytime you see a slide in this presentation, it's not meant to be an all-inclusive complete list. we're simply giving you examples but maybe meaningful examples that you might see somewhere that is meaningful to you for example but onboarding when you're bringing people into the organization you know some of the controls that you would apply related to security are the ones that you see here and at termination as well termination doesn't just mean someone's gotten fired and therefore security controls need to be applied. Termination also means the job has been terminated for whatever reason, maybe they're retiring or or going somewhere else to work, but the employment contract has been terminated which forces security to activate and do certain things related to, you know, like removing user access and enforcing non-disclosure agreements. But again, only with those people where the value is such that we need to enforce those security controls. Now, we did mention at the beginning of this that personnel security policies are controls that apply to people. But the way that the organizations operate today, they rely heavily on third party services. And so a lot of security controls need to be applied to vendors and contractors and consultants, for example, that the organization has come in and work with certain things that obviously represent value. And so the only way that you can enforce security controls to third parties that don't really belong to the organization is through contracts and agreements and service level agreements that security needs to be involved in and enforce as well. But these, as we'll see later, is also something that requires us to really understand the requirements before we can write those into the contracts and then we can enforce. those contracts and that's why security needs to be involved really in the contract negotiation at the beginning of the relationship with third party vendors and services and so on. so that we can write in the requirements and then enforce those using security controls such as contracts and agreements. Now one of the things that we have to highlight here and reinforce is that people within the organization may be working with things related to privacy like personal data for example or PII which as you know is in reference to personally identifiable information And because these are driven by very strict laws and regulations that govern privacy, it's very important to ensure that the people that are working with anything that relates to privacy are aware of what they might do in order to make the organization, for example, not conform to privacy laws. So this is where security comes in. We need to provide education, awareness, training, but also make sure that the people that are working with any personal data have the skills and the right architectures to be able to enforce privacy laws and ultimately the compliance requirements that organizations would have to meet on a regular basis. And that's why all organizations should have a privacy policy that can dictate very clearly what the expectations not only from a compliance perspective is, but also the accountabilities and responsibilities that each one of those employees would have related to privacy and so on. And this is where security comes in. we need to make sure that we communicate those policies and allow individuals, whether they're accountable or responsible, to be able to meet the requirements of those policies so that ultimately the organization meets its compliance requirements related to privacy legislation.

LESSON 30: Lesson 222
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Let's talk about business continuity management. This is where we're going to talk about terms like BCP and DRP and BCM. And we're going to later in the next module, we're going to get into a whole bunch of other terms, three letter acronyms like RTO and RPO and et cetera. So it gets kind of complicated quickly, but let's start with something simple. Let's start with what is a disaster? Here's a definition of a disaster that I like. It's a sudden, unplanned, calamitous event that causes great damage or destruction. That's a disaster. And business continuity management is ultimately about identifying when these disasters might occur. What ones do you need to be worried about? Floods, hurricanes, earthquakes, fires, things like that. And then figuring out how do you actually deal with these major disasters. And so ultimately, the focus of business continuity management is about figuring out what disasters might befall an organization and how best to deal with them. But importantly, in business continuity management, we don't focus on every single business process or system within an organization. We only focus on the most critical and essential business functions. And so a big part of business continuity management is actually figuring out what is most critical and essential to the business. What is of greatest focus? And it's for these most critical systems processes that we create DRP plans and BCP plans. So let's talk through these. What are these things? Let's first talk about disaster recovery plans, DRPs. Disaster recovery plans focus on recovering specific systems or technologies. So let's take the payroll system as an example. The disaster recovery plan would focus on recovering the payroll system if that system blew up. And let's say it took six weeks for the disaster recovery plan to recover the payroll system. The DRP plan would be entirely focused on recovering that specific system. BCP plans, on the other hand, business continuity plans, focus on overall business processes. So let's continue with the payroll example. The BCP plan would focus on the overall process of making sure our employees are paid. And in the example I just gave about a DRP plan, I said it might take six weeks for the DRP plan to recover the payroll system. I don't know about you, but I'd be a lot less excited to show up for work if I wasn't being paid for six weeks. So while the DRP plan was focused on recovering the system, the BCP plan would contemplate how do we make sure we actually keep paying our employees to make sure those employees actually show up for work. And so it might be a really slow, really manual, really painful process, but the BCP plan might contemplate having a stack of blank checks, and someone would have to figure out how to still use a pen again, fill in name, fill in the amounts, and then use the system that I'm not even sure if it exists anymore, but I think it's called the mail system, and manually mail these things out. And so that's the key difference between BCP and DRP. DRP focuses on specific systems technologies. BCP focuses on the overall business processes. Now the final bit of terminology we'll talk about here is business continuity management, BCM. Business continuity management is the business process of making sure that all these BCP plans are created, all these DRP plans are created, that they're updated, maintained, tested, that there are people who, This is their responsibility. They have accountability for all of this, that this function is paid for, that we create, that we go through the BIA process that we'll talk about in the next module. So business continuity management is the overall business process of making sure all of this disaster recovery stuff is done within a business.

============================================================
LESSON 31: Lesson 223
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

What can companies acquire? All sorts of things, hardware, software, services, even people. But let's focus on services because nowadays companies typically acquire all sorts of different services to support their business. And security has a very important function to play in the acquisition of services. And that is making sure that these new services that are being acquired are actually secure. So let's think about where should security be involved in the procurement process of new services? When should security become involved in this process? And the answer is at the very earliest stages. Because when you think about procuring services, the first thing that's required is to actually first understand the business requirements. What does the business actually want to do with the services? What does the service actually have to provide? And it's at this earliest stage that security should be involved because you need to really clearly understand exactly what the service is and what it's going to do for the organization and therefore what the security implications are. For instance, if the service that you're gonna be acquiring is gonna be processing PCI data, payment card industry credit card data, that suddenly brings with it a lot of additional security implications or say it's healthcare data or personal information. These different types of data, these different services that are going to be required have very different security requirements. So the place that security starts with acquiring any services is to first understand the business requirements, understand precisely what the business is going to be doing with the system, with the service, and then making sure that all the correct security requirements have been defined. And we often refer to these as the minimum security requirements. What are the minimum security that needs to be provided by the service to meet the business's needs? These security requirements get documented in a document called the SLR Service Level Requirements. This is a document that will list out what are all the different security requirements that need to be provided by the service. And this SLR document will actually be used as part of the procurement process. So the procurement team that is going out soliciting bids and proposals by different service providers and then evaluating all of those different services will be using this SLR document as part of the procurement process to make sure or to evaluate the different service providers against the requirements listed in the SLR and help the organization choose a particular service offering that's going to meet all the defined security requirements. And in the next module, we'll talk about when an organization starts actually acquiring a service. How do these SLR requirements get translated into another very important document? the SLA.

LESSON 34: Lesson 226
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We just spent a bunch of time talking about all the different types of malware that are out there. Now let's talk about anti-malware systems. These are systems that are designed to try and prevent malware from getting onto our systems, or if it has gotten onto our systems, detect it and hopefully remove it. As we talked about related to malware, one of the most common types of malware is a virus. And as you may recall, the way that a virus is triggered is by a user doing something, clicking on something. And so one of the best ways of preventing malware is actually not any fancy technical systems. It's actually user awareness training to make the users aware that there's this thing called malware out there, there's viruses, and that they need to be a little bit careful about what they do with their systems and what they click on. In fact, one of the most common ways of breaking into a system nowadays is through phishing attacks, which is entirely reliant upon users clicking on something that they shouldn't. So one of the best ways of preventing malware is actually through policies, through training, through awareness to make users aware that they're not supposed to click on stuff. Now let's look at some of the systems that we can actually use to detect malware. The first systems we'll talk about are what are known as signature-based systems. Signature-based systems actually use a signature to detect malware. So what's a signature? While all the big anti-malware vendors out there, they're constantly monitoring the deep dark web for sketchy new pieces of malware that are out there. And every time that they detect a new piece of malware, they write a new signature, a way of fingerprinting or uniquely identifying that piece of malware. And then they constantly push out those signatures. And we all have to update our signature based system so that they can detect the latest, greatest types of malware. And signature-based systems are by far the most common types of systems to detect malware. But they have a really big Achilles heel, and that is, can a signature-based system ever detect a zero day? And the answer, of course, is no, because zero-day implies that it's the very first time that this piece of malware has ever been seen, and signature-based systems can only detect what they have a signature for, what they know about. And so there's no way that these systems can detect zero-day on the first day it's released. And that's actually where heuristic-based systems come in. Heuristic-based systems don't look for a particular signature. Instead, what they look for is they look at what the malware does. They look at what a file does and they try to determine, is this in fact a piece of malware? There's two major methods that a heuristic based system can try and detect a piece of malware, static and dynamic. So static means that the heuristic based system is actually scanning through the source code or scanning through the compiled code of the malware to try and determine, is it doing something malicious? Is it making certain function calls, changes to the registry, things like that. So that's static. The other method that a heuristic system can use is dynamic. And what that means is that the heuristic system actually runs the code runs the executable in a sandbox. And a sandbox, as you recall, is a protected, safe environment. And so the heostric-based system will run suspected malware in a sandbox and then watch what the malware does. Well, that's not as effective as you think it might be because most malware nowadays, most malware writers, they're well aware of heuristic based systems and especially sandboxes. And so they'll write their malware to check, am I running in a sandbox? And if the malware determines it's running in a sandbox, what do you think it does? It acts completely innocent. hi, I'm the nicest, innocent, most wonderful piece of software ever. And it just basically lies and it doesn't do any of the malicious things that it would normally do. And then as soon as it detects that it's no longer in a sandbox, ah, it jumps out and it attacks your system. So heuristic based systems, great idea, very difficult to implement. They're very susceptible to false positives and they don't necessarily catch everything. There's one other set of systems that we can use to detect malware. The first is what are known as activity monitors. Now, an activity monitor looks at the applications that are actually running, the running processes on your system. so this is really the uh the line of last resort because if an application if a piece of malware has gotten to the point where it's actually running on your system i mean it's installed at this point right so activity monitors are very much the uh the last resort but they can be useful so an activity monitor looks to see if a running process might be a piece of malware And then the final type is what are known as change detection systems. And as the name implies, they look for changes. Changes to what? Usually certain key system files. So this is commonly used on Linux-based systems, for instance. And a change detection system will look at certain key system files and will look to see if those files have been changed. Because that might be an indication that there's malware on the system, changing the system. How do they detect if there's been a change? Well, when you first turn them on, they will, and point them at a particular file that you want them to monitor, the system will hash the file. And then a few hours later, a day later, whatever, the change-based system will rehash the file and... compare the hash values. If they're the same, no changes. If the hash files don't match, if the hash values don't match, then you know there's been a change. A great example of this is Tripwire, very commonly used on Linux-based systems. Now, as I mentioned, the most common type of anti-malware system are signature-based systems. And one incredibly important thing that we have to constantly do with signature-based systems is Update them. You always continuously have to be updating the latest, greatest signatures or you're never going to be able to detect the latest malware.

============================================================
LESSON 35: Lesson 227
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Let's finish talking about business continuity management by talking about what are the primary goals of business continuity management. And as a reminder, business continuity management is the overall business process of making sure that we create our DRP, and BCP plans and we test them and we have people that are trained and know what they're doing and all those sorts of things. So what are the overall most important goals of business continuity management? Number one, and I'll give you a giant hint here. It's the same number one goal of physical security. The number one goal of business continuity management is safety of people. And this is ultimately what is most critical to do in the event of a disaster because as we recall a disaster is a major unexpected massive damaging event to the organization and so the very most important thing to protect is people People are the most valuable resources, assets of any organization. And without people, you can't do any sort of recovery. So the number one goal of business continuity management, safety of people. Next most important goal is to try and minimize the damage. So whatever major disaster is occurring, figuring out how you minimize that damage. Those are the two most important critical things to focus on as soon as the disaster occurs. And then a little bit longer term is figuring out how do you ensure the survival of the business. So those are the three goals of business continuity management. And one final reminder, what does business continuity management focus on? Well, only the most critical and essential functions or services within a business.

As part of security management, Security Forever has been saying that security is everyone's responsibility and that's certainly true. Now what's important for security to be effective, however, is not only to say that message to everyone in the organization, but that message really should be communicated by upper management. And the failure that we've seen in security over the last number of decades, for example, typically has been because that message that security is everyone's responsibility, has actually come from security, and that is bound to fail. The message itself has to come from upper management. In fact, it would be nice if it came from the board of directors, and the person that is acting on behalf of the board of directors, which of course is the CEO. But imagine how much weight that would have and the culture and the tone that would be created, for example, if the CEO said on a regular basis that security is everyone's responsibility. Now we know that that's true. However, it's very important for us to understand the difference between two very important words that are often misused and interchanged in security. And those two words are actually the words accountability and responsibility. So these two words are often used interchangeably when they really shouldn't because the two words responsibility and accountability are not the same, especially in how we use them related to security. Like we've said, you know, security is everyone's responsibility and that message needs to come from the top. However, those that are accountable need to remain accountable. In fact, still remain accountable. That's the difference between these two words. responsibility and accountability. In fact, when you look those up, if you are accountable for something you can never delegate that accountability to anyone else. You will always remain accountable. And so if you look at what security does for any organization, it contributes towards its goals and objectives that are always focused on increasing value. There are people in the organization that are accountable for that. and that would be the board of directors and the person acting on behalf of the board of directors who is the ceo so the ceo is really accountable for increasing the value of the organization i mean the ceo has only one statement in their job description And that is to increase the value of the organization and its assets. Security must contribute towards that. So while we say security is everyone's responsibility, It really remains the accountability of the CEO to take care of that requirement that security really contributes towards increasing value. And so when you look at the two words, it's pretty important for us to understand the difference and use them correctly in the right context. So accountability is something that you can never delegate to anyone else. You can delegate the responsibility but you remain accountable. And so what might be really important for us to remember as far as the exam is concerned is how these words apply to some really important functions in the organization related to security. So if you look at, for example, executive management and the CEO, for example, and the board of directors, they would always be accountable. That's the word that you associate with. that group or that role within the organization. Us, and we are referred to information systems security professionals. In other words, the security function. We would be responsible for ensuring, for example, that the right controls are in place, that we are implementing, for example, effective, efficient, cost-effective controls in the organization so that ultimately we are aligned with goals and objectives. So in this group of people that you see here in this slide, for example, most of them are going to be responsible, but there are some very important groups like executive management and the owners, for example, the owners are going to be accountable. for protecting what they own. Obviously assets in the organization that represent value to the organization itself. And custodians, that word custodian comes from the word custody. So you have custody of something that doesn't necessarily belong to you. It belongs to an owner somewhere else. And so therefore, you would also be responsible for protecting those assets that you have access to while they're in your custody. So they remain, for example, the accountability of the owner, but you as a custodian. a user of that asset would be responsible for protecting it. So users and custodians are typically the same group of people. They are responsible for securing what they have custody of or what they're using for an extended period of time that obviously owns or is owned by someone else. And auditors, very important function in the organization. They may ask you questions related to what auditors provide in the organization. And if we were to summarize that, it would be the word assurance. Auditors provide assurance to those that are accountable our stakeholders, for example, not only upper management, the CEO, the board of directors, but your stakeholders could be also external, like compliance requirements for regulators, Or your clients, your customers, your shareholders, for example. They want to know that we've got the right levels of security and that security is contributing towards value as well. And so auditors provide a very important function related to security being assurance and confidence. Now, how would you answer this question, which is obviously very important for us to focus on? Who is ultimately accountable for security and therefore protecting all of those valuable assets? in the organization would be any of these answers that you see here, but it should be in the order as this that I'm going to highlight for you. First of all, the board of directors. and then the CEO that is acting on behalf of the board of directors and then of course the CEO may you know structure executive management to take care of those accountabilities so any of these groups if they're in the list of answers you would pick for being accountable Now this question should be easy for us to answer. Who is specifically responsible for security? And this is what we've been saying for decades and that is everyone in the organization.

One of the most important aspects of protecting assets is that in most organizations now, assets need to be kept around for a very long time. In fact, if we're talking specifically about data and information, There could be a requirement for organizations to keep information around for a very, very long time. In fact, here's an example, health organizations. Health organizations may have a need to keep that health information around for over 100 years. If you imagine a baby that has a medical condition, for example, that health organization has to keep that information around for the life of that baby plus 20 or 30 years. And so that could be an extensive period of time. The problem from a security perspective is how do you protect that information for over 100 years. In fact, what guarantee is there that we're going to be able to read that information 100 years from now? These are some of the challenges that companies face in protecting their assets, specifically, for example, information. And so how do we keep that information around for a very long time? First of all, we have to understand the requirements. And this is part of what any company would consider to be their compliance requirements. A lot of it will be driven by laws. regulations, in some cases industry standards, but also in some cases it could be company policies management would dictate or understand that we need to keep information or records around for a very long time. But that requires security to be involved now and make sure that we have proper security controls throughout that long retention period, which is typically taken care of through our archiving policies. Archiving policies is how we keep information around for a very long time. Now data archiving then, these are some of the questions that you might see. What drives the data archiving requirements of an organization are going to be things related to, again, compliance requirements that are dictated by laws and regulations. and industry standards and company policies. But to keep information around for a hundred years requires us to answer some pretty good questions. Like for example, what media do you store it on? that's going to allow us to not only keep it around for a very long time, but also possibly access it throughout that long retention period, but also how to protect it. Certain media is easier to protect than others. For example, simply printing it and throwing it in a box shipping it off to Iron Mountain may not give that information the level of protection that it requires based on compliance requirements. And so if security is something else that we have to think about throughout that long retention requirement. What about availability requirements? Subjects for example have through privacy laws the ability to request that information that you're keeping on them and facilitating that is something that needs to be done by the organization as well. If someone says, I need access to these records, you can't say, for example, well, it's going to take us three weeks to be able to get those back because First of all, we don't know where they are or possibly we don't know what format they're in and whether we can read them. So understanding the availability requirements and how certain subjects or custodians or owners may need access to that information at a certain point throughout that retention requirement. is very important as well. The retention period itself again is dictated by typically laws and regulations. And so what we need to have is an overall policy that talks about retention and archiving But that needs to reflect on things that you see on the slide here. You know, how long do we keep that information around for? How is it secured through that entire period? And how do we facilitate access to it when people need access to it?

We've been talking about how we need to protect architectures and we said that security should be involved right from the start and throughout the engineering lifecycle phases, for example. But one of the things that we really have to worry about and think about is that a lot of our security that we depend on today is actually purchased from vendors. We buy a lot of products from vendors that provide security for us. We buy firewalls, we buy intrusion detection systems, we buy data loss prevention capabilities, we buy SIEM solutions for logging and monitoring. what we rely on from a organization's perspective from a security perspective relies on products that we buy from vendors now the problem is how do we trust what the vendors tell us because let's say we're in the market for a firewall for example every vendor out there is going to say that their firewall is the best one and how do we really know that that's true and so it'd be nice to have some sort of measurement system that would provide us objective and independent measurements, for example, of the security capabilities of certain products that we can buy from vendors. And that's what we call evaluation criteria. So if you look at This slide here it's meant to highlight the problem that exists today because every vendor out there is going to say that their product is the best one. Whether that's true or not based on our requirements is something that we need to be able to certify for example. understand fully. And so a measurement system for security products and their capabilities is what we call in the industry evaluation criteria. So these measurement systems do actually exist and they've been around for a very long time and there are three significant systems that exist that can provide these measurements that we need. And the very first one is called the Orange Book. The second one is the European equivalent or improvement of the Orange Book called the ITSEC. And the third one, which we're gonna talk about later, is called the Common Criteria. And this is really a historical evolution of how we've evolved in being able to measure for example security products that we buy from vendors. Before we get into the Orange Book and the European equivalent of it which we call the ITSEC. We need to understand these very important concepts that relate to this and those are certification and accreditation. And what you want to remember about these two concepts is that they're always tied together. Accreditation will always follow certification certification will always be followed by accreditation and here are the perfect definitions that you're probably going to see word from word but certification is the comprehensive technical analysis of a product or a solution to make sure that it meets your needs for example if we have to buy a firewall let's say we need to protect our network from the internet. The obvious solution that we would implement is a firewall, but there could be several firewalls out there that would meet our needs. Which one would be best? Well that would require us to go out there and take a look at the different firewalls that we've decided to look at and to certify that each one of them would work for what our requirements actually are. That's the certification process. We are certifying We're doing a comprehensive technical analysis of the capabilities of each of those firewalls to make sure that it meets our needs, for example. But then let's say that we find that three of them would work. We're not going to implement all three firewalls. we're only going to implement one and it's probably going to be the most cost-effective one but that is what accreditation is accreditation as you see here as definition is going to be the official management decision to actually operate and implement one of those solutions and accreditation is typically done for a set period of time. Management would decide that we're going to use this firewall for the next 18 months at the end of which we're going to have to go through the entire certification and accreditation process again. Now at that time we may find that the same solution that we have been using for the last 18 months is still the best one at which point we would re-accredit that solution for whatever we decide at that point. And by we I don't mean the security function. Accreditation is never done by the security function. It is done by management or the owners. And so that's a key distinction that we need to apply here. Certification security does get involved in. but the accreditation part is always done by management and for a set period of time. Now let's get into these three measurement systems that have been built to allow us to trust products that we buy from vendors. The very first one is called the Orange Book. And you can see why it's called the Orange Book. Its cover is actually orange. And it's part of what we call the Rainbow Series, a series of documents that have been put out by the Department of Defense in the United States. Each one of these documents and these publications have something to do with security. For example, there's a light blue book that deals with password guidelines. There's a red book that deals with network security. but the orange one or the one that has the orange cover is what deals with evaluation criteria or being able to measure the security capabilities of products so that we can trust them. Now one of the things that we have to remember about these products or these systems, evaluation criteria systems, is that they need to be objective and independent. They would not work if we allowed the vendors themselves to measure their own products. And so the way that this works is let's say we are a vendor that wants to sell our product and we've designed it and built it and we're ready to market it and sell it. we would hire a, for example, Orange Book licensed organization to come in and rate our product, and if it gets a good rating, then people out there are gonna buy it, not because we, the vendor, are saying it's a good product, but because the independent and objective measurement system says it's a good product. So these systems are really good, not only from a consumer's perspective, Because we can trust the ratings that their products get because they've been given by independent objective organizations. But it's also good for the vendors themselves because once they rate their products, they can compare them to other products that exist out there that maybe their competitors are putting out. and so they want to probably have the same features or better features that other products have from competitors. Now the Orange Book is something that was developed back in the mid 80s and if you look at what architectures and products looked like back then They look much different than they do today. And so a lot of people say the Orange Book is obsolete, but it really isn't from one perspective. First of all, the Orange Book only measures confidentiality. And there's nothing better out there even today to measure some product that you might be interested in that only addresses confidentiality. I mean, when you think about where the Rainbow series and the Orange Book came from, It came from the Department of Defense. They're obviously an organization that's really focused on confidentiality. So the one thing you need to remember about the Orange Book is that it only measures confidentiality. And the other problem that it has is that You know, if you look at what things looked like back in the mid 80s, networks weren't what they are today. And so this system does not work well with anything that is meant to run in a network. It's really meant for standalone, type of box architectures for example single devices those sorts of things that are not meant for a network. Now when you look at the classifications or the ratings that the Orange Book uses it's what you see here on your screen and we basically start at the bottom And as we go up we get more secure. So a product for example that is A1 capable would be a most secure product. In fact I don't think anything has ever received a rating of A1 because that system would obviously be the ultimate from a security perspective. from a confidentiality perspective. Most firewalls, for example, end up being at the B1 level. Most operating systems that exist out there typically end up being at the C2 level. And what you need to remember here is that as we go up, each of the levels will inherit all of the characteristics of the previous levels. So for example, if we go to B1, We have everything that's included in C2 plus they add what's listed there under B1 which is security labels. So B1 would include everything that's below it plus security labeling and now it's one level that for example we need to remember as far as the exam is concerned. at what level of the Orange Book is labeling first a requirement and the answer is as you can see there B1 and as you keep going up we have more security requirements that need to be met. But basically this is what the Orange Book looks like. Now if you remember the two problems that the Orange Book has. First of all it only measures confidentiality. And secondly, it's only meant for single box type of architectures that don't work well in a network environment. The Europeans came along and they looked at the Orange Book and they said, wow, this is a great idea. but as usual you North Americans don't do a good job of something so we're going to take it and improve it and that's basically what the ITSec is. Think of the ITSec as the orange book with those problems that the Orange Book has addressed. In other words, this not only measures confidentiality, but it can also measure integrity and availability requirements. And this, the ITsec, works well in a network environment. And what you need to remember about the ITsec is that one of the other improvements that they made is that there are two measurements that are given according to the ITsec. One for functional requirements. and one for assurance requirements. Because if you remember back to what we discussed as being good characteristics of any control, any control really needs to apply functionality really well. but also the assurance that it's making the right decisions. And I think the example that illustrates this very well would be a firewall. The functional requirements of a firewall is that it needs to be able to allow or block traffic. And that is the functional pillar, the functional requirement. But what assurance do we have that that firewall is actually making the right decisions on a regular basis is the assurance part of how we implement that firewall. So those two pillars need to be addressed. The other improvement that the Europeans made with this measurement system or evaluation criteria is that they said let's look at products and give them two ratings, one for functionality and one for assurance because you could have products that do a really good job of functionality but maybe not so much assurance or vice versa. And so it'd be nice to have two ratings and that's what this gives. Now what you need to remember is that the ITSec uses the same classification levels for functionality as the Orange Book. So what you just saw on the previous slide for example, the classification levels that apply to the orange book are the ones that the ITSec also uses for functionality. So we've got level D where there isn't much security. And then we get into the C level, C1, C2, and then B1, B2, B3, and A1. All right, so those are the functional ratings that you get according to the ITSEC. But then there's also E level assurance. ratings that they created as part of this and what you see on the next slide here are the E ratings. Now you don't need to memorize these but all you need to know is that we go from E0 which means that that product does not provide very good assurance all the way up to E6 which means that product does give good assurance capability and so as we go up we increase the ability of that product to provide assurance.

LESSON 43: Lesson 235

============================================================

The BIA process, business impact analysis. Ultimately, the BIA process is about identifying the most critical systems and processes within a business, determining how long those business processes and systems can be down before, the company is in serious trouble or out of business, and then determining what resources would be required to get those systems back up in a timely manner. So let's walk through how the BIA process actually works. What are the steps that are involved and what's involved in each of those steps? The BIA process can be a very long and arduous process, especially in a large business. It can sometimes take months to get through this process. Because the first step that's involved is identifying all the key stakeholders, all the owners out there that own business processes and different systems. And so the way the business, the BIA process typically starts out is by sending out surveys, asking to all parts of the business, what are your most critical systems and what are your most critical processes? And it's very much a data gathering exercise. And once those surveys come back, then there's a big validation step. Someone has to go through all of those surveys and see what did we miss? What else is out there? And so this typically takes a couple of rounds of iteration before all of the kind of most critical systems are out there. The next step is to ask all of those business owners, system owners, How critical is your business process? How critical is your system? And inevitably, every single one of them is going to say, wow, mine's the most critical process. Mine's the most critical system. I mean, what business owner is going to say, you know what? Yeah, what I do here, not that important. Honestly, I'm not even sure why I'm here. I mean, no one is going to say that. So everyone is going to say mine is the most critical process, which is challenging because not everything is the most critical process. So then the next step is typically some workshops. You get all the business owners in a room around the table. and you get them to argue, and you get them to figure out who truly has the most critical processes, which truly need to be up immediately, and which can actually be down for a period of time. And so a big part of this whole information gathering process is to determine how long these systems can be down for and how quickly do they need to be brought up. The next step in the BIA process is then to do a bunch of work. where you have to figure out, okay, you've told me it can only be down for this amount of time. Here's what it's gonna take to bring it back up in this amount of time, and here's especially what it's gonna cost. And in most cases, it's probably gonna cost a lot of money. So then one of the final steps of the BIA process is to go back to the business owners and say, okay, you told me your system can only be down for an hour. Here's what's gonna be required to make that happen. Here's all the architectural changes we have to make. Here's all the new systems we have to have online mirroring, replication, et cetera. And here's the cost of all that. And inevitably, what'll happen at this stage is the business owners will go, well, you want that much money? It's not that important. And so there's multiple rounds of iterations you have to go through before you finally arrive at, here's our most critical systems, here's how long they can be down for, and here's what we're gonna do to bring them back up. And it's out of this BIA process that we define these four critical measurements of time that we've been talking about. RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD. We're gonna come up with these measurements of time for all these different systems. so that the final part of the BIA process is that we can then take these numbers, WRT, MTD, etc., and we can start to create the DRP and BCP plans.

============================================================

SLA's, Service Level Agreements. These are the agreements that organizations have in place with their service providers. And these agreements define the contractual requirements that are in place so that service providers know exactly what they're supposed to be doing and how they're supposed to be doing it. and maybe not doing stuff like this. So service level agreements specifically define the different service levels, performance levels that are expected in the contract or that are expected to be provided. They define the governance that should be in place to make sure that every party knows who is responsible for what, the consumer and the service provider. The SLA will define the security controls that the organization expects the service provider to have in place. And this very importantly relates to a concept we've talked about already, which is accountability and responsibility. An organization cannot outsource its accountability. It can only outsource responsibility. And what that very clearly means, and this is why SLAs are so important, A system or data owner that has accountability for protecting data, they can't just outsource that accountability to the service provider. Instead, what they have to do is very clearly define the controls that the service provider needs to have in place to appropriately protect the data and that way the business owner the data owner that's accountable for the data can make sure that their service providers are actually doing what they need to be doing and that accountable person can actually make sure that they're not gonna they're not gonna throw it that's gonna get choked if something goes wrong These SLA documents also include components around achievement of metrics and identification of issues, essentially reporting. So how does a business owner actually make sure that their service provider is meeting the SLA is actually doing what they're supposed to be doing. Well, the way this works is the SLA contains components around reporting back to the business owners of this customer that here's how we're meeting the requirements of the SLA. Now you've probably heard terms like, well we've certainly talked about SLA, but you've heard of probably contracts and maybe even PLAs, privacy level agreements. How do all of these things fit together? Well, SLAs and PLAs are addendums to the contract. The contract contains all the overall business terms, price of the agreement, all those, you know, what happens if we want to back out of the agreement. All those sort of business terms are contained in the contract and the SLA, and if there is one, PLA are addendums to that contract. And let's tie in one final piece that we've talked about, and that is SOC 2 reports. The primary way that most consumers get assurance that their service providers are doing what they're supposed to be doing is not through having a custom SLA. Because for a lot of service providers out there, think cloud, the big cloud service providers usually will not provide custom SLAs. And so if a consumer really wants to get assurance that their provider is doing what they need them to do, typically that assurance will come through a SOC report. And of course the type of SOC report we want is a SOC 2 Type 2.

LESSON 51: Lesson 243
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We've talked about DRP plans, disaster recovery plans, in a fair bit of detail. These are the plans that we use to recover specific systems or technologies. Let's now talk about how we test these plans. Because if you truly want to know if a deer play plan is actually going to work, you have to test it. And there's a bunch of different tests that we can do. So let's walk through each one of them and understand what is it that we do in these different types of tests. The first test is known as a read-through or checklist. Here's what that is. This is the person, the poor soul that's been chained to their desk in the basement for the last year writing this plan. This is where they simply read through the document, go through a checklist, of all the major components that should be contained within that DRP plan. Do we have all the right people named? Do we have phone numbers for them? Do we have alternate contact methods? All of these sort of components, the checklist, the read-through is where you make sure all those components are actually in the plan. So it's an entirely paper-based exercise, typically just done by the person that was creating the plan. Once you have successfully completed a read-through or checklist, then you can move on to a walk-through. A walkthrough is where you put all the various stakeholders around a table. Who are the various stakeholders? Are they relevant stakeholders? Well, this would be like the system owner, maybe someone from HR, from legal, maybe someone from senior executive, from media relations. you put all the relevant stakeholders around the table and you give them each a copy of the plan. And then you simply walk through the document. And because you've now got all the relevant stakeholders that are experts in their various areas, reading through this document, walking through this document, This is a good way of making sure that no pieces are missing, that it actually makes sense. So again, a walkthrough, just an entirely paper-based exercise. Once you've successfully done a walkthrough, then you can move on to a simulation. A simulation, again, looks very similar to a walkthrough. You typically put a bunch of people around a table, the various stakeholders, But the key differences in a simulation is you actually create some sort of scenario. And there's usually a couple people at the table that are running this scenario. And they'll be saying things, I've done this, it's quite fun. So you'll say things like, okay, now CNN is calling, or okay, now the worm is spreading across the network. What do you do now? And all the various stakeholders have to be reading through the plan, be figuring out what they're supposed to be doing, who should they be calling, what should they be doing. But very importantly, a simulation is still just an entirely paper-based exercise. You basically just put a sign on the door to say that, you know, we're going through a simulation. You do not touch any systems whatsoever. entirely paper-based. The next is a parallel test. After you've successfully completed a simulation, you can move on to a parallel test. Parallel test is very similar to a simulation. You're coming, you're simulating some sort of disaster, but importantly, in a parallel test, you actually touch some systems. you touch your backup or your redundant systems, you do not touch production systems. So in a parallel test, you're going through some sort of scenario and you're actually touching, you're actually recovering data, you're making backups on a parallel system. The final type of test is known as a full interruption, full scale test. And as the name implies, in a full scale, full interruption test, you're actually affecting not only parallel systems, but production systems. So in a full scale, full interruption test, you're actually causing a disaster and you're checking to see if your DRP plan truly works. Now, some people will sometimes say, well, do you actually need to do this? Or isn't this very dangerous? Yes, it is very dangerous. You can absolutely cause outages. And yes, you have to do this because if you truly wanna know if your systems are gonna work, it's critical to do this. Let me share a quick story. This is a true story. There was a bank, I won't even name where they are, but there was a bank and they wanted to test if their backup power system was actually going to work. Now, a quick bit of explanation here. This is a bank that had an office tower in a downtown setting. And typically when you have a data center, an office in a downtown setting, the generators will be up on the roof to make them accessible. And the fuel tanks will be in the basement. And there'll be big pumps that will pump fuel from the tanks up to the generators. Now this bank had done all the other tests, including a whole bunch of parallel testing. They'd run the generators for hours. They'd check the switching equipment. They were as sure as they could be that this backup system was going to work perfectly. So they decided to do a full interruption test. So on a Sunday night, middle of the night, when there was the least amount of activity, they decided to test their system. They turned off the power. and right away the ups provided instantaneous but short-term power the generators started to spin up they started to produce power the switching equipment all worked perfectly everything kept running And a few minutes in, everyone was starting to pat themselves on the back, like, woo, go team. You know, that was pretty scary, but we seem to have made it. And then, all the lights went out. The building went black. What had happened? Well, turns out, as I talked about, the fuel tanks are usually in the basement. The generators are on the roof. The generators typically have what are called day tanks. Day tanks are small fuel tanks directly connected to the generator and as these day tanks run out of fuel it sends a signal to the giant pumps in the basement to start moving more fuel up to the generators. The signal worked perfectly. The problem was the pumps were not connected to the backup power system. They were only connected to the mains. And the only way that they ever found this out was by doing a full DR test. So this is why it's so critical to do these types of tests. And that raises an important question. When and only when should you do a full interruption test? Well, you should only do it when all of the other tests have been completed successfully. And very important, CY, this is a bit of CYA, you have management's approval. Because it is absolutely possible that you can cause damage to systems, you can take things down. So you need to have management's approval to do this.

============================================================
LESSON 52: Lesson 244
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

In this section, we're going to describe how we actually protect assets as they go through their life cycle. By this point, we've classified assets into their classification, which obviously portrays value, but how do we actually give them the level of protection that they require? The answer to this is simply baselines. Baselines, as you know, are minimum levels of security requirements and so if we have classification levels such as top secret, secret, confidential, All we need to do at this point is come up with minimum levels of security requirements for each of those. So we could have for top secret, we could say, all right, anything that is top secret requires two factor authentication, real time logging. 256-bit symmetric encryption and so for something that is secret we would have less security requirements and those are those baselines that we establish for every classification level that is part of our asset classification systems. The idea is as assets go through their life cycle whether they increase or decrease in classification doesn't matter because we already know what the minimum security requirements for each of those classifications actually are. So the owner always keeps track and keeps an eye on their assets and if they increase or decrease in classification we simply need to apply the controls that we have established as baselines for each of the classification levels. Now obviously as assets go through their life cycle they can be in different states and I'm sure you've heard of the different states for example that data can be in or information can be in. Typically these are the three states that data can be in. You've heard of this data at rest, data in motion which is also referred to as data in transit and some people would argue that there's a third state that data can be in and that is data in use. Now let's talk about data in use first of all very briefly. Data in use which means it's being looked at or processed is extremely challenging to protect because typically it needs to be in its native format in plain text for example. And so the only way that you can protect data in use is typically through what are called enclaves. And the idea of an enclave is simply a isolated area where that data is being processed. but nothing else can get to that area. So we use the concept of isolation that we've talked about before in this class where you've got an area of storage for example that no one else or nothing else can get to while that data is being processed. Now let's focus on the other two states. data in rest or data at rest and data in transit. Now if you think about what this means data at rest is simply data that's being stored for example in a file or in a database or and the way that we protect it is based on the risks that can impact that data. So if you think of a file that's stored on your laptop on the hard drive, you've got risks associated with confidentiality, integrity and availability and so how do we protect anything that has risks associated with confidentiality integrity and availability typically the methods that we use are access controls encryption and from the perspective of availability we add redundancy. So your typical controls that we apply to anything that is being stored at rest is what we would address from a security perspective. Now data in transit which is also referred to as data in motion simply means data that is moving typically across networks and protecting data that's moving in networks is particularly challenging as well but if you think about sending an email or some information across a network, the risks associated with this are actually the same as the ones that apply to a file or something that is being stored at rest. So if you think about an email message moving across a network, how do we protect that? Well the risks are the same. We've got risks associated with confidentiality. We've got risks associated with integrity. We've got risks associated with availability. So we protect the exact same way using good access controls We use encryption and we use redundancy to address the availability issue. Now if you think about data moving across networks, one of the best ways to protect that data is to use encryption. Now as far as network encryption methods, and those are the key words that you need to look for on the exam. Data network encryption methods, there are only two. We can use what are called end-to-end encryption, and link encryption. So there's only two ways to protect across a network and these are them. End-to-end encryption, link encryption. So let's look at each of these as you'll probably see some questions that relate to this. When you think about end-to-end encryption, it describes perfectly what we're doing here. We protect the data from end to end. So this is best for protecting data and its value. The way that it works is we encrypt right at the start and then the data will travel throughout each of the nodes of that network until it gets to its destination and that's where it's going to be decrypted. So plain text is only available at both ends, not in between. That protects data best. But what has to be visible in order for this to work because of the nature of our networks is the routing information has to be visible across each of the nodes of the network. So data is protected from end to end, but what has to be visible is the routing information and that's why we say end-to-end is best for protecting the data but the routing information has to be visible so we've got some animation here that you're going to see on the slide that describes perfectly how this works. And so you see that as that data travels throughout each of the nodes, it is protected because plain text is only available at both ends, protects data best. Now the other way to do things is what we call link encryption. And the reason it's called link encryption is because what we need to do here is we need to decrypt and re-encrypt the entire thing at every node along the way, at every link along the way. So the idea is, if I start the process, I'm going to encrypt the entire thing. And if you remember how we send units of information across the network, they're called packets or datagrams. But packets are really made up of two components. the header portion where the routing information is, and the data portion. So we encrypt everything, the header and the data portion. But then what happens is when the first node receives it, it will decrypt the information that it shares with the key that sent that information. It will look at the entire information, the header and the data, decide for example where it needs to go next, and then re-encrypt the entire thing, the header and the data, with the key that it shares with the next device along the way. So when that device receives it, it will decrypt the entire thing with the key that it shares with a device that just sent it to it. It will look at the routing information, decide where it needs to go next and then re-encrypt the entire thing with the key that that device shares with the next device along the way. The problem with this is that plain text is available at every node because every node needs to decrypt the entire information including the header and the data portion. So link encryption does not protect the data best because every link, every node along the way can potentially be a attack point. But maybe one of the advantages of link encryption might be that you can hide the routing information but only from this device to device or link to link so this does not protect data best but it allows us to hide the routing information so the question is why would you want to use this and the answer is if you need to hide the routing information between certain devices. It would be very difficult to achieve link encryption across a network that you don't control such as the internet but you could do something like this across your internal network if it was important to hide certain routing information between certain segments of your network. And so what you need to remember is that in end-to-end encryption protects data best. In link encryption, we can hide the routing information. Now an example of end-to-end encryption is a VPN solution. We all use those, but that's a perfect example of what we call end-to-end encryption. Link encryption and examples of those are very difficult to find but something that the industry has come up with to try and address the problems of link encryption which is what we're showing you here through the animation on this slide Because then not only is the routing information available at every node, but also the data is available at every node. So something that the industry has come up with is what we call onion networks. And if you can picture an onion in your mind, it's exactly what we're doing here. An onion has a whole bunch of layers, for example. And the idea here is that if I initiate the transmission of information, I need to do a lot of work ahead of time. to be able to figure out exactly how many nodes that information is gonna go through. And what I do is I build each layer up understanding how many nodes it needs to go through. The idea is as it travels through a network, each node will peel off the outer layer and only be able to see the routing information to the next And then that node, when it receives it, will peel off the next layer, look at the routing information that is only available for it to understand where it needs to go next. So the idea of onion networks is to really protect the data, but to also provide anonymity because each node along the way is not able to understand for example ultimately where that information is destined for and we also protect the information so that it's only available really at both ends. So it's almost like you know the perfect way to address the requirements of end-to-end and link without any of the problems that are associated. So we've got some animation here that I think describes this purpose of onion networks perfectly. And so you see that the device that originates this is going to have to do some work and understand, for example, how many nodes that information needs to go through, and we add that many layers of protection or encryption. And then as we send it to the next node, that node will only appeal off the outer layer, see the routing information and send it to the next device. Now that device will peel off the outer layer, see the routing information and forward it on to the next device until it gets to its destination. And that's where the data will be available. But the advantage of this is that devices along the way cannot see the data or ultimately where that data is destined for, providing not only protection for the data, but also anonymity. you

============================================================
