We've been talking about evaluation criteria and once again evaluation criteria are measurement systems that can be useful to us in the security industry because we can trust what the vendors are telling us because it's not them saying what the security capabilities of their products actually are. It's a measurement system that is independent and objective that tells us the capabilities of those products so that we can trust them We've already talked about how these are very useful, not only for the vendors themselves because it creates confidence in their products, but from a consumer's perspective, we can trust what these evaluations actually tell us because it tells us exactly the security capabilities of products that we might be interested in buying from vendors. Now the latest and the third evaluation criteria system that the industry has come up with is actually called the Common Criteria. The reason it's called the Common Criteria is because it was a common system that was developed by a number of countries that got together and decided to come up with a measurement system that could be trusted anywhere around the world. The idea is you could have a product from a vendor that is rated in Europe but we would trust it in North America simply because it's been done using a rigorous measurement system that is the common criteria itself and what's important for us to remember about the common criteria is that it's the latest system but it's also the best because it addresses a lot of the concerns and the requirements that we may have not only as consumers interested in buying certain products that relate to security from vendors but it also provides vendors with that confidence element that's very important to them. Now what's important for us to understand as far as the exam is concerned is not that it is a ISO standard and what the number is for example but we need to understand the process the flow, if you will, that this provides and how it provides all of that useful information that is meaningful for consumers and for the vendors themselves. But this slide does a really good job of showing us what is referred to as the common criteria flow. And what we need to remember are some of these elements that make up this entire process, this entire flow. And as you can see here, everything starts with something called the protection profile. And if you think about those two words, protection profile, it's simply a profile of what the protection mechanisms should be for a specific type of product. So let's use a firewall as an example. A firewall would have or firewalls would have a protection profile that really starts this process and that profile would simply list all of the security capabilities that any firewall should have. So it would simply list the requirements that any firewall should have. like two-factor authentication, VPNs, encryption capability, secure logging and monitoring. It would simply list what the security capabilities of that product should be. So there's a protection profile that exists for firewalls. That's a category of controls that there would exist a protection profile for. There's a protection profile for access control solutions. There is a protection profile for DLP solutions. There is a protection profile for SIEM solutions. It simply lists what the security capabilities of that type of product should be. Now the target of evaluation or TOE is the next item on your list here. That is simply the product that is being rated. So if we are a vendor and we've designed and built a firewall and we're ready to sell it, we would hire a common criteria licensed organization to come in and look at our product and hopefully if it receives a good rating then people out there are going to buy it again not because we say it's a good product but because the common criteria system itself says it's a good product. I mean that's the whole point of this. But our firewall that is being rated would be referred to in common criteria terms as the TOE, the target of evaluation. It's simply the product that's being rated by the common criteria. It's the product itself. Now the security targets, and this is an important one to remember from an exam perspective, the security targets are each of the specific capabilities that we've built as a vendor, for example, into our firewall so that how does our firewall do VPNs? How does our firewall do encryption capabilities? How does our firewall take care of authentication, how does our firewall take care of logging and monitoring? They're going to have to look at each of those capabilities in our firewall and determine how well they're handled within our specific firewall. This is the part of the common criteria that really creates some meaningful documentation because the licensed organized, the common criteria licensed organization that we've brought in to look at our firewall is gonna have to look at each of the capabilities that we've designed into our firewall. Those are referred to as each of the security targets, because we may have built some really good capabilities into our firewall, but maybe we didn't do so good in some others. All of that will be captured as part of how they look at each of the security targets. And as you can see here, when they're looking at each of the capabilities, security targets, they're gonna have to look at how each of those capabilities address functional, and assurance. You'll remember the two pillars of a well implemented security control, how it needs to address not only functional but also assurance. And so they're going to look at the capabilities, the security targets. That's going to create a lot of documentation. that is going to be meaningful for us because we may choose to put that up on our website so that potential consumers can look at that information and know exactly the capabilities of our firewall and they'll trust all of that documentation because it was created as part of the common criteria process itself. It's not documentation that we created as a vendor to look really good. It's part of the common criteria and it provides objectivity and obviously trust that is very important in how we sell our products. Now once they've looked at all of the capabilities of our firewall, the common criteria works in such a way so that our firewall will receive one overall rating and it's referred to as the EAL level. or evaluation assurance level. Now it's unfortunate that they use the word assurance. The word assurance there is not in reference to just functional and assurance. It's an overall rating that our firewall would get that would tell everybody out there the capabilities and how secure and the capability of our firewall is according to the system that the common criteria has created. And what you're seeing here on this slide are the seven levels that can be achieved. So the idea is every product will receive one of these EAL levels which tells people out there how good our product is. So after they've looked at everything, they will assign one of these ratings or EAL levels and as you can see here it goes from level one which really would mean not very secure all the way up to level seven which means it's been tested and proven to be something that provides security to a really good level. These are the EAL levels that you can get as a rating, a final rating according to the common criteria. Now pay attention here because what's really important from your perspective is to possibly memorize these levels and what they equate to because we know that something that is required as far as the exam is to understand what each of these levels are and you may see a question that could be for example a drag and drop question that asks you, what does EAL Level 1 mean? What does EAL Level 2 mean? In fact, all seven of these. But I think it's pretty easy to understand these. As you can see, for example, Level 7 is talking about a rating that is the best and as you go down we get less testing or less meaningful ratings all the way down to for example EAL1 where we talk about functionally tested so it may be important for you to memorize these and match them up with the different levels that are part of the common criteria.

We have defined what the word cryptography actually means. It means taking something and turning it into a secret. Now some of the most valuable and strongest algorithms that we use today such as the ones you've heard of like AES or triple DES or RSA they all do something very similar and that is they provide substitution and transposition services so in this section we're going to define what that actually means but if you think about the words substitution means that we take certain things bits typically and substitute them for other bits or take letters and substitute them for other letters. Transposition means we keep the same letters or bits but we move them around in such a way so that ultimately we turn something into a secret. What we need to remember is fundamentally the strongest algorithms that are used today that are embedded into all kinds of products and technologies and protocols to provide encryption services do exactly this. Substitutions and transpositions in order to take plain text and turn it into ciphertext. So let's look at this. First of all, substitution means exactly that. We take something and substitute it for something else. Transposition means we don't substitute, we keep what it is, but we move things around so that ultimately we provide again a way to provide secret services we take something and turn it into a secret here's a perfect example of what substitution looks like so if we have the word gubbins that we want to turn into a secret There's different ways that we can do it, but the idea of substitution is we're going to take each of the letters and substitute them for something else. If you watch the animation here, what happens is we can shift the alphabet by three characters. so that the A is represented by a D, a B is represented by an E, the C is represented by an F. So if we have the word gubbins, that we have to turn into a secret. Based on this substitution cipher that we've created here, the G would become a J. the U would become an X and so on as you see here because what we've done is we've shifted the alphabet by three characters which allows us to substitute each of the letters of the plain text into ciphertext. In other words, we've turned something into a secret. And before I move on to the next slide, here's the one thing that we need to remember. Simple substitution and transposition ciphers are referred to as mono-alphabetic ciphers. In other words, we only use one alphabet. And so simple substitution ciphers, just like the one that we've created here, do not hide patterns. And that's why we've chosen the word gubbins as an example. There is a pattern that exists in the plaintext. And obviously it's the two B's that the word gubbins has. And so even though we've used a simple substitution cipher, the two B's will produce the same pattern. patterns in the ciphertext or in the secret. And so what you need to remember is that simple substitution ciphers do not get rid of patterns. And the worst thing that you can have in cryptography is patterns. Patterns is what kills cryptography. And so simple or mono-alphabetic ciphers do not hide patterns because the ciphertext that you've created still has patterns which allows attackers to figure it out eventually. what the key is now here's what transposition is and we're going to use the exact same word as an example but transposition means we don't substitute each of the letters we're going to move them around in such a way so that again we create a secret that hopefully isn't too obvious for attackers to figure out So we move the G to that position and then we move the U to a different position and so on. So if we keep doing this with the entire word gubbins, we might come up with something like bin bugs for example but again we've moved things around so that someone looking at the resulting ciphertext doesn't immediately know that the real word that we've hidden is the word gubbins but here's the point that we just made. Even though we're using transposition here as opposed to substitution it does not get rid of patterns. Patterns are still visible. So when we had for example the two bees we still have the two B's in the resulting ciphertext. Does not hide patterns and as we said patterns are not good in cryptography. You want to avoid patterns. Here's another way that you know a lot of ciphers and algorithms out there take plain text and turn it into ciphertext. In this case, we're looking at something called the rail fence. And what you do here is you create different rails that are gonna come up with or build your fence, for example. So let's say we've got three rails here. and we write our secret message, which in this case is security through obscurity. But the way that we write it is that zigzag that you see up on this slide here. And then to come up with our ciphertext, we're gonna take each of the letters that appear in the first rail, and then we're going to put the next letters after that from the second rail and the third rail. So if you look at this, what it creates ultimately as ciphertext is what you see on your screen. Now somebody looking at that is obviously going to think that this is very secure. and it's not very easy to interpret, but the bottom line is we've used a different way to do transposition. We've still kept the same letters, and guess what? This does not hide patterns either. because the same letters and whatever patterns appeared in the plaintext will still be somewhere in that resulting ciphertext. So even though it looks like a really complex way to turn something into a secret, it does not hide patterns. And as we've said many times, patterns need to be avoided. in cryptography so that we don't have any weaknesses that result in how we implement cryptography solutions. Here's another way to look at how transposition works and this is a typical method that a lot of algorithms will use and that is a table. And what we do is we write our message into the table, as you can see here, security through obscurity, but then how you use the table to come up with your ciphertext is really what becomes the key in how you produce the resulting ciphertext. So we could go down the columns, which would mean S, T, U, C, and so on. And if we keep going, our resulting ciphertext is what you see here up on the screen. or we can be more creative. We can start in a corner of that table somewhere and go diagonally to come up with our ciphertext. But regardless, The way that we use the table becomes the key that has to be passed to the receiver at the other end to be able to read that message. But again, these are simple substitution and transposition ciphers that ultimately do not hide patterns the patterns will still appear no matter how we've used this table the same patterns will always still appear. Now the last thing that we're going to talk about here in this section is that obviously cryptography algorithms will use each letter one at a time But that's how we've shown you what substitution and transposition actually looks like. Real cryptography does not use letters in order to turn something into a secret. It will always use bits. So if we were really to encrypt the word Gubbins, any real cryptography algorithm would actually take those letters, turn them into the bits, the zeros and the ones that represent those letters and then substitute and transpose the bits of information. So if you think about how we've got data that is represented by bits, zeros and ones, how you work with those bits is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous. Synchronous typically means we work with each bit synchronized through clock signals, for example. and asynchronous simply means that we use possibly chunks of bits and the input is dictated by the user somehow. So that's the difference between synchronous and asynchronous. Synchronous is dictated by clock signals, asynchronous is dictated by some input that is required by some other element or entity.

you We just talked about BCP plans and DRP plans. Now, let's talk about the numbers that drive the creation of these plans. RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD. Now, before I get into explaining exactly how we come up with these numbers and what they mean, It's very important to understand one thing about them, and that is all of these numbers are a measurement of time. Minutes, seconds, hours, days, weeks, months, years. Every single one of these numbers, RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD, they are all measurements of time. So let's get into explaining what each of these numbers means. And to explain this, we have a little diagram here. And this diagram shows time going forward. So we're starting off as business as usual. Everything is working perfectly. And now time starts rolling. And the first thing that happens is disaster strikes. A disaster has occurred. The first measurement, RPO, is what's known as the maximum tolerable downtime. And it's actually a measurement of how much data you're willing to lose as a measurement of... time. So how many hours worth of data? How many seconds worth of data? How many minutes worth of data? RPO is a measurement of maximum tolerable data loss as a measurement of time. And what's important to understand about RPO is that it's actually something that you have to plan for ahead of the disaster occurring. Because if you've said your RPO is only 10 minutes, you're only willing to lose 10 minutes worth of data, That means you have to architect your systems in a particular way. You can't, for instance, just be doing a nightly backup every 24 hours because then what would your RPO be? What would your maximum tolerable data loss be if you were only backing up every night? Well, it's up to... 23 hours and 59 minutes. So RPO is all about how you architect, design your systems before a disaster occurs to only lose a certain amount of data as a measurement of time. Now, the next number, our TO, Recovery Time Objective, is a measurement of how long does it take from when the disaster occurs to when you're up and running at some defined service level. So for instance, if the disaster was your primary data center blew up, Your recovery point would be how long does it take to get up and running in your backup data center. And also RTO is a measurement of how long does it take you to get to that defined service level. The next measurement, WRT, Work Recovery Time, is a measurement of how long does it take to get from your recovery point all the way back to business as usual. And WRT is really a measurement of how long does it take you to do all your validation and checking to make sure that all the systems that you're bringing back online are working correctly. The final number is what's known as the MTD. And this is the most important number. The MTD is your maximum tolerable downtime. It's the maximum amount of time that a business process or system can be down before the business is out of business. And so, MTD is made up of your RTO and your WRT, or to put it another way, your RTO and your WRT together have to always be less than whatever your maximum tolerable downtime is. And we'll talk again about MTD when we talk about incident response, because MTD is a very important number that we use in that process for when we decide whether or not we're going to declare a disaster. And the final piece I'll mention here is the way that we come up with these numbers, the process we go through to come up with RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD is the BIA, the Business Impact Assessment. And we'll talk about that one next.

In this module, we're going to talk about the importance of applying and enforcing secure coding guidelines and standards within the organization. It's really important for developers and the development environment to use the latest standards and guidelines that ensure the development of secure applications and what we call this is secure coding of course and so it's important for developers and the analysts and others that work in your development environments to understand what secure coding actually is. And the industry has really come up with frameworks and other tools that allow organizations to employ what we call secure coding guidelines. That means the developers and others at work in this environment are aware of some of the most common vulnerabilities and exploits that are typically available through applications. And so if we develop applications and design them in such a way to address these in the first place, then overall the applications that we're ultimately using in the organization are going to be more secure. But that means developers and analysts need to understand some of the exploits that you see here up on your screen. So for example, covert channels. I know we've talked about that before. But the way that we develop the functionality and intelligence in applications should prevent any sort of covert channels from existing. And of course you remember that there are two types of covert channels. storage and timing and anything related to applications would probably involve storage cover channels, but if we develop and design applications to address those. It's a better way to address security overall. We also need to address something that we've had a problem with ever since we started to develop applications. and that is what we call buffer overflows. And the idea of buffer flows, we're gonna talk about in the coming slides, but basically when you look at what applications do, they take input, we process it and we provide output. But when we do that, we have to design the application in such a way so that it uses storage in order to be able to take that input and process it and provide output. And so we have to allocate certain storage for data and other things to be handled within the application itself and its logic. We call those buffers. The problem is we have to set up the buffer to be the perfect size. But then once we're using those applications, if somehow that application is subjected to much more information than the buffer was set up to handle, then we may have what's called a buffer overflow condition. And if you look at the history of applications, it's not uncommon for us to see dozens of new buffer overflow attacks that appear in the span of just a few weeks. And unfortunately the only way to address this is to patch the application for known buffer overflow attacks. But by doing that you may be addressing the ones that we know of, but you may be introducing a whole bunch of new ones that we don't know about. And so buffer overflows is a problem that we've had for decades ever since we started to develop applications. And unfortunately, it's not really a proactive approach to how we prevent them. We have to keep applying patches and changing the logic. and it would be nice to have dynamic buffers that we can use in applications but that's just not something that we can use and so we have to constantly apply new logic to be able to prevent all of those as you'll see. Some of the other issues that secure coding really needs to address are what we call object reuse and if you remember back to when we discussed the concept of object reuse it simply means the ability to overwrite storage where we've written for example secure and sensitive information. The idea is most applications don't have the ability to overwrite storage where we've kept sensitive information. it would be nice to be able to do that automatically. I don't know any operating system or application that has this functionality built in where once we write some sensitive information to an area of storage, we are able to overwrite it automatically so that further subjects that come along and use that same storage would not be able to interpret that sensitive information that we may have left there. So object reuse is the ability to overwrite sensitive information that we've left in certain storage areas within the application itself. We also have what's called executable mobile code. And if you think about those words, mobile code is simply code that moves. This typically happens when you click on a link on a website, for example. And what happens at that point is that the code that needs to be run is downloaded to your machine and that's where it's executed. The problem obviously with this is that that code may be malicious and so it would be nice to be able to look at that code before we let it run in our production environments and so one of the ways that we can address executable mobile code is to test it in what we call sandbox environments and you'll remember what a sandbox environment actually is it's simply a safe play area that we establish as part of that architecture where we can test certain things to make sure that they are not malicious. So anything that is mobile, executable code that we don't know whether it's malicious or not we would want to test in a sandbox environment before we let it loose in our production environments. The next item that you see on your list there is something that we've described in a different module. It's called TALK2 and those letters really stand for time of check versus time of use. It's when control information is used to make security decisions and control information is useful in things like access control and to enforce other security requirements. But the idea of this is if those parameters change, between the time that we actually look at them and when they're actually enforced. If there's a delay between those two periods, it may lead to problems related to exploits and vulnerabilities. And I think one of the examples that we always use to describe what this is all about is if you come in in the morning and you log into your network At that point is when you're given access to network services. But let's say the network administrator, you know, an hour later deletes your user ID. When is it that that system is going to make sure that you're still a valid user on that network? Well, it's the next time you log in. but as far as the network is concerned you still have privileges across the network but you shouldn't because your user ID has been deleted and so that's a problem related to time of check versus time of use and that's just one example of where this might happen where again control information used in order to enforce security rules but there's a delay between the time that we look at those parameters and the time that we actually enforce them. The next item that you see there is back doors, also referred to as trap doors, sometimes also referred to as maintenance hooks. And these typically are put in by developers to make their jobs easier, especially through the development process. so that they can go into an application and fix things as they need or to troubleshoot very quickly. So these are legitimate ways for developers to handle problems but typically during the development process. The problem is when these applications are put into production and those back doors and trap doors still exist. If people figure out how to use those back doors to get into the application then they may be able to bypass your normal access controls that everybody should be subjected to. And we've seen all kinds of examples of back doors and trap doors that have existed in not only operating systems but also systems and applications where you might press a certain key sequence and you're into some portion of that architecture that you shouldn't be able to get into. That's an example of back doors and trap doors. Problem is when we buy software we are typically not given the source code to be able to identify whether any back doors or trap doors actually exist. And so we're really relying on the vendors to provide code and applications that are free of back doors and trap doors. The next one. And again, these are just examples of what developers and designers in our programming environments need to be able to address as what we call secure coding guidelines. Typically these are vulnerabilities that appear and exist in every application, every system, so we need to address them. as easily as possible or as good as possible. Malformed input simply means not doing a good enough job of data and input validation because we don't always think of all of the different ways that we need to validate input. And if you look at the most common attacks that exist out there, especially with web applications like cross-site scripting, SQL injection, cross-site request forgery. You know, they're all really due to one problem and that is inadequate data validation or inadequate input validation. so it's pretty important that we think about those requirements when we're designing and building the applications so that we don't have to address them later but as you know it's pretty difficult to think of all of the different ways that attackers can modify our input or inject certain things in our input that is already trusted by application and that's what leads to a lot of the exploits and vulnerabilities that we experience today. The last one that you see there is a term that the industry has been using for the last number of years. and it's called citizen developers and what it really means is you know all of us today have some powerful tools available to us that really make us developers and the ability to use some of these tools is very dangerous unless we are given the right levels of education and awareness and understanding of how those tools can be misused and can be used to provide exploits and vulnerabilities. perfect example is SQL I mean these are tools that we typically give right to the users to be able to allow them to query and interface with their own databases but when you look at some of these very powerful tools like SQL A lot of the people using those tools are not aware of secure programming and what some of these commands may lead to from an exploit or a vulnerability perspective. And so what security needs to focus on is providing the right levels of awareness and education to the people that we call citizen developers, normal users that have access to very powerful tools that provide opportunities for attackers. and that's what we call citizen developers. Now, we did talk about something called buffer overflows. This is one of the biggest problems that we've ever had. I mean, ever since we started to develop applications decades ago, we've had this problem of buffer overflows and as I mentioned earlier buffer overflows simply means we have to establish a size for the buffers that are used really to allow input and process that input which is basically what's happening any application the problem is if we've set up a buffer to be this big but somehow when that application is being used it is subjected to this much information how does the application handle the overflow amount is or could be a problem and and if that attack has been done creatively it may allow the attackers to put that overflow amount in an area where executions are run according to that system which may allow them to elevate their privilege levels And so we've got an animation here that really describes what this problem looks like. So if you look at Program B instructions and data, that's what we've allocated in storage for Program B. But Program A has been influenced, for example, by the attacker. And what they've done is they've subjected program A to much more information than its buffer, for example, is built and capable of handling. That means the yellow area that you see on your screen there is the overflow amount. And if the overflow amount That includes executions, like clever executions that the attacker has put in to overflow into program B that may allow that attacker to elevate their privilege levels and be able to do things that they shouldn't. So that's what we call a buffer overflow condition. And like we said before, happens a lot. We've seen all kinds of examples. It's one of the biggest problems that we've ever seen related to applications and secure coding that we need to address. The problem is You know improper bounce checking is what really causes buffer overflow conditions, so the proper fix for this is is to properly implement and enforce bounce checking but that's easier said than done and so when you look at the guidance that exists out there for preventing buffer overflows it's what you see here on this slide so doing proper bounce checking improving your software development process so that the developers and the designers are actually aware of not only the ones that we know of but possible ones that could result in how we've set up how buffers are being used within our applications. you know checking for example and detecting you know things that are happening in your runtime environments may allow us to react before these things become problem And of course, again, educating the developers so that we're always using the latest tools that maybe have some capabilities built in to prevent buffer overflows is really how we address this from an industry perspective. Now one of the other really important things that we need to address today in our development environments is the use of APIs. And you'll know what API stands for, Application Programming Interfaces. What we create today are complex environments that require a whole bunch of different components of applications to talk to each other. And the way that typically these components talk to each other is through what we call APIs. Think of APIs as the go between the translators that really allow different components of applications to be able to talk to each other. So APIs will pass a lot of data between components. And what you see on your screen here are the two most commonly used API formats and protocols that exist out there, REST and SOAP. They are typically different. and companies will choose, for example, what may make more sense for them to use. But the difference here is that Rest is more lightweight, whereas Soap has more security capabilities built in. But regardless, as far as the exam is concerned, you don't have to go and learn everything about rest and soap but just understand that there are a lot of vulnerabilities associated with each and if you're going to use these as your API formats for example in your web environments it's pretty important for the people that are designing and developing how those different components talk to each other to understand the vulnerabilities that exist with these and address those in how we implement. Implementation of these is always what dictates whether they become secure or not. And if you look at the industry, there is guidance that is provided in how we protect any API format including REST and SOAP. And what you see here are some of the guidance that people out there, the experts, will give us, you know, do proper authentication and identification of any tokens that are passed by using those APIs. We use encryption to protect the confidentiality of very sensitive information. We do adequate data validation as much as possible so that we prevent injection and other problems related to input. We use gateways that include applications that can look at what's being passed and enforce security rules. enforce quotas and throttling so that attackers cannot impact the volume and anything else that's being passed. And of course, just like anything else, especially related to applications, it's pretty important that we do the proper level of testing and validation to ensure that our applications and APIs that are talking to different components are doing it in the best way possible which means the most efficient way possible but also the most secure way possible. And so here is what as a summary we can provide as secure coding practices. I mean this is really a summary of what guidance that is provided out there really tells us to be able to address in any development environment so that ultimately your applications are as secure as they can be. We do adequate data validation or input validation. We do good authentication and password management. We do good session management. We use cryptography to address the five services that it can. We handle errors properly so that it doesn't provide any problems related to security. We configure the way that APIs and other components talk to each other to provide the ultimate security capabilities. We protect not only the application but all of the other components that make up the entire architecture. For example, the database and the data and the hardware and the operating system. And of course, one of the last things that they talk about is how we also need to do secure memory management, how memory is used within the applications and the systems. need to be done in the most secure way possible. So this is a summary of some of the things that your developers and designers need to be aware in what we call secure coding guidelines and practices. In other words your developers need to also be very aware and knowledgeable in security these days for organizations to produce and use secure applications and systems.

Music playing We just spoke about risk management, which is the overall process of where we identify our most valuable assets. We identify the risks associated with those assets, and then we determine how we're going to treat those risks. Let's now dig into the second step, risk analysis, where we identify the risks. Now, I'm gonna have to apologize here. I have a bit of a fetish for Venn diagrams and there's far too many of them in our slides, but here's another Venn diagram where we talk about what do you need to have a risk? Well, in order to have a risk, you need to have an asset. You need to have a threat that applies to that asset. And finally, you have to have a vulnerability. Where you have an overlap in all those, an asset that has a vulnerability that there's an applicable threat out there for, that's where you have risk. It's where you have significant exposure to the threat or the vulnerability. Now let's talk through these terms threats, vulnerabilities, impact, and probability and define each one of them and understand what they mean. And we'll start with a threat. What's the perfect definition of a threat? Well, a threat is any potential danger. Anything that could harm an asset within the organization. So this could be things like acts of nature, force majeure. It could be fires. It could be earthquakes. It could be threat actors like hackers. Any of these are examples of dangers. So then now, what's a vulnerability? Well, the perfect definition of a vulnerability is it's a weakness that exists. So this could be an unpatched system. It could be a facility that's susceptible to flooding. It could be... a system that's been misconfigured. These are all examples of vulnerabilities or weaknesses that exist. Next, we have the impact. The impact is what's the negative thing that happens to the organization? And the impact could be just about anything. It could be damage to reputation. It could be financial. It could be confidentiality, integrity, availability. It could be legal, privacy. Any of these things are examples of impacts. And the final piece we have to look at is what's the probability or the likelihood that this threat will exercise a given vulnerability and a risk will occur. So probability, likelihood, the degree of exposure, these are all synonymous ways of saying that what's the probability, the likelihood this will occur? Now, the final bit of terminology I want to introduce to you here is that we only have risk when we have a significant exposure to a threat or a vulnerability. We don't have a risk unless we have a significant exposure. So remember that terminology. As part of risk management, I talked about how we can use quantitative and qualitative analysis. Let's just review and go into a little more detail of what that means. So qualitative, as you recall, is a relative ranking system. It's where you make up names like high, medium, or low, or you can even use numbers. You can use like one, two, three, four, five, where five is a low and one is a high, or the opposite. It doesn't matter what you choose. It's a relative ranking system. And then you decide something is higher or lower than another thing. And I'll give you hints on some terminology if you're ever talking to management about this, okay? I mean, relative ranking system means essentially that you made it up, but you never wanna use that terminology. It sounds bad. So I'd suggest the term that you use is professional judgment. Sounds much better. Okay, now let's talk about quantitative analysis. Quantitative analysis means you use numbers, but very specific numbers, monetary values, like Canadian pesos. So those, anything where you're using dollars, pesos, anything like that is quantitative analysis. And as I mentioned before, quantitative analysis is what management loves. It's great to know exactly how much in dollars a risk is going to cost us, but it's often very difficult to come up with quantitative values. And in the next module, we'll talk about the calculation we use to perform quantitative analysis, the ALE calculation.

LESSON 72: Lesson 264

============================================================

backup and storage strategies. These are different techniques that we can use to backup our data. So if we have a failure in one of our systems, one of our hard drives, we can recover our data. Now we're gonna talk about different types of backup strategies like incremental and differential. But before we explain those, we have to talk about one very important bit. It's known as the archive bit. Now in an operating system, we have some metadata, data about data for each file. You can see this in Windows if you write or in any operating system. If you right-click on a file, hit Properties, it'll list last creation date, who the file was created by, creation date. This is all metadata. One of the pieces of metadata that's stored for every single file is the archive bit. And the archive bit is simply set to either one or to zero, one binary bit, that's all it is. And if the archive bit is set to zero, it means no backup is required. Whereas if the archive bit is set to one, it means the file needs to be backed up. So keep that in mind because this is a key difference. How we treat this archive bit is a very important difference between incremental and differential backups. To explain incremental and differential backups, we have a simple little graph here. It shows you the amount of data that's backed up on this axis, and the days of the week. And a lot of backup strategies start on, say, a Sunday, so let's start on Sunday night. With an incremental backup, you start the first day of the week, typically Sunday, with a full backup. So we'd back up all the data. And what do we do with that archive bit? Well, for all these files that we've backed up, we set the archive bit to zero. Now, Monday morning rolls around. People come into work, they start modifying, creating files. For every file that is created or modified, the archive bit is set to zero. One, meaning the file needs to be backed up. Monday night rolls around. What do we back up? Well, we back up all the files that have been changed on Monday. And in an incremental backup, we reset the archive bit to zero. So for all the files that we backed up that night, we reset the archive bit to zero. Now, Tuesday morning rolls along. People come into work, they create modified files. For any new modified files, those files, archive bit is set to one. Tuesday night, we back up all the changes for Tuesday. So in incremental backups, it keeps going like this. Every single day, we just back up the changes that have occurred that day. So essentially what we're backing up is all the changes since the last incremental backup. So that's incremental. Let's look at differential, and then we'll talk about the differences between these. So here's differential, same exact graph, starts out exactly the same way. With a differential backup, you do a full backup. And when you do that full backup, you reset the archive bit to zero for all of those files. Monday morning, people come into work, start creating, modifying files, those files archive bit gets set to one. And so Monday night, you're backing up all the changes since the last full backup looks exactly like incremental so far. But here's the key difference. in a differential backup on Monday night during the first backup, you do not reset the archive bit. You leave the archive bit set to one. And so now, Tuesday rolls around. People start coming into work, modifying files, and now what do you backup Tuesday night? Well, because you didn't reset the archive bit on Monday, you're backing up all the changes that occurred on Monday and all the changes that occurred on Tuesday. So you're backing up more data. Same thing Wednesday, you're backing up all the changes that occurred on Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday. And so every night, the amount of data that you're backing up grows. So what are the pros and cons of these two different strategies? Pros of incremental. It uses less data overall. You can see you're using less data. And the nightly backup jobs for most of the week run much faster. But the big disadvantage of incremental is that if you have a data loss and let's say that you have it on say like Saturday night, how many tapes do you have to pull? Well, you have to pull the full backup and then every single incremental up to the day that you had the failure. So how many tapes would you potentially have to pull? up to seven. Whereas in differential, it's kind of the inverse. What's the maximum, what's the big advantage of differential? It's how many tapes you'd ever have to pull. And what's the maximum number of tapes you'd ever have to pull? Well, it doesn't matter when you have a failure. The maximum number of tapes you'd ever have to pull is two. the full backup and the most recent differential. So the big advantage of differential is the recovery time is the fastest, but the downsides uses a lot more data and the backups each night get longer and longer as the week goes on. So here's a summary of the different backup strategies. And by the way, a good way of summarizing differentials backups is that you backup all the changes since the last full backup. And here's that summary of the different backup strategies. Now, I haven't talked about mirroring yet, so let me talk about that one here. What's the idea of mirroring? Well, mirroring is where you make a full and complete copy of a system's data. And the important part with mirroring is that you do not compress the data. Most of the time when you're doing backups, in order to make the backups fit in as little space as possible, you're compressing the data. And that compression stage actually takes a fair bit of time. And the same thing when you read the data back off the tapes, the decompression can take a while. So in mirroring, there is no compression. It's just an exact copy, which means that the backup time, very fast, the restore time, very fast. But the amount of storage required because there's no compression, very, very high. Full backup, we already talked about. You backup all the data. And what's the difference between full backup and mirroring? Well, in a full backup, you are backing up, in both of them, you're backing up all the data. But in a full backup, you are compressing the data. So that means it's quite slow to back up all the data like that. And the restoration time's decent. And we already talked about incremental and differential. So just remember differential ads, you're backing up since the last full backup. And incremental, you're backing up since the last incremental backup. Now, a couple other pieces to talk about here. These are different sort of backup strategies. First, offsite storage. This is the idea that when you're creating backups, backup tapes for instance, you don't wanna store the backup tape right beside the primary system. Because if your primary system has some sort of failure, power outage, it burns down, you don't want your backup tape sitting right beside it. Next is this idea of electronic vaulting. Electronic vaulting is simply using an automated tape management system. It usually looks like a big silo. It's got an automated robotic arm inside. It's filled with tapes and that robotic arm can grab tapes, put them into the readers automatically. It automates the whole process of managing all of these tapes. And then finally, tape rotation. You've probably heard of different techniques for reusing backup tapes. And there's things like father and grandfather and etc. Never get questions about that on the exam though, so don't worry about all these different tape rotation strategies that are out there. I don't think you need to know them. So there you go. That's backup and restoration. you

============================================================

So let's refocus on what we are talking about here in domain number one. This is where companies fail. Typically, all companies will have a policy that says security is everything. is important and is everyone's responsibility. And what's important with this as well is that it cannot be something that is communicated once by the CEO. It needs to be constantly re-communicated on a regular basis. The more often the better. But not only does it need to be re-communicated on a regular basis, it has to be demonstrated by those that are at the top. the CEO and others that are directly accountable for achieving the goals of the organization which needs security to focus on those things. This is something that is the most important and in my years of experience I've seen this is what makes security functions and security programs fail, but it is the most important thing that sets the tone and creates the culture. What we have after this, and note the arrows going downwards because this is a top down model for security security today does not work bottom up any longer that structure does not work in fact it will fail for sure it has to be a top down you need this first which sets the tone creates the culture and then what we need are what we call functional policies and some people will call them functional implementing policies and you'll see why in a few minutes here but note the word policies plural. This means that you are going to have a whole bunch of policies that reflect on goals and objectives of the organization and it's not uncommon for companies to have many, many policies. They could have a policy that deals with data classification. They could have a policy that deals with remote access. They may have a policy that deals with anti-malware protection. They may have a policy that talks about asset management and so on. It's pretty common for companies to have a whole bunch of these. Now, if you know what the word policies means, which we said is a corporate law that dictates what is, what is not acceptable. A policy simply communicates management's intentions as it relates to something that is very important. The policy itself does not really provide security. It simply communicates something that we all have to understand at all levels of the organization itself. So let's use an example here. Let's say in our organization we have a policy that says every workstation that's connected to the network has to have malware protection on it. Now that is a typical policy that you would find in most organizations. It simply tells everyone that you cannot connect anything to the network unless it has malware protection on it. But does that policy actually protect the network against malware? The answer to that is obviously no. What we need to do is have these, each of the policies that exist at this level need to be implemented. And so the way that you implement each of the policies that you see at this level is through what we're going to put down here and of course I will apologize ahead of time for not being able to draw really well but that's why we've got slides as part of your materials that will look much better than what I can ever draw but what we're gonna have down here and note that the arrows are always going down and so here we're gonna have what are called standards. And here we've got procedures. And here we've got baselines. And here we've got guidelines. And so when you look at these four elements, these four structures, it is how we implement each of the policies that exist at this level. So if our company had 100 policies, that dictate what's important for everybody to understand at this level. Each one of them would require the implementation of really security controls that are really a combination of standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines. Now we need to understand what the difference of these are. Standards are defined as specific hardware and software solutions. That's really what the word standard used in this context actually means. I know we use that word a lot in security but in this context it actually means the specific solutions that we have standardized on to implement that policy which at this level says every workstation that's connected to the network has to have malware protection on it so the company may have standardized on Norton or McAfee or something like that. That would be the specific hardware and software solution that we have chosen. Now why did we choose that standard? Probably because in our organization it is the most efficient cost-effective solution to implement that policy that says every workstation that's connected to the network has to have malware protection on it. Now procedures, you know what procedures are. Procedures are simply step by step required mandatory actions. An example of a procedure that we would need to implement in order to address that same policy that we're using here as an example Might be here are the steps that you have to go through in order to install McAfee on your workstation or laptop before you can connect it to the network. Or maybe a better example might be here are the steps that you have to go through in order to update your virus signature files that your copy of McAfee on your laptop uses to scan from the latest signature files now those are step-by-step required actions we may automate those in fact most companies do if you rely on people to do procedures properly there's always going to be the A chance of errors and mistakes that get made. So as much as possible when it comes to procedures, we want to automate those. So maybe when you log in in the morning, there's a script that runs as part of your log on process. that goes out, fetches the latest signature files that are stored on a server somewhere across the network and brought down to your machine so that your copy of McAfee can use the latest signature files. Even though we've automated those steps, they are still procedures. The word baseline means a minimum level of security. And so we would establish baselines to also support this implementation of this policy. And so that could be related to configuration parameters. Here are the minimum levels of configurations that you need to establish for all of your Windows laptops. Whereas other technologies that might also be connected to the network could have different configuration parameters. Each of those would be reflected and known as baselines, minimum levels that must always be met. Anything below that line is not good enough, anything above that line would be good enough, but we call those baselines. and guidelines as the word implies means just suggestions recommendations not anything that is mandatory but you should or you should not like you could say all right you should not plug in any untrusted usb device into your laptop when it's connected to the network It simply says you shouldn't do something, so they are guidelines. But the bottom line is, when you put these things together, standards, procedures, baselines, and guidelines, It really is the security controls that you and I are typically involved in implementing in the organization. And certain policies, depending on what they are and what they address, may require several standards, several procedures, several baselines, and so on, to implement each of the policies so each of the policies at this level would require the controls but look and don't miss this point look how we got to the security controls in a top-down fashion with support commitment conviction and involvement and direction from those that are accountable like the CEO and the board of directors implemented through a foundation of well-written, well-communicated, well-understood policies that people know and provides that foundation for effective security to exist and implemented through the actual security controls that are standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines. Now this looks pretty good, but we're not finished yet because there are some key points that are missing from this. We've talked about how asset classification is there to protect assets. The way that we protect assets is to classify them because classification is something that affords assets the level of protection that they require based on their assets but before we can classify assets there is something very important that companies need to be able to do and that is we need to be able to first of all know what we have to classify and protect And so this is where organizations typically fail. But the first step in protecting assets is actually to know what assets you actually have. Because unless you know what you have, how can you protect those assets based on classification systems? And so some sort of asset inventory is what's required by organizations, but that inventory needs to be able to capture all assets as we acquire them or create them or inherit them you know whatever way we get assets we need to know what they are and capture them into that inventory once we know what we have the next step if you think about it logically is to identify who the owners are and that's a very important step and again where most companies fail because the owners are always going to be in the best position to to be able to understand the value of those assets and therefore classify them which starts the entire asset protection life cycle so knowing what we have identifying the owners because the owners will classify those assets based on value is really the process that we need to go through in order to protect assets properly. So here we say data classification, but it should more appropriately say asset classification. But the reason we say data classification is because that's what it says in the exam outline. but anytime you see the word data in any especially this domain number two which is all about asset security data and information is only one example of important valuable assets that any organization would want to protect so if you see the word data or information even on the exam I think what you should do is replace it in your mind with the word asset because we want to protect assets based on value and classification systems is what allows us to do that. So fundamentally, whenever you see data classification, replace it in your mind with asset classification. And here is that process that we talked about, right? Before we can actually protect assets based on value, we need to know what we have. And so we need to be able to identify assets that need to be protected using classification. And of course, once we know what assets we have, the next step is to identify who the owners are, because the owners will start that process. They will classify from the inventory so that we can start protecting those assets based on classification systems. So here's a really good definition of data classification but again you can replace that word data in your mind with the word asset. Asset classification is something that affords assets the level of protection that they require based on their value. And so the important thing to relate here is that the classification systems are there really to allow us to protect assets, not just show the value. Obviously, showing the value is something that's very important, but the whole purpose of why we classify assets is to protect them based on their value and what's important to remember and we'll make this point again later assets as they go through their life cycle may increase and decrease in value. Your asset classification systems need to be able to support that too. An increase in classification or a decrease in classification so that we can always protect assets based on their value. That's what dictates how we protect things. So that's a pretty good definition. Now you should know the answer to this. Who determines the classification level? At least what we start with. Always going to be the owner. But that's where a lot of companies fail. in being able to identify who the actual owners are because they are in the best position to understand what the value of those assets actually are. And here's another problem. I mean, what owner does not want to say that what they own is the most critical, valuable in the organization. Every owner wants to say, oh, I own the most valuable thing to this organization. until you explain to them the accountability that goes along with that in protecting those assets and how much it might cost. And then the owners will take a step back and say, oh, I don't want that much accountability. So maybe I should under classify because that requires less accountability. So now there's a tendency for owners to under classify. And so how do you address this problem? Well, the way that you can address this problem, and most companies do this, is you establish what's called an asset classification board or committee that's going to be made up of key people, from key areas of the organization that can oversee not only the initial classification process that the owner will start with and drive, But oversee the entire classification system so that we can ultimately protect all assets of the organization based on and always framed in the perspective of goals and objectives of the organization itself. Pretty important for us to do that. Now, as a summary, what we provide here is a life cycle or an overall look of how the classification process looks. So we're going to start with that asset inventory, which should capture all of your assets as you buy them. as you inherit them, as you collect them, for example. That starts the process. And of course, once you know what assets you have, the next step is determine and assign ownership. We have to know who the owners are. because they are the ones that are going to classify based on value. They are in the best position to understand the value of those assets. And then from that point, what we do is we protect all assets based on that value. So as those assets go through the entire life cycle, we protect them and whether they increase or decrease in classification it's not going to make a difference because we know how to protect based on the classification levels that those assets may increase or decrease to and then of course at some point We're going to assess and review how this is working and then we repeat the entire cycle itself. So this is a summary of what classification really means and what it looks like. And again, the whole purpose of asset classification is to protect the assets as they go through their life cycle. But it has to start with the owners because they are in the best position to understand what that value is that drives how we protect it.

LESSON 78: Lesson 270
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Within risk analysis, we've talked about how there's two major methods that we can use to rank the risks, qualitative and quantitative. And for quantitative analysis, monetary analysis, there's one formula that you need to know about. It's known as the ALE calculation, annualized loss expectancy. Let's go through that calculation. And before we go into it, the whole point of the annualized loss expectancy calculation is to determine exactly how much would a risk cost us annually per year in some sort of monetary values, Canadian pesos. Now, in order to calculate the ALE, the annualized loss expectancy, we're actually gonna start with the SLE, single loss expectancy. The SLE is how much we're going to lose if the risk occurs once. That's our SLE. Now, in order to calculate the SLE, there's actually two numbers that we have to look at. The first of which is what's known as the asset value. And the asset value is simply how much is the asset worth? So let's use an example here. Let's talk about, say, my laptop. And we'll say that the hardware of my laptop, that's $1,000. And the data that I have on my laptop is another $1,000. So what is my asset value? $1,000 plus $1,000, $2,000. And that highlights something important about questions you'll see on the ALE calculation on the exam. The math is not difficult. You're not gonna need your TI-83 graphing calculator for any of these calculations. What is going to be difficult is picking out the relevant numbers. It's not uncommon that you'll see these as part of a scenario based question. They'll give you a whole bunch of information and it's your job to pick out what exactly is the asset value? What's the exposure factor, the ARO? it's difficult to pick out exactly what they're asking you about. What are the key numbers that you need to use? So make sure you really understand the definitions of what these numbers are, because that's truly what you need to know here. Don't worry about having to do any difficult math, okay? So our asset value is simply how much is our asset worth. And this is where we actually encounter the first problem of why it's so difficult to do the ALE calculation. There's a lot of assets where it's very difficult to calculate exactly how much they're worth. Like my laptop. I said the hardware was worth $1,000. That's very easy. You can easily look up what's the cost to buy a new laptop. But I said my data was worth $1,000. That's a lot harder to calculate. How much exactly is my data worth? I'm giving you an IC's number, $1,000. You know, all these slides on here, all this work that I've put into creating all these presentations, that's probably at least five bucks, right? I don't know. Very difficult to calculate. So our asset value, how much is our asset worth? The next number you need to know is what's known as the exposure factor, and it's a percentage between 0 and 100%. An exposure factor is a measure of what percentage of our asset are we going to lose if the risk occurs. So if our exposure factor is 0%, we don't lose any of the asset. And if it's 100%, then we lose all of our asset. So what if I told you that I backed up all the data with my laptop and then my laptop was lost? What would be my exposure factor? Well, I obviously would have lost the hardware and the data that was on that laptop, but my data was backed up. So really, how much of the value of my laptop did I lose? Only 50%. So this is kind of what you're looking for with exposure factor. You're looking for the percentage of something that is lost if the risk occurs. And this is again often very difficult to calculate. Like let's say that your risk that you're concerned about is a fire. What percentage of the burning of the building would burn down if there was a fire? Was it 12% or 37%? It can often be very difficult to come up with your exposure factor. But let's say in this example I'm giving you, our exposure factor is 50%. So our asset value was $2,000. We multiply that by our exposure factor, 50%, and that gives us our single loss expectancy. How much are we going to lose if my laptop is lost once? So $2,000 times 50% is... $1,000. Our SLE is $1,000. Now, in order to figure out the ALE, there's one more number we have to look at, and that is what's known as the ARO, the annualized rate of occurrence, or in other words, how many times do we expect this risk to occur annually per year? So, if you expect the risk to occur once every year, then the AIRO is going to be 1. If we expect the risk to occur twice a year, then the AIRO is going to be 2. Okay, for the math geniuses out there, if the risk is going to occur every two years, what's a ARO? 0.5. So again, math, not complicated. But again, coming up with the ARO can be often very difficult. How often do your employees lose their laptops? What's the chance of an earthquake? What's the chance of a plane landing on your building? It can often be very difficult to come up with the ARO. Where can you get this data from? Well, if you've got historical records, often insurance companies keep actuarial tables. Those are some places you could try and find this data, but often difficult to find. Now, from my own historical experience, I lose my laptop on average about twice a year. So let's say the ARO is 2. Okay, so asset value, $2,000. Exposure factor, 50%. Means our SLE is $1,000 times 2. Means that our annualized loss expectancy is... two thousand dollars so there you go that's the a le calculation very simple math sometimes difficult to figure out what the numbers are and one other really important thing to watch out for on the exam they sometimes use different terminology for these different things not to confuse you but because in the industry there isn't general consensus on exactly what to call all of these things. So the exam will use different terminology that different frameworks out there use. So for example, the ALE might be referred to as, what would your potential loss be? And your SLE might be the impact of an event, because SLE is a single event. And your arrow, for instance, might be frequency of occurrence. So watch out for that similar type of wording that means the same sort of thing.

you In this section we're going to talk about the three goals of security which everyone is aware of and sometimes referred to as the CIA triad. LESSON 82: Lesson 274
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Thank you. All right, one of the most important things that we have to protect from in security management is what we call social engineering. We've just talked about threat modeling and some of the methodologies that exist out there to allow us to gauge how important certain threats are to defend against but one of the most important threats that every organization has is what we call social engineering and social engineering is very important for us to address simply because it attacks the human element. In fact, a good definition of social engineering is any method that uses intimidation or deception to try and get people to provide information that they shouldn't. And by nature people are They tend to be really helpful and provide information. And that's one of the ways that attackers are always getting in through social engineering attacks. And these are some of the examples of the ones that are most prevalent. You've all heard of phishing attacks, for example. Phishing attacks are any method where the attacker pretends to be a legitimate source like your bank or something else. to try and get you to provide information that they shouldn't. And, you know, there's different clever names that people have come up with to talk about the different flavors of phishing attacks like farming and spear phishing and whaling and vishing and so on but they're really just examples of the same thing and that is to pretend to be some authorized source that tries to get information from a unwilling or uninformed victim for example. The best way to protect against social engineering attacks is always through education, awareness and training and to have really good policies in place that dictate what you can and cannot do or what you should. and should not do. Now pretexting is another example. Pretexting is where the attacker provides some sort of scenario, invented scenario that makes it more likely that the victim is gonna provide information that they shouldn't. So they'll call you up and pretend to be your bank, or they'll call you up and pretend to be your help desk and say things like, we've seen unusual activity with your account, we're trying to stop it here before it impacts the entire organization, So you better give us your user ID and password. In other words, they've created some sort of pretext or scenario that makes it more likely for you to provide that information that you shouldn't. That's called pretexting. The next one is called baiting. Baiting is where attackers may put USB sticks in the front lobby of your building. And on that USB device or that USB stick, there could be some hidden executable files that provide back doors to the attackers to be able to get into your network. And so when you come into work and you see a USB stick on the floor, you're probably going to pick it up and plug it into your workstation or your laptop that's connected to the network and as soon as you do it executes that hidden executable that provides a back door for attackers to be able to get in. Again the best way to protect against these is to have really good policies but also make people educated and aware and have the proper skills so that they don't fall for these types of social engineering attacks that you're seeing here. Tailgating also referred to as piggybacking is something that relates to physical security only and this is where someone authenticates through a door and then someone follows through without having to authenticate because that door is still open. That's what is referred to as piggybacking or tailgating. And the one way that we protect against this, and in fact this is what you want to remember for purposes of the exam, is that we implement what are called man traps. And yes, they're still called man traps for whatever reason. But a man trap is a double door facility that only allows one person to go through at any given point in time. So that would prevent this problem of tailgating and piggybacking. And we call those man traps. Double door facilities that can prevent piggybacking and tailgating.
