LESSON 86: Lesson 279
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Music playing We've talked about risk management and the three process that we go through in risk management, identifying our most valuable assets, identifying the risks associated with those assets, and then treating those risks. And we've even talked in a little more detail about how we treat risks. We can avoid, we can transfer, We can mitigate and we can accept. Let's now get into a bit more detail on how we actually mitigate the risk, where we try and reduce the risk by implementing controls. And so let's talk through those controls that we can implement, because there's a number of different ones. And let's start with directive controls. So what is directive sounds like? Well, it's directing someone's behavior, getting them to do a certain thing in a certain way. How do we direct employees' behavior within an organization? Well, through our corporate laws, through policies. So essentially, directive controls are policies which direct behavior. Next category of controls are what are known as deterrent controls. So what's a deterrent? Let's look at a couple of examples that'll give you a sense of what deterrent controls are. So an example from physical security, a deterrent control would be not the fence, but the sign that says, You know, there's hungry dogs beyond this fence or you'll be prosecuted to the full extent of the law if you enter this restricted area. That deters someone from entering the property, but does it prevent them? it doesn't so that's a deterrent control it deters another example from logical security would be a warning banner so when you log into a computer system and there's that warning banner that comes up that says this is a confidential system and you'll be prosecuted to the full extent of the law if you misuse this system something like a warning banner like that is another example of a deterrent control. Now, what about preventative controls? Preventative controls actually prevent someone from doing something. So if we think about logical security again, an example of a preventative control would be, say, a fence, or landmines, or hungry dogs. Although, don't use those last two, because the number one goal of physical security is safety of people. landmines especially, not good for safety of people. And what about if we continue the analogy within logical security? What would be an example of a preventative control within logical security? Well, the actual login mechanism where you have to provide the correct username and password, otherwise you are prevented from logging into the system. Now, as much as possible, we would like to direct away from, you know, deter, prevent risks from occurring, but we can never prevent everything from occurring. That's why we need detective controls. Detective controls will detect if a risk has occurred. So this would be examples from physical security would be something like a camera system. In logical security, it'd be things like IDS, IPS type systems. These are detective controls that detect if a risk has occurred. As soon as we've detected that a risk has occurred, we want to try and minimize, mitigate the damage of that risk, and that's where corrective controls come in. Corrective controls try and minimize the damage. So an example of a corrective control in say physical security would be your fire suppression system. If you've detected that there's a fire, your corrective control would be say your water-based fire suppression system, which is gonna flood the water, flood water down into the room to put out to correct the fire. And this actually is a good thing to think about because corrective controls, while they try and minimize the damage, they certainly don't get us back to business as usual. If our fire suppression system is just activated, our room is gonna be flooded with filthy water out of these years old pipes. And so we're certainly not back to business as usual. And that's where recovery controls come in. Recovery controls are controls that we put in place to get us back to business as usual, back to normal operations. So clean up the mess, get everything reinstalled, running again, recovery controls. The final control is what's known as compensating controls. Compensating controls are controls that we put in place where we don't have a better control or a better control would be far too expensive. And so compensating controls are things like supervisory reviews, maybe encrypting some data or doing things like segregation of duties. So that's compensating controls. Now, there's one final important concept that we should cover here, and I want you to watch the animation here very carefully because it took me way too long to figure out how to animate this in PowerPoint. There's three of these controls that are critically important and we refer to them as being a complete control. So we always, whenever we're implementing controls, we always want to have a complete control and a complete control is a combination of preventive detective and corrective because we'd like to try and prevent everything that we possibly can for those risks that we can't prevent we want to be able to detect them as quickly and efficiently as possible and then correct those risks. So we refer to that as a complete control. And later on when we're talking about defense in depth and layering and any of these concepts, the idea of a complete control is that at every single layer you want to implement a complete control, a combination of preventive, detective, and corrective. Thank you.

We've been talking about how we securely manage the requirements of the organization, which is what we call security management. We manage the security requirements of the organization in such a way so that we are always aligned and focusing towards and contributing to goals and objectives that are always focused on increasing value. One of the most important things that the security function needs to do is ensure that it does everything related to security based on due care and due diligence. Now, I know we've heard these words many times before. They're used in different contexts. but here it's important for us as security professionals to understand what due care and due diligence actually means from a security perspective. You may see some questions, that relate to what is the difference and what do these mean from a security perspective. So if you were to look up the word do care in a dictionary or the words do care in a dictionary, you might see something like it's doing what others would do in similar circumstances, but that doesn't really focus on what we're talking about here, which is security. So what we need to do is take that concept of due care and due diligence and apply it to security. What does it actually mean? Well, the perfect definition of due care is what you see up on your screen here. It is defined as the responsible protection of assets based on goals and objectives of the organization. And that's really important for us to understand and focus on. I happen to think that a better word would be the word accountable. As we've been saying, the accountable protection of assets based on goals and objectives would probably be the perfect definition of how do care relays or relates to security management however we've put the word responsible in here because that's probably what you're going to see and need to remember all right so do care responsible protection of assets based on goals and objectives What is due diligence? Well, due diligence can simply be summarized as being able to prove that we are doing the responsible protection of assets based on goals and objectives. In other words, proving it to the people that matter. And the people that matter are always gonna be your stakeholders. We've talked about that, but stakeholders are the important people that security really is contributing value towards. So that could be upper management, the CEO, the board of directors, but it could also be external stakeholders such as your shareholders, your clients and your customers, your regulators and possibly others that need to know on a regular basis that we are doing due care from a security perspective, responsible protection of assets. So just remember that responsible protection of assets is due care. Due diligence means being able to prove it to those that are mattering to us, which we refer to as our stakeholders.

you We've talked through all sorts of different risk management terms. I've got this little video here that will summarize all of them, put these terms together so you can kind of see how they fit together. So let's start with a threat agent. What's a threat agent? A threat agent is something that's going to cause a threat. So an attacker, a hurricane, a fire. These are threat agents. Now, threat agents give rise to threats. And if you recall, a threat is any potential danger. And threats exploit vulnerabilities, because a vulnerability is a weakness that exists. And vulnerabilities lead to risk. And a risk can damage an asset. An asset is anything of value to the organization. And this causes some sort of impact or exposure. What's an impact or an exposure? Well, it's loss of reputation, monetary, loss of confidentiality of data, a breach, loss of integrity. Any of these can be an exposure or an impact. And that leads to how do we counter this? How do we break this chain? How do we prevent these threats from exercising vulnerabilities and causing risks and so forth? Well, we can implement safeguards or countermeasures. And as you recall, safeguards are the controls that we put in place to try and prevent a risk from happening, are directive, deterrent, and preventative controls. And countermeasures are the controls that we put in place to detect and react or correct risks that occurred. So those are our detective, corrective, recovery, and compensating controls. And if we put these in place, they can help to reduce the threat agents, the threats that might occur to the involvement, and we can even, by putting in place controls, remove vulnerabilities. That's a quick summary of some of the key terminology within risk management.

LESSON 104: Lesson 297
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We've discussed the defining characteristics of cloud. Now let's talk about the different cloud service models, deployment models, and some of the roles related to cloud computing. Now let's start with the service models. You've probably heard of these before. You've heard of infrastructure as a service, platform as a service, software as a service. But let's provide nice, simple, concise definitions of each one and what they provide to you. Because fundamentally, the cloud service models are what services are being offered in the cloud and what services can we consume as a consumer. So let's start with perhaps the easiest to understand software as a service. Software as a service is this concept that we can rent access to an application that is provided in the cloud. Typically, it's a web based application. And this rental part is important because this is a big shift in how we use software. It used to be software was on premise and we would pay for licenses or we would buy the software. With software as a service in the cloud, one of the big defining characteristics is that you are simply renting access, usually on a monthly basis, or maybe it's even free, to access this software, this application in the cloud. That's all software as a service is. What about infrastructure as a service? We've talked about how in the cloud, we don't have access to any physical hardware, but we have access to all of these virtual resources, compute, network, and storage. So the idea behind infrastructure as a service is you can essentially lift and shift your entire physical data center and move it to the cloud. So all the components that you'd have in a physical data center, firewalls, routers, switches, servers, IDS, IPS appliances, DLP appliances, you can lift and shift all of these physical components to the cloud, but instead of being a physical server or a physical router or a physical switch, it's going to be a virtual router or a virtual switch or a virtual server. And so the idea of infrastructure as a service is you can recreate your entire infrastructure as virtual components in the cloud. So that's the concept of infrastructure as a service. Now the final one is sometimes the most confusing for people. Platform as a service. Let's say that there's, you wanna have access to some software in the cloud and you've looked at all the different software as a service vendors and no one just quite provides the software that you need. So you decide you're gonna have to create your own application. That's what Platform as a Service is for. Platform as a Service provides essentially two major capabilities. It provides a development environment where you can code, create your own application, and also a runtime environment where you can actually run that application. So platform as a service allows users, individuals, organizations to create their own custom applications and then run those applications in the cloud. And oftentimes, when you're using software as a service, for instance, what you're actually paying for is some company out there has seen a niche, has seen an opening in the marketplace. They've created, through platform as a service, their own custom application, and then they start renting access to that application to other people. And so from those consumer's perspective, that actually looks like software as a service. So a lot of the software as a service that's out there, the way it's built is on top of platform as a service. Now, you can find all sorts of edge cases and different types of services that blur the lines between these things, but these are the basic definitions. And there's actually one more that I'll add because it's becoming so incredibly popular nowadays, and that's the idea of containerization or containers as a service. The idea where you place your code, your application within one of these standardized modular containers like a Docker, and then you can run that container in a containerization environment. And so a lot of the big cloud providers nowadays have this concept of containers as a service. This next slide that I'm showing you to you here, there's really only one thing that I want you to look at and think about. You absolutely do not have to memorize any of the words in any of the boxes. What's important is the color of the boxes, the pink versus the purple. And what you'll see here is in infrastructure as a service, there's a lot more pink boxes. And in software as a service, there's almost no pink boxes. They're all purple. What's the coloring mean? The purple boxes are where it's the cloud service provider's responsibility to do these things. So anything related to physical security, physical devices, the underlying hardware compute nodes, even the hypervisor, all of these components are very clearly going to be the cloud service provider's responsibility. Now, what's important to understand here is that where does the consumer have the greatest degree of control? Where do they have the most control over the cloud environment, the greatest ability to put in place their own controls, have their own governance over the cloud, et cetera? And the answer is obviously in infrastructure as a service. And this makes sense because in infrastructure as a service, a consumer can create their own entire network. They can create their own virtual servers. They can install their own operating systems, configure them exactly as they want them, install their own applications. They have a huge amount of control of the environment. Whereas at the other end of the spectrum, software as a service, the end user has, the consumer has essentially very little control of the environment. There's one other really important thing that I want to highlight related to this diagram, and that is that you should really focus your time as a security professional on the boundaries, where there's shared responsibility. Because you're probably familiar with this, whenever there's shared responsibility, if there's multiple people that are responsible for something, who's doing it? And the answer is usually, no one. Because if it's a shared responsibility and it's not clearly documented who's doing what, then there's lots of finger pointing and no one ends up doing it. And so this is something I want to highlight related to cloud security that's so extremely important because most cloud is outsourced, outsourcing to a service provider. So you have to be very, very clear who is going to be doing what, who's responsible for what when you have a service provider. Let's look at a couple examples. In software as a service, who's responsible for access control? Is it the consumer or the service provider? And the answer is, it's a shared responsibility. The service provider is responsible for creating the security kernel, for creating a secure network and operating system and all the components underneath that. That is absolutely the service provider's responsibility. But what about creating accounts and giving those accounts appropriate rights and reviewing the access of those accounts and removing those accounts when they need to be removed? Who's responsible for that? Obviously the consumer, only they can know that. So this is a perfect example of a shared responsibility. And it's critically important to very clearly define who's responsible for what. And ultimately, who maintains the accountability? The consumer. Now let's talk about the deployment models. Deployment models is essentially where is the actual hardware? I mean, a cloud doesn't just magically run in the cloud, it has to run on top of some hardware. So deployment models is essentially about where is that hardware? In the public cloud, it means the hardware is at a service provider and it's entirely their equipment and they have control over it. And who has access to the public cloud? Everyone. So public cloud is the most commonly used type of cloud, but it's also the least trusted because it's entirely run by a service provider and it's accessible by anyone. And there are absolutely lots of attackers, malicious individuals, threat agents on the big cloud service providers. They love their systems. So that's public cloud. The other end of the spectrum is private cloud. Private cloud implies that it's only accessible by one consumer. So public cloud means it's accessible by anyone. Private cloud is accessible by only, it's dedicated or accessible to only one consumer. Where is the hardware actually stored? This is where it gets very confusing. Private cloud could be in an organization's own data center with their own systems, entirely private, could be provided by a service provider, can even be things like VPCs, don't get into all that just think of the basic levels public cloud accessible by everyone less trusted private cloud dedicated to one customer therefore more trusty trustworthy You've also got community cloud. A community cloud is where a small number of organizations that have similar needs get together and build a small cloud that they all share. A perfect example of this, and in fact, the largest example I'm aware of of a community cloud in the world is the US GovCloud. The US government has this requirement that any of their agencies that are using the cloud, that cloud has to be FedRAMP compliant. Well, the U.S. federal government went to Amazon and said, hey, we'd like you to make your AWS environment FedRAM compliant. And Amazon said, no thanks, but we'll create an entire dedicated community cloud just for the U.S. government that will be FedRAM compliant. And that's exactly what they did. And so GovCloud is a community cloud accessible only by US government agencies. And then the final thing here, deployment model, is what's known as hybrid. And hybrid is simply any combination of the other three. Commonly, it's a combination of private and public. And that's where I'll introduce one final term, and that is cloud bursting. Cloud bursting is the idea that if you run out of resources in, say, your private cloud, you can burst into the public cloud and run some of your systems and store data, say, in the public cloud. It doesn't have to be private to public. It's simply the idea of you run out of resources in one cloud, so you burst into another cloud. But that's the idea of cloud bursting. Now let's talk about some of the roles related to cloud computing. It's extremely important to understand who's responsible and accountable for what. And there's a few roles that you'll have to know about. And frankly, these are very easy to understand. You first of all got your cloud consumer. This is the person or organization that is consuming, using the cloud services. You have your cloud processor. This is the service provider that's providing the services. Could be a third party, could even be another business unit within the same company that's providing the cloud services. there's then all sorts of cloud service partners. And the only really major partner that you need to know about are brokers. Brokers act as intermediaries between a consumer and a cloud service provider. And one of the major things that you need to know about cloud brokers is that they don't offer new or unique services on their own, but they can layer services on top of an existing cloud service provider. I'll give you a perfect example. You've probably heard of this concept, a cloud access service broker, CASBs. A cloud broker can sit between a consumer and the cloud provider. And an example of that would be, let's say that a company has decided that all their employees can use Box. Now, the company may not trust sending their data to Box and having Box encrypt the data, because if that was the setup, if Box was compromised, the attacker would have both the encrypted data and the encryption keys, because if Box was doing the encryption, both of those pieces of data would be there. So the company might decide, you know what, we do want to use Box, we want to have that capability for our employees, but we don't want Box performing the encryption. And we also want to, you know, have a little bit of control over what data is being sent to Box. implement some DLP, data loss prevention type capabilities. So what an organization might do is use a cloud service broker that would sit between the consumer and Box. And one of the key things that the broker could do is they could layer on a service of encryption. So when a consumer goes to send data to Box, it would be intercepted by the broker, the data would be encrypted at the broker, and then just the ciphertext would be sent to Box. And now if Box is compromised, all the attacker has is the ciphertext and not the keys. So that's an example where you might use brokers. And one final piece that we have absolutely already talked about but is worth reiterating here. It's this difference between accountability and responsibilities. When organizations outsource data, processes, systems to the cloud, Who maintains the accountability? Can an organization outsource the accountability they have for protecting data, for instance? And the answer is absolutely not. You cannot outsource accountability. You can only outsource responsibility. And so this is why it's so incredibly important that organizations are very clear on what their accountabilities are and very clearly defining what their cloud provider needs to be responsible for to protect the data of the systems appropriately because the accountability remains with the owner.

============================================================

Let's talk about a couple of ways in which we go about protecting our resources. And let's start with one of the major assets, resources that organizations are gonna have, and that is data. Now, as John talked about, it's very difficult to protect data or protect the media the data is stored on for a very long period of time. The challenge here is that sometimes we have to keep data for a very long period of time. For example, the longest retention period that I've ever come across was 150 years. Sounds crazy, right? So I was working for a pension provider and they'd done the math, they'd figured this out. They looked at someone might start working at say, 18 years old and start collecting a pension. So they have to start storing information at that point. And then that individual might work up to 60 or 70 or, I don't know, when do we work now? When do we get to retire? About a year or two before we die? So they work right up until 60, 70, 80. And so you have to maintain their pension data over all those decades. And then we're on their deathbed. They marry a 20 year old. And now that 20 year old starts collecting their pension benefits for the next 50, 60 years. And so they had done the math on this and they had determined that they needed to keep data for 150 years. How do you do that? As we've talked about, most of the media that we use out there doesn't last anywhere near that long. The MTBF, the mean time between failure for something like a hard drive is three to five years. For things like optical media, like CDs, DVDs, it's something like seven to 20 years. Paper can last a good long time, but even after 50, 100 years, especially if you leave it in a moldy basement, it's only gonna last a couple years. The point is this, none of this media is gonna last reliably for 150 years, unless you're chiseling it in stone. So if you need to keep data and protect data for a very long period of time. How do you actually do this? And the answer is, it's all about a process. You have to put processes in place to constantly move the data to new media. and not just move the data to new media, but also probably update the file format. I've got files from when I went to university and they're like word perfect. How do you even open a word perfect file now? I have no idea. So you've got to continually move the data to new media, update the file format to more recent types of files, and you also have to look at the protection or the encryption that's being applied to that data. So 20 years ago, was a 56-bit DES key good enough to protect data? Absolutely. Is a 56-bit DES key good enough nowadays? Absolutely not. So when you boil it down and you think about it, what does it take to protect data for a very long creative time? It's about having processes in place where you periodically move the data to new media, update the file format, update the encryption mechanisms, etc. And some of the considerations you should look at when you're putting in place one of these media management processes is the confidentiality that you need to attain. Is a 256-bit key gonna be good enough 30 years from now? What sort of access speeds do you need? If you're storing these tapes in a mountain somewhere and you need to access them in an hour, that's not going to work. How portable does the data need to be? How easily do you need to be able to open up these old file formats and get at the data? I've heard of cases where people that have been wanting to access old data, they've literally had to go to a museum and get their hands on an old type of magnetic reading device or something like that. What's the media format? In other words, what's the file type and how do you access this data? The final small piece that we'll talk about in this section, and we're not gonna spend a lot of time of it, because frankly, we've talked about these concepts in other places in the course material is, how do you protect your software your hardware well it all comes down to having inventories know what you have for each of those systems those assets that you have assign an owner. The owner is then going to be accountable for protecting whatever that asset is, that software, that hardware, making sure that it's patched in a timely manner, making sure that it has the right licensing, making sure that there's a whole configuration management lifecycle and that the device is properly and securely configured before it's deployed out into the environment and on an ongoing basis. So that's how we do hardware and software asset management.

We are at the network layer and as we said before, the network layer is really the captain of the team. There are some really important protocols that operate here that really drive and coordinate a lot of what happens in networking, specifically the IP protocol. The IP protocol is probably the captain of the TCP IP team and one of the things that we have to look at is the difference and one of the problems that the industry has identified and when they developed TCP IP decades ago They didn't foresee the growth that networking would grow and show to what it is today. And one of the problems that we have with IP version 4 is if you look at the header of a IP packet you'll see that there are some fields in here that really allow IP to do what it's supposed to do and it's two major functions are as you know fragmentation and addressing and so there is information in the header portion of the datagram or the packet that you see here listed so there are things like source ip address destination ip address some really relevant fields like time to live which gets decremented by one every time a router handles a datagram, and when it gets to zero, that packet is actually dropped. Imagine what would happen if we didn't have field like that Denali service attacks would last forever across the internet so that's a pretty useful field but so are the other ones that you see here now if you look at this IP version 4 is what we've been using ever since TCP IP was invented and developed and It is a 32-bit address that is used for source IP and destination IP address. And what they do is they divide those 32 bits into four groups of eight bits. That's why your IP addresses end up looking like four numbers separated by dots. There are no dots, we just put those in for readability purposes. But if you do the math, And you're going to see this on a slide coming up. 2 to the 32 only has a finite number of unique IP addresses that anything that is 32 bits. can represent, and that number is just under 4.3 billion. Now when you think about how big the internet is, everything that is the internet and connected to the internet has to have a unique IP address, which means something like that could never grow to more than 4.3 billion things connected to it. There are organizations out there that try and keep track of how many things are connected to the internet right now. And that number is much higher than 4.3 billion. In fact, they estimate that we are probably by now over 30 billion things that are connected to the Internet, which means that they each have to have unique logical addressing called an IP address. So how is that even possible? Well, it's because of something that we've already talked about, and that is NAT, Network Address Translation, allows organizations to allow a whole bunch of devices to go out with the same IP address. What keeps them unique is going to be their port numbers that are also specified. through NAT so that when the request comes back we can redirect it to the right machine. But that NAT really has saved us and from a security perspective it can also provide a way to hide what our internal addressing schemes actually look like. But this idea that we would run out of unique IP addresses really led to the development of what we call IP version 6. And IP version 6 has already been around for over a decade in fact a number of years and what they've done as you can see here we're trying to depict what the ip version 6 header and packet looks like you'll see that one of the things that they've done is they've expanded the addressing to 128 bits for both the source IP address and the destination IP address. So this is going to provide a whole bunch more unique IP addresses. There are already some organizations that are already using IP version six. In fact, it is. backwards compatible which means if companies are using it they can still talk to other companies and other networks that are still using IP version 4 because it is backwards compatible. And in fact, I believe as of Windows 7, which came out more than a decade ago, support for IPv6 was already built into that operating system, which makes it easy for companies but I think the industry has mandated that by some point in the future everybody has to use IP version 6 and so our IP addresses are going to look much different they're not going to be four numbers separated by dots they're going to look more like mac addresses although mac addresses are only 48 bits these addresses will be 128 bits they'll look more like hexadecimal numbers it's going to be very difficult for us to remember IP addresses but that just goes to show you the value of something that remembers for us which is DNS of course. But one of the other things that we need to remember that they've also built into IPv6 is something called IPsec which very appropriately stands for IP security and that is something that's going to be built in natively into the IPv6 protocol and IPsec as you'll see later provides really two distinct capabilities And that is authentication and encryption built right into natively into the IP version six protocol. Now, if you look at the next slide here is just a comparison of IP version 4 compared to IP version 6 the year that they were both developed the address size which goes from 32 bits to 128 bits now that number that 2 to the 128 represents is pretty large and so the experts are saying things like every grain of sand on earth can have its own IP address and we would still not run out and so that's going to be a tremendous number of IP addresses that we will be able to use when everybody's using IP version 6 but the significant thing from our perspective and yours is to understand that IP version 6 has security built-in through mandating the use of IPSec which again provides two distinct capabilities from a security perspective and you'll need to remember these authentication and encryption built right into the protocol itself now We are still at the network layer and so significant protocols that operate here at the network layer are of course IP which we just talked about which is like the captain of the team. But there's also some other ones like ICMP. ICMP is used for the messaging aspects of networking. So reporting troubles with routes. destination not reachable messages are handled by the ICMP protocol but as you can see on the slide here the ICMP protocol is also the basis for two very important commands that we have to focus on from a security perspective and they are ping and Traceroute. They both use the ICMP protocol to do what they do. Ping tries to see if a host is reachable, whether it's alive and operating. And that is much more information than you might be willing to allow people out on the internet. to be able to find out. In fact, Traceroute is much more dangerous because it tries to map exactly what your IP routing schemes look like. And so they try and map what your addressing schemes and network segments look like on the inside of the network. That's what the trace route command actually does. And that's why it's pretty standard for all organizations out there to block incoming ICMP traffic right at the firewall because you don't want potential attackers out there on the internet and the web to be able to know certain hosts that you may have that are operating and have them map what your network elements actually look like. So blocking incoming ICMP is pretty standard for all organizations. There are some other significant protocols that operate here like IPSec that we just talked about which is what is built in natively into the IPv6 protocol. We'll talk more about that later on as it is really a tunneling protocol that provides authentication and encryption. capabilities. IGMP is used for group membership information. So when routers need to learn about the segments that they control across networks, typically it is the IGMP Internet Group Membership Protocol that is used to provide that information and what you see here OSPF is just only one example of what we call routing protocols. Routing protocols are used by routers to make decisions. Think of them as the language that routers understand to be able to do what they do and that is route properly and in the best way possible. There are different languages that have been developed, in other words different routing protocols like BGP and RIP and OSPF and so on. They're just different languages. Some of them have more security than others. For example, it is pretty standard not to use RIP because it is considered to be very vulnerable. But those are routing protocols that also operate at the network layer here. So these are the significant devices and technologies that operate at layer two. Of course, this is the first layer within the OSI model where we can start making some significant security decisions. But very simple decisions and that is simply a router that makes decisions based on the header portion of that datagram That is what we call simple packet filtering firewalls, simply making decisions on what's available in the header portion such as source IP address, destination IP address and some other very simple types of decisions. Routers operate at the network layer of course as you know and specifically layer three switches which are basically switches that have been given some ability to do things at layer three but again caution on the exam unless they specifically mention layer 3 switches then you wouldn't assume that a switch operates at layer 3 in fact it does not it operates natively at layer 2 but at layer 3 switch is a switch that has some routing capabilities at layer 3

And we've all seen examples of these, the Coke's logo, Nike swoosh, things like that. Those are trademarks. The McDonald's arches. Right, exactly. There you go.

Continuing our discussion of risk management, value, risks, treatment. And within treatment, we have avoid, transfer, mitigate, and accept. and within mitigation we've talked about the different types of the different controls we can have we can have directive deterrent preventative detective corrective recovery and compensating Let's now talk about how we can take these different controls and put them into different categories. So it's all those controls we talked about, but we can organize, categorize them in different ways. The first categorization we're gonna talk about is the difference between safeguards and countermeasures. Safeguards are controls that you put in place to try and prevent a risk. Because as much as possible, we always want to try and prevent a risk from occurring. So out of the controls that we've talked about, how do you try and prevent a risk from occurring? Well, those are your directive, deterrent, and especially your preventative controls. But unfortunately, we can never prevent every single risk from occurring. So that's where we need countermeasures because countermeasures are what we use to detect and respond to a risk recurring. And so What's the category that when you think about what controls fit within the category of countermeasures? Well, first of all, it's your detective controls because you have to detect if a risk has occurred so you can then do something. So if you've detected that a risk has occurred, what can you then do? Well, this is where you have your corrective recovery and possibly compensating controls. So that's the difference, safeguards, try and prevent a risk, countermeasures, detect and then deal with the risk as if it occurs. Another categorization that you've likely heard about is this idea of having administrative, technological and physical controls. And the idea here is that for all of the controls that we've talked about, you can slot and find examples of each of these controls under administrative, technological or physical. And so you can create a table like I'm showing you on your screen here. And so, for instance, an example of a directive administrative control would be a policy. An example of, say, preventative logical technical control would be a password. And the example of, say, physical recovery control would be rebuilding a building that's burned to the ground. So you could fill this entire table with examples of how each of these match up. Administrative detective controls would be reviewing documents, for instance. So you can fill this entire table, but you don't need to memorize that. What you do need to understand is what do these different categories mean? Administrative controls are the people and the process type things. So this is where Administrative controls are things like policies, business processes of reviewing things and creating documents, that sort of stuff. Those are all administrative controls. Technical logical controls are the technical stuff, like our firewalls, our security kernels that we're going to talk about later, our access control mechanisms, our IDS IPS systems. Any of these are technical logical controls. Now here's a big hint for the exam. We tend to lump technical and logical together as though they are the same thing. But there is a difference that sometimes is questioned on the exam. So what's the difference between technical and logical? Well, whenever we have a device, think of a firewall as an example. A firewall is essentially made up of two major components. You have the hardware and the software. The hardware is the technical. The software is the logical. And so if you get a question between the difference of what is technical and logical, technical is the hardware, logical is the software. And then the final category of controls is physical. And these are all of our physical controls, like signage, fences, hungry dogs, security cards asleep at their desk, camera systems, doors, windows, all of this, UPSs, generators. Those are our physical controls. Now, this next concept is really important. And it's this idea that every single control has two components to it. The first is functional. What does that mean? Well, every control that you implement is meant to do something. So what is an access control mechanism meant to do? Well, it's meant to control who can get access to the system. What's a firewall meant to do? Well, it's meant to control traffic between two network segments. This is the functional aspect of a control, whatever it's meant to do. But we also want to make sure that our controls are working correctly, that they're operating effectively. And that's the assurance piece. So for every control that we implement, it has to have a functional piece. It's got to do what it's supposed to do. But then we also want to make sure that it's actually working correctly, get assurance that it's working correctly on an ongoing basis. And we usually do this through logging and monitoring and that sort of thing. But the important part is whenever you implement any type of control, directive, deterrent, preventative, corrective, compensating, any of them, That control should have both a functional and an assurance aspect. Now, I have a fun way for you to remember this perhaps. So when John draws this, he always talks about how there's pillars. There's the functional pillar and there's the assurance pillar. But whenever he draws this, I'm making sure he's not paying attention right now. Whenever he draws this, he tends to draw the pillars as kind of like angled outwards, and whenever I see him drawing this, I don't know, my mind goes to a weird place, and I imagine that instead of being pillars, what they actually are are like tractor beams, and coming from an alien spaceship because he always draws the uh the controls this kind of like oval shape at the top and so uh here you go i've got this lovely slide that i've built for you which shows a uh a spaceship beaming up cows because that's what they beam up right so that's a way that you can think of functional and assurance it's um it's the control spaceship beaming up cows I don't know how that's gonna help you, but there you go.

LESSON 114: Lesson 307

logging and monitoring. This is where we're gonna start to talk in a little more detail about SIM systems or SIEM systems, security information and event management systems. Now, don't bother memorizing any of the stuff I put on the slide. I just had this cool little graphic of the evolution of a person. And I just wanted to show that there's been an evolution of these systems. They've become a lot more intelligent in the way that they're able to analyze and look for suspicious activity in the mountain of logs they were able to generate. So let's first talk about what do these SIM systems do? And I've got a really simple graphic here that kind of illustrates the power of these systems and what they're meant to do. So this little basic setup here, we've got the sketchy internet out here, we've got our little firewall and a DMZ with some bastion hosts in the DMZ, and then we've got our internal network. And a baddie pops up and starts poking away at the firewall, trying to find a way through the firewall. And this starts to generate some event data, which is forwarded from the firewall to the SIEM system. And just someone poking it away at a firewall is probably not worth looking at. It happens all the time. But now our baddie finds a way through the firewall. They find their way into say the web server. Again, not a very big deal. This is just some more event data that's sent to the SIEM system. It probably doesn't care. But then, let's say that our baddie figures out a way to compromise the web server and find a way back through our firewall into our internal network. That is probably some information, some events that we do care about. And so the same system might finally raise this to the level of showing it to an analyst on the console. And some analyst is going to have to look at this, interpret it, understand what's being shown to them. And this is a big part of what a SIM system will do is it's aggregating, collecting all this event data from all across the environment, correlating these events to look for things that in combination might be be meaningful and then show this to an analyst and do things like data enrichment, where rather than just saying IP address 172.169.12.11 has had error 157. OK, what does that actually mean? Well, that IP address might relate to this high value system. And this error message might mean this. So we can do some data enrichment to help the analyst understand exactly what the issue is that's being raised here, so that they can then make some sort of corrective action. Maybe this was a false positive and the system needs to be tuned to try and not report that again, or maybe this is a real event and someone needs to do something. It's not, my apologies, it's not an event, it's an incident, and someone needs to do something about this. So at a high level, that's what they do. And let me show you one other simple slide that highlights the power of these systems. So we've got a user here, Bob. Bob is a valid user. He has a valid Active Directory account and he decides to log into Active Directory. Because he's a valid user with a valid account, Active Directory logs him in. Active Directory logs this event occurring and replicates a copy to the SIM system. And the SIM system records this login event. Does Active Directory care about this? No, it's a valid user. Does the SIM system care about this? No, it's a valid user. Nothing of note here. Okay, now a few minutes later, Alice here comes along. Alice is a valid user with a valid SAP account and she logs into SAP. And SAP logs this event and replicates the event information to the SIEM system. Now here's the question. Does SAP care? No. Alice is a valid user. Does the SIEM system care? Potentially. Why? Well, if you see here, it's the same IP address, which looks suspicious. In most organizations, you should not be allowing shared computing. So why is Alice logging into SAP just a few minutes after Bob logged into Active Directory from the same IP address? This looks suspicious. Now, in some corporate environments, this might be fine. But maybe this is a good example of something that you might want to look at. And it's also a really good example of the power of SIM systems. Because in isolation, would Active Directory or SAP ever have identified this? No. Because in isolation, both of these events look totally fine on those two systems. But it's only when you look at these events in combination, when you correlate these events, that you see something suspicious. So this is one of the very powerful things about a SIM system. Now let's go through all the components or processes that a SIM system doesn't. This is a very high level generalized view because again, this exam does not focus on any specific technologies or systems. So we'll just talk in general terms about what SIM systems do. The first thing they do is aggregation. They're pulling in log event data from all sorts of devices across the organization. What can we pull? What can generate and provide events? pretty much everything. Our switches, our routers, our firewalls, our IDS IPS systems, our DLP systems, our servers, the operating systems, the applications on top of them, databases, data stores, the list goes on and on and on. All of this event data can be fed into our SIM system. The next step is normalization. Normalization is about cleaning up the data. So if you've got one system that's reporting the time in 12-hour clock and another system that's reporting the time in a 24-hour clock, and one is year, month, day, and another is day, year, month, or something crazy like that, it's very hard to correlate these events. So normalization is all about cleaning up the data, doing things like deduplication, so that you can then move to correlation, where you try and line these events up and see which of them might be important in combination the next step is secure storage and it doesn't actually necessarily belong at that particular spot in the in the sort of phases here can go anywhere but secure storage is about this idea that our sim system is storing all this event data that's being produced by all these devices across our network And a lot of those individual network devices, servers, things like that, especially network devices, they have much more limited storage capabilities for event data. And we might have a requirement to store event data for a long period of time. And so our SIM system can do longitudinal storage of the log data. And because we are storing event data in both the device that generated the event and the SIM system itself, it now makes it a lot more difficult for an attacker to hide their tracks. Because not only do they need to compromise say the firewall, and then wipe out their tracks on the firewall, they also have to then compromise the SIM system in trying to race their tracks there. So that's secure storage. Next is analysis. This is where we use a lot of advanced techniques to try and identify what's an event versus what's an incident. Look for that proverbial needle in the haystack and have an analyst actually look at this and decide, is this something that needs to be actioned or not? And then finally, you move on to some sort of reporting where you escalate, you document what's occurred, and maybe you even go back and tune the system to try and reduce the number of false positives and so forth. Now, where can we pull event data from? As I said, pretty much everywhere. And this is just an example. We can pull it from our DLP system, from our applications, from our servers, all sorts of places. Now, the final piece that's worth mentioning here is this concept of continuous monitoring. And you don't have to memorize the phases of continuous monitoring, but the point is this. It can be a very long, arduous process to set up a SIM system. It can take months to set these systems, maybe even longer. Once you're done setting it up, once you're done pulling in all the event data, setting up all the rules for analysis, once you've gone through all of this work, it's not like you can just kind of say you're out, I'm done, never have to do that again. No, you have to constantly, continuously be updating these systems. Because the threat environment is constantly changing. The vulnerabilities in your environment are constantly changing. The assets that you have are constantly changing. New rules need to be constantly programmed into these systems. We need to be constantly trying to reduce the false positives while not getting to the point where we're getting false negatives. So these systems need to be continuously, constantly changing. Updated. And there's one final piece that I'll mention around these systems that is of critical importance. Often there's usually way too much of a focus simply on the technology, like let's set up the connections, let's do the analysis, let's set up the system that does all of this. And what gets forgotten is the people and the process. And so I've been involved in a number of breach responses where the SIM system actually detected that the breach was occurring and even notified an analyst. And there's one event, one I can think of in particular, where the analyst was specifically notified that an event occurred and the analyst even said, whoa, this is bad, and they escalated it. and there's a major breach that all of you have heard of i can't mention the name but this is a major breach that occurred and all the technology was in place that detected the breach and the breakdown was not in the technology but in the people and the process so what's so important with these systems is not only do you have to spend all this time and money building the actual technical system You also have to have well-trained analysts that truly are able to understand what they're seeing and the right processes in place to make sure that any of these things that are occurring are getting escalated properly and actually actioned. So that's SIM systems.

LESSON 115: Lesson 308
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Incident response, this is the process that we use to detect incidents and to try and reduce the impact of incidents that occur. Try and make sure that the business function, the system keeps running or we're able to restore it as quickly as possible. and to learn from our mistakes. So when something bad, an incident has occurred, how do we learn from that and try and make sure that that bad thing, that incident doesn't occur again? So these are overall the goals of incident response. Now, before we get into all the details of explaining exactly what the steps of instant response are, there's two terms that we have to understand clearly. And that's the difference between what's an event and what's an incident. An event is an observable occurrence. Me knocking on the table. That is an event. There's tons of events that are constantly happening in organizations. Do we care about the vast majority of events? Absolutely not. What we do care about are incidents. So what makes something not an event, but rather an incident? Well, the definition of an incident is that it's an event that has an adverse effect on the organization. So if an event occurs, you know, the CEO stubs their toe or a system goes down or something catches on fire, these are probably events, well, except for maybe the first one. These are events that we care about and we want to call them, give them a special name. We call them incidents. And it's these incidents that we want to be able to detect out of the sea of events that are constantly happening. We need to be able to detect which ones are in fact incidents and then have a well-defined process in place for effectively dealing with these incidents. So that's what incident response is all about. This next slide here shows you some examples of how you would go about detecting if an incident has occurred. You can use things like your IPS IDS, your intrusion detection, intrusion prevention systems. You can use your anti-malware systems, often your SIEM or SIM systems. Could be cameras, could be guards. These are all examples of how you could detect that something was not an event, but actually an incident. And what are some examples of actual incidents? Well, it could be hackers, could be a terrorist attack, could be a malicious insider, could be employee error. These are all examples of incidents. So now let's walk through the incident response process. In order to have an effective incident response process, the first thing you need to do is you actually have to prepare ahead of time. If the first time that you have an incident is the first time you say, hey, I wonder how we're going to respond to this and then try and figure it out, it's probably not going to go very well. So the first thing you have to do, create an incident response process, assign people with the right responsibility, accountability, start to create the processes of how you're going to detect stuff, how you're going to respond, what you're going to do in different types of scenarios. You know, what happens if it occurs in this physical location, in this data center? What if it's this type of system? Do some preparation. I want you to listen very carefully to what I'm about to say here. It's not uncommon on the exam to be asked what the first step of instant response is. And while you obviously have to have done preparation, the answer you're going to look for is, in fact, detection. If you see any questions asking you what's, you know, put the steps in order for instant response or what's the first step, always look for detection as the first step. It's the most important. If you can't detect that an incident occurs, you can't have an instant response process. You can't do all the things that you're supposed to do. So the first most important step in instant response is detection. This is how we actually detect if something bad has happened. As soon as you've detected something, the very first thing you want to do is response. And this is where you activate your incident response team. And one of the very first things they're going to do as part of the response is do an impact assessment. They're going to figure out how big of a deal is this impact. How long is it going to take to cover? Who needs to be involved in this response? And remember that, okay? Because this is going to come up later when we talk more about BCP and DRP. This impact assessment is one of the very first things that the incident response team does. Next is mitigation. Mitigation is all about trying to contain the damage, try and minimize the impact on the organization. And so if this was a fire, for instance, the mitigation phase or step in incident response would be activating the fire suppression system, put out the fire. Or if it was a virus outbreak, the containment might be to, if it was a worm, for instance, spreading quickly across your network, you might actually start to unplug systems to try and contain, minimize the damage. Next step is reporting. And you actually do reporting throughout this entire process. But there's one important thing we have to talk about related to reporting. And that is that there's often a lot of stakeholders who want updates, especially if it's a major incident. You're gonna be telling your senior management, You're going to be telling the system owner. You're going to be telling your customers. You might be telling the media. You might have to pull in HR and legal and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. There can potentially be many, many stakeholders. And one of the problems that can occur is that if you've got tens, hundreds of people that are like constantly bugging the incident response team to say, hey, what's going on? Where are we? What's happening? What are we doing? The incident response team is not going to be able to focus on what they need to be doing, which is responding to the incident. So as part of reporting, you need to figure out who are all the relevant stakeholders that need updates, at what cadence, and importantly, designate one person on the incident response team who's responsible for communicating with everyone else, and then leave the rest of the team focused on actually doing what they should be doing, which is responding to the incident. Next step is recovery. And this is where we start to try and return things to normal. So in the virus outbreak example that I gave, this is where you'd start to remove the malware, the worm from the systems, where you'd start to plug these systems back into the network. In the fire example I talked about, this is where you'd clean up all the water and fire damage from the firing that had occurred, start putting walls back up, reinstalling systems, electrical, et cetera. So recovery is about getting back to normal. As part of this is remediation. We don't want to just return to exactly the state we were at before because that allowed us to have a malware outbreak or a fire. So a part of remediation is to say, how do we try and prevent this from occurring again? What improvements can we make to our processes, to our controls to try and prevent reoccurrence? And the final piece of this is where you do that soul searching. It's called lessons learned. This is where you ask yourself, how did I get here? What went wrong? Why am I even doing this? It's to figure out how do you improve the process. And so there you go, those are all the steps of instant response. And remember, what's the very first and most important step? Detection. One final piece I'll layer over this is that you're sometimes asked to categorize these controls. And this categorization kind of comes from the military and from the medical field if you ever worked there. The first two steps you can group together and you call it triage. So detection response is the initial triage. Figure out how bad it is, how badly is the person bleeding, whatever. Can you treat them there in the field or you have to bring them back to some hospital in Germany or something. Next step is the next categorization is action and investigation. And this is where you do your mitigation and your reporting. And then the final piece is recovery. And that groups together your recovery, your remediation, and your lessons learned. So if you ever see that categorization, triage, action investigation and recovery, that's how it lines up to the different incident response steps that we've talked about.

LESSON 125: Lesson 318
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We've gone through an overview, an initial discussion of how we conduct investigations. Let's now delve a little more deeply into the digital forensics part of this. How do we conduct digital investigations? And One of the challenges we have with this is how do you actually apply all these investigative techniques to a digital environment where sometimes the evidence is a lot harder to find and extract and where reporting that evidence to people can often be difficult for them to understand and know truly what it means. So let's start with one of the first major challenges we have, and that's this concept of live evidence. So live evidence is data processes in a system that are running. So think of it this way. If you walk up to a computer system and you jiggle the mouse to wake that computer system up and you start checking to see what's in the browsing history and what files are stored, what are you doing to the state of that system? You're changing it. You are contaminating the evidence. And so one of the challenges we have is that when we conduct forensic investigations on computer systems, we want to try and capture all this live evidence, the CPU registers and cache, the data that's in RAM, the data that's in the network buffers, things like this. We want to try and capture as much of this data as we can while also corrupting that data as little as possible. And that's a very difficult thing to do. And so that's that concept you ever hear, if you hear of live evidence, it's capturing data from volatile memory. where even interacting with the system in any way corrupts that evidence and so this is a great example of where you need very specialized tools and knowledge to know how to do this in a proper way And this is really difficult on physical systems. It gets a lot easier if we talk about, say, virtual machines or systems in the cloud, because that's where we can use this idea of snapshotting the system or creating a complete copy mirror of the system, usually called a snapshot of a running system. We can create an exact copy of a running system without changing the state of that. the state of that system in the copy. Now, the major source of evidence you're going to look at when you're conducting forensic investigations on a computer system is the hard drive. And there's an important process that you need to go through when you're conducting investigations on the data on that hard drive. and that is you never want to touch the original drive. This is a perfect example. You take the original drive, you tag, bag, and carry it. You never do any analysis on that original drive because what might you do to that evidence? Corrupt it, contaminate it. So when you're dealing with hard drives, what you want to do is you want to take the original drive and make two copies. And then what you do is you want to make sure that they are exact copies. And I don't just mean like if you go into Windows, for instance, and you copy all the files out of the hard drive from a Windows system, what is actually being copied? Is it all the data on the hard drive? No. So in Windows, for instance, when you delete a file, what actually happens to that data? Is the data actually deleted? All that happens is Windows deletes the reference to the data. That data is still sitting there until later on when it might be overwritten. So when we talk about making copies of hard drives, what we're talking about doing is making a bit-for-bit identical copy of the original drive, which would capture any of that data that's sitting there, not referenced anymore, it's being deleted, but it's still sitting on the hard drive. And so when we talk about making two copies, they have to be exact bit for bit copies. And how would we verify that they prove that they're exact bit for bit copies? Well, you would hash the first drive, hash the two copies, and you would make sure that all three hash values are the same. And then you take the original drive, the first copy, put them in evidence bags, never touch them. And it's only that second copy that you actually perform your forensic investigations on. Now we briefly talked about this concept of the chain of custody in the previous video. And the whole idea behind the chain of custody, as we talked about, is to establish and associate it with the word control. Think of it this way. You show up in a court of law to present your evidence, to prove your case. And the judge or the defense attorney asks you, where was this evidence from this date to this date? And your answer is, You didn't have control over the evidence at that point. You have no idea where it was. Is that evidence going to be admissible? Is it going to be accepted? Of course not. So chain of custody means you've established control. You know exactly where that evidence was from the moment it was collected till years later where it might be used in a court of law for whatever type of investigation you're conducting. And you want to have control of that evidence over its entire life cycle. But evidence chain of custody by itself does not guarantee admissibility. And this is where we'll talk about the five rules of evidence, as they're often referred to. And the five rules of evidence are what are sort of required to achieve admissibility. So let's go through each of them, okay? Let's start with the first, which is authentic. The evidence has to be authentic. And what this ties back to is you are able to prove that the evidence actually came from the scene. And this goes back to where we talked about the first thing you do when you arrive at an investigation is you want to secure the scene. Take photographs, document where, what's where. And if you can say, look, here's the first photos I took when I arrived on site. There's the knife right there. O.J. Simpson's glove or whatever, you can prove the authenticity that the evidence actually came from the scene. The next is accurate. And accurate simply means the evidence has integrity, hasn't been tampered with. And like we just talked about with hard drives, you make bit for bit copies, hash the copies to make sure they are the same, the evidence has integrity. integrity. Next is complete. What this relates to is you're not allowed to, if you've collected some evidence that doesn't support your case, you're not allowed to just burn it or hide it or pull an Enron and shred it. Complete means all the evidence that you've collected you have to share with everyone and you're not allowed to hide any pieces that may not support your case. The next one is really challenging. The evidence must be convincing or reliable. There's actually another word I would associate with this, and that is that the evidence must demonstrate a high degree of veracity. What's veracity? Well, it's truth. The evidence must demonstrate a high degree of truth. And this is where we actually have quite a challenge in digital forensics, and that is that our evidence has to be convincing to someone. And if someone doesn't really understand computer systems and how they work, and what's this particular log event mean, and I'll hash the hard drive, and if they don't understand a lot of this very technical jargon, it's very difficult to make the evidence convincing to them to demonstrate to show that it has veracity and so this really this part here this rule of evidence really comes down to how you present the evidence and how you talk someone through what it means and what it's supposed to what it should actually be showing them And then the final piece is if you've achieved all these, hopefully the evidence will be admissible. That means it will be accepted, say in a court of law or by a regulator or whoever is conducting the investigation. Now let's talk through some of the techniques that you can use to conduct your investigation. One thing you could do is what's known as media analysis. Media analysis is where you're looking at the media, where the data is stored. So John would have talked through how you can do something known as purging, for instance, which means that the data, or sorry, let me use another example. John, for instance, talked through where you can do this concept of overwriting, where you overwrite the data with random ones and zeros. What do you recall about what he said about that? Is overwriting a defensible destruction technique? No, because it turns out people have studied this. If you just overwrite a hard drive, a hard drive is made up of the physical media of a hard drive. When you overwrite the data, there's still some data that remains from the original data and you can recover that. And so media analysis is this concept that you take the media, if it's a hard drive, a CD, a flash drive, an SSD drive, whatever it is, and you're doing very, very careful analysis of that media, typically to recover data that's being deleted or attempted to be destroyed. Next is software analysis. This is obviously where you're looking at the software. And what you're looking at here is you're trying to figure out exactly what did the software do? Was it trying to encrypt my data? Was it exfiltrating something? And you're also often trying to do what's known as attribution here. You're trying to figure out who wrote this software. And you can sometimes figure that out by looking at the names of files, the names of variables, things like that, because if someone speaks a certain language, maybe the variable names and stuff are in that certain language or certain types of hacker groups are known to use certain coding practices. So you might be able to figure that out. And then the final piece you could look at is network analysis. You're looking at how someone might have penetrated the network, how they might have traversed the network, what systems they might have infected. And in network analysis, your primary source of evidence is going to be log files. Now, the final sort of quick overview of digital forensics tools here is what are some of the techniques you can use? And I've mentioned a lot of these now. You can do malware analysis, log analysis, traces, those sorts of things. And what this all boils down to is you need to be able to report and document your findings. And the important part to understand here is something we've talked about a few times now, and that is you've got to think very carefully about who is your audience. If you're reporting to senior management, if you're reporting to the media, if you're reporting to a judge or a jury, the way that you format, the way that you present, the level of detail is going to vary by audience. So the final piece you have to think about when you're conducting your investigation is, who's the audience? What way do I present this evidence to them so that it's gonna have veracity, so they understand it?

Music playing Foundation Operational Security Concepts. This, I'm happy to report, is a very easy section. We're going to review a lot of the security concepts we've talked about already. For instance, we have talked about need to know least privilege already. This is the concept where you don't give anyone any more access than they absolutely need to do their job. And from the system administrator's perspective, they're going to look at their system. They're going to see each user in my system. What's the least amount of privilege I could give them so they could still perform their task? on my system and no more. So that's need to know, least privilege. We apply that everywhere, especially in access management. Next, separation of duties. And I'll give you an example of this, right? Separation of duties is where we take certain important processes and we separate out the tasks involved in those processes. So as an example here, let's use the check issuance process. For instance, if I was in charge of the check issuance process, and I had complete control of the process, I could do every step, I would probably be writing myself a check for $1 million every day and sticking that in the mail and sending it to myself. I should probably retake the ethics section. So check issuance is an example of where we might implement separation of duties. We'd separate out so that the first task where it might be where you fill the first person here fills in who the check is for and what the payment amount should be. The check would then be sent to the next person who would actually have to sign, validate the check. And then the check might be sent to say a third person who might be responsible for doing one final check and then actually putting that check in an envelope and putting it in the mail or activating the EFT transfer. The point is we take a critical, valuable business system process and we separate that process and have multiple people involved. What's the whole point of separation of duties? To prevent fraud. And for fraud to occur, you have to have collusion. Are you going to do this for every single business process? Absolutely not. Separation of duties is expensive. It's onerous. You're only going to do it where it's cost justified. Next, we talk about privileged account management. What's a privileged account? Well, it's a high value account, an administrator account, a root account. And what do we want to make sure that we do for these high value accounts? put more controls in place, restrict the access to only those that need that sort of access, do regular reviews of their access, use increased authentication, stuff like multi-factor authentication, and even put in place increased logging and monitoring to know exactly what these individuals are doing. job rotation. This is the idea where you rotate people through different jobs. I've got a fun animation that shows us here. I'm not going to talk about how long it took me to animate that in PowerPoint. Why do we do job rotation? A few different reasons. One, we want to cross train people. So if you have someone that's hit by a bus or something, and they were doing some critical job that no one else knew how to do, you're in real trouble. But if you've done job rotation, you've rotated people through that position, other people will know how to do it. So that's one reason. Another is it's a very good deterrent control. If you're a sketchy individual and you're doing something that you shouldn't be doing, maybe you're performing a salami attack on a financial system, If you know that someone else is going to be rotated into your job and they're going to be looking at what you've been doing, they're going to be looking at the changes you've made to the system, they're going to be essentially re-performing your job, that's a good deterrent to maybe not prevent fraud. And it's, as the situation I've just outlined implies, it's also a good detective control. When you rotate people through jobs, someone else might see something wrong. They might detect that some fraud has been occurring or some data integrity issues are occurring. So that's why we do job rotation. Let's talk about the information lifecycle. We've briefly talked about this concept yet, but the information lifecycle is made up of six phases. We first create data. As soon as data is being created, it needs to be stored somewhere. We can then use the data for various purposes, share the data with various different internal or external stakeholders. When we no longer need the data in active use, we can archive it. And then eventually, when we no longer need the data, we will destroy it. Defensible destruction. Now, we need to put controls in place that's going to protect data at all of the phases of the data lifecycle. But there's one really important thing I want to highlight that you need to do as soon as you create data. So what's the most important critical thing that you have to do at the creation step? classify the data because classification implies how valuable the data is to the organization. And it's what drives all the rest of the steps. The classification will define when we store the data. How much encryption is required? Where are we allowed to store it? Transborded data flow laws, as an example. Who's allowed to use the data? For what purposes? Who can it be shared with? How long do we have to archive it for? And do we have, say, defensible destruction requirements? So that's the data lifecycle and the types of controls that we would put in place. And remember, classify the data as soon as it's created is critically important.
