Patch management, a proactive process we use to create a consistently configured environment that is secure against known vulnerabilities. Very helpful for me to read the definition there, right? So I mean, what is this actually telling us? One thing, start with the very first word, they're proactive. Patch management is absolutely a proactive process. Organizations have to proactively be making sure, are there new vulnerabilities out there? Are there new patches available? Are there new threats that are applicable to our environment so that we know we need to patch our environment? The next part of it there is to create a consistently configured environment. You want to make sure that if you've got 100 servers that are all the same operating system, the same type, that all of them are properly patched, consistently configured. And the final part there is why do we actually do patching? Well, the whole reason we do patching is kind of two major reasons. One, we want to implement some new functionality, some new capabilities into our system, or to fix some flaw, something broken, some vulnerability. And that's what that last part relates to. You can only patch against known vulnerabilities, which again relates to the fact that you've got to know when you're supposed to patch your system. So here's what the patch management lifecycle looks like. And this is not one of these things that you need to memorize. You don't have to memorize the whole patch management process. And really, I only want to spend time on the first three. The first of which is monitoring for when a new patch or when there's a new vulnerability out there, a new threat that you have to be concerned about. And I really wanna spend a moment on this because it is critically important for organizations to proactively know when they're supposed to patch their systems. And for some systems, that's very easy. For Windows operating systems, for iPhones, for some of the systems we have out there, it's an easy process because the vendor jabs at us, right? Hey, there's a new update available. There's a new update. And it's very easy to know when you're supposed to patch your system. But for the vast majority of systems hardware out there, the vendors don't proactively tell us that new patches are available. We often have to check things like their Twitter feeds, like their RSS feeds, go to their website to see if they've posted something. Or even more proactively, do threat monitoring. Look out there on the deep dark web, know what new threats are emerging and which of those threats might be applicable to our environment. And then make sure that we are able to patch our environments for those new threats or these new vulnerabilities that have surfaced. And so that's the very first step of patch management is making sure you're proactively knowing when you have to patch. And then once you know, then you can go and acquire the patch, get your hands on it. And then basically the whole rest of the process is change management. Once you know that you need to patch a system, that's now just another change that needs to be made and you follow your change management process. Let's talk about how we determine the patch levels of different systems. It's kind of three approaches that you can use. One is you can use agents. An agent is a little piece of software installed on the device, on the server, on the laptop, on the workstation. So agent-based checking means there's this little software agent and through that agent, you can easily query it and it'll check the patch levels of the system. But using an agent-based approach means we have to have little software agents installed on all of our systems. Another approach is agentless. And this is where there is no little software agent installed on the server. Instead, we let, or we use a system that's able to log into and check and query the system remotely. Perfect example of this. We talked about how we can use a vulnerability scanner in credential mode. In other words, we give the vulnerability scanner the ability to log into a system by giving it a set of credentials. And then it can look at the detailed configuration, what patches are installed on the system. That would be an example of agentless. The final approach we can use is what is known as passive. Passive means we're not using agent, we're not using agentless, we're instead monitoring the system using very subtle techniques. An example of this would be where you're monitoring traffic packets that are coming from the system. And what you could do is, this is actually referred to as fingerprinting. If you very, very carefully look at the packets that are being sent out by a system, you'll find that there's some differences between systems. Although there's standards for how packets are supposed to be created and different protocols are supposed to work, there are very subtle differences on how different systems formulate their packets. And so if you look at this very carefully, you can actually passively detect the exact version of a system by the way it's formulating and creating its packets, or even by sending it specially queried, specially crafted packets and looking at the response. But you can determine a system's patch level without actually having to log into the system. Why would you ever want to do passive detection of patch levels? Well, what if you had a vendor where you're not able to log into that vendor system and check? This is a perfect example of where you might want to passively keep an eye on your vendors to see, are they actually patching their systems? And there's even services that you can pay for that will do this for you. They will passively monitor your vendors and tell you, are they keeping up with their patching? Final thing we'll talk about here is how do you actually deploy the patches? Two major methods, manual or automated. Manual means someone is manually logging into the system, entering or installing the patch and checking to make sure it's installed properly. This is obviously a lot slower, more laborious process. So why and where would you want to do this? Well, for high value systems like servers, it's not uncommon that when you patch a system, it causes problems for the system. It breaks something. And so if it's a very high value system, you probably want to do manual patching so you can very carefully check, do regression testing and so forth to check to make sure you haven't broken anything. Automated, on the other hand, means we use software, we use automated systems to deploy the patches automatically. Perfect example of this, Windows Update or our iPhones. You can push patches, the idea behind automated is you can push patches to hundreds, thousands, tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of systems simultaneously or in very short order. So where would you want to do automated testing? Well, in an example like that where you've got to update, patch a lot of systems very quickly. So in an organization, a large organization that might have tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of laptops out there, there's no way that the IT department could actually manually patch all those systems. Instead, it's much better to deploy all the patches to all these systems automatically. And yeah, you're probably going to break a few systems doing this, but it's better to get the vast majority of the systems quickly updated, quickly patched, and then those few systems that break, bring them into IT. So that's manual versus automated.

We've talked about forensic investigations and how you conduct an investigation. The last little piece that you need to know about here is that there are, in fact, different types of investigations. Let's start with criminal investigations. Criminal investigations are obviously where you're investigating a crime, the punishment of which is where you might get put in jail. So who conducts, who drives criminal investigations? And the answer very obviously is law enforcement. so here's the tie okay it's very common in an organization for the organization to discover a breach or something that one of their employees have done something wrong and one of When an organization discovers this and realizes, hey, this looks like it's a criminal offense, then very quickly, right away in fact, the organization needs to notify law enforcement and say, we think there's something criminal here. Law enforcement will take a look at this, and if they decide to investigate, it is law enforcement that will drive the investigation, with the support of the organization. The organization can provide log files, things like that, but ultimately who's driving the investigation for a criminal investigation? Law enforcement. Similar with civil investigations. Civil investigations is where it's disputes between different organizations or people. And you may not end up in jail. You'll probably just be fined. But again, with civil investigation, it's still law enforcement that's driving the investigation with the support of the organization. Regulatory investigations are where there's been some sort of regulation that's been violated or breached or something. If there's been a privacy breach, for instance. And who's going to drive a regulatory investigation? The regulator, the privacy commissioner, or whomever is the appropriate regulator. There are industry standards out there by which we can conduct investigations and ISO And ISO 27043 is an example of one of these industry standards that we can use to conduct investigations. The final one that we'll talk about here is what are known as administrative investigations. This is where an organization is conducting their own internal investigation of their own employees or of their own systems. And for organizations, this is by far the most common type of investigation. But remember that tie. If an organization discovers civil or even especially criminal activity that's occurring within their environment, they need to notify law enforcement. Or if they found that they've had a privacy breach, they probably need to notify the regulator. And then if it's criminal or civil, law enforcement's gonna start driving the investigation. And if it's regulatory related, probably the regulator's gonna drive the investigation.

LESSON 136: Lesson 329
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Threat modeling is the systematic identification, enumeration, and prioritization of threats related to an asset. In other words, these are the methods that we use to somehow figure out what are the threats that are applicable to a system. And the reason we have threat models is that it can sometimes be a bit of an intractable problem to try and figure out what are all the threats that are applicable to a system, especially our modern systems that are so unbelievably complex. Trying to think through and figure out what are all the possible threats that we might have to think about related to this asset can be a daunting task. Threat models are a way to help us systematically think through this. And there's a lot of different threat models out there, two major ones you need to know about, stride and pasta. Stride was created by Microsoft, and the first letter of each letter in Stride is basically what this whole methodology is about. So the S stands for spoofing. Spoofing is where an attacker pretends to be somewhere else, someone else, usually to get unauthorized access. And so what spoofing is all about is it's a violation of authentication. The T stands for tampering. And this is where an attacker tries to modify the data at rest or in transit to try and change the state of the system to maybe to give himself elevator privileges, whatever. But tampering is a violation of integrity. Next, repudiation. This is where an attacker is somehow able to do something on the system and we can't attribute it to them. We don't know that they did it. So it's a violation of non-repudiation. The I, and this is kind of where the acronym starts to fall apart, the I stands for information disclosure. And this is where the attacker is somehow, the threat here, is where the attacker is able to get information out of a system that they should not be able to. And so information disclosure is a violation of confidentiality. The D stands for denial of service. And denial of service is simply when an authorized user isn't able to get access to some system, some resource, for whatever reason. There's lots of different ways to cause a denial of service attack. And so denial of services ultimately is a violation of availability. And the E stands for elevation of privilege. This is ultimately what any attacker wants to do on a system. They want to figure out how do they go from, you know, just regular user level access to root or administrative access. Because with elevated privileges, it gives you a lot more control of the system, gives you the ability to cover your tracks. And so elevation of privileges isn't a violation of authorization. And that's the STRIDE methodology. STRIDE's a good methodology, but there's a better one that you can use. It takes a lot more time, it's a lot more involved, but it'll give you much more thorough results. And that method is... PASTA, which stands for the Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis. The big difference with PASTA is that it is risk centric. It looks at all the possible, it takes risk into consideration when you're conducting a threat analysis. And it takes into, for instance, it takes into account just how critical, how valuable, what business purpose does a system provide? Because that makes a big difference to what the threats are that are applicable to a system. And so it looks both at the business and the technical aspects of a system. It's a much more thorough analysis. It takes a lot longer, but it's more thorough. Here are the six major steps involved, or sorry, the seven major steps involved in PASTA. You don't have to memorize these, but it gives you an idea of how it's a much more thorough and sort of risk-based analysis. You start with defining objectives, and then you define the scope, the application decomposition, then you do the threat analysis, the vulnerability and weakness analysis, the attack modeling, and then the risk and impact analysis. It's a long and arduous process, so produces some very thorough and useful results. And so if you get a question asking you, what's the difference between Stride or Pasta and which would you use in which situation? If you want to do a quick, simpler analysis, use Stride. If you want to do a much more, especially risk-based or an analysis that takes into account business perspective of a system, use Pasta. We'll be right back.

LESSON 139: Lesson 332

An additional little bit to add in here related to role-based access is the different types of role-based access. This is a concept or this is an item that's shown up fairly recently on the exam. So there's four types of RBAC that you need to know about. And essentially what these are about is to what degree has the organization deployed role-based access within their organization. So the first type of RBAC is known as non-RBAC, which means there literally is no role-based access. Everything's probably rule-based access. So non-RBAC simply means that there's no role-based access being used in the organization at all. Limited RBAC, and listen carefully to the definition of this, because it's a subtle point. Limited RBAC is where within specific applications you can create some roles. So for instance, let's say app one there is our finance system. Within the finance system, you could create a finance payroll clerk level one and a finance payroll clerk level two and a finance payroll manager. So you can create some roles within the finance application. But what do you think? Would those roles in the finance application determine a user's access in, say, the CRM application? No, so that's what limited RBAC means. There is some application-specific roles, roles that you create within a specific application that just define a user's access within that specific application. So that's limited RBAC. The next option is what's known as hybrid RBAC. And again, listen carefully here, because this is where there's a subtle definition again. Hybrid RBAC is where you have some limited RBAC. There's some applications that you can just create some roles within those applications. But the big difference is when you get up to hybrid RBAC, you can now create a role that would allow a user to get access to a number of different applications. So through a single role, you could give a user access to the payroll system and the CRM system and something else. So that's the idea of hybrid RBAC. It's a combination of... application specific roles and the addition of where we can now create a role that gives a user access to multiple different systems. And then as you can probably guess, full RBAC is where role-based access has been fully deployed throughout the entire organization. And through a role, you could give a user access to any system that you want throughout the entire environment. That's full RBAC. Role-based access has been implemented in every system throughout the entire organization. And that is an incredibly hard thing to do, if not almost impossible, especially in larger organizations with a lot of legacy applications and things like that. So typically most organizations are not gonna make it all the way to full RBAC. They'll be sitting somewhere between limited or hybrid RBAC.

============================================================
LESSON 140: Lesson 333
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We just discussed the cryptanalytic attacks, which are all focused on one thing, which is trying to deduce the key. We're now going to talk through the cryptographic attacks. And the cryptographic attacks have one thing in common, and that is they have nothing in common. There's a whole smorgasbord of different stuff. Some of the cryptographic attacks about finding the key, many are not. So in this next series of videos here, we're going to walk through a whole bunch of different types of cryptographic attacks. And we're going to start with a very commonly used, very generic type of attack known as a man in the middle attack. And by the way, we should really, as an industry, be calling this like a person in the middle attack. It seems very genderist to call it a man in the middle attack. But anyways, that's what you'll probably see on the exam. So what is a man-in-the-middle attack? The man-in-the-middle attack, as I mentioned, is a very generic type of an attack where an attacker puts themselves in between two individuals, two users, two systems that are talking to each other. And the attacker intercepts their communications. And this can be for all sorts of reasons. Maybe the attacker just wants to read the information and then pass it on. Maybe the attacker wants to modify the information. Maybe the attacker wants to try and block some of the information. There's all sorts of reasons why a man in the middle attack can be used. Very generic, the attacker puts themselves in the middle of communication between two users, allowing the attacker to intercept and potentially modify those communications. Now, a type of man in the middle attack is known as a replay attack. And the basic idea behind a replay attack is that the attacker records some cryptographic information, can't actually understand it, but then just replays it later on, usually to gain unauthorized access. Let me give you an example of what this would look like. Let's say I'm building a very secure application, and I want my users obviously to log in with a username and a password, and they have to do so across the network. The insecure way of doing this would just be to have my users send their username and password in plain text. If anyone was performing a man-in-the-middle attack, they could easily see my user's credentials, and that would be bad. So to make my system more secure, here's the tricky thing I'm gonna do. I'm gonna get my users to, before they send in their password, I'm gonna get them to first hash their password. I'll get them to leave their username in plain text. That doesn't really matter. But I'll get them to send their password as a hash value across the network. And this is pretty smart, right? Because this way, even... You know, if an attacker is performing a man-in-the-middle attack and captures the hash value, remember, hashing is one way. Just, you know, if an attacker has a hash value, that's not going to allow the attacker to figure out the user's password because hashing is one way. And so this seems brilliant, right? I could get my users to log in by providing their username in plain text and their password as a hash value, send that across the network, and here's what that might look like, right? Our user Alice here is sending the hash value across the network to our server, Bob. But the attacker is capturing a copy of that hash value. And this is exactly what a replay attack is. The attacker is capturing a copy of the hash value. Now, the attacker can't actually figure out the user's password. but the attacker doesn't need to. The attacker doesn't need to know what the user's password is. All the attacker needs to do is later on say like, hey, I'm Alice, and here's the hash value of my password. And the server's going to assume, oh, you know, you provided the username Alice, and you provided the correct hash value, which means you must know the user's password, and therefore I'm going to authenticate you. The baddie here doesn't actually know the user's password. The baddie is performing a replay attack. The baddie is recording some cryptographic information, can't actually understand it, but then just replays it at a later date or at a later time, usually to gain unauthorized access. That's a replay attack. Put one more time, the attacker records some cryptographing information, can't actually decipher it, but then just replays it later on. That's a replay attack, which is a type of man-in-the-middle attack. The final one we're going to talk about here is a pass the hash attack, which is a type of replay attack. And it's actually what I just described there. A pass the hash attack is exactly what I've described where the attacker captures a hash value, can't figure out the user's password, but just replays the hash value later on to gain unauthorized access. So just remember this. Man in the Middle is a very generic type of an attack. A replay attack is a type of Man in the Middle attack. And a pass the hash attack is a type of replay attack.

============================================================
LESSON 141: Lesson 334
Source: network-intercept-vtt

All right, let's keep going through our cryptographic attacks here. The next type of cryptographic attack we're gonna talk about here is a temporary file attack. The idea behind this is that any time that, say, a CPU is encrypting and decrypting some information, that CPU is going to need a copy of, obviously, the plain text or the ciphertext, but also a copy of The key. Now typically, the key would be stored in a very secure location, like the TPM module or the HSM module. These are custom hardware that are very locked down, very secure, and very difficult for an attacker to try and extract a key from. But this is where the idea of a temporary file attack comes in. Rather than the attacker trying to break the security of the TPM or the HSM module, the attacker is just going to wait for the point when the CPU is going to perform some encryption and decryption and therefore need to grab a copy of the key and probably store it somewhere in the CPU's cache or registers or in RAM. And these storage locations, cache, registers, RAM, they are not as secure as the TPM module. And so the whole idea behind a temporary file attack is that the attacker waits for some sensitive information, like say the encryption key, to be stored in a less secure temporary location, and then grab a copy of the sensitive data, grab a copy of the key from that temporary file location. That's a temporary file attack. All right, next, let's talk about implementation attacks. The basic idea behind an implementation attack is that the attacker is not actually attacking the cryptographic algorithm itself. The attacker is instead going after weaknesses in how the algorithm has been implemented. That's the whole idea behind an implementation attack is essentially a developer takes an excellent algorithm and then does a really crappy job of implementing it, leaving weaknesses in the system. And let me give you a textbook example of a implementation attack. Anyone remember WEP, Wired Equivalency Protocol? This was a very pervasive, when wireless routers first became a thing, WEP was the protocol that was used to encrypt wireless traffic. This was a very, back in the day, this was an extremely important protocol because this is how you encrypted your wireless traffic. Unfortunately, a major issue was discovered in WEP, and it basically made WEP Totally useless. WEP encryption could be easily broken. Why? Well, it was a perfect example of an implementation attack. And actually, maybe I'll test your knowledge here. See if you know, do you happen to recall what was the algorithm used at the heart of WEP? And I'll give you a hint. It's a stream cipher. RC4. There you go. John knows. RC4. Yeah, exactly. RC4 was the algorithm. It still is, but you shouldn't use it. RC4 is the algorithm used at the heart of web. RC4 is an excellent algorithm. There have been no major issues found with RC4. And yet I'm telling you, WEP, which uses RC4, is totally broken. Why? Well, because the developers of WEP took this excellent algorithm, RC4, and did a terrible job of implementing it. One of the problems is that the initialization vector is Static. Part of it is static and the rest of it is too short. If you remember, right, the initialization vector, it's supposed to be a big random number. That's the whole point of the initialization vector. It should be a large random number. And yet the IV in WEP, part of it's static. And the rest of it's too short. This is one of the issues. There's a couple of others, but this is one of the major issues with web, or sorry, with how RC4 has been implemented in web. Perfect example of an implementation attack. And by the way, you should remember all of these. You do need to know about wired equivalency protocol. John will talk about it more in domain four. You do need to know that the algorithm used within WEP is RC4. And you do need to know that this is a perfect example of an implementation attack. And specifically, that the specific problem you need to know about is that the initialization vector is too short and part of it is static. And there's one other piece that you should remember related to this. When this was first found, this was a massive problem. Everyone was using WEP and that meant that none of the wireless traffic was secure. And the proper solution was to create and ratify new protocols. And so that work got underway right away to create and ratify WPA and then WPA2. But that was going to take a long time. And so a temporary bandaid solution needed to be found to deal with this WEP fiasco. And that temporary bandaid was TKIP, the Temporal Key Integrity Protocol. So remember those four related to each other, right? WEP is very insecure, despite the fact that it uses the excellent RC4, perfect example of an implementation attack. And the band-aid solution to deal with this fiasco was TKIP, Temporal Key Integrity Protocol. Okay, next type of cryptographic attack, fault injection. The whole idea behind fault injection is basically just to F up a system. It's not to try and get access to sensitive data, it's nothing like that. It's basically just to mess a system up, to cause corruption of the data, corruption of the system, maybe even crash the system. That's the whole purpose of fault injection attacks. And there's quite a number of interesting ways that you can cause faults in systems. One of the very obvious ones is just mess with the power going into the system. If you mess with the voltage going into a sensitive computer system, it's gonna cause faults in the system. So that's the idea of voltage glitching. The next one here is quite interesting. Clock glitching. The idea here is that you mess with the system clock time. You either, you know, you basically just change the time. You might wonder like, well, why is this going to cause issues? Well, especially related to cryptography, the system clock time is often very important. If you mess with the system time while a system is performing various cryptographic operations, it will very likely cause falls. So that's the idea with clock glitching. Mess with the system time. When a system is performing encryption or decryption, it will often cause faults. Laser injection is basically the idea that you point lasers at something. If you point lasers at something, it will excite electrons. You get errant electrons in a... In an electronic system, it's gonna cause faults. So that's laser injection. And then the final one is an electromagnetic injection. Tons of the components within our computer systems, hard drives being a perfect example, rely on magnetism. If you put a strong magnetic field near a computer system, it's often gonna cause pretty major faults. So these are all different ways of causing faults in a computer system. All right, the next cryptographic attack we're gonna talk about are side channel attacks. And the name explains what this is. These are attacks where the attacker doesn't, they're not logged into the system that they're attacking. Instead, they're kind of attacking from the side of the system. And these are usually essentially kind of hardware attacks. So, Let's start with timing and power attacks. These are very closely related. And by the way, these are extremely sophisticated attacks that require really precision hardware. These are very sophisticated attacks. This is not some dude, some person in their parents' basement doing this. This is nation state level stuff, perhaps kind of university researchers. So let's start with a timing attack. The idea behind a timing attack is that the attacker very, very precisely monitors a CPU. And specifically what they're monitoring is exactly how long certain calculations are taking. So quick, what's two plus two? There we go. Okay, that was pretty quick. All right, what's 176 billion times 209? Four. Quick but wrong. 42. The meaning of life, also wrong. Okay, but the point is, right, it's gonna take you a lot more time to perform that calculation. And this is the idea of a timing attack. If you vary, precisely measure exactly how long the CPU is taking to perform certain calculations, that gives you clues as to the numbers involved. Bigger numbers, it's gonna take a little more time. Smaller numbers, it's gonna take a little less time. And so if you're able to measure precisely enough, over a long enough period of time, apply statistical analysis, you can start to suss out what the numbers might be involved in, so in say a cryptographic calculation. And that's the idea of a timing attack. You might actually be able to figure out say the encryption key just by measuring exactly how long something takes. And to give this a more specific example and to maybe test your knowledge, Think about DES, right? John talked about the data encryption standard. How many rounds of substitution and transposition does DES do? Ah, I was going to say don't answer, John. Because I know you know. Yeah, 16, right? DES does 16 rounds of substitution and transposition. So if you very precisely measure exactly how long each of those steps are taking, that can give you clues as to say what the encryption key might be. So that's a timing attack. Very closely related to this is a power attack. Same sort of setup. You're very precisely monitoring the CPU, but instead of measuring how long a certain calculation takes, you're measuring how much power it consumes. And this exact same idea. Bigger numbers are gonna consume more power. Smaller numbers are gonna consume less power for each calculation. And so you measure precisely enough over a long enough period of time, apply statistical analysis, you might be able to figure out sensitive information like the key. The other side channel attack listed here is radiation emissions. And this is the idea that, again, it kind of goes back to like what we talked about with emanations. our modern day computers and any computer system is going to emanate all sorts of different electromagnetic waves and if you have sensitive enough equipment and especially if you're close enough you can potentially detect some of these emissions and therefore an attacker could detect some of these emissions electromagnetic emissions from a computer system and allowed them to gather sensitive information that you wouldn't want them to. And this is a type of side channel attack because of course they don't have direct access. They're not logged into the system. Instead, they're kind of listening in from the side to pick up, detect some of these radiation emissions. And actually, I'll just share this, not gonna be on the exam, but I think this is a really cool real world example of side channel attacks. So there's this, a university researcher group discovered this first attack that got called the really cool name Row Hammer. And this relates to memory. Oversimplifying here. The way that data is stored in memory, in RAM, is essentially in rows and that's why this is called the row hammer attack so you have rows of memory and remember memory segmentation each application is going to have its own little bit of memory space and each application should only be able to access its own memory space and not you know another memory space that might belong to another application idea behind the row hammer attack is what researchers discovered is that you've got these rows of memory the rows of memory are really close together because you're trying to pack as much data as you can into ram and what they discovered is that if you quickly really quickly write write write write write data to a particular row of memory because the rows are so so close together if you write enough memory Eventually, you're going to get what's known as a bit flip, where writing data to this row of memory is actually going to cause one of the bits of memory in this row of memory to flip from, say, a 0 to a 1 or a 1 to a 0. And I mean, statistically, this is very rare. So this is why you have to hammer away. This is why it's called Row Hammer, because you hammer away. You write, write, write, write, write, write data to this memory space, and maybe even the one below it. You write, write, write, write, write data higher. And if you do this enough, if you hammer away at this memory space and this memory space, eventually you're gonna cause bit flips in this bit of memory space, in this row of memory, corrupting the target row of memory. So that's why it's called a row hammer attack, and it causes integrity issues. You can corrupt another application's data without having direct access to its memory space, which is why it's an example of a side channel attack. Super cool. even cooler, again, not on the exam, but pretty cool, is something known as RAM bleed. The same group of researchers extended this, and what they realized is that when you're, like say you're hammering away and you're writing data to this row, not only are you eventually gonna cause some bit flips in this row of memory, but based on the data that's in this row of memory, that will cause some bit flips in the row of memory that you're hammering away in. And so what you basically do in a RAM bleed attack is you hammer, hammer, hammer your writing data here, which is not only gonna cause some bit flips in this row, but it'll also cause some bit flips in the row that you're writing to. And based on statistical analysis, you can figure out like there's a higher probability, like let's say there's a one in this row, that that will cause a bit flip to say a one in this row. Or if it's a zero in this row, that'll cause a bit flip to a zero in this row. The specifics are obviously beyond this discussion here, but the idea is that by hammering away and writing data to this row, the data in this row will cause certain bit flips in this row. And by doing this enough, you can actually figure out, you can read the data, in a row of memory that you have no access to. And that's why RAM bleed is actually a confidentiality attack. And when this was first discovered, it freaked a lot of people out because this was a way that an attacker, like say in the public cloud, could read another customer's memory space. So pretty cool example of a side channel attack.

Last video on cryptographic attacks. There's just a few more that we need to talk about here. First one, birthday attacks. So we actually, John actually talked about a birthday attack in relation to hashing. And if you recall, the whole purpose of the birthday attack is to try and detect collisions. The whole reason that we're trying to look for collisions is we want to try and defeat hashing, essentially. And so there's basically four terms that you should just associate in your mind. So how do we achieve integrity? With hashing. How do we attack hashing algorithms? By trying to find collisions. How can we try to efficiently find collisions? with the birthday attack. So just remember those, birthday attack, collisions, hashing, integrity. Integrity, hashing, collisions, birthday attack. Just remember those four terms together and that will help you answer a lot of questions, right? The way that we achieve integrity is with hashing. The way that we try and attack hashing is by finding collisions. An efficient method of finding collisions is the birthday attack. Now, Kerberos. Kerberos is a single sign-on protocol that we're going to talk about in a lot more detail in Domain 5. Kerberos has been around for decades. Version 4 of Kerberos was ratified in the 80s. The most recent version of Kerberos, version 5, was ratified in the 90s. And that's the most recent version. Kerberos is a very old protocol. And it has a lot of issues. And... Despite that, despite the fact that it has quite a few issues, it's still a pervasively used protocol. And as we'll talk about in Domain 5, the reason it's so pervasively used is just because it's built into everything. It's become essentially the default standard for single sign-on. And so I'm going to just cover it a little bit here, and then we'll talk about it more in Domain 5. But there's a couple of major issues with Kerberos. The first that you need to know about is that Kerberos version 4 only supports DES encryption. And as John talked about just a little while ago, DES only uses a 56-bit key. And is a 56-bit key considered to be good enough nowadays? Absolutely not. You can pretty easily, especially organizations like the NSA, they can probably brute force a DES key in seconds. And so version 4 of Kerberos only supports DES, which is not considered to be secure nowadays. Version 5 of Kerberos has a nice upgrade to Triple DES and IDEA, and those are both considered to be secure algorithms nowadays, right? Anything greater than 80 bits is reasonably secure, and Triple DES, of course, is well above that, and so is IDEA. So that's great. It uses secure algorithms, but they're symmetric. algorithms. And one of the major issues you need to deal with in a large Kerberos realm is you're going to have a lot of users, you're going to have a lot of services. There's a ton of encryption keys that you have to exchange. And of course, what's the Achilles heel of symmetric cryptography? key exchange. And so there's a real challenge with securely exchanging all these keys with Kerberos, all these symmetric keys with Kerberos version 4. So that's kind of the major high-level issues you should know about Kerberos. Final thing we're going to talk about here in the cryptographic attacks are social engineering cryptographic attacks. So this is where obviously the attacker uses various social engineering techniques to try and break encryption algorithms or encryption systems. And by the way, we did not make up the names of these. These are the industry standard names. that you need to know for the exam. The first type of social engineering attack that you need to know about is the purchase key attack. And this is exactly what it sounds like. If you want to figure out the, say, the encryption key for a system, just bribe someone, right? Give them a suitcase full of money and... Bribe them. That's a purchase key attack. And it's at this point that I'd like to highlight something very important. Thank you. We've now talked through all the major components within a public key infrastructure. So I've got a really good diagram here that puts all these pieces together and shows how they all work together. And just before we get into this discussion of a public key infrastructure, fundamentally what a public key infrastructure is, is it's all the components that work together to allow us to trust who we're communicating with on the internet. If we didn't have this entire public infrastructure, the internet would still work in the sense that you could send and receive data, but our modern day internet, where we do online banking and shopping and all sorts of stuff, would not work. Because without this entire public key infrastructure, you'd have no way of trusting who you're actually communicating with. You'd have no way that you're actually sending your credit card information to Amazon or providing your banking credentials to your actual bank. The public key infrastructure is what allows us to trust who we're communicating with on the internet. So let's go through and discuss all the major components that are required to create this public key infrastructure. And let's start with Alice here. Alice decides that she wants to communicate with someone else on the internet and she wants them to be able to trust that she is in fact Alice. So the first thing Alice is going to need to do is she's going to have to generate her own public and private key pair. So she generates her own public and private key pair. And now she's going to need to store them securely somewhere, especially the private key. She needs to secure... securely store her private key somewhere and where she does so on her end point device is in what's known as the certificate store the certificate store and this is a very important term to understand for the exam the certificate store is the secure storage location on an end point device like a cell phone or a laptop or a server. It's an endpoint device. And typically, your certificate store, where you're securely storing those keys in that certificate store is usually going to be in your TPM module, your Trusted Platform module. So just remember, the certificate store is where you securely store encryption keys and certificates on an endpoint device. Now, of course, if Alice is going to want to... have anyone trust who they're communicating, like trust that they're communicating with her, she's going to need to send them her public key. But of course, remember, you never just send someone your public key. Instead, what Alice needs to send someone is her digital certificate. So what Alice is going to have to do is go to a certificate authority to get a digital certificate. So Alice sends her public key and a bit of information on herself to a certificate authority. And there is a secure process to send this information. She's not just emailing it. But she securely sends this information to a certificate authority. And the first thing the certificate authority is going to do is what's known as identity proofing. Remember, this is the idea where the CA wants to confirm that Alice, if she says her name is Alice, the CA wants to confirm she is in fact Alice. Now the specific entity within the CA that does this is known as the registration authority. So each certificate authority Komodo, Entrust, GoDaddy, etc. Each of them is going to have their own registration authority, and the RA does the identity proofing. Once the registration authority has proofed Alice's identity, confirmed she is in fact Alice, then the CA will move to the next step of actually issuing the certificate. And remember, which is the entity within the CA that actually issues the certificate? The perfect answer to that is the issuing CA, or perhaps the intermediate CA. That's the CA that actually issues, encrypts Alice's information with the issuing CA's or the intermediate CA's private key. Now, of course, this is all based on the root of trust, the root CA. And remember, this whole entire trust that we have in PKI is on the internet. It is entirely reliant upon the secrecy of the root CA's private key. And so we have this root CA. that has a private key that's entirely offline, because we would never want it to be compromised. Now, going back to the issuance process, because we have all these different certificate authorities, millions of cert... you know, anyone can run their own certificate authority, we need to make sure we have a standard by which we issue certificates to make sure they all follow the same standard. And the standard that is followed for the creation and issuance of digital certificates is known as the X-509 standard. So the certificate authority, the issuing CA is going to generate a certificate based on that X519 standard. And now that Alice has a shiny new certificate, the CA is going to send it back to her and she's going to store it in her certificate store. And now if Alice wants to communicate with anyone on the internet, she's going to send them a copy of her digital certificate. So let's say Alice wants to communicate with Bob over here. She's going to send Bob a copy of her digital certificate. Now, the first thing that our relying party Bob needs to do here is decrypt Alice's digital certificate. What does the relying party, what does Bob need to do that with? He needs the CAs. public key, because remember this digital certificate was at the most basic level encrypted with the CA's private key, therefore it will decrypt with the CA's public key. Now how Bob, so what this means is that Bob or the relying party here needs a copy of the CA's public key. How does a relying party Bob here have that? Well, it's built into his system. Remember, we talked about this. The public key of the big certificate authorities are going to be built into his operating system, especially into his browser, like Chrome and Safari and so forth. So pay really close attention here, okay? I'm showing a dotted line here. showing that the root certificate containing the CA's public key is coming from the certificate authority. But really importantly, the CA is not sending this to Bob across the internet, because then of course we'd be susceptible to man-in-the-middle attacks. And so Bob already has the root CA's certificate built into his system. And that's how he's able to decrypt Alice's digital certificate. Once he decrypts Alice's digital certificate, he needs to check for two things. One, has the certificate expired? The way he's going to check that is just by checking the expiry date on Alice's certificate. That's easy. The next thing he has to check is, has the certificate, has Alice's certificate been revoked? And the only way that he can check to see if a certificate has been revoked is he needs to query the certificate authority. And the two protocols that he can use to do that are CRL and OCSP. So those are the two major protocols you need to know about that a system, a user can use to query the certificate authority to say, hey, has the certificate been revoked? And the entity... within the CA that responds to these revocation requests is the validation authority, the VA. So it's the validation authority that responds to all these checks to see if the all these queries to see if a certificate has been revoked. And the final piece in here is that each certificate authority maintains their own certificate database of all the certificates they've ever issued and which of those might have been revoked. And so just remember, and this is an important distinction, okay? The certificate database is this giant database of all the certificates each CA has ever issued and which have been revoked versus the certificate store is this tiny little storage, secure storage location on an endpoint device. All right, and here's just a summary of that terminology. So remember, the certificate authority is often known as the root of trust because all the trust we have on the internet of who we're communicating with is based on the secrecy of the root CA's private key. They are the root of trust. The registration authority is the entity that does the identity proofing The intermediate or issuing CAs, best answer is the issuing CAs, the entity that actually issues the certificates. You then have the certificate database. This is that large database that each certificate authority maintains. of all the certificates they've ever issued and which have been revoked. And then you have the certificate store. This is that endpoint storage location. So those are the key ones that you should really remember here related to PKI. And just as an important reminder here, the whole point of this PKI is to allow us to trust who we're communicating with on the internet. And yeah, the internet would still sort of work without a PKI, but not the way that we use the internet today. And so without this whole public key infrastructure, we'd have no way of verifying the identities of who we're communicating with. And therefore, we'd have no trust on the internet. Thank you.

Here's a topic that's been showing up in the exam recently, skimming. Unfortunately, probably many of us know what skimming is. Skimming is where an attacker captures your credit card or debit card information from a valid transaction. So this is basically where you go to pay for something in say a point of sale system or at a gas pump or maybe you hand your credit card to a serving person and your credit card information is captured. That's the whole idea of skimming. And here's some examples of what skimmers can look like. So here's one on an ATM machine. You can see it's like this extra little cover that goes on top of the ATM such that when you put your card in, it captures your card information. And the way they capture your pin is there's usually a little They'll paste what looks like a little mirror so you can see behind you on the ATM machine, but it actually has a camera in it that's looking down at the pin pad so they can capture your pin code as you type it in. And here's an example of what a skimmer would look like on a gas pump. You can see the image on the right. It's got this little extension on the end of the gas pump. That's the skimmer. Here's a really crafty example. This is an example of how organized crime is getting involved in this. Someone has spent a lot of money building and creating a plastic injection mold, creating a new cover for an Ingenico point of sale system that perfectly fits over top of the existing Ingenico point of sale system. And the way this works is that you can see there's this little tag down at the bottom of the point of sale system that says out of order. So the skimmers want you to use your less secure magnetic strip on your card. They want you to swipe your card through the reader, not put your card into the much more advanced Smart card capability so there they put that out of orders tag at the bottom Forcing you use the magnetic strip and if you flip this skimmer over you can see here when you slide your card through it captures your card information off the magnetic strip and then when you type in your pin code Your pin code is still registered on the actual point of system sale underneath, but this false cover also captures your code. This was a lot of effort to build this. Someone spent a lot of time and money building a plastic injection mold, silk screening this thing. It's a great example of how organized crime has gotten involved in skimming. So yeah, one more time, skimming is just this idea that an attacker captures your credit card information from a valid transaction.

The next piece we have to talk about here related to power into a building is different types of power degradation or power outages. And we've got this nice little table here because there's kind of six major power degradation or power outages that you need to know about. And they all kind of relate to whether it's over a very short period of time or over a very long period of time, and whether it's no power, not enough power, or too much power. So hopefully this is a good way of helping you memorize this and summarize these different types of power outages or degradations. So let's start with no power. No power for a very short period of time. And by the way, when we say short period of time, we're meaning usually just a few milliseconds, a handful of milliseconds. No power for a very short period of time is called a fault. And no power for a long period of time is called a blackout. So a blackout would be where you, you know, seconds, minutes, hours, days of no power. That's a blackout. Next, let's move down to not enough power. And by not enough power, what we usually mean there is that the voltage is too low. So like here in North America, if you plug something into the wall, you should see somewhere between 110 to 120 volts. In a low power situation, you might see 80 volts coming out of the wall. That would be not enough power. Not enough power for a very short period of time can be known as a sag or a dip. So say low voltage for just a few milliseconds, you would call that a sag or a dip. and then not enough power for a long period of time is known as a brownout. An interesting little bit of trivia here, brownouts are usually intentional. So if a power company realizes like, uh-oh, it's like a really hot summer day, everyone's running their air conditioners and we're not able to produce enough power, and we're about to have a blackout, what the power company might do is actually lower the voltage. And yeah, that is going to cause all sorts of mayhem, and it's going to cause all sorts of electrical devices to fail, especially computer equipment. But some stuff will keep running. And so the idea is, you know, a brownout is still slightly better than a complete blackout. So that's a brownout. Not enough power for a long period of time. Seconds, minutes, days. And then the final power degradation issue we have to talk about here is too much power. So imagine a lightning strike or something like that. And ultimately what too much power is, is it's usually too much voltage. So instead of getting, you know, like 110 volts out of the wall, maybe you get 500 volts or a thousand volts or something like that. Now too much power over a very short period of time for just a few milliseconds is known as a spike. So a spike is too much power for a very short period of time. And then a surge is too much power for a long period of time. So if you're expecting 110 out of your wall socket and you're getting 200 volts for the entire day, that would be a surge. And obviously is going to very much damage equipment as well. So a surge is too much power over a long period of time. Thank you.

Locks. Locks are obviously a very important control in physical security, but one really important thing to remember about locks is that there has never yet been a lock built that is unpickable. As a result, locks are a perfect example of a delay control. A lock is only gonna delay an attacker from getting in. Never going to prevent an attacker. So locks are considered a delay control. If you want a great example, let's go on YouTube and look up the lock picking lawyer. Hilarious channel. He can break into pretty much any lock in like three seconds with a blade of grass and an eyelash. It's just amazing. So locks are considered delay devices. Now you do, for the exam, need to know about a number of different types of locks. At a high level, there's kind of two major categories of locks. Mechanical locks and electronic locks. It's just like it sounds, like mechanical locks rely on some sort of mechanical mechanism and electronic locks rely on computer systems. So let's go through each of these and define them. So a mechanical keyed lock is what we're all very familiar with, how we probably get into most of our homes and things like that. A mechanical keyed lock is where you have a key that you use to unlock a door. The big downside of keyed mechanical locks is key management, trying to keep track of all the keys. If someone loses a key, I don't know where it is, you might have to pay to rekey all your locks. Or if someone leaves the organization under not great circumstances, under duress, maybe, and they take the keys with them, you might have to pay to rekey all your locks. And keeping track of all these keys is a really challenging thing to do. So that's kind of one of the major issues with mechanical key locks. Mechanical combination locks, a great example of that is what you can see here, right? We probably all had these in high school at our gym locker, right? You turn the lock a little bit this way and then back to this number and then forward to that number. That's a mechanical combination lock. A magnetic mechanical lock is, a great example of this is when you go into, say, a clothing store and they have those security tags on the clothing. And you may have wondered, right, if you're like trying to pull it apart, it won't pop open. And yet when you go up to the tilt of pay, the checkout person just kind of waves the tag over a little spot on their checkout counter and it just pops open. How does that work? There's a really strong magnet built into the checkout counter. And it's a magnetic lock. So you put the lock over that strong magnet, it causes the lock to pop open. So that's an example of a magnetic lock. Now kind of the major downside with all these mechanical locks is they do not have any built-in logging and monitoring capability. A mechanical keyed combination magnetic lock, they don't tell you when they've been unlocked or relocked or anything like that. So if you wanna have a monitoring capability, you have to provide it entirely separately with a camera system or something like that. And that leads to one of the big benefits of electronic locks. It's quite easy to build in a logging and monitoring capability. With proximity RFID cards, electronic combination locks, and especially biometric systems, it's very easy to keep a log of when the door was unlocked, with what combination, or with what proximity card, or with biometric systems, specifically who unlocked. the door. So let's go through each of these. So a proximity RFID, this is where, I mean there's even older school, older style magnetic swipe cards, but this is where someone has to have some sort of card in their possession. And if it's wireless, they just have to put the card near the reader. And the wireless technology that's usually used here is RFID, radio frequency identification. So if you have like an employee ID card that you just have to hold near the door and the door goes beep and opens up, That's a wireless proximity card or RFID card. Now the older school way of doing this, you've probably experienced this in hotels and stuff, right? You have to, they give you a card, they have to swipe through a reader. That's a magnetic reader. It's still an electronic lock. An electronic combination lock is where you have to type in a pin code, but there's an electronic system behind the scenes. And so one of the cool things you can do here is that you can give every employee, say, a different combination. And so to an extent, you might know who's typing in the code because each employee might only have their own combination. but you don't entirely know, right? And that's the big downside with combination and card access systems, proximity cards. You don't specifically know who actually has the card in their possession or who's typing in the combination. Maybe someone just found a card on the ground and it said, you know, this belongs to this organization. So they walked up to the building and used it and got in. You know, whoever, you don't specifically know who's holding the card or who's typing in the combination. And that's where we get to biometric locks. The huge advantage of biometric locks over every other one listed here is that a biometric lock can uniquely identify a specific individual. And we'll talk about different types of biometric systems in domain five. Finger paint, facial recognition, iris, retinal, hand geometry. There's all sorts of different types of biometric systems that can be used to uniquely identify an individual. which is obviously very beneficial. Now here's a good exam question that you might come across. What determines the security of a combination lock? This is a really interesting question to kind of think about for a second. There's obviously a number of factors that determine the security of a lock. Is it the type of metal the lock is made out of? Or is it how many tumblers it has inside the lock? Or is it the number of different combinations that might be possible? What is overall the biggest determining factor for how secure a combination lock is? What do you think? The answer is it's the complexity of the combination. That is the most important defining factor of the security. What defines the security of a combination lock is the complexity of the combination. And final little piece we'll cover here, which we're actually gonna talk about in a lot more detail in Domain 5, is smart cards and biometric systems. These are two commonly used methods that are used to unlock doors and gain physical access and physical security. And we'll talk about them, like I said, in more detail in Domain 5.

LESSON 147: Lesson 340
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Bye. There's one more really important piece that we have to discuss related to biometric systems, and that is templates. So let's first start with the definition, what is a template? A template is essentially hashed biometric data. So the idea here is that You never want to store, like a good biometric system should never store someone's raw biometric data. Because imagine someone broke into that system and stole all of that raw biometric data. They would have someone's fingerprints or their iris pattern or something like that. And that would be pretty catastrophic for the individual. If your password gets stolen, it's not the end of the world, right? Just go and memorize a new password. If someone steals your fingerprint data, What do you do now? Like you can't just grow new fingerprints or you can't grow a new eyeball, right? So this is a massive problem. And this is why good biometric systems will never just store the raw biometric data. Essentially what the system will do, it's not quite hashing, but it's relying on similar techniques. The biometric system will essentially hash the biometric data, generating what is known as a template. So you can kind of think of a template as like a hash value of biometric data. And the whole point of this is that if an attacker were to break into a biometric system and steal all these templates, They would not be able to use this template to figure out the original biometric data, the facial recognition data, the iris or retinal pattern, the fingerprint pattern, the vein pattern. The template is essentially hash data. It's one way. Now the next piece we have to discuss here is the two major ways that templates can be used. The first thing we're going to talk about here is what's known as a one-to-n search. So imagine this scenario. You walk up to a door and you just put your fingerprint on a fingerprint reader to unlock the door. What's happening here? Well, the fingerprint reader is capturing your biometric data, your fingerprint data, and it's generating a new template. That's the one that you see listed here. So the fingerprint reader on the door has captured your fingerprint data and generated a new template. Now the door doesn't know who you are. So what needs to happen next is that template that's just been generated from your fingerprint, the system now has to search through a database of existing templates to see if there is a reasonably close match. And if there's a template in the database that reasonably closely matches, then the system will go, aha, I know who this is. This is Rob. And so a one to end search where a template is generated and then used to search through a database for a reasonably close match This one-to-end search is used for identification, right? Because when you just walk up to your door and scan your fingerprint, the system has no idea who you are. So primarily what's happening here in a one-to-end search is identification. The other major use case for templates is in a one-to-one comparison. So here's how this might work. Imagine you walk up to your laptop, you type in your username and your password and you hit enter. The system has now identified you and actually verified your identity through one factor of authentication. But maybe the system, right, it's a fairly secure system, so maybe you want to do a second factor of authentication. Here's how this would work, right? So you type in your username, you type in your password, hit enter, and the system says, okay, before I let you get access, Rob, please put your finger on the fingerprint reader. So I put my finger on the fingerprint reader. The system captures my biometric data, generates a template. Now, because the system already knows who I am, because it's already looked, you know, I've already identified myself by typing my username. The system knows who I am. So the identification's already happened. So what the system is going to do is go and look in the database, look up the template that it already has for me, and compare the template that was on file for me to the template that was just generated from my fingerprint and compare them one to one. And if there's a close enough match, then the system will use this as a second factor of authentication. The system will say, aha, hey, this is in fact Rob because the templates reasonably match. So this is being used as a second factor of authentication. So remember those kind of major two use cases of templates. A one-to-end search is used for identification. A one-to-one comparison is used for authentication. Bye.

Continuing our discussion of physical security, a really important question you're gonna have to answer is, how do you decide which physical security controls to put in place? In the next few videos here, we're gonna be talking through all sorts of different physical security controls. Camera systems, locks, doors, windows, generators, UPSs, fire suppression systems, all sorts of stuff. How do you decide which of those controls to put in place? Which ones are cost justified? You of course do not have an unlimited budget, so you're gonna have to be selective. Fundamentally, what you're gonna need is a process to help you figure this out. And in fact, we've already talked about a really good process that you could use to do exactly what you need to do here, which is identify what are your most valuable assets, figure out what the risks are associated with those assets, and then figure out what controls, what mitigating controls would, well, how to treat those risks You know, maybe either avoid, transfer, mitigate, or accept. And specifically, you know, mitigation. What mitigating controls are you going to put in place to mitigate the risks? And of course, that process is risk management, which we've already talked to. You've already seen this exact slide, right? And what's fundamentally, what are we doing in risk management? We're identifying our most valuable assets. We're identifying the risks associated with those assets. And then we're treating those risks. That's the process that you want to use here. But in physical security, we don't call this risk management. What we call it is the security survey. Fundamentally, it's the same process as risk management. But to be cool and different in physical security, we give it a different name. We call it the security survey. and here's what you're doing in the security survey number one target identification as part of target identification you're essentially saying what are our most valuable assets what data centers do we have what hardware do we have in those data centers what you know what different physical assets do we have that might be a target. This is where you're thinking about what are your valuable assets. In threat definition, you're thinking about what are the threats that are applicable to those assets. Could they're in a building? Could there be flooding, hurricanes, tornadoes, fires? theft, you're thinking about what are the threats that are applicable to the assets. And then the final thing that you're thinking about is facility characteristics. And this is fundamentally where you're thinking about what are the vulnerabilities associated. If you have a wood-framed building, it's gonna be vulnerable to fire. If you have a building that's built in a floodplain, it's gonna be vulnerable to flooding. So in the security survey, target identification, what are the valuable assets? Threat definition, what are the threats? Facility characteristics, what are the vulnerabilities? This is what's gonna help you figure out what are the risks and then therefore what would be the appropriate controls to mitigate those risks. as part of selecting the appropriate mitigating controls this is the site planning process that's what it's called the site planning process is where you're deciding which mitigating controls to put in place and there's a few major goals that you want to be thinking about as part of the site planning process the first most important thing that you always want to prioritize above all else in physical security is life, the safety of people. So when you're designing your physical security controls, you're going to make sure that those controls always prioritize the safety of people above all else. And then you're going to be thinking about how do we protect certain very valuable asset properties and certain key operations. And as part of this, one of the important things that you wanna do is think about where are your high value areas? Because obviously higher value areas are gonna, more controls are gonna be cost justified. So for instance, if you're a bank, your high value area is probably gonna be your bank vault. If you're a police station, it's probably gonna be your evidence locker. In office buildings, it's often the wiring closets. On each floor of an office building, there's usually a wiring closet with some switches and routers and stuff in there. If you're running a data center, it's gonna be your data center floor. So think about what are your high value areas? Where would more controls be cost justified? And let's start talking about a few of those controls, starting with the controls that you have around the perimeter of your building. And there's just a few that I wanna focus on here, starting with landscaping. Landscaping refers to the foliage around your building, the trees, the plants, and things like that. And the key thing that you wanna keep in mind with landscaping is you want You want the plants, the trees and stuff kept back away from your building. Because if you've got big trees, bushes, everything else like that growing right up to your building, you're not going to have good sight lines. Your camera systems, your guards are not going to be able to see if someone's sneaking up to your building. Or if you've got trees growing right beside your building, maybe they can climb up the tree and jump onto your roof. So... With landscaping, you want to keep the foliage back from the building, have good sight lines. And as an example of this, I've been to a few really secure facilities over the years and often super secure facilities are like out in the middle of a farm field in the middle of nowhere because you can see someone coming from miles. So that's the idea of landscaping. Grading is about how the ground is sloped. Now, would you want the ground sloped down towards your building or away from your building? Obviously, away from your building, right? If you get a huge amount of rain happening, if the ground is sloped down towards your building, you're gonna end up at the bottom of a lake. not good so you want the ground sloped away from your building and that way any water is naturally going to drain away from the building if there's huge flooding hopefully you're sitting nice and dry in the middle of a little island and not the bottom of a lake the last one i'll mention here physical security control is ballards what are ballards Ballards are big objects that are specifically designed to prevent vehicles from just driving through your building. The primary sort of preventative control that you're gonna have around your building is the walls of the building. And a really effective way of breaching the walls of a building is to just drive a big truck or car through the wall of the building or through the front doors of the building. And so if you want to prevent that, you can put bollards around your building. A great example of this, after the Oklahoma bombing, every US federal building in the US has to have bollards around it to prevent trucks, vehicles from getting anywhere near the building. You see this at US embassies. Go and look at Target stores. In front of every Target store, there's those giant red concrete balls in front. Those are there for a reason. They're bollards designed to prevent vehicle traffic from just driving through the front door of the building.

Okay, we just talked about different types of testing. And one of the types of testing we talked about, a form of dynamic testing is fuzzing. We just have to dig into fuzzing in a little more detail. There's actually two major types of fuzzing, mutational fuzzers and generation or generation fuzzers. And you can see it here in the blue text, the kind of major difference between them. Mutation fuzzers are kind of considered to be dumb fuzzers and generational fuzzers are considered to be intelligent fuzzers. What does this mean? Because what they're both doing is generating essentially very random, very chaotic input, right? Because remember, that's the whole point of a fuzzer is to provide very chaotic input to a system, right? Because programmers tend to be very logical people. They expect logical input, which will provide logical output. If you throw chaos at a system, it'll often cause that system to fail in unique and interesting ways. And so a mutation or dumb fuzzer doesn't really have any understanding of the application and how the application works. It's truly just providing completely random input. And that's why it's considered a dumb fuzzer because it doesn't have any understanding of the application that it's fuzzing. Whereas generation fuzzers, they do have at least a basic understanding of the application. They understand, for instance, are they fuzzing a username or a password or an email address as simple examples. They have a little bit of understanding of what input is being expected by the application for various input fields and input parameters. And the fuzzer will modify its approach based on these different inputs, right? Like the way that you would fuzz a file upload versus a password is a little bit different. And so that's the idea behind a generation fuzzer, an intelligent fuzzer. So just remember that basic definition of the exam. A mutation fuzzer is essentially a dumb fuzzer. And a generation fuzzer is an intelligent fuzzer because it understands the application in a little more detail that it's actually fuzzing.
