LESSON 150: Lesson 344
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Before we get into talking about all these sorts of fun and interesting recovery strategies like hot spares and cold spares and clustering and redundancy and hot sites versus warm sites and all this kind of fun stuff, there's actually three different failure modes that I first have to define for you here. Essentially, three different ways that equipment can be designed to fail. The first two are really closely related. They're essentially the inverse of each other. Fail soft versus fail secure. What fail soft means is that a system is designed to fail to a less secure state. which is why Failsoft is sometimes referred to as failing open. So a great example would be, say, a firewall. A firewall is designed to normally let through some traffic. If a firewall was designed to fail soft, to fail to a less secure state, then instead of letting some traffic through, it would probably let all traffic through. And that's another example of why we sometimes refer to failing soft as failing open. Just remember that the technical definition, failing soft means failing to a less secure state. And of course the inverse of that is failing secure, where something is designed to fail to a more secure state. So in the example of the firewall, if the firewall was designed to fail secure, it might just block, if it failed, it might block all traffic. And that's why fail secure is sometimes referred to as fail closed. Fail secure means something's designed to fail to a more secure state. So here's a really good question. Which is better from a security perspective? Seems like a super obvious question, right? You obviously wanna fail more secure, but it's not actually that obvious. Because it depends on what you're prioritizing, confidentiality, integrity, or availability. If you're prioritizing confidentiality, integrity, obviously failing secure is the better option. But what if you're prioritizing availability above all else? In that case, failing soft would be the better option. Because if you're prioritizing availability, like here's a great example I think of. I live on Vancouver Island where there is going to be a massive earthquake that's gonna kill everyone. There's gonna be a huge tsunami and it's gonna suck. there's actually a tsunami warning system on the island. The film's cure laughing is because we all live on Vancouver Island. We're all going to die, so that'll be great. Hey, guys? Yes, not me, says John, who lives in Manitoba. Could not be more landlocked, so John will be fine. So he can keep teaching after I'm dead. All right, so... Think about the tsunami warning system. If there's a tsunami about to hit the island, if there's been a massive earthquake and the tsunami's about to hit the island, would you want the firewall in front of that tsunami warning system to fail soft or fail secure? You'd want it to fail soft, right? To prioritize availability above all else. The third failure mode is different, and it's called fail safe. And what it's all about is when a device, and this is primarily related to physical security controls. It's physical security controls that are designed in a way that if they fail, they prioritize the safety of people above all else. So a great example is door locks in a building. In a lot of buildings, the doors will automatically unlock if the fire alarm goes off or someone pulls the fire alarm. Because what are you trying to prioritize above all else? Above all else, the safety of people. And this is a great example of that. If there's a fire, you want to make it as easy as possible for people to get out of the building. and for firefighters to get in to help save people. So doors unlocking in the event of a fire is a great example of a fail-safe mechanism.

Music playing All right, we're still in domain number eight and one of the specific items that exists in the exam outline and that people have been seeing lately questions on is what's called canary testing or canary deployment. and you might recognize the word canary here and the reference is in how canaries, the birds, are used or used to be used in coal mines, right? So you've probably read about this but you know miners would walk around certain areas of the coal mine with canaries in a cage to see whether there was toxic air in those areas of the mine because if the poor canaries started to you know, struggle with breathing, then obviously the coal miner would know that that area of the mine was having some toxic air and needed to get out of there quickly. That's where the reference comes from. Because if you think about that, the whole purpose that canaries were used in coal mines is for early warning. So that obviously we protected the safety and life of people. That's the same concept that this addresses in canary deployment and canary testing. And so the idea in software development and application security is that, for example, if you're making a change to something, you know, to the functionality of an application, for example, what you do is you make that change, but you only subject a very small user population to it and you watch what happens. and then you react accordingly. If everything goes good, then you would roll out that change to a larger user population, right? So the whole idea behind this is to provide early warning. And there's a whole bunch of companies that are really doing this in a great way. Tesla comes to mind, for example, when they roll out software for their cars. They only subject a certain number of cars to a specific version of that change, for example, and they watch. And then they'll make modifications and issue a new release to maybe a different user population, for example. So that's the whole idea behind canary deployment. So that once we understand that everything is good, we can start rolling out that change gradually to all users. And it's meant for obviously early warning.

Bye. One more quick video here to finish up our discussion of the authorization philosophies. As you may know, there's a certain type of question on the exam known as a drag and drop question, where you're given a list of terms and a list of definitions, and you have to line the appropriate term up with the appropriate definition. This is a really good example of a drag-and-drop question. So this is just a summary of all the different authorization philosophies that we've gone through. And I just wanted to highlight for you here kind of the key defining characteristic of each of these authorization philosophies. In case you see a question like this, it'll make it a lot easier for you to answer. So remember, the defining characteristic of discretionary access control means that the owner decides who gets access to what. And this is absolutely what we want, of course, right? Because our owners are accountable for the system, it should ultimately be the owner that's deciding who gets access to what in their system. Role-based access control means, of course, where you create roles and then you assign those roles to users and it's the role that defines what the user gets access to. rule-based access control is just a file with a set of rules in it. That file is often referred to as an ACL, an access control list, and it'll just have rules in it. You know, this person, Bob, gets access to this directory, Alice gets access to this directory, etc. Attribute or sometimes referred to as context-based access control is where you're making a much more informed decision as to who should get access to what by looking at a whole bunch of different attributes. And then of course, mandatory access control means the system decides who gets access to what. And a really important part, a really important requirement of mandatory access control is that it requires labeling. Because recall, labeling is how you mark the classification on an asset. And, of course, mandatory access control is where the system decides who gets access to what. How can the system decide? Well, it means that every single employee has to be given a security clearance, and every single asset has to be given a classification. And based on those security clearances and classifications, the system can decide who gets access to what. And that's why mandatory access control requires labeling. So there you go. Quick summary of the access authorization philosophies.

We're talking about network security, of course, and one of the most important things that you need to think about in network security is have an inventory, for example, of all of your different connections that you have from your network to untrusted networks out there and just the nature of how we've evolved in networks and so on is that typically we are connected to all kinds of different architectures out there. Not only the internet or the web to allow our clients and customers to be able to interface with us and order our products, for example, and so on. But you may have connections to other third parties, including vendors and consultants and others that you have relationships with. And so, if you remember back to when we talked about physical security, we said the best way to protect anything is to ensure that you only have one entrance and one exit, for example, so that you can monitor who's coming in and who's leaving. The same concept applies in network security. We only want one entrance and one exit so that we can monitor everything that's going in and everything that's leaving so that we can enforce really good rules. But the way that, again, we've evolved in network security is that we may need a whole bunch of different connections to third parties that we have relationships with. And so part of network security needs to really understand those connections that you do have with third parties and secure them by using obviously firewalls and intrusion detection systems and have the ability to respond you know, if there are problems, for example. So, you know, this is really a summary of what third party connectivity means from a security perspective, specifically network security. So for example, know exactly the connections that you have, maintain an inventory of those connections and apply firewall rules to be able to protect each one of them, but also protect based on the services and the type of traffic, for example, that's going in and out and have proper policies to be able to dictate, for example, the rules that you're going to apply at those connections that you have. And then of course, There are security standards that exist out there that can guide organizations in how to secure connections with third parties that are providing, for example, services to you that may include cloud and others. One of the most important things that we need to do at those connections is to monitor in real time so that we can react using our incident management capabilities very quickly.

All right, in this video, we have a few more quick definitional things that we have to go through, edge computing, embedded devices, and HPCs, high performance computing. So let's start with edge computing. And this is the one we're actually gonna spend just a couple of minutes on here. So the idea of edge computing is to get computational power as close to the user as possible. And the whole reason for this, and this is funny because we're recording some CISSP and CCSP videos here today, and I just talked about this earlier today, but I'll explain it again. We talked about it for our CCSP class. So what is edge computing? Edge computing is this general term where you want to try and get computational power, both computational power and storage, as close to the user as possible. And that's what edge computing means, getting computational power and storage as close to the user as possible. A subset of edge computing is what's known as a CDN, a content distribution network. And let me explain that because it's probably the easiest to understand and then I can generalize to edge computing. So who remembers the video Gangnam Style? Anyone? Yeah? Alright, that's pretty awesome music video, right? Oh, even John, alright, excellent. So, here's why I always remember this, okay? So, I think Gangnam Style was the very first YouTube video to hit a billion views. When Gangnam Style was in its heyday, there was literally millions of people watching that video simultaneously. Imagine the bandwidth requirements for that. Like, yes, Google has vast data centers, but even for Google, trying to stream, you know, trying to have like say 10 million simultaneous streams of like a 1080p video out of one data center in Utah would strain even Google's resources. So how did they do this? How were they able to simultaneously stream this same video to millions of users simultaneously? Well, they did it because they have a CDN, a content distribution network. Google and other big cloud providers and other companies like Akamai, for instance, they pay money to put their servers in telecom companies' data centers. So I've actually seen them. You'll be walking through an AT&T data center and you'll be like, hey, there's a Facebook server, there's a Netflix server, there's a Google server. And you might wonder, like, why are... Why is there a Google server? Why is there a Netflix server in an AT&T data center or a Verizon data center or a Telus data center? And the answer is that these are CDN servers, content distribution network servers. They are basically just storage servers. And the reason that they're in these telecom data centers is because they're really close to the endpoint user. So for instance, say you live in New York City, all the big cloud providers and other companies like Akamai, they will have content distribution network servers, basically caching servers, in the telecom office that's probably just a few miles from your house. And that way, when you pressed play on the Gangnam style video, it would have played near instantaneously because it wasn't streaming from halfway across the planet. It was streaming to you from like 10 miles down the road from your house. So the huge advantage of a CDN is that it massively reduces the latency. When a customer wants to watch a video, they can do so very quickly because the file is probably only a few miles from their house. So it really improves performance. it also helps to enormously save bandwidth. Like, say in New York City where there's 10,000 people all simultaneously watching the Gangnam Style video, all of those 10,000 simultaneous streams don't have to go across the internet backbone. Instead, those 10,000 streams are just happening from a small number of servers just a few miles from each of those people's houses. And all that traffic is not going across the internet backbone. You know, the big cloud service provider just basically has to copy the file once to their CDN server, and then it can be streamed a whole bunch of times, viewed a whole bunch of times by users, local users nearby. So that's the idea of a content distribution network. Massively improves performance and helps save kind of core internet backbone bandwidth. And as I mentioned, a CDN is a type of edge computing. Edge computing is more of a general term where edge computing is not only about bringing data storage, but also computational power as close to the user as possible. Next little, very brief thing we have to cover here is this idea of an embedded device. An embedded device is simply a device that's a combination of hardware and software for a very specific function. Think pacemaker. A pacemaker is a perfect example of an embedded device. It's a highly specialized piece of hardware and software for a very specific function keeping your heart rate regulated. And this kind of highlights why the security of embedded devices is very important, because they're often used in pretty mission-critical situations, like say pacemakers. So that's embedded devices. And then HPC, high performance computing. This is simply supercomputers. If you see this term at all on the exam, HPC, high performance computing, it just means supercomputers. Thank you.

ðŸŽµ Okay, we've been talking about the classification process. And in fact, we just completed looking at the life cycle of assets, for example, and how we need to classify them. right at the beginning so that we can start protecting assets right from the start and as they go through each of their phases of their life cycle. In this section, in this module, we're going to talk about how any asset needs to be protected from two perspectives. So if you think of data, for example, we'll use data as an example of an asset that needs to be protected, and therefore we can use data classification to be able to protect it. We need to classify that data asset. The owners will do that, of course, because they're always accountable for protecting that value of the data. But once it's been classified, any piece of data really can be handled and processed and viewed in two different types of environments. Like for example, we people, maybe using that data as part of a process. But at some point, that data is going to be handled by a system or an architecture, like for example, maybe stored in a database environment. And while people are doing part of some process, they're going to be looking at that data as part of that process. And so protecting data needs to be done from two different perspectives. That's what these words really imply here and will show us the difference between. All right, so if you look at a lot of the guidance that exists out there for data classification systems and so on, you're gonna see that these words that sometimes we use interchangeably don't mean the same thing. And it's important for us to understand the difference because again, that may be reflected in the exam all right so apparently classification and categorization are not the same thing but it highlights the difference of how we can protect data items in whatever state they're being used in so for example classification is the system of classes That's the system itself that allows us to be able to protect data based on its value. So think of classification as the entire classification system that we use to protect, for example, assets within the organization. Categorization is the actual act of taking your assets and placing them into those classification levels, for example, that you've created, like top secret, secret confidential. So the entire system itself is what we call classification. Categorization is the act of taking assets and placing them into those classification levels for the purpose of protecting, for example, those assets themselves. Now here's where The industry provides guidance, for example. But when we look at the typical classification levels that government and military uses, We're all familiar with that because we've all heard, you know, top secret, secret and confidential, for example. And we typically understand that top secret is your most valuable and then secret would be the next and confidential is below, for example. But in industry, for example, we typically don't use those same classification levels. So your organization may be using classification levels like you see here. These are just meant to be examples, financially sensitive, proprietary, you know, it could be related to, for example, privacy information, we call it PII, or it could be company restricted or whatever. The important point that the exam is going to make is whatever classification levels you are using, people need to understand the value of each of those. And this is where security comes in. Security has to provide security. the education, awareness, the training, the skills, for example, for not only owners to be able to understand what the true value of your classification levels are, but also others within the organization like custodians and users. so that when they see something that has been classified at your classification levels, they not only understand the value that is being obviously aligned with those classification levels, but how to also protect based on those classification levels. So it's important for security to support your entire classification systems. And again, the value of data classification and asset classification is not just in having three or four different classification levels that equate the value, but in the underlying mechanisms and technologies and architectures and security controls that the security function would support that really supports the entire best way to protect all assets of the organization, and that is asset classification systems. Now, here's the difference that I was sort of alluding to earlier, and that is any asset can be in different states at different times, whether it's being stored or processed in a system, in its architecture, or whether it's being used by people. as part of a process. And that is the difference between these words that you see here, right? So labeling is what allows systems and architectures and technologies to be able to understand the classification level of a piece of data, for example, so that it can be protected based on that classification level. So perfect example here, let's say a data item has been classified at the top secret level. The system needs to be able to understand that so that it can apply the proper security controls that would apply to top secret. So labeling would allow the system, the technology itself, the application, for example, to understand the classification level of a piece of data so that it can be protected properly. So labeling allows the system to understand the classification level so it can apply the proper security controls. Marking is what allows people that are using that data as part of a process to understand the classification level so that again, we can protect that data item properly based on its marking. All right. So if you remember this, labeling allows the system to understand the classification level. Marking allows people to understand the classification level so that ultimately we can protect data items properly as they're being used, protected, stored, processed, whatever. All right. And so examples of labeling that we talked about before, and of course these, you know, highlight the military, the government ones that are typically used, top secret, secret, public, and so on. But it could be others that you're using within the organization itself, right? Now, how does the system understand this? Because it's the system and the architecture that uses labeling in order to be able to protect that data properly. Well, what we can use are things like watermarks and metadata, for example, which is referred to as data that describes the data. So that could include, for example, its classification level. digital signatures and electronic labels and all kinds of other things that the system itself can use in order to understand the classification levels of that data so it can apply the proper security controls to protect it at that level. LESSON 161: Lesson 355
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

there's a really important risk management framework that you really need to know the specifics about for the exam. It's known as the RMF, the Risk Management Framework, which is also NIST 800-37. The reason you really need to know the specifics of this one is because there's a ton of questions on the exam very specific to this risk management framework. And it's a pretty pervasively used framework, which is why it's showing up in the exam a lot. So I'm going to take you through the seven steps of the RMF, and you definitely need to know what's happening at each step, and there's a few important things that I'll highlight. And you definitely need to know the order of the steps as well. One other important thing I'll point out, the RMF was updated fairly recently, and the current version of the Risk Management Framework has seven steps. The previous version only had six steps. So make sure if you're looking at old documentation of this, make sure you're looking at the most recent documentation, if you're ever looking up this standard, because the exam is based on the latest greatest version of RMF, which has seven steps. So let's go through them. Step one is prepare to execute the RMF. And this is essentially where you're just saying, okay, Let's take a step back for one more second. What is the whole point of this RMF? of this risk management framework. It's to help an organization think through what are the risks associated with a particular system and ensuring that the organization has identified those risks, selected the appropriate controls to mitigate those risks, implemented the controls, make sure they're working properly, And then someone has to sign off and say, yeah, I'm happy with this, and then go monitor those risks in production. So essentially, this is all about making sure that risks are being identified, mitigating controls are being implemented, and that those mitigating controls are operating effectively. That's fundamentally the whole point of this risk management framework. So step one, in detail. Step one is prepare to execute the RMF. And this is essentially where the organization basically just says, okay, we're going to evaluate the risks associated with a particular system. And then step two, categorize information systems. This is where you're going to be determining essentially the exact risks associated with a particular system or process by looking at what are the operations, what are the assets, what are the individuals, what would be the loss, what would be the impacts to CIA confidentiality, integrity, availability. Fundamentally, in categorized information systems, you're saying, What are the assets and what are the risks associated with those assets? And then step three, select security controls, is exactly what it sounds like. You're selecting the appropriate mitigating controls to mitigate the risks that you just identified in step two. And so step three is where you're specifically selecting the controls that you're going to implement and you're tailoring them to the exact system or process. Step four, again, exactly what it sounds like, implement the security controls and document what you've done, update baseline configurations, all that sort of stuff, properly implement the controls. And then step five, assess security controls. This is where you're checking. Have the controls that we implemented, were they implemented correctly? And most importantly, are they mitigating the risks that we identified in step two? And so assess security controls is where you're checking to make sure the controls that we implemented, are they appropriately mitigating the risks? And then step six, authorize information systems. This is where The official framework says a senior manager. The best possible answer you should be looking for on the exam as to who should authorize is the owner. So what are we getting at here? Think about this in the context of developing and putting a new system into production. Before that system goes into production, before someone says, yeah, go ahead, put into production. This is the point of the authorization step, where someone needs to be saying, okay, I've looked at all the risks, I've looked at the controls that were implemented, I've looked at the results of the assessment to make sure that those controls are appropriately mitigating the risks. And then someone needs to make the call to say, yes, I'm comfortable, I approve this system going into production. Ultimately, who should be making that authorization system? What's the perfect answer you would look for on the exam? Well, the person that is accountable for the system. And that's going to be the owner of the system. And so this is a really good testable point on the exam. Who would make that authorization decision in step six of the RMF? The owner of the system, the individual that's accountable for the system. And then, of course, once the system's in production, you need to do ongoing monitoring of the controls to ensure that they continue to operate effectively. And as new threats, vulnerabilities, new risks are identified, you want to make sure that the controls you have in place are appropriately mitigating those controls. Maybe you need to go back and update things. So the point of step seven is to just recognize that we, you know, you don't just implement controls and then just forget about them. You need to do ongoing monitoring.

Okay, let's talk about privacy. We have condensed all the information on privacy on the CISSP exam down into this one section here, this one video that we're going to go through. Privacy is obviously a very important topic. and a lot of the organizations need to spend a lot of time thinking about privacy and making sure they're properly protecting people's personal information and ensuring their compliance with various appropriate laws and regulations and security has a big role in this because there's this really well-known expression right you can't have privacy without security. The way that we achieve confidentiality of personal data, integrity, availability, and so forth is by implementing various security controls. And so security often has a very important role in relation to privacy in implementing various security controls. So let's start here with a definition of privacy. And this is a really good definition. In fact, it's probably wording verbatim that you'll see on the exam. So privacy is the state or condition of being free from being observed or disturbed by other people. And when you first read that, it seems like a bit of an odd definition. Why is that the definition of privacy? Maybe to put this into context, it's useful to think about where did this definition come from? And maybe to help you remember this, I'll give you a bit of history behind this. So there's a particular country in the world that's very well known for having excellent privacy laws. And maybe for you at home, I'll let you think about that for a second. Which country do you think has really excellent privacy laws? And when we teach this live in class, we usually get answers like, oh, Switzerland. And Switzerland does have really good privacy laws as it relates to protecting banking information, but not generally overall. But the country I'm thinking is actually just north of Switzerland. It's Germany. Germany, for many, many decades now, has been one of the leaders in privacy laws and legislation. And for a very good reason. Because Germany has a bit of an interesting history, right? I mean, during World War II, the nazi party in germany was collecting vast amounts of personal information and they were using it for absolutely horrendous and despicable purposes they were collecting information on people's gender ethnicity religion etc and they were using that information for horrendous purposes And so Germany took a pretty strong stand after World War II to say, we are not the Nazi party and these are not the beliefs of Germany as a whole. And Germany started enacting many, many privacy laws to make it very clear that you know, someone's personal information is their information and that when you read this definition now, right, the state of being conditioned for being free from being observed or disturbed by other people, you do not want a government or a company collecting your personal information and using it in a way that would disturb or harm you. And so, you know, maybe given that context, it makes it a little easier to remember this definition. And that's something really important here. Someone's personal information should belong to them. It should be their information that they have the right to protect. And others shouldn't be able to use, say, my personal information in a way that would harm or disturb me. Now, when it comes to defining exactly what is personal information, thankfully, this is beyond the scope of the CISSP exam. There's a lot of, I mean, if this was a privacy exam, we'd have to get into all this terminology here. You'd have to know the subtle and important differences between PII and PHI and so forth. And if you look at different privacy laws and regulations around the world, they all define exactly what is personal information or personal data with subtle but important differences. But thankfully, this is the CISSP exam, a security exam, not a privacy exam. So for purposes of the CISSP exam, you can essentially treat all of these terms as interchangeable. personal data, personally identifiable information, personally sensitive personal information, personal information, et cetera. These all essentially mean the same thing, which is information that can be used on its own or in combination to identify an individual. That's basically what personal information is. Information that can be used on its own or in combination to identify an individual. Now you might be wondering about what does this part mean about on its own or in combination? What that's getting at is what are known as direct versus in direct identifiers. A direct identifier is something that's on its own uniquely identifies an individual. A perfect example of a direct identifier is something like a social insurance number or a passport number or a driver's license number. These are numbers that on their own absolutely, directly, uniquely identify an individual. And typically any direct identifiers are absolutely going to be considered personal data under just about any countries or jurisdictions laws. Where things get a lot more murky is with indirect identifiers. Indirect identifiers are bits of information that on their own don't uniquely identify someone. So you can see great examples here, things like age, gender, ethnicity, someone's postal code. On their own, they don't uniquely identify someone. But when I say where it gets murky, the challenge with indirect identifiers is that if you have a lot of indirect identifiers, and especially if the population size is small enough, you can suddenly start to uniquely identify someone. So let me give you a couple of extreme examples, right? You go to New York City and you just have a couple of indirect identifiers, like someone's You're just looking for someone that's 32 years old, male, and has green eyes. You have no idea who that is in New York City. But you go to a small town of 50 people and you say, I'm looking for the person that's 32 years old, male, and has green eyes. And you're like, oh yeah, that's Bob. He just lives over there. So this is where indirect identifiers can be used to uniquely identify someone, if you have enough of them, and especially if the popularity size is small enough. And that's why certain privacy laws around the world will consider certain indirect identifiers to be personal data. And some laws and regulations even segment out and identify online identifiers, like email addresses, IP addresses, and cookies, and things like that, for example. It varies significantly, though. Don't worry, for the CISP exam, you don't have to memorize all the various subtleties between all these different privacy laws around the world. And there are a lot of privacy laws around the world. We'll get to those in a second. One important bit of terminology you have to take away from this slide. There's a lot of text on this slide. Really only one thing I want you to focus on. It's the text in yellow as usual. Data controllers. This is a really important term that you absolutely need to remember for the exam. A data controller is the owner, the person that's accountable for the protection of personal data. Certain important privacy legislation or regulations like GDPR, the European Union's privacy regime, which we'll talk about in a couple minutes here, GDPR defines this role of a data controller, and that's the exact wording they use, data controller. And what they define data controller to mean is this is the individual that is accountable for the protection of personal data. And GDPR requires that any organization that's collecting personal data have someone in the organization that has this role of data controller. Maybe it's the chief privacy officer or something like that, but someone in the organization has to have this role of a data controller. They have to be accountable for the protection of personal data. So remember that. Now, as I mentioned, there's lots of different privacy laws around the world. You don't really need to know the specifics on any of them, with one major exception, which is GDPR. We'll talk about that in a second. Now, I will mention actually one other, not just because I'm Canadian, but because you may see it on the exam, PIPEDA. PIPEDA, the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, PIPEDA is Canada's privacy law. That sometimes shows up on the exam, so just be aware that PIPEDA is Canada's law. And some of the other ones, in fact, we talked about earlier, more in relation to security control frameworks. If you recall, we talked about SOX and COPA and HIPAA. They also, in the US, those frameworks also contain some privacy components as well. But the major one that we need to talk, spend a few minutes talking about here is GDPR, the General Data Protection Regulation. This is the European Union's overarching privacy regime. And the reason that we're going to spend a few minutes talking about here is because you'll see questions, you could very well see questions about it on the exam. and really not any other privacy law or regulation. If you're wondering why, it's because GDPR is generally considered to be one of the absolute best privacy laws around the world. And what a lot of countries are currently in the process of doing is modeling their own privacy laws after GDPR. So GDPR is kind of considered sort of a global bellwether for privacy regulations. And in fact, a great example of this is California just very recently enacted their own new privacy regime, very much modeled after GDPR. An important part of GDPR is that it is an overarching privacy regime, and by that I mean it covers every sector of the European Union's... any type of organization. government organization, law firm, sole proprietorship, corporation, doesn't matter. GDPR applies. And it's for every sector of the economy, from finance to resource extraction to healthcare and everything in between. GDPR covers everything. The reason I'm highlighting this is because a lot of you will be Americans that are taking this class. America is the exact polar opposite of this. America has what's referred to as a sectorial privacy regime, which means that different sectors of the economy have different privacy laws. For example, within healthcare, you have HIPAA for just protecting the online privacy of children online. You have COPPA. So it's very fragmented and it's very kind of broken up by industry. GDPR is different. It is overarching. Now, there's a few other key bits of information you need to know and memorize about GDPR. The first is this supervisory authority, the SA. What this is, is a supervisory authority is meant to be an independent authority. body within each European Union state, so within Germany, France, Spain, Hungary, you know, every European Union state has to establish their own supervisory authority. And the role of the supervisory authority is that they will investigate any complaints, any potential privacy breaches. And so this next line here, a data subject, that's the individual, has the right to lodge a complaint with the supervisory authority. So let's say I'm a citizen of the European Union. If I say that, hey, I don't think Facebook is using, you know, or if I say, I think Facebook is using my data in a way that I find inappropriate or, you know, in violation of GDPR. Maybe I asked for them to delete all my data and they didn't, right? That's the right to be forgotten. Who would I lodge the complaint with? The supervisory authority. One more other little tidbit about GDPR that's not on this slide is how quickly an organization needs to report a privacy breach. And it is very quickly, within 72 hours, within three days. If an organization has discovered that they have a privacy breach, they are required to report that within 72 hours. And that is a very stringent requirement. And that doesn't matter where the breach was first identified. So if the breach was identified first with, say, the organization's cloud service provider, the organization still just has sorry, 72 hours in which to report the breach. So organizations have to think about this. They have to make sure that if they're outsourcing systems and data to a service provider, that the service provider will notify them, hopefully within something like 24 or 48 hours. So that gives the organization time to then report it to the regulator. Privacy breaches have to be reported within 72 hours. Now, the next piece we're going to talk about here are what are known as the OECD guidelines. The OECD stands for the Organization of Economic Cooperation and Development. This is a multinational organization that's all about trying to facilitate global commerce and trade, trying to make it easier for companies to operate around the world. And what the OECD noticed is that there's a lot of privacy laws out there and a lot of organizations were struggling with how should they structure their privacy program to try and make sure they're in compliance with all these various privacy laws around the world. And so the OECD came up with what they refer to as their privacy guidelines. A really key word here is the word guideline. If you recall, as John talked about, guidelines are suggestions, recommendations. They're not, thou shalt do this, right? They're not requirements. And that's an important thing to remember here. These are guidelines. They're recommendations. for basically to sort of suggest to an organization, here's how you might want to structure your privacy program to make it easier to be in compliance with all these various privacy laws and regulations around the world. And that's fundamentally what OECD is. It's a set of guidelines to help a company structure, have the right foundation in place for their privacy program, which will then make it a lot easier to be in compliance with the very specific laws and regulations of each country and jurisdiction. So let's take a moment here and walk through these privacy guidelines. It starts with the collection limitation principle. And this is the idea that an organization should only collect only the personal information that they need and nothing more. I mean, this is just a good idea from a security perspective. If you can minimize the amount of personal data that you have, you have to worry much less about a breach, right? No personal data, you don't have to worry about a breach at all. And so if organizations can minimize the amount of personal data that they're collecting, it makes the job of protecting that personal data a lot easier. And so the collection limitation principle is about only collecting absolutely what you need. And of course, make sure you do it lawfully and so forth. The data quality principle is about this idea that if an organization collects personal data, they're now accountable for the integrity of that data. And this is important. In certain extreme cases, the integrity of personal data can quite literally be life and death. Let me give you an extreme example. Imagine I walk into a hospital emergency room and I'm like, oh, I got a pain in my side. And the ER nurse checks me in, asks for... For instance, am I allergic to penicillin? And I say, yes, I'm allergic to penicillin. And they gather a few more bits of information, and then I go, ah, and I pass it on the floor. So they rush me into an operating room. And as part of the operation on me, they check the system to see if I'm allergic to penicillin, because they want to give me penicillin. And the system says, nope, Rob is not allergic to penicillin. And so they give me penicillin. What could the result of that be? Bad stuff and death. Exactly. I mean, this is obviously a very extreme example, but this is the idea. When an organization collects personal data, they have to maintain the accuracy, the integrity of that personal data. Next one here is the purpose specification principle. And what this is about is that an organization should define exactly why they're collecting someone's personal information. They should specify, we're collecting your personal information for X, Y, and Z purposes. And then closely tied to that is the use limitation principle, which means, of course, the organization should only use the personal data for the reasons that they've specified. Now the next one here, the security safeguards principle. This is just emphasizing what I talked about at the very beginning, which is you cannot have privacy without security. You need good security controls in place to achieve privacy. And so the security safeguards principle essentially calls that out. The openness principle, and this is a bit of a subtle one, but the openness principle is essentially about an organization's culture. If an organization is gonna be collecting and using personal information, that organization should be open and transparent and have things like a very clearly defined privacy policy and make sure that they're open and transparent with their customers and everyone else about here's why we're collecting personal information, here's what we're using it for, et cetera. Openness principle is essentially about the organization's culture. The individual participation principle is this idea that my personal data belongs to me and I should have the choice as to who I share it with and what they do with my personal information. And maybe, not maybe, but even I could even say to an organization that's collected my personal information, you know what? I don't want you to have my personal data anymore. I want you to erase all of my information. This is sometimes referred to as the right to be forgotten. And this is all sort of this core idea that it was the data subject. It's my personal data. I should be able to choose who collects it, what they use it for, and whether or not I want them to retain it anymore. And then the final principle here is the accountability principle. And this is the idea that just emphasizing that when an organization collects personal data, they are now accountable for the protection of that data, right? There has to be a data controller with accountability for the protection of the personal data. So there you go. Those are the OECD privacy guidelines. Guidelines. Recommendations. And the final thing just to close out this section on privacy here is this is why we're talking about it on a security certification, right? You cannot achieve privacy without security.

One small but important topic that we need to talk about here related to cloud security is cloud security roles. And there's really, for the CISSP exam, there's really only two major roles that you need to know about that I've actually been mentioning, but let's formally define them here. So the first one is the cloud consumer or the cloud customer. This is the individual or organization who's consuming, using the cloud services. And then, of course, the other major one is the cloud service provider, the CSP. This is the organization that's providing the cloud services. And there's two really important words we have to talk about here related to this accountability and responsibility. Who's accountable here? The answer unequivocally is the consumer. The customer is gonna be accountable for any system, any data, any systems that they run in the cloud, and the cloud service provider will be responsible for any customer data, any systems that are running in their cloud. Because remember, of course, you can't outsource accountability. It's a really important concept to understand. But here's a good question. Could a single organization be both the consumer and the provider? The answer is yes, right? Like if an organization was using, say, a private cloud deployment model where they built their own private cloud on-prem in their own data center, they would be both the consumer and the provider of that cloud. So these two roles don't necessarily have to be different organizations. They could potentially be just within one organization. The other roles here, things like cloud service partners and all these other things here, brokers, etc. This is actually a slide from our CCSP deck. So don't worry about memorizing all these for the whatever this is, the CISSP exam. It's getting late in the day here. Okay. And you've already seen this exact slide before, but I just, I really want to kind of beat you over the head with it. Accountability versus responsibility. This is such a critically important concept related to cloud security, right? And the owner remains accountable. And it is impossible to outsource accountability. All the owner can do is outsource responsibility. And this is why we spend so much time talking about contracts and SLAs and everything else because... the owner remains accountable. They have to make sure that they've identified the risks, that they've identified the controls that will mitigate those risks. And then they've communicated, they need to be making sure that the cloud service provider who's responsible is implementing the right controls and that they're operating effectively and so forth, right? So all that the owner can outsource is the responsibility, but not the accountability. So really important to keep that in mind.

And as I mentioned, heuristics-based systems use either static or dynamic scanning. Now, heuristic systems are a brilliant idea, but they just aren't really that prevalent today because it's exceptionally difficult to explicitly program a heuristic system to look for and figure out what is suspicious code. And there's a lot, this is true for a lot of problems that we have in computing. It's really hard to explicitly program computer systems and to do certain things. Let me give you a kind of another interesting example, and that's walking, right? For years, people have tried to build robots that can walk, especially bipedal robots. If you want to look into this, right, go and look up Boston Dynamics. They've been at this for close to a couple decades now. And some of their earlier prototypes were pretty primitive. That has changed rapidly though. The core idea behind a lot of these machine learning and AI algorithms is that instead of having to explicitly program a system how to do something, you get the system to learn how to do something by itself. And that kind of mimics a lot of biological systems, right? Like, as human beings, no one explicitly tells us how to walk, right? We just figure it out by falling over a lot. I've got a couple of kids. It was amazing watching them learn to walk and start to toddle around. It was such a cool thing to see and experience. And that's kind of the idea behind these machine learning and AI systems is... if you can essentially train them to do something without having to explicitly say, do this, then do that. Often they're trained on very large data sets. So you give them a whole bunch of kind of learning data and they'll develop an algorithm that will solve certain problems. And what's often fascinating about this is once these algorithms have been trained, the researchers that kind of built these systems, they don't understand the algorithms. Like they've learned something quite unique and often undecipherable on their own. And these techniques are now, of course, being heavily applied in the security space. I mean, the big cloud providers especially, other companies as well, but the big cloud providers, they're investing massively. Like Google's a great example. Google employs a vast number of AI researchers, and quite a few of them are working in the security space, trying to figure out essentially how to make our computer systems more resilient, have more like an automatic immune system and learn to identify problems on their own and potentially even start to fix these problems. And so here's some of the kind of major upcoming applications of AI that we're gonna see, I would bet, more and more of in the next few years. One of the big one is in threat detection and classification. So instead of having to explicitly program your SIM system to look for these types of attacks, these systems should be able to start to train themselves. Network risk scoring, helping to sort of suss out the impact and the severity of certain types of risks. Automating a lot of certain routines. You know, there's a bunch of different applications of this. Again, don't expect any specific questions about this on the exam because this is a very early nascent space, but it is rapidly developing. And I think we'll see some really amazing innovations in this space in the next few years. It'll be quite exciting to watch. We're in network security and we're still at layer three, which as you know, is the network layer. And we just talked about the differences between IP version four and IP version six, which was obviously developed to address some of the needs, for example, expanding the addressing scheme to 128 bits, but also including very nicely for us something called IPsec, which includes really two capabilities, authentication and encryption. right into the IPv6 protocol. In this section, very quick section, we're going to talk about something that has really allowed us to still use IPv4, even though we've known about this limitation as far as addressing is concerned. And, you know, IP version 4 uses a IP addressing scheme that is 32 bits, as we said earlier. And what the industry has done is they've divided those 32 bits into... four groups of eight bits. They're referred to as the four octets of the IP address. And that's why IP addresses end up looking like four numbers separated by dots. There are no dots. We just do it for readability purposes. But within IP version 4, there had to be some rules that were set up so that companies like yours could use what are called private IP addresses internally. And other companies, for example, can use the same IP address ranges internally. And then, of course, we need something like NAT, network address translation, to allow machines from your internal network that are using private addresses to be able to go out to the internet and so on. So NAT would do that translation, keep track so that when the request comes back we could redirect it back to your machine. An example of a proxy which we'll talk about. so the industry had to allocate what are called private address ranges that companies could use internally what you're seeing here on this slide are those private address ranges that are allocated as being private meaning any company for example can use and the typically you know most companies are using the 10 dot ip address range internally and then of course as we allow machines to go out to the internet we need to translate those to routable IP addresses and then route them back to the internal IP addresses ranges as those requests come back. Now, as far as the exam is concerned, we decided to add two slides here to allow you to answer those questions correctly if you do see those questions on the exam. But people have seen questions on the exam that relate to, you know, which of these are identified in RFC 1918 as being, for example, private address ranges. And so what they're going to give you is a range within the middle one that you see on your screen here the 172.16 range all the way up to 172.31 is also allocated as being private address range so they're probably going to give you that you know one ip address within that range as the answer to the question which of the following is identified as being a IP address range that is private, for example, or IP address that is private. All right, so keep that in mind, 172.16 all the way up to 172.31. The other question is, that is specifically referenced in the exam outline that people are seeing relates to what we call subnetting and of course this is something that your network administrators will get into and we don't really have to learn about but You know, the one question that some people are seeing is, it goes something like this. How many hosts are supported in a class B, as in Bravo, subnet, for example? And this is getting into, as I said, subnetting. And the way that you figure this one out, because it's class B, is that class B has two to the 16, two raised to the power of 16 hosts that are available for it in that address space, for example. Although two are specifically reserved for something specific. So the answer to how many hosts are allowed in a Class B address space is 2 raised to the power of 16, which as you can see here is 65,536. But because two of those IP addresses are specifically reserved for something, the answer is 2 to the 16 minus 2, which is 65,534. If you remember that, you'll be just fine.

LESSON 168: Lesson 362
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Here's a really common security challenge related to access management that many organizations have. Let's say you've bought some application from a vendor, and it's a complex application, and you're probably gonna pay for some sort of support contract to allow that vendor to remotely log, well, first of all, to help you install the application, you're probably gonna create an account in the application for the vendor that allows them to remotely log into the application and usually gives them root privileges so they can help you walk you through the installation process. And then these accounts are often maintained for years because you've got an ongoing support contract where let's say either you need to install a new update to the application or you're having some sort of issue with the application, you want that vendor to be able to remotely log in and help you troubleshoot the problem. And so it's really common that organizations will have quite a few of these remotely accessible accounts that provide root access into an application and this is the most dangerous type of good account to have around right an account that can be remotely accessible that provides root privileges to a system By default, these accounts should always be disabled, and they should only be enabled for a short period of time when that vendor's coming in, helping to support the application, and then right away that account should be disabled again. But I can tell you from years of conducting audits and looking at these accounts many, many times, most organizations don't remember to disable them again. And so it was really common, just from my own experience when I was conducting access reviews. You know, you'd ask for a list of all the access to an application and you'd find these vendor accounts, remotely accessible, root access, that were sitting there, enabled. Dangerous. And so this is what just-in-time access is meant to address. Just-in-time access is essentially the idea that it's a tool that will ensure that these accounts are disabled and other sort of privileged accounts are automatically disabled all the time. And these accounts will only be enabled when they're needed. And usually only for a short period of time, maybe a few hours, and then those accounts, because this is an automated process, will be automatically disabled again. And so one of the big security benefits of just-in-time access is that by default, all of these sort of dangerous accounts are always gonna be disabled by default. And they will only be enabled for a short period of time when they're needed. And the way that just-in-time access systems typically works is that this is an automated process. There'll be a process that a vendor or even an employee could go through to say, okay, I need this access to this system to do this. And there can be an automated approval process in place where once they've gone through that automated approval process, This account will be enabled for just a short period of time and then automatically disabled. That's the idea of just-in-time access.

============================================================
LESSON 169: Lesson 363
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We're now going to talk about a few important testing considerations, specifically as it relates to how you handle errors and remediate issues that be identified. And we'll also touch on this kind of ethical minefield that is ethical disclosure. So let's start with automated error handling. What this is about is, you know, a lot of the testing that we do nowadays is automated testing. We have all sorts of various scripts that are running. And what you don't want to have happen is, let's say you've got, you know, 100 scripts that are meant to run. If the first one fails, that shouldn't prevent all the other ones from failing. If one of the scripts fails, it should fail gracefully. An error should be captured and someone should obviously look at why that test failed. But then, that shouldn't prevent all the other tests from continuing on. So this is just an important thing to make sure, you know, because often our automated testing, we're wanting this to run sort of like 24 by 7 periodically. And it's a good way that we go about detecting problems, issues with our systems. And so we want this testing to keep going, even if some errors occur. Now, when it comes to remediation, if an issue is identified as part of testing, there's a really important part to think about here, and that is who's accountable for making sure that whatever issue, whatever bug problem is identified is fixed. And of course, the answer is the owner of the system. And that's really important to understand because often bugs issues will be found by, say, the security team through things like pen tests and so forth. But ultimately, like the role of security in this is to advise and say, OK, here's what we found and here's advice we would give on how to remediate this issue. But ultimately, the accountability for fixing the issue rests with the owner of the system, the person that's accountable for the system. And they're often the ones that are going to have to, you know, because sometimes remediation can cost money. It can take time. And so they're the ones that have to usually pay for it. And of course, they're the ones that are accountable. So it should ultimately be them that are accountable for dealing with any issues that they've identified. One final piece we'll talk about here, which I don't think you'll see much of anything about on the exam, is ethical disclosure. This is becoming an extremely challenging and fraught space in security nowadays. One of the reasons is that there's a lot of people out there around the world that are looking for issues, bugs, vulnerabilities in systems. and it's you know it's a bit it's kind of like the wild west it's it's not clearly defined if you know if someone finds something who do they have to report it to how quickly do they have to report it all these sorts of things and so you get some pretty extreme cases happening where you know researchers will discover things And then the question arises, are they going to report this to the owner of the system? Or are they going to sell the bug to someone else? This happens all the time, right? There's organizations that will pay lots of money, nations and companies that will pay lots of money for bugs. And to help combat this, a lot of organizations are now starting to offer things like bug bounty programs where companies like Apple, Microsoft, Google, they'll pay, in some cases, vast sums of money for issues identified in their software to encourage sometimes somewhat ethically challenged researchers to report their bugs and the vulnerabilities that they have found to the company. Like I know for Apple, for instance, they just recently increased the payouts. For certain vulnerabilities, they'll pay up to a million dollars. And those are pretty significant amounts of money. And so what they're really trying to encourage here is a couple of things. They want to encourage people to look for bugs in their software, and if they find them, to report them to, say, Apple. That's the whole idea behind these bug bounty programs. But all this is in a rapidly evolving space. And there's a lot of ethical quandaries that will sometimes come up. Another great example is a researcher might discover a vulnerability. They'll report it to the organization. They'll do the right thing. They'll say to a company, hey, we found this bug in your software or this vulnerability in your software. And then the company will say, yeah, we don't think it's that important. And now the researcher is stuck in this position saying, like, no, this is a big problem. Like, this is going to affect a ton of your users. And you're not taking this seriously. You're not investing the time and the money necessary to remediate this. And now, like, what does the researcher do? Do they disclose it to the public, which is then going to leave a lot of people vulnerable? Like, this is why it's a bit of an ethical minefield. So not going to get questions on this on the exam, I don't think, but certainly an interesting space to keep an eye on.

The idea is we've introduced this concept of an architecture as being a whole bunch of things that work together, a whole bunch of components. Well, if you keep those components to have very simple functionality, then overall you're going to have a much more secure architecture, right? So if we keep the functionality of each of the components to as little as possible, as small and simple as possible. That means that there are less vulnerabilities and less exploits that are possible with each of those components. And so the idea is we keep it as simple as possible from a functionality perspective of each of the components that make up the architecture itself. The industry sometimes also refers to this as economy of mechanism. So economy of mechanism, keep it simple, means basically the same thing. all right here's the next concept that has become really popular in the industry and there are some you know really large organizations like Google for example that are really driving this and it's called zero trust very appropriately because that's the basis for how we design architectures. The idea is you trust nothing and verify everything regardless of whether that transaction or request is coming in. Like when you look at traditionally, and historically how we've built architectures anything inside of your network perimeter for example you know has this implied level of trust already because it's inside of your network this concept of zero trust removes that So the idea is it doesn't matter where anything originates, whether it's inside of your network or outside of your network, you start with no trust at all, which means that if you're going to allow certain things, It requires a higher level of authentication and verification and real-time monitoring and device authentication and everything else. So we start with trusting nothing. And what this requires really in order to implement is really granular level of security where we have to be able to do things like device authentication and understand the health of those devices and then we do authentication to really good levels of anything that is trying to do something with anything else and then of course you know a really good understanding of how this can work is to have real time monitoring. You can't really do this without real time monitoring and have the ability to react in real time as well to anything that might look suspicious. That's the concept of zero trust. Being pioneered and used in a lot of the industry these days requires really granular you know, attribute based access control, for example. In other words, more logging, more real-time monitoring, for example. You can reflect on that by saying, well, they're not the same, privacy is not the same as security, but you cannot achieve your privacy requirements without properly implemented security controls. When people say privacy by design, We know that that means security by design to address obviously compliance requirements related to privacy. So we've been saying this for decades. Security needs to be designed properly into your architectures. to address, in this case, the right level of privacy protection. All right, so we just introduced the concept of remote access through VPNs. And I'll just remind you that a VPN really is a two-step process. requires tunneling first, which we defined as simply encapsulating one packet or datagram inside of another. And then if you want a VPN, it requires encryption on top of that. right so this is why especially when the industry refers to terms like tunneling and vpns we have to be careful and you have to be careful as far as the exam is concerned right because When the word VPN or the letters VPN are used, then that implies that you've got tunneling plus encryption. But you can also tunnel without encryption. And you might be wondering why do you want to do that? And the answer to that is really for two reasons. I mean tunneling is used first of all to force something to go down a specific path that is specified by the new header. So if you think about what tunneling is, we take a datagram that includes the original header and the data and we place it into the data portion of the new datagram then we're going to force that datagram that we've tunneled encapsulated regardless of what it had in its original header based on the new header that forces it to go down a specific path. And in fact, this is why it's called tunneling. We're forcing something to go down a specific path that is specified by the new header. But once you place something into that data portion, it's not automatically protected. It can still be seen. And that's what tunneling is. We're not protecting the data portion of the new datagram that contains, for example, the original packet or datagram. So the other reason why you want to do this is to carry across your network certain types of packets and datagrams that maybe cannot travel across those segments. And so they may be non-IP packets related to things like SCADA systems. It could be IPX or something like that, that you need to take and tunnel across for example packets that can travel across your network so that's the other reason why we want to do tunneling and so there are some protocols that appear specifically in the exam outline like GRE for example so GRE is a tunneling protocol that does not support encryption and therefore it cannot be used as a VPN but it can be used to tunnel for example non-IP packets across your IP network or we can use it to force something down a certain path as we said before so the animation here shows you what this really does but it does not support encryption so it cannot technically be used as a VPN because a VPN would be tunneling plus encryption All right, so that's what GRE, remember, GRE does not support encryption, can only tunnel, for example. Now, one of the other items that appears specifically in the exam outline is what's called split tunneling. If you think about these words, it tells you exactly what we're trying to do here. So let's imagine that you work for a company and you're traveling around, for example, and you check into your hotel you need to connect back in to your internal network obviously the your VPN solution that you have on your laptop is going to host that all right so let's say you connect to the hotel network and then you activate your VPN solution that'll create a a tunnel with encryption, because that's what a VPN is, right into your corporate network. Now let's say that you need to go out to the internet for whatever reason. The way that this would typically be hosted is obviously through your corporate network and the encryption that you're using to protect the traffic. What split tunneling allows is the bypassing of your corporate network and therefore the encryption and the policies that your organization may be imposing on you. So split tunneling solutions, what they allow you to do is even though you're connected, to your company's network through a VPN. It allows you, for example, to go out to the internet using the hotel network directly. And the reason that this really exists is to save bandwidth. But the problem from a security perspective is that now any policies that your organization may be imposing on you are going to be bypassed. And so really with split tunneling, you have to be careful. Because you do have a connection into your internal network, but now you also have an untrusted connection into the internet, bypassing your policies and therefore security controls that would normally have been imposed on by something that does not allow, for example, split tunneling. Thank you.

So continuous delivery automates all of those steps up until the point where you allow, obviously, users and owners to be able to review things and acceptance. So we've been talking about digital forensics here and the forensic investigation process. And there's one little piece that we have to add in here, which is we have to talk in a little more detail about some specific types of evidence. Because there's four of these, especially four of these, that you might get questions about on the exam. We're gonna walk you through all the different types of evidence, but there's four in particular that we're gonna focus on. The first important one that you need to know about is what's called real evidence. And what real evidence means is that it's a tangible, physical object, so like the literal smoking gun at the crime scene, that would be real evidence because it's a tangible object. Now, really importantly, and this is important to remember for the exam, data on anything, on a hard drive, on an SSD, on a CD, whatever, is not considered to be real evidence. Because as human beings, you look at a hard drive, you can't see the data. You can't see the ones and zeros. And even if you could see the ones and zeros, we can't translate that in our minds into a picture or a video There's no direct proof of its veracity, right? That's the word that they use. It's degree of truth. Yeah, exactly. Yeah. So just remember, real evidence means it's a tangible physical object, which data is not. Yeah. The next one that you see there is direct evidence, keyword direct, which means it speaks for itself and there's no inference element associated with it. It's direct evidence that obviously is provided through You know, confessions, for example, also that smoking gun and so on. But it's direct evidence that obviously, you know, hopefully would be very valuable as far as investigations are concerned. Yeah, it requires no interpretation, no inference, right? The smoking gun with the killer's fingerprint on it and the ballistics match the victim. Excellent. Yeah, no inference required. Now, the next two, next three, actually, circumstantial, collaborative, and hearsay evidence, these are, you know, these are obviously exist from a legal perspective, but you're very unlikely to get questions on them. So we've included them here for completeness, but we're not going to go through them in And if you watch crime shows, you hear those words all the time. Hearsay evidence is not direct evidence that can be admissible, for example. Someone heard someone saying something else. Very reliable. Yeah, that's right. Next one is best evidence. And best evidence, again, keyword best here. I mean, original evidence. It's not a copy. There's no secondary element to it, right? It's the original, best evidence. Yeah, imagine you're in a courtroom and you're presenting your evidence. What would the court prefer, the original or a photocopy? Exactly. Yep. That's the best evidence rule. And then the final one, this ties back to the very first one we talked about, right? Real evidence. Secondary evidence is a reproduction or a substitute and data is considered to be secondary evidence because we have to reproduce it with an algorithm or something like that for us to be able to understand it and so remember that for the exam secondary evidence is data is considered to be secondary evidence yeah and it might be worthwhile just reiterating here I know we've done it before but what you usually see in yellow on our slides is what's really important because it certainly addresses what people are seeing on the exam and what's in the exam outline, which obviously is important. Yeah, there's so much you have to memorize, we try and make it easy for you. So there you go, types of evidence. Excellent.
