LESSON 175: Lesson 369
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

There's two additional types of testing that we need to talk about here, decision table analysis and state-based analysis. These are both fairly specialized types of testing that you'll use in certain scenarios. Let's start with decision table analysis. The main reason that you would use this type of testing is if you have an application that has quite a few different inputs and therefore quite a few different outputs, And what you're testing for here is that it can quickly become very difficult to test an application and make sure you've tested all the possible combinations. So let me give you a really simple example, our little flashcard app. It's an incredibly simple app. but it still has a few options. You can go into the menu and you can decide, do you want to see terms first or definitions first when you go to the next card? So that's one thing you'd have to test. The next thing is you have kind of three options. Do you want to see cards that you haven't seen yet? Do you want to see cards that you've marked as don't know? Do you want to see cards that you marked as don't know? Even with those just few options, right? Do you want term first or definition first? Do you want to see only cards that you don't know or only cards that you do know or cards that you do know and the ones that you haven't seen yet? Even with just these few options, you can see there's suddenly all these different combinations that we have to test. All right, we have to test term first, only cards we haven't seen. Term first, only cards we've marked as we know. Definition first, you know. You can see how this suddenly gets very complicated. And that's the whole point of decision table analysis. You can build a giant table that shows all these various input combinations and you can make sure that you've got really good coverage with your testing. You've covered all the possible combinations. So that's what decision table analysis is for. State-based analysis is particularly useful when you want to test an application or a protocol or a system that can exist in different states. So let me give you a great example. The TCPIP protocol. This is a protocol John's going to talk about in great detail in Domain 4. This is what's referred to as a connection-oriented protocol. And there's kind of like three major states that a TCP IP connection can exist in. The first state is when you're establishing the connection. And as John will talk about, when you want to establish a TCP IP connection, you have to go through what's called the three-way handshake. The first system has to send a synchronized packet. The second system then sends back a SYN ACK, like I acknowledge, I want to synchronize as well. And then the first system replies with an ACK. This is called the three-way handshake. And so if you're testing TCPIP, the first state that you would test it in is in this handshake and this establishment of the protocol phase. Once the connection has been established, you now need to do different testing because now you're in the phase where data can be sent back and forth. And then there's even a third phase where you're tearing down the connection. And so the idea here is that lots of systems, lots of different protocols will exist in different states and they act differently in different states. So you obviously need to modify your testing based on the state that the system is in. And that's the whole idea behind state-based analysis.

============================================================
LESSON 176: Lesson 370
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

There's one more threat modeling methodology that we need to talk about that you may encounter on the exam, and it's known as DREAD. DREAD's quite a unique threat modeling methodology compared to the other two that we've talked about, STRIDE and PASTA, in that DREAD is not actually about identifying threats, Rather, the whole point of dread is to prioritize threats that have been identified. And so, remember when we talked about stride, stride is kind of this quick and easy way of identifying threats, but it doesn't help you prioritize the threats in any way like pasta does. And so stride and dread are often used together, with stride being used to identify the threats and dread being used to prioritize the threats. So let's talk through this dread threat modeling methodology. And essentially the whole purpose of this, like I said, is to help you prioritize threats. How do you do that? Well, you need to evaluate, essentially, how easy is the threat to exploit? How damaging is it going to be to the organization? How easy is it to discover? By sort of thinking through all these things, you could think about, therefore, how concerning is a particular threat. And that's exactly what Dread does. And it's another one of these acronyms where the letters D-R-E-A-D each stand for a different key point that you're going to evaluate and then score to figure out how concerning a threat is. So the first thing you're looking at is damage. What's the total amount of damage? In other words, if this threat were to occur, how big of an impact would it have on the organizations, users, and so forth? And you give this a score between one and three. One meaning, yeah, not that big a deal. And three, a very significant amount of damage to the organization. So that's the first key point you would measure. The next one is reproducibility. And this is basically measuring how easily can the threat be reproduced. And this is actually an important measure because some threats are really easy to for an attacker to replicate. It works every single time. Maybe there's not even that many steps involved. It has really high reproducibility. And obviously, a threat that is easier to reproduce is more concerning. So again, you give it a score. The next thing you're looking at is exploitability. And this is very specifically measuring how much effort is involved by the attacker. Some threats, you just run a tiny bit of automated code and boom, you can exploit the threat. Other threats, this is one I'm thinking about related to S-MIME. It took like two weeks to exploit the threat. And the attacker had to send very specific messages at very specific times over a two-week period. And it didn't even work every time to reproduce a particular threat and to exploit a particular threat. So again, you're measuring exploitability, giving it a score. Next thing you're looking at, the A stands for affected users, and this is where you're looking at how many of your customers, how many of your own internal employees, how many people would be affected if this threat were to occur, and you give it a score. And then the last one that you're looking at is discoverability. And this is exactly what it sounds like. How easily can this threat be discovered? Maybe it's just a really simple scan that anyone could do of the internet and they can find this. Or maybe it's really difficult, really involved. Again, you give it a score. The easier it would be to discover the threat, the higher the score. And then you add these up. And so you go through this process for each threat. You come up with a total score. And if you're evaluating, say, 20 different threats, you're going to end up with different scores for each one. And that's how you can rank which threat is of greatest concern on down to least concern. So there you go, DREAD. One more threat modeling methodology you need to know about.

============================================================
LESSON 177: Lesson 371
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Physical security. Physical security is absolutely integral to the security of an organization. And physical security controls start all the way from outside the perimeter of a building to all the way within the building. And physical security is, as I mentioned, of paramount importance to an organization. If an organization wants to achieve the three goals of security that we've talked about, right? Confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Physical security is... critical to achieving all these things. Availability, for instance. Without physical security providing a good, clean, consistent supply of power and network connection and, especially in a data center, air at the right temperature and the right humidity, you're not going to have systems that are running. If you don't have a good clean supply of electricity in environmental systems, you're not going to have systems that are providing the integrity that you need. And physical security is of paramount importance to confidentiality as well. There's an expression that I love in physical security, which is, if you can touch the box, you own the box. In other words, if someone can gain physical access to a system, It's usually trivially easy to gain access to that system. Remember earlier we talked about bypass controls. Bypass controls are intentionally built into a system. And a lot of bypass controls are very easy to access if you can get physical access to a device. If you can touch the box, you own the box. So physical security is of paramount importance to achieving logical security. Now, when it relates to the goals of physical security, there's one thing you should remember. There's one primary goal of physical security that trumps everything else, that is most important above anything else. What do you think that is? Safety of people. Safety of people is the most important goal in physical security. For good reason, right? I mean, ethically it's the correct thing to do. People are the most valuable asset of the organization, and you can't run an organization without people. And so, the most important thing to do in physical security is ensure the safety of people and that overrides this most important primary goal of physical security the safety of people overrides many other controls and i'll show you some examples of that later on in this domain Now, the controls that we have in physical security are named slightly different than the logical security controls. You'll recall the logical security controls were directive, deterrent, preventative, detective, corrective, and recovery, and compensating. Slightly differently named in physical security, we have deter slash prevent, delay, detect, assess, and respond. But if you recall, when we talked about the logical security controls, we talked about how what you always want to have at a minimum is a complete control. A complete control is at a minimum preventive, detective, and corrective. And it is exactly the same underlying concept that we want to have in physical security. We always want to prevent as many risks as we possibly can, but we can't prevent everything. So therefore we need good detective controls, so we can detect when a risk has occurred, and then, if we've detected a risk that occurs, we want to correct it. So at a minimum, we always want preventive, detective, and corrective. It's exactly the same concept in physical security. Deter, prevent, that's your preventive control. Detect, it's your detective control. And then assessing and responding, that's how we correct in physical security. By assessing what's happened and responding to it, that's how we correct. So it's the same underlying concept. Now you'll notice that there was one control I skipped over here. Delay controls. There's lots of different things that are considered delay controls that slow someone down in physical security. The textbook example of a delay control that you should remember are locks. We'll talk about this more when we talk about locks, but locks are considered a delay control because there has never yet been an unpickable lock created. Locks just slow someone down, so they're considered delay control. Now, the major sort of categories of threats that we have to physical security are natural environmental utility systems and human-made political events. So when we're talking about natural environmental threats, what we're talking about here are acts of nature, force majeure. So things like hurricanes, floods, tornadoes, earthquakes. These are all major environmental threats that can have a huge impact on a building and the operations of a building. So we're concerned with any environmental threats. In physical security, we're very concerned with any threats to the three core utilities, power, network, and HVAC, heating, ventilation, and air conditioning. And then kind of the final major category of threat that we're concerned with is human-made political events. I mean, when we're filming this right now in, what is it, 2022? Is it 2022? Okay. It's getting late in the day here. I'm not even sure what year it is. I mean, in 2022 right now, there's some pretty horrific stuff happening in the world. The war between Russia and Ukraine is happening. There's some pretty amazing, crazy stuff happening in Iran right now. These sort of really tragic events. I mean, obviously the biggest concern is what's happening to the people. This also has a big impact on business and their operations. So, physical security is concerned about these sorts of things. In a slightly lighter view on things, take a look at this picture here. Anyone, do you recognize what major event this is from? If you're a hockey fan, you might recognize it. This is a few years ago when the Vancouver Canucks lost the Stanley Cup to those bastards, the Boston Bruins. People in Vancouver lost their minds. And the reason I like to show this picture is I was actually living in Vancouver at this time. And I was right in the middle of this insane riot of people lighting cars on fire and crazy stuff because their team lost a hockey game. By the way, that's me that's up the telephone pole. I decided that was a good place to go after I lit the car on fire. But actually, the reason I like to show this photo is that at the time, one of my clients was a major telecom provider. And they had a really high-end data center just a few blocks from where this picture was taken. And that data center was actually knocked offline as a direct result of a hockey team losing a hockey game. What happened was in the alley beside the data center, another car had been lit on fire. And up above the car in the alley were some transformers. And if you're familiar with transformers, they're typically cooled with oil. If you get a transformer hot enough, they literally explode. And that's exactly what happened. Three of these transformers popped. blew up and that's caused such a massive shock of electricity that it just fried the equipment in this data center. And, you know, that was despite the fact that this was a really high-end data center. They had all sorts of proper electrical equipment to try and make sure that, you know, lightning strikes things like that wouldn't take out their equipment but this was just such a massive surge in power that it ended up knocking out a ton of their equipment i was actually in the data center a few days after this happened the whole whole place like reeked of ozone there was just so much fried electrical equipment so these are the sorts of things physical security is concerned with Some additional threats to physical security, a couple that we have to highlight here, dumpster diving. This is where you're going through someone's trash. A more modern version of this, go on eBay, look for equipment that companies are disposing of, retiring. You can find all sorts of highly sensitive data. There was a police department in the US that sold off some big Xerox printers and they forgot to wipe or remove the hard drives from these printers. And so when you go to make a copy of a bunch of pages on a printer, it will store those pages so it can make multiple copies. Police station didn't think about this, sold their printers on eBay, and there was hundreds of thousands of highly sensitive police records that was on these printers. They had to declare a privacy breach. The other one I'll hit on here is shoulder surfing. Shoulder surfing is where you look over someone's shoulder to see what's on their screen. The modern day example of this one is a lot of companies nowadays are putting these really high resolution 4K cameras in their buildings and some of them are like pzt cameras pan zoom and tilt i have another fun story on this one there was actually a pen test so i was managing a team that was conducting a pen test And one of the guys on my team sent me a link and he's like, dude, you gotta check this out. And to be very clear, I had a signed NDA, et cetera. It was appropriate for me to be looking at this. But when I checked the link, it was a link to a really high resolution PZT security camera in the back of the security operations center of the company that we were performing this pen test on. So we were actually able to like zoom into the analyst screens and watch what alerts they were getting on their screens and checking to see if the, you know, the actions that we were performing the pen test were generating alerts on the analyst screen. So that was obviously not great. So it's kind of a modern day example of shoulder surfing. One more little piece that I'll cover before we wrap up this video. And that's just a concept that we've covered before, but I'll reiterate here. It's this concept of defense in depth or layered security. And this is that basic idea that we never just want to have a single layer of defense. We want to have multiple layers of defense. And at each layer, we want to have a complete control, preventive, detective, and corrective. So in physical security, often the first layer of defense will be the perimeter, the fence around the building. The fence will be your preventive control. Then maybe things like electromagnet or sorry, optical tripwires or microwave tripwires or there's sensors that you can install in fences to detect if they're being cut. That could be your detective control. And then your corrective control might be releasing the hungry dogs or getting a security guard to go out and look at what is happening. So the first layer of defense around the building's perimeter would be the fencing. And then your next layer of defense are probably the walls of the building. the walls will be the preventative control things like camera systems will be your detective control and then guards or something like that will again be your corrective control and that's you know it's your layers of defense aren't just going to end at the perimeter like the the walls of the building In a secure facility, you'll have often multiple additional layers of defense, additional doors and sort of zones that someone has to go through to get to, say, the data center floor. And at each layer, you want to have preventive, detective, and corrective controls. Now, there's one more fun little thing I want to ask you here. I'll back up a second here. It's a fun question, Mil, I want you to think about for a second. What do you think would be the most secure number of entrances and exits to have in and out of the building? John, what do you think? What's the most secure number? Uh, zero. Zero? Yeah, you're right. It's like you've done this before, John. Yeah, zero would be the most secure number. Not very functional though, right? Only having no, if you had no entrances and exits. So you're going to want to have at least one entrance and exit. But here's kind of interesting a point to make. Almost no buildings are only going to have a single entrance and exit. Why? Well, what do we prioritize above all else in physical security? Safety of people. And that's why even in really secure facilities, you'll have all sorts of fire doors around the perimeter of the building. Does that make the building less secure? Yes, but it's all about prioritizing the safety of people. If there's a fire, you want to make sure that people can get out as easily as possible. And so you'll have fire doors. It's required by fire laws usually, right? So this is a great example of how we prioritize the safety of people above all else.

ðŸŽµ ðŸŽµ We just completed talking about software-defined networks and how through this centralized software and intelligent application, we can basically control everything that's happening within the network. So if you think about those components that we just talked about, in order to allow things to happen across a network, you know we have what's called the control plane that makes the decisions right that's the decision maker and then of course the data plane is what forwards the actual packets across that decision that's been made by the control plane And because of software-defined networking and really virtualization, especially if you look at things like cloud environments, we've got really intelligent applications that control everything that's happening across a network. and across an architecture. You've got the control plane that really makes the decisions as to where, for example, packets and datagrams need to be able to go to. And then of course, it's the data plane that actually forwards those packets. Those components need to be connected and they need to talk to each other, and that's what APIs do. So if you look at the control plane as being the reference point here, because it's in the middle, then we can understand very plainly the difference between northbound APIs and southbound APIs. If you look at the control plane as the reference point, if you want to connect the control plane to a higher level component, which is obviously the application plane, you're going north. you're going up. And so that's what northbound APIs do. They connect the control plane to a higher level component called the application plane. Because you're going north, then it's called northbound APIs. And if you're connecting, for example, the control plane to a lower level component, which means you're going south, then that's what southbound APIs are. do they connect the control plane to a lower level component so the direction that you're going always using the control plane as a reference point is the difference between northbound apis and southbound apis Thank you.

One more important type of discretionary access control that we need to talk about is attribute-based access control, sometimes referred to as context-based access control. So watch out for that on the exam. It can use those two names interchangeably. attribute-based access control or context-based access control. So what is attribute or context-based access control all about? Let's first remind you of something really important we talked about, which is zero trust. This idea of trust nothing, verify everything. This is a really good Security principle that a lot of organizations are adopting, which is never assume that you have any sort of trusted network. Never assume that whoever is trying to connect to your application is a trusted individual. Trust, nothing, verify, everything. And this is especially true as organizations move more and more things into the cloud. And one of the defining characteristics of cloud is broad network access. You can access many cloud applications from anywhere in the world from any type of device. And so we're tending to lose a lot of our layers of defense. And people that run network administrators now, organizations that are securing their networks should never assume that they have a secure network, that they have a trusted network. You should always assume that anyone that's trying to connect your application is a potential baddie. And so that's where attribute or context-based access control comes in. Because the idea here is rather than just, you know, like we have multi-factor authentication, but let's, how can we go even beyond that? You know, multi-factor authentication is excellent. You should absolutely be using it. But we can do even better than that. So the idea here is rather than just looking to see if they have the right username, the right password, the right one-time password, we can also look at a bunch of attributes related to someone's system and what they want to do and who they are to make a much more informed decision as to whether or not it's appropriate for them to access the application and even whether the specific action that they're trying to perform is appropriate. And so you can kind of keep doing this. You can keep watching what they're doing. And for each thing that they're wanting to do, make sure it seems appropriate by looking at all these different attributes. So what are some of the different attributes that we can look at here? There's a ton of them. But let's start to go through some of them here. So the first thing, kind of major thing you could look at is you could look at some attributes related to the user. So what's this person's role in the organization? How many years of experience do they have? What department, what country do they work in? What time zone do they work in? All this is really useful information to look at, really useful attributes. Take time, for example. If this is an employee that normally works Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Pacific Standard Time or something like that, and now they're suddenly trying to log in at 3 a.m. on a Sunday, that looks suspicious. Or, you know, let's say that they work in the... I don't know, Zimbabwe practice. And now they're trying to log into systems in Switzerland or something. That could look suspicious. Or let's say they work in the accounting department and they're trying to log into engineering systems. That could look suspicious. So by looking at all these different attributes, we can start to get a much more rich picture of like, is this appropriate? So we can look at a bunch of attributes related to the user. We can look at a bunch of attributes related to their system. We can look at things like their operating system. If every single employee in the company has been issued a Windows laptop and it's running Actually, let's start with this, the manufacturer. If every employee's been issued a Dell laptop and someone's trying to log in from a Lenovo or even more sketchy from a Mac, that would look suspicious. Or, got John to laugh at that one. Or if anyone's, if everyone in the employee, like all the employees systems, like operating systems, have been upgraded to Windows 11 and someone's trying to log in from Windows XP or a Linux distro, that would look suspicious. You can look at IP address, MAC address. You could look at the certificates that they have installed on their system. And a lot of devices nowadays even will have like GPS receivers in them. So you could even look at the specific latitude and longitude of someone logging in to see if that looks suspicious or not. So there's a ton of attributes we can look at related to their environment. There's a bunch of attributes we can look at related to the resource that they want to access. You know, do they want to access a database or a SharePoint site or an individual file? And what's the classification of the resource they're trying to access? There's a very big difference between accessing the company's internal wiki site, where there's just the company policies and other public information like that are posted, versus someone trying to log into... some super secure database or something, or even a very different type of web server. So the point is, you can look at a lot of the attributes related to the resource that they want to access, And one more major thing you can look at, you can look at what action do they want to perform. There's a huge difference in a database, for instance, between just looking up the value of a particular field and deleting the entire database, right? So you might be totally fine with someone just looking up a value, and you probably are not fine with someone dropping a database table. So the point is, attribute-based access control or context-based access control, you're looking at all these different attributes and you're feeding them all into what's referred to as an authorization engine. This is a piece of software that is essentially making a risk-based decision to determine, by looking at all these different attributes, the system is determining, is this appropriate or not? And this is often risk-based, and I'll give you an example of that in a second. Now, the way that this authorization engine is going to make a decision is based on a policy document that you have to create, which contains a whole bunch of sort of logic statements. So this is essentially how we program the authorization engine so that it can make decisions to ultimately permit or deny certain actions. So that ultimately is the idea behind attribute-based access control. And this is a brilliant idea. And a lot of organizations now are adopting this. And it really does help improve security. Now, one thing we have to dig into a little more detail here is related to this policy document, there's a specific language that you need to know about. It's known as XACML, the Extensible Access Control Markup Language. This is the language that's used to essentially write this policy document. So you can use XACML to, it's a sort of a markup language that allows you to architect and create these policy documents. So just remember XACML, the language that we use to write these policy documents. Now, I have a fun little bit of trivia for you here. Maybe when you're watching this video, you can think about this for a moment. Do you think you've ever encountered an attribute-based access control system or a context-based access control system? Do you think you've ever seen one of these yet? And I would bet a lot of you might be like, well, you know, I'm not sure. But let me ask you this. Have you ever seen this? Gone to a website and there's this button there that you're supposed to click that says, I'm not a robot. This is Google's new reCAPTCHA system. This, by the way, replaced this old horrendous system where we had to type in these mangled text to prove that we weren't a robot, right? And we all hated having to decipher this horrendously mangled text. So the new system just asks you to click a checkbox to prove that you're not a robot. If you've ever wondered, How does clicking a checkbox prove that you're not a robot? The way this works is it's an attribute-based access control system. Google knows an enormous amount about each of us. They know what devices we tend to log in from, what operating system, what times of day. They know a huge amount about us. And one of the other really cool things they track is your mouse movement. When you go to click on that little box, it's tracking your mouse movement. So, you know, how would a robot click on that box? Well, a robot would go like right to the dead center and click on it. How would a robot click on a box? Yeah, we'd like drunkenly stagger up there with our mouse barely catch the edge of the box, right? That's how we click on stuff, right? So the point is, Google is looking at all these different attributes to make a decision as to whether or not you're a robot. And the kind of cool part about this is you can actually tell it's a risk-based system. Because sometimes, you may have noticed this, sometimes when you click on the checkbox, it just lets you right in. Other times, it'll show you a grid of 3x3 pictures. What's happening there is that if the system's not entirely sure you're not a robot, it essentially makes you go through a second step of verification. And it asks you to pick out certain images in that 3x3 grid. Pick out all the stop signs, pick out all the buses, whatever. So this is an example of an attribute-based access control system. And this is one other important type of discretionary access control that you need to remember for the exam.

You'll remember earlier we were talking about the different generations of programming languages that we've gone through. And really what we're using today is what's called object-oriented programming capabilities. And the word object simply means in this context a piece of code. And we talked about how, you know, today obviously we've evolved in better tool sets to be able to allow us not only to program really good code, but also involve security as part of that. In fact, when you look at you know, some of the capabilities that these entire tool sets have, a lot of these can be useful, very useful from a security perspective. And they include what you see here on this slide. So let me talk about these, like for example, inheritance. Inheritance is the ability of an object, a piece of code, to be able to inherit the characteristics of other objects, pieces of code, that you've previously developed. So the idea behind inheritance is that we can save on programming time because we don't have to keep reprogramming the same characteristics into further objects that we're just creating. We can simply allow those objects to inherit those characteristics automatically. So a really good example of this would be, let's say you've come up with a really good object, a piece of code that does, for example, I don't know, two-factor authentication. Instead of having to reprogram that over and over again into objects that require two-factor authentication, you can simply have those new objects inherit those characteristics. And so that's really good from the perspective of, again, doing programming and developing in a better way, in a more efficient way. What's really important though is to ensure that you're starting with really good characteristics for those objects that are being inherited. Because imagine if you have an object that has vulnerabilities or exploits. If you inherit those characteristics into further objects, then all of those objects are going to have the same exploits and vulnerabilities. So you need to start with something good and have those properties be inherited to further objects. Encapsulation, we've already talked about in the context of, for example, in domain number four, which is all about network security, we talked about encapsulation in the context of tunneling. And we define tunneling as, for example, taking an entire datagram and placing it inside of the data portion of another datagram, right? In other words, we've encapsulated something inside of something else. We can do the same thing here. with objects pieces of code we can take that piece of code that object and encapsulate it within something else either to protect it or to call it within that other object and so that can be useful as well and it's referred to as encapsulation of objects Polymorphism, this is the next one. If you take this word apart, poly means many, morphism means changes. So this is how objects can change based on what they see, for example, in their runtime environments. And in fact, you'll remember Rob talking about polymorphic malware, for example, when he covered this in the malware section. But and that means that, you know, you've got a piece of malware that tries to change its signature basically every time it infects something, right? It's the same concept here. We can have, you know, code or objects that recognizes certain things in its runtime environment and adapt. accordingly. It's almost like smart code that can adapt based on what it sees in its runtime environment. Again, based on us having given it that functionality, it's able to adapt. based on new requirements pretty cool stuff and here's the last one poly instantiation you may remember rob earlier in this course giving you a cliffhanger And I'm going to try and see if you remember where he talked about polyinstantiation. But he would have said something like, and John's going to tell you what polyinstantiation is later in this course. And I'll just give you a couple seconds here to try and think of where that was. All right, if you don't remember, I'll give you the answer to it. He would have been talking about specifically unauthorized inference when he talked about database environments. So this was back in domain number three, where we talked about inherent vulnerabilities that exist in certain architectures. And he would have talked about how one of the problems that we have, especially in database environments, is unauthorized inference. And you have to use the word unauthorized here because inference is actually good. Inference on its own is what we want. And if you think about it, this is why we collect information and store it in databases and update it on a regular basis because we want authorized individuals to be able to look at that information and infer more business decisions, better decisions for example. So inference is good from the perspective of our own organization and those that are authorized. What we don't want is unauthorized inference. Those that don't have a need to know should not be able to infer more sensitive and valuable information. So that's what polyinstantiation can be useful for to prevent unauthorized inference. And again, inference is the ability to deduce more sensitive, more valuable information than someone could. So here's the perfect definition of what polyinstantiation actually means. In other words, if you take the word itself apart, poly means many, instantiation means instances, right? So the definition that you see here allows us really different versions of the same information to exist at different classification levels for the purpose of preventing unauthorized inference. Now, you may be thinking about that and maybe it doesn't quite make sense until I give you this example. I've been using this example for years and people have taken it and used it as well, which I'm totally okay with because as long as it helps you understand and pass the exam. I'm totally fine with that. But here's an example that I've been using for years. And it's, I think, one that carries this concept across really well. But let's say you and I are working in a military environment. and we're maintaining a system. And one day the general comes to us and says, I need you to add a new military unit to our system and call it Fifth Infantry Division or something like that. So we go into the system and try and add a new military unit called 5th Infantry Division. And as soon as we try and do that, the system comes back and gives us an error message. The error message says you cannot add that new military unit called 5th Infantry Division. As soon as we see that error message, we've allowed unauthorized inference because automatically we can infer that there already exists a military unit in the system that's called 5th Infantry Division, right? But we can't see it because it's at a higher level of classification. But now we've allowed inference to happen. We know that there's a 5th Infantry Division that exists in the system. But now we know, but we shouldn't have known. And so if that system had been built using polyinstantiation capabilities, the system would recognize that we are trying to add a new military unit called fifth infantry division that already exists at a higher level of classification than you and i are cleared for so the system would handle it on its own it would say okay whenever John uses 5th Infantry Division at his classification level, I'm going to map it to something else that the system creates on its own. So the system's going to handle it. Every time we or I use 5th Infantry Division at my classification level, the system is going to create some totally different variable or name and map it to whatever, whenever we say for example 5th Infantry Division at our level. In other words, the system's going to handle it on its own without returning a error message that will allow unauthorized inference, right? That's what poly-instantiation allows. And the whole purpose of it is to prevent unauthorized inference by allowing different versions of the same information to exist at different classification levels. Now, before we finish off this section here, we need to talk about something that is mentioned in the exam outline, but also people have been seeing questions on very recently, and that is something called software or code obfuscation. And if you think about that word obfuscation, it means to hide. So the whole purpose of this is to hide certain specific things about code so that you make it very difficult for people to figure out what your code is doing. So think about how a developer develops some code that does something, but they want to be able to protect that code so that it's not stolen or figured out by others. So the idea behind software or code obfuscation is to make it very difficult for someone that's looking at that code to figure out what it does obviously to protect intellectual property and and copyright and all of those things that you'd be interested in protecting if you were developing code all right so the idea behind this is to make it very difficult to reverse engineer and figure out what that code is actually doing. You're concealing or making it very difficult to understand the logic and the purpose of that code that you've produced. Now there's different versions of software or code obfuscation that you see here. There's lexical code obfuscation, there's data code obfuscation, there's also control flow code obfuscation. We've given you the exact definitions here, but the only one that you will see on the exam is the first one, lexical code obfuscation. This is where you're changing the look of the code to again try and make it very difficult for someone to backwards engineer and figure out what your code is doing so in other words you're removing comments You're changing meaningful variable names to non-meaningful ones. You're removing bugging information or, you know, all kinds of things that, again, might give clues as to what that code is doing. So you're removing things that are focused on the look of the code itself so that you're making it very difficult to backwards engineer. That's the whole purpose. of lexical code obfuscation. And just to finish off this section, and this is why, you know, obviously we've been focusing on how security needs to be involved in the entire lifecycle of applications, not just the software development lifecycle. Once we put applications into production, there are a number of things that security needs to be able to support. And we've talked about that many times, but we help in, for example, testing and change management and other things that security needs to be involved in. But the other thing that we need to certainly enforce from a security perspective is that those that are working with development activities, we need to physically and logically divide the environments that they may have access to. This is a form of separation of duties, and I think companies do this pretty well. But for example, If you've got developers that have access to the development environment, they should not have access to the production environment. And they shouldn't have access to the quality assurance environment or the test environment. So through the implementation of security controls. we need to ensure that developers only have access to what they should, based on, again, need to know. Especially dividing your test environment and development environments from production becomes pretty important. and that is something that we always need to enforce. I think companies do this pretty well.

Hi, welcome back for another video. This one deals with how a competent security professional needs to be able to, first of all, understand and secondly, apply security concepts. Now, I think all of us will feel familiar with the contents of this one. You know, traditionally, we've referred to security as being three things, confidentiality, integrity and availability. In fact, for the longest time, people have referred to those as the CIA triad, although nobody understands what that means and we shouldn't refer it. using those three letters. But the way the industry has evolved and what security has to focus on these days, first of all, includes those three focuses of security, but much more as well. In fact, over the last number of years, there are some really important focuses of organizations to not only protect all valuable assets of the organization from obviously the perspective of confidentiality, integrity, and availability, but also two others. Those two others are authenticity and non-repudiation. So we can say, and the CISSP and certainly the industry, because everything that the CISSP does is really focused on what the industry does. Today, security has to focus on five important security services. And that's what this section deals with. And like I said, I think we're all familiar with these, but let's explain them from the perspective of, what the common body of knowledge of security really requires and therefore the exam outline and exam requires as well. So typically these have been referred to as the three of security, for example, but there are now five that you can see here, right? So when we focus on security services, As competent security professionals, we need to understand that we're focusing on confidentiality, integrity, availability, but also authenticity and non-repudiation. And one important thing to highlight here is that even though we're using information, As an example, it's important for us as competent security professionals to understand that we are talking not only about protecting information. Information is only one example. of valuable assets that we need to protect using, for example, these services in any organization. Now, the reason why we're using information as an example of a valuable asset is because that is specifically mentioned in the exam outline. But as competent security professionals, one of the things that we need to always focus on and highlight is that security needs to protect all valuable assets of the organization from these perspectives, including information, but anything that represents value to an organization. is considered to be a asset. In fact, the word asset is defined as anything that represents value to an organization. And as we've said, and we will say many times, an asset is always protected based on its value. That's what dictates, for example, the level of security that we need to apply to valuable assets. We need to understand the value of that asset so that we can protect that value. Now, obviously, security is not in the best position to understand the value of an asset. The owners are. And that's why this concept of ownership in security is very important. In fact, of paramount importance to the success of security. And that message needs to come from security. Hopefully, the executive and the CEO, for example, that this concept of ownership is really the basis for how we protect all assets of the organization through security and these security services that you see here. So these can be referred to as the five pillars. of security used to protect and address the value of really valuable assets within the organization itself. Now let's look at each of these in more detail. Although again, I think most of us are pretty familiar with the focus of each one of these. But for example, confidentiality. That really means that if we are protecting an asset from a confidentiality perspective, we're trying to make sure that it's protected from the perspective of only allowing access to those that have been authorized to be able to access that asset. And again, we are using information as an example because that's what the exam may use. But we need to focus, for example, on all valuable assets. I want to emphasize that important perspective that we need to have even though we're using information here as a really good example of a valuable asset that obviously would need to be protected from a confidentiality perspective. So confidentiality controls, are trying to prevent the unauthorized access to those assets so that only authorized individuals and subjects that have a need to know can access that asset based on, again, need to know and least privilege concepts that we're going to talk about later on. Now, unfortunately, from a security perspective, we haven't really done a good job of explaining what security really means. and most people when you mention the word security they often think of confidentiality controls but it's important to highlight that today especially today in our environments it may be very important and in fact just as important to protect assets including information from these other pillars or perspectives that you see here. The next one, of course, is what we call integrity. And again, this is one that we haven't really done a good job of explaining to our stakeholders. But when we apply integrity controls, The best way to explain this and to understand it is to reflect on words like accuracy and meaningfulness and timeliness and currency, for example. In fact, again, if we're using information as an example, when we apply integrity controls to that information, We're trying to make that information more accurate, more timely, more current, more meaningful, because if we can make information or any other asset, more accurate, more timely, more current, more meaningful, it will drive better business decisions. So that's the whole focus of integrity controls. We're trying to make assets more accurate, for example. The next one, which I think all of us are also very familiar with, is availability. And this is probably the one that is easiest to understand from a stakeholder perspective. We're simply trying to make sure that valuable assets are always available to us whenever we need them. or whenever they're needed by our stakeholders that are authorized to access, for example, those assets. And so typically the way that we best address availability needs is through redundancy. Cost-effective redundancy controls is what we typically apply to valuable assets to make sure that they are always available whenever they're needed. Now here are the two that the industry has really added and it's important again for us to reflect on these as being just as important as your traditional CIA triad that you see here. But the next one is what we call authenticity. Now, pay attention here because this does not say authentication. So don't confuse it with authentication that is usually used in the context of access control, like how we authenticate individuals or subjects to be able to access something. That is not what this is focused on. In fact, if you look at the definition here, We've highlighted something called proof of origin. Authenticity really means that we can prove the source of something. We know where it came from. we can authenticate the source of where something originated or came from. And that may be a very important security service to address in today's environments. And here's a practical example of where this might come in. Let's say that in our organization, we want to patch our Microsoft operating systems. We want to make sure that that patch is actually coming from Microsoft and not some other organization or website pretending to be Microsoft. And so we may need to prove the authenticity of that patch. In other words, we may need to prove the origin of that patch, that it actually originated and came from Microsoft and not something else pretending to be Microsoft. Now, this service, authenticity, also referred to as proof of origin in the industry, is best addressed in the cryptography section that we're going to cover, obviously, when we cover domain number three. Because the best way to prove authenticity or proof of origin is to use cryptography services. So we will describe this in detail when we cover cryptography in this course. pillar of security or important focus of security is what we refer to as or what the industry refers to as non-repudiation. The key word here is the word repudiation. And if you look that up in a dictionary, it actually means to be able to deny. That's what to repudiate means. Repudiation means to be able to deny. So the important security focus here is non-repudiation, which means the inability to deny. So if a subject or someone initiates or starts something, they cannot say later that it wasn't them that actually did that. That's the whole focus of non-repudiation. We want to be able to prevent subjects or applications or services from being able to deny that they actually did something or initiated something or started something. Now this, just like authenticity, is best addressed when we talk about cryptography because the best way to address non-repudiation is to use digital signatures. and digital signatures are a direct service that is provided through cryptography. So when we get to the cryptography section, we're going to explain both authenticity and non-repudiation in detail. Now what you need to remember about non-repudiation is that there are two flavors of it. There is non-repudiation of origin and non-repudiation of delivery. And it's important for us to remember the correct terminology. And again, Anything that is addressed on the CISSP exam comes from the actual security industry, if you will. It's not ISC2 that comes up with terminology that you need to understand as far as the exam is concerned. It all comes from the industry and that creates something called the common body of knowledge of security, which in turn is what's addressed through the exam and the exam outline, for example, and the exam questions. All right, so the correct terminology as it relates to non-repudiation. is that there are two flavors, non-repudiation of origin, which basically means that the sender or the initiator of something cannot say that it wasn't them that sent something or initiated something later. will be able to prove it that it was them through digital signatures. That's non-repudiation of origin. Non-repudiation of delivery is the other flavor. And that basically means that, for example, a receiver of a message or someone that has received something cannot say later that it wasn't them that actually received that particular message or data. We can prove it, again, through data. digital signatures. And again, we'll talk about this in detail when we get to cryptography. All right. So these are referred to as the five pillars of not just information security. We're using information as an example of a valuable asset. These are important focuses that we as competent security professionals need to be able to address based on the requirements and the value of all assets that represent value to the organization itself. Now, one of the things that I know is important to reflect on is that we use words like security and protect and so on. And in fact, that's what the organization wants us to do, to be able to protect valuable assets by really applying any one or more or all of these pillars or services that you see here. But if you think about this from a stakeholder or management or CEO perspective, What those stakeholders really want security to be able to do is to add value, not just protect the value of assets to the level that it exists at. Security should be able to actually add value, not just protect it. And so that's a really important focus. In other words, if you're explaining these services to a stakeholder management, a CEO, for example, they'll want to know how by applying these types of security controls, we can actually add value to assets. And so I'm going to give you an example here that may be important to remember. And I'm going to use integrity as an example. How do we actually add value to an asset by applying integrity controls, right? We're not just protecting it. We're actually increasing the value of that asset by applying integrity controls to it. And if you remember just a few minutes ago, how I explained what integrity controls really mean, it means we're trying to address the accuracy, the timeliness, the currency, the meaningfulness of a particular asset. And yes, let's use information as an example. Let's say we have a database that contains obviously very important information that is being used to drive business decisions. If you explain to a CEO, for example, that because of our integrity controls that we apply to that database, it makes information more accurate, more timely, more current, more meaningful. That's what our integrity controls are doing. They're making sure that the information that's contained within that database is always more accurate, more timely. It reflects current trends and analyses and information that we reflect on. In other words, by applying those security controls that are focused on integrity, we're making that information more accurate. more timely, more current. Because if that information is used to drive decisions, because it is more accurate, more timely, more current, it will drive better business decisions. Again, the best way to think of this is if you think about accuracy. If you can make an asset more accurate, it becomes more valuable to an organization. We've increased its value to the point where, for example, it's driving better business decisions. It's making the organization itself more accurate, more timely, more valuable. This entire domain is focused on how security supports the organization and its initiatives. In other words, the business itself. Typically, it's what we call business continuity management. That is the accountability of any CEO and board of directors, for example, right? Because the CEO is obviously there to ensure that the organization achieves its goals and objectives, increases its value. But part of that means that it has to be able to survive and withstand any negative eventuality that we would refer as being a disaster. That is the accountability of a CEO. All of these different sections that we're going through are focused on how security supports a lot of these activities and so on. And that includes business continuity management. In fact, if you think about the words business continuity management, It's how we manage business continuity in the event of a disaster. And so that means that whatever plan, which is usually referred to as business continuity plans and disaster recovery plans, better have been written and created with security in mind. If an organization is going through a disaster, the requirements to protect its assets become even more important than they were before the disaster. And of course, it becomes really important for security to focus not only on protecting valuable assets as the organization is dealing with whatever disaster it is experiencing, But it's also important to protect people and relationships and anything that obviously may be at risk as a result of the disaster itself. And what's important to remember here is that we're not just focused on natural disasters. We can constitute a disaster as being anything that has the opportunity of putting the entire organization at risk. And so it's especially important during the disaster to protect all assets based on value which is what security is focused on to begin with. But it's also important to ensure that security has been involved so that the organization can do other things while it's dealing with the disaster itself and a perfect example of that is compliance. complying to laws and regulations and privacy requirements becomes even more important in the event of a disaster. I mean, an organization cannot use an excuse like, well, we're experiencing a disaster and therefore we can't meet the requirements of these privacy laws or data protection laws or maybe other requirements that it has related to compliance. In fact, it may be even more important in the event of a disaster. to show and prove compliance to very tough laws and regulations and protect things obviously related to corporate reputation and the relationships and the image that the organization has worked really hard to instill with its clients and customers and other stakeholders. And so this section is a perfect example of where a lot of material overlaps in the different domains. So this is a way to walk through your entire organization and understand what is truly critical and essential, but also, and pay attention to this, how critical and essential each of those things are, like for example, products, services, applications, systems, network segments. how critical and essential each of those things actually are based on time parameters. Let's now talk about some risks associated with the acquisition of products and services. So when you're buying, if you're a company and you're buying new servers, new software, things like that, What are some of the risks that you have to think about? And then in a moment here, we'll talk about how you can mitigate those risks. So a few specific risks that you need to know about. First is product tampering. Product tampering is where a manufacturer makes some product. And then the baddie, the attacker, somehow gets their hands on that product and tampers with it, messes with it in some ways, alters or modifies the product in such a way that it's probably not going to be great for you, the consumer. They might install... Perfect example is they might install like a key logger inside of a keyboard. Or they might implant some chip in a firewall that allows them to get remote root access to that firewall once it's installed in the organization. So there's all sorts of, and those are pretty sophisticated attacks, there's simpler ones as well, but product tampering is where an attacker gets their hands on a correctly manufactured product and then they tamper with it in some way, they mess with it in some way. which is different from counterfeits. Counterfeits are where, it's not like this was a valid product that was then messed with. Counterfeits is where basically an invalid product is made. It's where the attacker makes a replica or an imitation of a genuine product. Usually to make it more cheaply, so it might be made with hazardous materials, it might not work properly, it might not last as long as it's supposed to. So there's a bunch of issues with cheap counterfeits, because they're typically not manufactured as well as the real thing. And that, in certain circumstances, can have pretty dire consequences. And then the final risk here is implants. And implants is actually a subset of product tampering. So what implants specifically means is the attacker is implanting something into a product to perform an unauthorized activity. And the example I gave of putting a key logger, of the attacker installing a key logger in a keyboard, that's the perfect example of an implant. So just remember that product tampering is a broader term where the attacker kind of messes with the product in various different ways. And an implant is a subset of that. An implant is specifically where the attacker is inserting some sort of unauthorized something into a product, like a keylogger into a keyboard. what are some ways that we can mitigate these risks? I mean, generally high level, we can use some of the techniques that we'll talk about throughout this course. Things like doing third party assist assessments, um, looking at service level, um, requirements, things like that. You know, making sure you have good processes in place to make sure you're acquiring proper products, that you trust your vendors, things like that. But some specific things that you need to know about, some specific risk mitigations. First one we'll talk about here is the silicone root of trust. And this is basically the idea that you can essentially build a unique identifier into a piece of hardware at the hardware level. So it's like literally built into the hardware is a unique identifier for, say, every microchip that comes off the manufacturing line. And then you can use this unique identifier to obviously uniquely identify the hardware. And there's no way to fake it. And one of the things that you can then do with the silicone root of trust is kind of one of the techniques you can use for it. you can build up what's often referred to as a secure boot process. So the hardware, for instance, will check to make sure that the firmware that's about to run is the valid firmware. And by the way, this often relies on something we're going to talk about in the cryptography section, which is digital signatures. Digital signatures allow you to verify the authenticity and the integrity of some software, of some code, some data. So what you can do with this sort of silicone root of trust is the hardware, before the hardware, like when you first power on a system, before the hardware will allow the firmware to run, the hardware will first check to say, hey, Is this actually the valid firmware? And by the way, we'll talk about what firmware is later on as well. But it'll check using the digital signature to say, hey, is this valid hardware? Authenticity means, you know, who something came from. It's proof of origin. So you could make sure that this firmware actually came from the right manufacturer. And integrity means you can make sure that the... code hasn't been modified in any way. So you can basically do this check before the firmware was allowed to run. And then before the firmware loads some software, the firmware will do the same thing. The firmware will check to make sure the software that's about to run has a valid digital signature, checking the authenticity and the integrity of, say, the operating system. And then once the operating system is loaded, it will do a check on any applications that want to run to make sure that those applications have valid digital signatures, authenticity, and integrity. And this is often, like I said, referred to as a secure boot process, where each step of the boot process is checking the next piece of software that's loading to say, hey, Is this valid? And you can build that upon the silicon root of trust. You can build very secure systems this way that will only ever run or hopefully only ever run valid software. Physically unclonable function. What this is about is that even when a manufacturer intends to manufacture completely identical, say, microchips, there are still going to be subtle variations between them at the atomic level. And you can use these subtle variations to... to help you generate unique encryption keys, say, that are just associated with that particular bit of hardware. So this is what's known as a physically unclonable function. Each device, just based on the slight variations in how it's manufactured, can be a way of uniquely identifying it. And there's no way to fake that. That's why it's known as a physically unclonable function. And then the final idea here of limiting the risks associated with what you're acquiring is having a software bill of materials. Any application that you acquire nowadays has been built with functions, libraries from different sources. and so you can basically do an inventory of that where you're basically checking to make sure that you've understood what all the libraries are that are used who they've come from and and make sure that they're valid and so you could do that if you're acquiring some piece of software you could look to see okay well you know vendor wrote this part but they use this library and they use these functions who wrote those supply chain management this is basically like secured supply supply chain management understanding where your software came from and do you trust where it came from and how do you trust where it came from if you know like open source libraries are being used how do you make sure that those are valid and so forth so that's kind of the idea of a software bill of materials it's a comprehensive list of components libraries modules that are used in a piece of software and you understand where they all came from and you're confident in or at least you've understood the risks associated with each components and you've got controls in place to mitigate those risks should they ever occur. So that's kind of the idea of the software bill of materials. It's basically supply chain management.

Source: network-intercept-vtt

Let's talk about scoping and tailoring as it relates to security. Now, when you look at these two terms, they can be used in all kinds of different contexts, but we need to relate it to security here, obviously. And here are pretty good definitions. of scoping and tailoring and how they differ from each other. So if you think about scoping itself, what's in scope? For example, if you're doing an audit, you can't audit everything. So you're going to think about what you're going to include as part of your audit. That would be what we refer to as scoping, right? You're selecting only those controls for example, that apply to what you're looking at. You're not going to look at everything. You're just going to look at those things that really apply. You're scoping your project. You're scoping whether you're doing a vulnerability assessment. Which controls are you going to look at and include as part of your vulnerability assessment? That's what we call scoping. Tailoring, on the other hand, right? You're modifying, for example, based on what your needs are. right so modifying the baseline to become more applicable to align with goals and objectives and you know a perfect way to think of tailoring if you go to a tailor for example right they're not going to throw away your pants because they don't fit they're going to tailor your pants so that they do fit right so they modify things based on what your requirements are and that's the best way to think of what scoping and tailoring is so from a security perspective scoping means okay which security controls are we going to be looking at, for example, and tailoring, modifying your baseline so that you become more applicable to whatever your needs and your project and your requirements and your objectives are. So this is referred to in the industry as managing the data lifecycle. These are some of the issues and the elements that we need to remember as data is going through those phases of its life cycle from the create phase all the way to the destruction phase. So what you're going to see here are elements that are obviously very important to keep in mind as data is going through its life cycle. We start with data collection. And so this is something that obviously organizations have to take very seriously and be able to dictate and meet the requirements of in deciding, for example, the precise location of that data and where it is and obviously there are technological advancements and issues that may impact this like for example cloud you know having the ability to know exactly where your data is is something that is very important at any given point in time so these are all issues related to data location The next thing that we're going to talk about here is data maintenance.
