We're now gonna go through a number of different tools, technologies that can be used to help protect data. Let's start with DRM, digital rights management. At the most basic level, what DRM is, is you are encrypting some data. And then by encrypting that data, it allows you to then control who can do what with that data. Who can decrypt it and look at it? Who can modify the data? Who can copy it? Who could print it? Who could run it on? So where you may have come across DRM technologies is old DVDs. If you remember DVDs, right? We used to watch movies off of laser discs. They're off of little like disc things. DVDs were often region restricted. So if you bought a DVD from Japan, it wouldn't work on your DVD player in Canada or the US. This is DRM technology. It's encrypting data. And then because that data is encrypted, you can control how that data is used. And you can apply this to an individual file, to an entire DVD, et cetera. And you can apply it. Another perfect use case of this is if you download music from the Apple Music Store onto your iphone there's drm in place there that or there has been in the past to make sure that you can't then copy that music file onto another device so like i said the most basic level drm is the idea of encrypting data And then because you've encrypted the data, you can now control how that data is used, who can use it for what purposes, who can edit the data, who can print the data, play it in certain places, copy it, etc. So that's the whole idea of digital rights management, encrypting data, which allows you to control who can do what with the data. IRM, information rights management, is a subset of DRM. And IRM tools are typically used in organizations to control documents. That's why it's referred to as information rights management. So a perfect example of this is PDF documents. You can control who can do what. Like if you have Adobe, the full version of Adobe Acrobat, you can actually put a bunch of controls in place for a PDF document. You can make it so no one can edit it. You can make it so no one can print it. You can make it so no one can... you know, copy, like make some, I don't know, I've already given you examples. You can put a bunch of controls in place. I ran out of examples there. You can put a bunch of controls in place on a PDF. This is a perfect example of information rights management. So just IRM is a substantive DRM typically used in organizations for documents like PDFs. DLP, Data Loss Prevention Tools. Fundamentally, what DLP systems are all about is protecting sensitive data. And let's spend just a second on here. This is important. DLP data loss prevention tools are about preventing the unauthorized usage, transfer, copying, et cetera, of sensitive data. And the key word here being sensitive. Because DLP tools can't prevent every document from being emailed, or every document from being saved, or every bit of data from being copied from here to there, right? If you just prevented everything from moving across the network from being stored, you know, you wouldn't be able to do anything anymore. To have any sort of effective DLP system, the first thing you need to do is you need to know what is your sensitive data. which implies you need data classification. You need to know what is your sensitive data. And the way you know that is you've classified the data. So this is an extremely important requirement of having an effective DLP solution. You first need to know what is your sensitive data. Is it credit card numbers? Is it social insurance numbers? Is it some sort of intellectual property? Is it certain images or graphics that are being produced by an advertising company? You need to know what your sensitive data is so that you can then have DLP tools that will control what is done with that sensitive data. DLP tools typically provide kind of three major capabilities. The first is they will help with classification, with helping you identify what is or isn't sensitive data. So, for example, it's typically very easy to recognize a credit card number because it's usually 16 digits. There's a check digit there. So it's very easy to look at a 16 digit number and say, hey, is this a valid credit card number? And so your DLP system can essentially help you classify what is a credit card number just by knowing what the structure of a credit card number is or a social insurance number. So DLP systems can be used to help you identify what is your sensitive data. They can then be used to control what is done with that data. Is it allowed to be, like let's say on an endpoint device, is someone allowed to email certain sensitive data? Are they allowed to copy data out of a database into an Excel sheet? Are they allowed to transfer certain data onto a USB stick or store it on a hard drive somewhere? So this is often referred to as data in use. So DLP systems can control how data is used. And typically, the way the DLP systems will control the data in use is through endpoint devices by having a monitoring agent on an endpoint device, like a laptop or something like that. Another major use case for DLP systems is data in motion. So this is data that's moving across the network. So for the DLP system to be able to look at what data is transiting the network, you're going to have to have DLP sensors spread across your network segments. So the DLP agent can then see what data is being sent from system A to system B or egressing out of the network. So this is another sort of major category of DLP systems where they'll actually be looking at data in motion. And then the final major category of DLP systems is data at rest. So this is data that's being stored in a file server, in a database somewhere, et cetera. There are DLP systems that can look at data in rest. DLP agents, high level, right? They're helping you identify what is sensitive data. They're looking to see if there's unauthorized usage of that sensitive data. And if there is, they can perform some sort of enforcement action, like prevent the data from being emailed, prevent the data from being copied to a USB stack, prevent the data from, stop the data from leaving the network. et cetera, so they can perform enforcement actions. And then they can do this across these sort of three major use cases of data, data in use on an endpoint device, data in motion across the network and data at rest on a hard drive or storage server or database somewhere. So that's super high level. That's DLP technologies and how they can be used to help protect sensitive data in an organization. And then the final one I'll mention here, protection tools, is CASBs, Cloud Access Service Brokers. I'll talk about the details in a second, but let's just first talk high-level, like what are CASBs? So of course, you know what the cloud is. There's all these cloud services, software as a service, things like that. So let's say a company has decided to use Box as a data storage service. So you've got now two entities, right? You've got the company and their employees here, and then you've got Box over here, which is the cloud service provider. Where a CASB would sit is in between the two. So the CASB is almost like, if you're familiar with proxies, a CASB is essentially a proxy. A proxy means that all the traffic going between, say, the customer and the cloud service provider has to pass through the proxy through the cloud service broker. Cloud Access Service Broker. So the CASB essentially acts as a system in the middle, as a proxy, such that all the communications between the consumer and the cloud service provider back and forth pass through the CASB. Why? So the CASB can make decisions. So for example, a perfect example of this is a CASB solution could have, say, a DLP capability in it. So if a customer, sorry, if an employee went to store some file full of credit card numbers in it, the CASB solution could look at that file, say, oh, like there's a bunch of sensitive data in this. And they just wouldn't the CASB would not allow that data to pass on to the cloud service provider to box that should not have a copy of that data. So because the CASB is acting as an intermediary between the consumer and the cloud service provider, it allows the CASB to make decisions as to what it's going to permit or deny. And so this is why when you see statements here like policy enforcement, the CASB can make sure that, you know, what data is being sent to the cloud service broker is appropriate. Or another really important thing CASBs will do is they'll be used to try and eliminate shadow IT. Shadow IT is where consumers go in, like employees at a company, go and use cloud services that they're not authorized to access, like Google Drive or Box or something. If there's a CASB sitting between employees and all of the cloud services, the CASB can control which cloud services the employee is allowed to access. And if they try to go and access something they're not supposed to, the CASB can block that. And so obviously your policies would say what is or is not permitted, and the CASB would be making decisions then as to what it's gonna allow through or not. And these CASBs can have all sorts of different threat protection capabilities built into them, things like DRM, or sorry, like DLP and stuff like that. So that's the idea of CASBs. Basically, just think of them as an intermediary between the consumer and the cloud service providers. And the CASB is making decisions as to what it's going to prevent or deny to help you enforce your security controls.

Okay, so what we're gonna do now is we're gonna start to go through some of the various components within a computer system. Now I wanna preface this. You absolutely do not need to be an expert on these components on things like the cpu the central processing unit or ram random access memory or hard drives or north bridges or south bridges or gpus or any of this stuff you absolutely do not need to be an expert on any of this specific hardware remember right this is a management level certification But I'm still gonna cover this stuff, why? Well, because you might get questions that will reference these things. The example I always love to use is, you might get a question in, say, digital forensics, as to why primary storage is particularly challenging from a digital forensics perspective. So the question is not specifically about primary storage. The question is about digital forensics, but you need to know what is primary storage to be able to answer that question. And we'll talk about, by the way, we'll talk about what primary storage is in just a minute here in the next video. So the point is. you need to know at a high level what some of these components are within your computer system and what they do. Not because you'll get direct questions on the components themselves, but you might get questions that reference these things, that reference a CPU or reference RAM. So you need to know at a high level, like what do these things do within the computer system? and of course i'm going to sprinkle in some security aspects of this as well that will be important to remember okay so let's start here let's start with processors cpus central processing units put overly simply cpus are essentially the brain of your computer system what cpus are doing incredibly quickly, blindingly quickly, is they're fetching instructions, decoding those instructions, executing them, and then storing the result. And they're doing this Billions of millions, maybe billions of times per second. Modern day CPUs are incredibly fast. And of course, what are they fetching? Code. They're decoding it, executing it, and then storing the results. And so this is why you can essentially think of the CPU as the brain of your computer system. The CPU is what's running the code, which is, of course, what makes the programs, what makes your computer system run. And I'll just highlight one more time, CPUs are doing this blindingly quickly. It'll be important to understand why in a second. Okay, now let's talk about CPUs from a security perspective. We're going to talk about what are known as the different processor states. What this is about is that in CPUs, there's essentially two different privilege levels. A higher privilege level where Any code that's running in that or any process that's running in that higher privilege level would essentially have access to all the CPUs capabilities. And then we have a lower privilege level where any process running in that lower privilege level would only have access to a limited set, not all the CPUs capabilities, just the more commonly used ones. So we have these two different privilege levels, supervisory state and problem state. And if you just had to guess based on the names, which do you think is the higher privilege level, supervisory state or problem state? Yeah, obviously supervisory state. And that's exactly right. Supervisory state is the higher privilege level. So any process that was running in supervisory state would have access to all the CPUs capabilities. And modern day CPUs can do all sorts of stuff. You wouldn't want... just any program or any process running on the system to be able to access. So that's why we have this higher privilege level that's reserved. The lower privilege level is known as problem state. Now you might be wondering, why is it called problem state? Like is the CPU having some sort of issue? No. I mean, if you think about what's the job of CPUs, CPUs are meant to solve problems. So problem state is actually the normal operating mode of the CPU. And the vast majority of processes running on your computer system, so like right now, whatever web browser you're watching this video in, Maybe you have Zoom open or Outlook or PowerPoint or whatever. Any of these processes would typically be running in problem state. So problem state is the lower privileges level that most processes, most applications will operate in. It's only a few very select processes that get to run in supervisory state. We'll get to that. OK. Now, one of the really cool things that our modern day computer systems can do is multitasking. We can have multiple processes running simultaneously, which is obviously super helpful from a productivity perspective. but it does introduce some security challenges. If we've got multiple processes running simultaneously, how do we make sure that we keep those processes separated so they can't negatively impact each other? like let's say process a here has a memory leak and eventually you know or there's some bug in the code and so process a crashes ideally right if one process on your system crashes you don't want that taking down the entire system so this is one reason that we want to keep processes separated so if one of them is having an issue it doesn't take out everyone else with it or maybe process A here ends up being a nefarious baddie process. You wouldn't want this malware, this process here to be able to get into and steal data from a bunch of other processes and things like that. simple point here is from a security perspective, it's really important to enforce process isolation, to keep these different processes separated, isolated from each other so they can't negatively impact each other. How do we achieve this? There's two major ways that we can achieve process isolation that you need to remember. Memory segmentation and time division multiplexing. So remember that. The two major ways of achieving process isolation are memory segmentation and time division multiplexing. So let's go through these and explain them. Here's what time division multiplexing is. Imagine there's a few processes running on your computer and those processes all want to be able to send and receive data. So let's say you're on a conference call on Zoom and you've also got your email program Outlook open. These two different processes, Zoom and Outlook, are both going to want to be able to send and receive data across the network. So they're both going to want to have access to the NIC, the Network Interface Card. Now, what we would not want is both Zoom and Outlook accessing the NIC at exactly the same time and conflicting with each other. So one way that we can keep these processes isolated is by implementing what's called time division, multiplexing. And the basic idea here is let's say we're talking about access to the NIC, the network interface card. And let's say we're looking at time going forward here. What we would do is we would slice up, we would slice the time into little slices here. Time division. So we're dividing up the time. And let's say each little bit of time here is, I don't know, one millisecond or something like that. That's not truly what it would be, but just so you understand this high level. We would divide up access to the network interface card into little one millisecond slices. And the whole idea here is that During one of these slices of time, it's going to be, say, Zoom's turn. So during this little slice of time here, Zoom will get complete access to the network interface card to send and receive whatever data it wants. And very importantly, when it's Zoom turn, nothing else has access to the network interface card. Outlook doesn't have access, whatever processes that might also want to be accessed to the internet or the network. None of them have access. It's just Zoom's turn. But once Zoom's turn is up, then it'll become maybe Outlook's turn. And so when it's Outlook's turn, Outlook can send and receive whatever data it wants, and none of the other processes have access to the NIC. And then, of course, you just keep switching back and forth, time, division, multiplexing. What you're doing is you're just switching back and forth between different processes. So maybe we go back to Zoom, and then we give it Outlook, and then maybe we switch to WebEx or whatever. So you're just switching back and forth, or your computer system is just switching back and forth. And of course, it's doing this so quickly that it looks like all the different processes all have access to the network interface card at the same time. But they don't. Each process only has access at one time. And this is a way, of course, of achieving process isolation, because no two processes are accessing the network interface card at the same time. So this is time division multiplexing, one way of achieving process isolation. Let's now talk about the other way of achieving process isolation, which is, as you can hear here, memory segmentation. Let's first start with what is memory? Random access memory. So as I said, and kind of highlighted a couple of minutes ago, modern day CPUs are blindingly fast, right? They can be performing billions of calculations per second. And of course, what's the CPU constantly doing? Fetching instructions, decoding execute, and storing the results. So the CPU constantly needs to be fetching data and storing data. Now, the major place that we store data on the computer system is on the hard drive. And even modern day, much faster, solid state hard drives, they're way faster than older hard drives. But compared to the CPU, even modern SSD drives are still glacially slow compared to the CPU. And this creates a big problem because the CPU had to constantly, every time the CPU needed some more data, if the CPU had to send that request directly to the hard drive and then wait for the hard drive to respond, it would slow your system to an absolute crawl. Because the CPU would say, OK, I need this data. And it would send that request to the hard drive. And the hard drive would glacially slowly go to look for the data, glacially slowly retrieve the data, glacially slowly send it back to the CPU. And the whole time the CPU would be sitting there going like, come on, come on, come on, come on. I'm waiting, I'm waiting, I'm waiting. Let's go. So it would really slow your system down if the CPU had to talk directly to the hard drive. So to massively speed up our systems, we have some intermediary storage space that we call RAM, Random Access Memory. RAM is much faster than the hard drive. And so the whole idea here, what happens is every time that you load up a new program, what's actually happening is instruction code and data is being copied off of the relatively slow hard drive, and it's being copied into the relatively much faster RAM. And then once all that instruction code and data has been loaded into RAM, the program will start running. And now if the CPU needs access to some data, to some instruction code, the CPU doesn't have to ask the really slow hard drive. The CPU can ask the much faster RAM because that data has been temporarily stored in RAM. So that's kind of the whole idea of RAM. It's this like temporary storage location for data and instruction code for any running processes. And here's where the process isolation thing comes in. Any process that's running in your computer system will have some instruction code and data loaded into RAM. So if you have Zoom running, there's going to be a little bit of memory space in RAM that's being allocated to Zoom. And if you have Outlook running, there's going to be a little bit of memory space allocated to Outlook. And of course, you have an operating system running. There's going to be a bunch of memory allocated to the operating system. And now here's where the process isolation part comes in. Obviously, if Outlook is accessing RAM, what should Outlook be able to access? Only its own bit of memory space and not another process's memory space or the operating system's memory space. So the whole idea here is Any program that's running on your computer system and any process that's running on your computer system is going to have some space allocated for it in RAM. And each process should only have access to its own memory space and not another process's memory space. That's memory segmentation, and it's a way of achieving process isolation. So one final time, right? The two major ways that we can achieve process isolation, memory segmentation and time division multiplexing. OK. Let's now talk about secure memory management, which is going to sound deeply familiar to one of the things that we just talked about. So let's say we have process A running here. And you can see here is memory. Here's our RAM. You can see we've got some memory space allocated to the operating system, some memory space allocated to process A, process B, process C. Which memory space should process A here be allowed to access? obviously its own memory space. Should it be allowed to access another programs, another processes memory space? No, definitely not. So of course, this sounds just like memory segmentation that we just talked about. So what's the difference between secure memory management and memory segmentation? The answer is this. Here's the subtle difference. Memory segmentation is what I just described there, right? It's where the operating system controls and makes sure that each process only has access to its own memory space. Secure memory management is actually a broader term that encompasses memory segmentation. And it also covers a few other ways that we can protect memory, basically, by trying to prevent things like buffer overflows and stuff like that. So just here's the subtle difference. Honestly, for the CISSP exam, they're basically the same thing. But the subtle differences is that memory segmentation is specific to making sure that a process can only access its own bit of memory space. And memory segmentation is one of the techniques contained within overall secure memory management. Overall secure memory management is, as the definition says here, focused on protecting memory from unauthorized access and corruption. And one of the ways that we can do that is through memory segmentation. It's also by doing things like bounds checking and so forth to try to prevent buffer overflow, stuff like that. So that's secure memory management, a broader term covering a bunch of different ways that we can try and protect memory.

LESSON 191: Lesson 385
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Okay, let's talk briefly about SASE, the Secure Access Service Edge, SASE. This is a term that was coined by Gartner in 2019, I think. And they're hailing it as the future of wide area networks. So what is SASE? SASE is basically combining security and wide area network capabilities into a single cloud-based service. The basic idea is this. As companies become more distributed, as more people are working from home, there's less and less of this reliance of where everyone's in a single office building, in a single location. And because everything's becoming so much more distributed, we should probably also distribute our security controls. So our firewall, our DLP system, our VPN, all this sort of stuff. If we keep all of this in one central office, it can provide for a lot more latency and it can be a lot more difficult to manage this for all of our remote workers and remote offices and things like that. So the whole idea behind SASE is to basically get these security controls as close to users as possible. So SASE is also sort of combined with edge computing. Remember, edge computing is the idea of getting computational power as close to the user as possible. So the whole idea of SASE is that you basically move these services out onto your edge network, your VPN, your firewall services, your DLP systems, all these various security controls. You move them out onto the network edge as close to users as possible, and you kind of combine it with your WAN technologies, your wide area networks. And the basic idea is to just distribute these technologies basically as a cloud service, get them as close to the user as possible, physically as close to the user as possible. And it's going to make for higher performance, lower latency for them, et cetera. So that's the idea of SASE.

============================================================

Okay, this is a quick video on MIME headers. What is MIME? MIME stands for Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension Headers. So this protocol was originally designed to allow to basically understand the nature and format of a document file being sent. So the MIME headers identify things like, is this a text document? Is it an HTML document? Is it a JPEG file, et cetera. And as you can tell, multipurpose internet mail extensions. This was originally designed for email, but it's used in lots of other protocols, such as email hypertext transfer protocol for web pages, things like that. That basically indicates the nature and format of a document being transmitted. So now we know what MIME headers are, basically just a simple way of identifying what is being sent. Let's now talk about SMIME. And as you can probably guess, the S in SMIME stands for Secure Multipurpose Internet Mail Exchanges. And what SMIME is, is it's an extension that allows for the secure transmission of this MIME data. And SMIME uses both digital signatures and digital certificates. So FMIME is kind of an integral part of PKI's public key infrastructures. And the reason that S-MIME uses both digital certificates and digital signatures are for the exact reasons that you would think. The digital certificates are being used to authenticate who you're talking to and provide the public key. And the digital signatures are being used to provide the integrity and the authenticity of some data that's being sent in, say, the MIME header. That's all you really need to know about SMIME. It allows you to securely transmit this useful information, and through the use of digital certificates and digital signatures, you can verify the authenticity and the integrity of the MIME data that's being sent.

Let's now look at how actual traffic is sent across networks. And so this is referred to in network terminology as transport architecture, right? We've already defined words like architecture But in this case, we're talking about transport. How does, for example, network traffic actually move from device to device across a network? And the first thing that we need to understand is the word topology. In fact, if you look at the word topology and look it up in a dictionary, It's going to say how something is mapped. And so if you remember what a network actually is, it's just a whole bunch of devices that are connected to each other. How we logically and physically connect those devices together is referred to as its topology or network topology. And we'll look at this in more detail. But basically, a topology is how something is actually mapped, how the devices that make up our network are actually connected to each other physically and logically is referred to as the topology. Now, each device that makes up a network, for example, has to be able to handle traffic. And so if you think of devices as having different logical constructs, one of those logical constructs that it requires is something called the data plane. And you can think of the data plane as the construct that actually moves the frames. So let's take a switch as an example. a network switch, right? A network switch when it encounters frames, it actually has to move that information through the switch. That's what the data plane does. It actually makes and forwards the traffic based on decisions that have actually been made by something called the control plane, which we'll describe in more detail in a few minutes here. But think of the data plane as the low-level component of a network device that actually forwards packets and frames and so on, right? That's what the data plane is considered to be. It's the doer, basically. And it can do it in two ways. That's what cut through switching and store and forward switching is all about. So there's two ways that the data plane can actually move those frames. if we're using a switch as an example here, right? And the difference between cut through and store and forward is very simple. Cut and through starts moving the frame as soon as it understands or receives the destination address. It doesn't have to wait for the entire frame, for example, because it can start moving that traffic as soon as it understands the destination address. So this would be more efficient and faster, but the problem with this is that it cannot do error correction, which is what store and forward, as these words imply here, store and forward will store the entire frame, and look at the destination address, but move that frame only after it's done some error checking. Because if we store the entire frame, for example, then we can look at it, we can do some error checking, and then if everything's okay, then we start moving it. That's the difference. Obviously, store and forward would introduce a little more latency, because we have to store that frame first. before we can actually move it. Whereas in cut and through, as soon as we have a few bytes where we understand the destination address, we can start moving it. So cut and through, is faster, more efficient, but doesn't do anything related to error correction. Where store and forward does error correction, but introduces a bit more latency. So those are the two differences. So from a security perspective, right, because we always have to focus on security when we describe anything related to, in this case, networks and network security. Obviously, the integrity of transmissions is something that store and forward could give us. but we're gonna pay for it in efficiency. Whereas cutting through is very efficient, but doesn't provide any sort of integrity of the transmission or error correction of the transmission itself. Now, just to complete the picture of what we're talking about here, which is transport architecture. The data plane is what actually moves the traffic and the information, but it can only do that once it has received instructions from something called the control plane. So these are the two different logical constructs that every device, for example, network device needs to be able to logically separate. So the way that you can think of these is that the control plane makes the decisions and then the data plane will act on those decisions and actually do and move the traffic across those devices. So these are interconnected, obviously, but you can think of the control plane as what makes a decision and the data plane forwards the actual information based on the decisions that were made by the control plane. Then we can take this to another higher level as well, and that is to talk about what we call the management plane. A key word, obviously, management. but it makes decisions for the entire network from a logical perspective. And certainly, these constructs that we have here have given growth to software-defined networking that we're going to talk about later. in this presentation as well. But the management plane, as you can see here, its purpose is to configure, manage, maintain, and do all kinds of things related to the management of the entire network itself. and then of course the control plane will make the decisions that it will pass to the data plane that will actually act on those decisions that were being made by the control plane itself.

We just finished a section on transport architecture. Of course, we're in the architecture section of domain number 4, which is network security. All right. So there's a bit of a flow problem, but it's not intentional, I suppose. because that's the way things appear in the exam outline. And because we want to be completely aligned with the exam outline, this is the way that we want to present things. But be assured that at the end of all of this, Everything fits together. The pieces of the puzzle will fit together nicely for you, all right? But let's talk about this next section that again comes directly from the exam outline. which is focused on network topologies. And again, I think if you look at the word topology, that's the key word here. A network topology is how we physically and logically connect devices together. And so what you're going to see on this slide are the five most commonly used network topologies. And of course, we have to look at them from a perspective of security. Everything has to be related to security. This is a security exam. And therefore, when we look at especially very technical things, we have to try and relate it to security. But always look at it from the perspective of management, for example. So by far the most commonly used and implemented network topology today is what's called bus. And the reason it's called bus is because all of your devices, as you can see here, are connected to a central bus. And the bus is essentially a wire. So imagine one long wire running through a building, for example, all of your devices are going to be connected to that wire. That's essentially what bus topology looks like. Now, the reason this is very popular and the foundation really for all networking today is because it's very scalable, right? I mean, if we wanted to add more devices to the network, we simply extend the bus. It's very easy to do. The only problem with that is what we call attenuation, which means loss of signal strength over distance, but we can handle that very easily with boosters and repeaters for example so that's not actually a problem at all all right so it's very scalable and in fact you know by how we implement this this can be a very efficient and scalable type of network topology and why it's the foundation for really all networking today. And the other reason why this is the foundation for all networking today is because you're going to see when we go through the others, that this supports all of these others. So typically most networks today are going to be essentially bus for its foundation, but then you're going to have other topologies that you're going to see here. basically for functionality, but also for security. That's what we have to keep in mind. All right. Now, because we have to relate this to security, we obviously have to think about the security advantages and disadvantages of something like bus. All right. So when you look at connecting everything to one central bus, the bus itself or the wire itself becomes a single point of failure. And so we have to address that. We have to make it redundant. And implementations of bus topologies can certainly do that very easily. Now, some of the other problems that you might see here are things like, for example, collisions. What happens if two devices need to send information and they do it at the same time? Well, a collision is going to happen, which means none of that information is going to get to where it's supposed to. So we have to be able to handle collisions because essentially only one bit can be carried across that wire at any given point in time. So if you have two devices that send information at the same time, It's going to result in collisions. We have to be able to handle collisions. The other obvious problem here is lack of confidentiality. Because look, everybody is connected to that same bus, that same wire. So if someone's sending information, who has opportunity to look at that information? Well, the answer to that is everyone. Because we're all connected to the bus, the wire that information has to go across. We all have the opportunity to see it. So this obviously does not provide inherently confidentiality. So if we want confidentiality, we have to achieve it using other methods. But there are some good things about this. We already talked about obviously scalability, but it also permits what's called node failure. So any of these nodes can go down. That doesn't really impact the rest of the network. The rest of the devices can still communicate and do things across that network, even though some of these devices may have gone down. So we call that it permits node failure. Here's the next topology that we need to look at, and it's called tree. And they've done a pretty good job of obviously naming these for what they typically look like. They've done a pretty good job of naming these properly. So tree topology looks like something like this. You can start branching down. so that you can limit or restrict the traffic to go down a certain branch. And you can see an immediate advantage of that from a security perspective, because if we force the traffic to go down a certain branch like this one, These devices over here will not have an opportunity to see that traffic. So we can start again implementing bus topology as the foundation. But now we can start adding, for example, segments that may be tree based so that we can start doing things related to security. and also functionality, all right? Here's the next topology that we need to look at. And again, appropriately called, it's called star topology because every device is connected to a central other device, like a switch or a hub. And so obviously what you see here, first of all, is a single point of failure is going to be the central device itself. So we need to, again, address redundancy because redundancy is the way that we address single point of failure, cost effective redundancy. But obviously, by utilizing, for example, switches, which we're going to talk about later, this gives us the opportunity to start taking advantage of what star topology really gives us because we can connect devices to different ports. at the back of that switch and we can limit for example traffic to go out to certain ports so that now we can start creating really what are called virtual lands and again we'll talk about these things later on as well. Now, it was hard for us to show this, but again, this is why bus topology is the foundation for everything because it supports all of these other topologies that you see here. We could have easily taken for example this entire star diagram that you see here and place it up here as part of bus which is essentially what all networks do right networks are essentially bus but they support all of these other topologies not only for just functionality but also for security right here's the next one that we need to mention And I think this one's going to be pretty easy to reflect on from a security perspective, and that is mesh topology. Mesh simply means that every device is connected to every other device specifically. That's what they refer to as full mesh topology. although most organizations will implement what's called partial mesh topology where your most critical devices are connected to your most other critical devices. And obviously what this offers as a benefit or advantage from a security perspective would be redundancy, because every device is connected to every other device specifically. So that gives us really good availability and redundancy and so on. mesh topology is what this is. And the last one that we're going to look at, again, appropriately called ring topology. And you may have heard terminology in the past, like IBM token ring. which would have been an implementation of ring topology, where you still connect devices by using wires, obviously, but it's a closed loop. Obviously wouldn't be as scalable as something like bus here because you can't really stretch the ring. So you're limited, for example, in how many devices you can connect. And the more devices you can connect, the less efficient this type of network topology becomes. Obviously, it's not as scalable, it's not as simple to add more devices because you've got restrictions based on the size of that loop, if you will. What this shows is a benefit of ring topology, if you look at the little animation that we've got here, and that is something called a token. A token is passed from device to device, and that really dictates the priority on who can send information so the idea is this gets rid of the problem of collisions because if you want to send information you have to wait for that token to get to you But in the same token, it's not as efficient, is it? Because if you've got a large network and you're waiting to send information, you have to wait for that token to get to you, which might take a while, depending on the size of the network itself. So it's not as efficient. as, for example, bus topology or some of these other ones that you see here. And in fact, there's another problem related to that and really why this has disappeared. But what happens if a device goes down? Now we don't have the opportunity of passing that token from this device over to this device. So one node going down may affect the entire network. And obviously that's not a good thing. We can maybe take care of things by, for example, reversing the ring. or we can add a secondary ring for redundancy purposes but it's not as efficient as for example bus all right so these are really the five most commonly implemented topologies and we looked at them from a security perspective which is obviously what you need to focus on all right Now, related to this, there's two ways to really connect devices together, and that's the difference between what you see here. I think the words used is self-explanatory, and it tells you exactly what we're talking about here. Topology, again, means how we map things together, how we physically and logically connect devices together. And that's what this slide is talking about. And logical topology really defines logical. You can think of software as being logical, for example. Regardless of its physical layout, we can logically structure it based on some of those topologies that we just looked at before. That's the difference. Now, I think we introduced these earlier. but they are specifically mentioned in the exam outline. We'll just review these and look at these specifically. It is the part of the device, like we used, I think earlier switches as an example, but these could be routers as well. Any network device ultimately will have a data plane that actually does the work. It forwards the packets or the frames based on the decisions that were made by the control plane. And if you think about those words, software defined networking, it's networking that is defined through software. So imagine a centralized application that manages everything that the network does. So it manages the entire network and its devices and its constructs and how we logically and physically take care of for example the topologies but also how security is handled throughout the entire network itself is something that we can centrally manage through centralized software that is really what we call the management plane right so these are the constructs that really allow us to move information across networks, regardless of what the topology looks like. So the data plane can ultimately do switching of information using cut through switching and store and forward switching. And if you think about these words, it tells you the difference. In cut through switching, we can start forwarding the packet or the frame as soon as we understand the destination address. As soon as we receive a few bytes of information, the switch can look at the destination address and simply start forwarding it, even though it hasn't received the entire packet or frame. This is more efficient. But as it says here, does not allow for error checking because we haven't received the entire packet or frame. All right. So this is more efficient, but doesn't do anything related to error checking. Whereas store and forward is actually the opposite. We have to wait for the entire packet or frame to be received, and then we do some error checking, and then we forward that information based on the receiving address or the destination address that we have. So cut through starts right away as soon as we understand the address, but does not do anything related to error correction. Whereas store and forward, because we have to wait for the entire thing to be received and stored, we can do some error checking and then forward the packet, which obviously means this introduces more latency because we have to wait to receive the entire thing before we can check it for errors and forward it to its destination. All right, so if you look at it from a security perspective, this is faster but doesn't do anything related to the integrity of the transmission. This is slower but does offer us some error correction capabilities.

Continuing with our architecture discussion on networks, we've talked about network topologies, we talked about transport architecture, Let's see how devices can actually communicate with other devices specifically. So how can a specific device talk to other devices is what we call transmission methods, sometimes referred to as LAN transmission methods. But obviously, if you focus on these words like unicast, that means one device talking specifically to one other device. That's unicast transmissions. One to many is called multicast. And one to all is called multicast. Broadcast, of course, right? Now, again, we have to relate this to a security perspective, right? And on the surface here, if you ask, okay, which of these are more secure? You have to be careful with answering that question because most people would think, okay, well, Unicast is obviously more secure. And you'd be correct if we're thinking about that from a confidentiality perspective, right? So obviously, if you can restrict the transmission to go from one device specifically to another device, that is best from a confidentiality perspective. But what about an availability perspective, which is also a focus of security? then obviously in that case, if the exam, for example, asks you which of these is more secure, that's not enough context to be able to answer the question, right? Because if the exam question is talking about security from a availability perspective, then obviously one to all would be more secure from an availability perspective. So the lesson here is to obviously read the context of the question. Don't automatically assume that if the exam is focusing on security, it's always focusing on confidentiality. Remember, Security really means five things now, right? Confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation, right? So the exam question has to give you more context before you can answer which of these would be more secure. Alright? And there's actually another one that is specifically mentioned in the exam outline as far as transmission methods are concerned. And that is what we call anycast. Anycast transmission. And if you focus on the word Anycast, this is where incoming requests are forwarded to any number of different locations or a variety of different locations. All right. So we need to be able to decide where we're going to send that because it could go to any device, for example. That's what Anycast means, all right? So incoming requests are forwarded to any number or variety of locations. This is especially useful for architectures like CDNs, content distribution networks, like Netflix, for example, right? Where obviously incoming requests are going to be forwarded to the nearest or best node or device based on obviously providing more efficiency and less latency and in some cases more security as well. All right, so anycast means to any. We have to make that decision. But typically, especially used in CDN networks, we're sending it to the nearest or best within a group. All right. Focused on whatever it is that we may be focusing on, whether it's fastest or more secure and so on. We decide which of the nodes is going to be faster, maybe the nearest and so on. Right. So that's what any cast transmission is all about.

If we try and apply this concept of zero trust, that obviously is something that companies are moving towards or have moved towards. then this traditional way of looking at things from a north, south, east, west perspective really gets clouded to the point where obviously it doesn't matter Even though traditionally we've trusted east-west traffic more than in zero trust, we can't do that. Because it doesn't matter where something originates and where something terminates, whether it's from outside, of your data center or your network, or inside, we start with zero trust. So it's important to understand this concept that is still being used, right? North, south, east, west, but also how it applies with zero trust. So the key point here is to remember that even though we still use this terminology and traditionally we've trusted east-west traffic more than north-south and north-south traffic has required stricter policies. That's not the case with Zero Trust. In Zero Trust, everything requires stricter policies, regardless of whether it's north-south or east-west. And the way that we address this through zero trust is through more granular security that is focused on really granular things like attributes based parameters that your access controls and so on are able to look at and of course your trust requires real-time constant monitoring and also the ability to be able to react in real time as well.

I just realized that Rob always wears his nice destination certification sweatshirts and so on. And I should be too. So here I am. Nice theme. We're going to get you to the top of the CISSP mountain. That's the whole idea here. All right. Let's get into this next topic here. Earlier, when we talked about topologies, We talked about bus topology and star and mesh and ring and tree and so on. A lot of those topologies have this problem of collisions. So the industry has to obviously address collisions. That's what this section is going to focus on right so if you imagine that bus topology for example where every device is connected to a single bus which is essentially a wire if two devices send information at the same time gonna result in a collision, right? So we have to be able to handle collisions. And the industry has really come up with really three ways to handle collisions and be able to deal with them efficiently. and the two that you see on the outside here polling and token based we already talked about token based and i think i said that an example of that would have been ibm token ring where you have this token that's being passed from device to device, right? So the idea is if you need to send information, you have to wait for that token to get to you. And that could be problematic, first of all, if it's a large network, because then you have to wait, it's not efficient. Secondly, the biggest problem with that, too, is that if a device goes down, then maybe it may impact the entire network because that token cannot pass from one device to the next if there's one in between that has gone down, for example. It's basically why token-based handling of collisions has really gone away. And those networks have really disappeared as well. Polling used to be popular in the past as well. You may still see it, and that's why we cover it here. But in the old days, ArcNet type of networks would have used this. And basically what this means is that if you're part of a network, every device is asking or polling every other device whether that device has any information for you. All right. So the idea is, you know, I'm asking everybody that's part of my segment or network saying, OK, do you have any information for me? Okay, no, I'll move on to the next one. Do you have any information for me? Do you have any information for me? And so this isn't very efficient because while I'm doing this with every device that I'm connected to, so is everyone else. So there's a lot of energy, for example, that is being wasted by devices asking others whether there's any information. so that's gone away as well everything today is basically what you see here this carrier sense multiple access csma for short and if you think about these words right carrier sense multiple access tells you exactly how this works right the idea is and again if you picture bus topology In your mind, all devices are connected to that bus, which is essentially the carrier, right? And because you're connected to the bus, the wire, you can sense it. So the idea is, if you've got any information to send, you have to wait for that wire to become free. And so the idea is you wait and you wait until it becomes free. And how will you know when it does become free? Well, typically the way we send bits across media is using voltage, electricity. So the idea is if you've got information to send, You've got to wait for that wire to have zero voltage on it. In fact, I think what we use is plus five volts to represent a one and a zero is minus five volts. So if you're waiting to send information, guess what? You have to wait for that wire to have zero voltage across and then you send your information. And then you wait for an acknowledgement basically to come back from the machine that you sent it to. That's basically what this really indicates, right? You just have to wait for the wire to become free and then you send information. that's not going to totally eliminate the opportunity of collisions because collisions are still going to happen. Here's a scenario where that might happen. Let's say there's two devices. that are waiting to send information right so we're both sensing the wire and as soon as the wire becomes free guess what we're both going to detect that at the same time and probably send information at the same time which is going to result in a collision. So collisions are still going to happen even though we wait for the wire to become free. And that's why there are two flavors of CSMA. There's CSMA with collision avoidance, which means we totally avoid collisions. And the way that this is handled is that instead of, for example, using one wire to connect devices together, we use two. And so one wire is used to receive information. The other one is used to send information. So the idea is you've got lanes of traffic. And if you've got lanes of traffic, collisions should never happen. And this conceptually is the concept behind this. Instead of lanes or specific physical wires, we can use different frequencies and so on to receive and send information to avoid collisions. And very appropriate symbol that we've used on top of this because a technology that actually uses CSMA with collision avoidance are all of your wireless standards like 802.11 b g n a c a x and so on. They all use CSMA with collision avoidance. And so when you're talking to a wireless access point or your wireless router, for example, we're sending and receiving bits, but we're using different lanes to do that so that collisions are actually avoided. All wired LANs and technologies and networks will use CSMA with collision detection. So collisions will happen, but you have the ability to be able to detect them. And so the idea is, again, we're waiting for the wire to become free. When it does, we're going to send our information. But because you're still sensing the wire, you'll be able to detect collisions if they happen, right? Because a collision will cause the voltage to spike. So if two bits collide, for example, that's going to cause the voltage to spike. And so if you've just sent information, you're still monitoring the wire. And if you detect a collision that just happened, a spike in the voltage, then you can say, oh, I just sent information. that probably has affected my transmission. I need to resend the information, right? So that's what CSMA with collision detection tells you exactly what these do, right? We avoid collisions here. Wireless is what uses that. Collision detection, CSMA with collision detection is what all wired LANs and networks will use where we have the ability to detect collisions if they happen and resend the information.

All right, let's talk about physical segmentation. Of course, we're focused on domain number four here, which is all about network security. Physically segmenting your network is one of the best ways to provide security. And what this section really deals with is the management of the network and some of the options that you would have if you are physically segmented, for example. And so typical terminology that we talk about here is what you see on this slide. First, we have what's called in-band management. And the key word here is obviously in-band. The management of the network itself and its functions and so on is part of the network and on the same network itself. All right. So managing the network is done through the same network that all of the services and transmissions and everything else is happening. That's in-band. Now we say that this is less secure because unauthorized users may have access, because they have access to the network and its services, may also have access to the capability to be able to manage that network and therefore interfere with it. What's better from a security perspective is what's called out of band. Now we did talk about out of band in some other contexts like key distribution. in cryptography for example and we said that's a totally different channel that is not available to the attacker and so that makes it a really good way to distribute keys for example. It's the same concept here. You have a separate network that is used to manage the other network, for example, the main network. And so this is more secure because unauthorized users would not have access to the management network because there is really no physical connection between that and the actual network itself. And there's even a better option, in fact, when we talk about this, and that is what we call air-gapped networks. This is where there is no connection possible. Everything is air-gapped so that it stands alone. There is no physical or logical way to connect to that network which makes it the most secure. So there is no way to physically connect to it or even wirelessly connect to it. This is the most secure way. to set up physical segmentation because there is no possibility for anything external from that network to be able to connect to it.

So if we define latency as being the arrival time of frames, how long does it take for us to get those frames and arrive to our location, for example, the variation in those arrival times over a period of time is considered to be jitter. Right. So you don't want a lot of jitter, obviously, because it affects the arrival time of frames. Okay.

But he would have talked about how really the ideal number of entrances through a perimeter is actually zero, right? Because if you're not allowing any traffic in or traffic out, then that gives us the most secure type of entry. For example, perimeter. Although that's not very practical, of course. So he probably would have talked about how having one entrance and one exit is ideal because then you know who's coming in and with what. and who's leaving and with what. It's the same concept that applies in network security. We only want one way in and one way out of our network so that we know exactly what's coming in and also what's leaving. And I've been pretty deliberate here in talking about how we need to understand what's coming in, but also what's leaving. And these are concepts that are related to what the industry calls ingress monitoring, which means what's coming in. That's what it's referred to as. So that's the demarcation point that the industry talks about. Now, something that has been recently added to the exam outline is this concept of edge networks right and this is very appropriate terminology because it's putting your network to the edge as close as possible to your end users and your customers and your clients the idea being the closer you are to your stakeholders like clients and customers and others right the more you can service them and be able to provide faster and reduced latency and all of those things that your clients and your customers are really looking for. Because you are closer to them, right and your devices and your technologies and your services are closer to them that means that we have reduced latency and increased throughput and performance because physically geographically The devices and the networks and the segments and the applications and everything that you're providing to your clients and customers are closer. to the user, which means from their perspective, we have reduced latency and increased performance. This is especially good for things like content distribution networks. So their infrastructures that are providing that ability for you to be able to stream all of their services. The closer they are to you, obviously the better it is from your perspective. Right.
