So let's say we have, for example, You know the cloud if we want to call it that the cloud and you know, typically we've got devices in here and You know services and so on and we're trying to deliver those to your clients and your customers alright, so We've got vehicles, we've got, you know, other technologies that require obviously services and, you know, all kinds of computation and storage and so on. mobile devices, and even your homes, for example. I can't draw, but hopefully I'm doing a good job of this. And of course, what we're talking about here is providing services to all of these different locations and so on. This is the typical model, and we can call it cloud computing, where obviously computation is actually happening in here. So if you think about what cloud computing actually is, we've got really three components, compute, storage and network but all of that exists in the cloud in here for example and we're trying to deliver services to all of these different locations outside of the cloud, for example, to your devices, your cars, your homes, your TVs, your internet of things, if you want. So this is the typical model of cloud computing. What edge computing or edge networks do is they try and move a lot of that computation as close as possible to the end users. So let me just draw this if I can. If we put a layer here and we call this edge technologies, or edge networks that will contain, for example, compute capabilities and storage capabilities and object capabilities and cache, for example, and DNS. In fact, some of those technologies that I just mentioned are actually called things like Edge Compute and Edge Storage and Edge Cache and Edge Objects and Edge DNS, for example. The idea is all of those services are being provided here closer to the end user, which reduces latency, increases throughput and performance. For example, that's the idea of edge networks and so on. Because the whole idea here is that we have distributing data and computational resources across multiple nodes. And the whole idea of what we had before in typical cloud computing is that computation is being done in here in the cloud itself, which may create problems. In edge networks and edge computing, we're removing this computation from here and we're actually moving it to here right so we rely on the computational capabilities of the end devices themselves which again increases throughput and makes things better and reduces latency, all of those things that we typically have as problems in the traditional cloud computing model. So the advantages here is because we're processing locally, All right, this reduces traffic and latency and bandwidth consumption and so on. And so this is really cool for things that have appeared recently, for example, for artificial intelligence and so on. This is really good for artificial intelligence. And we can drive really good things like autonomous driving and the ability to do industrial automation because all of that computing is really here now. as opposed to being in the Cloud through servers, for example, that may experience delays and latency and all of that kind of stuff. But of course, we have to focus on a security perspective as always. And so what's important to understand here is that because you're moving things closer to the users, This becomes more of a problem from a security perspective. You may have seen a video earlier on that Rob talked about how it's easier to protect things at a server level. So from a security perspective, the easiest and best way to protect things is right at the server itself because we can do things centrally at that server to address things like confidentiality, integrity, and availability, and so on. But because we're moving things closer to the end user here, now security becomes a challenge because it's harder to protect. Imagine having to protect, for example, data and privacy requirements. in a edge technology type of architecture and environment, because now you have to be able to apply security controls at each of these different locations closer to the end user, which makes it much more difficult. So some of the disadvantages that we have in edge technologies and edge networks, for example, is related to security and privacy. Because we are distributing data and computation, across multiple nodes, that inherently increases the risk of data breaches and tampering and unauthorized users, for example. And, you know, another challenge might be related to what I talked about before, and that is, you know, the implementation of artificial intelligence, for example, as the number of devices grow, where that computation and data resides, for example. then so does the complexity of being able to not only protect all of that information at each of these devices, for example, but also maintaining and updating and coordinating, for example, all of these things and how they work together using artificial intelligence. So as complexity increases, so does our goal of protecting things, which is something that obviously we need to take care of in edge computing and edge resources and so on. All right. So just to summarize the advantages of this, is that it reduces latency and increases throughput and performance, which is obviously what the end users are looking for, especially with edge technologies and edge networks. But this complicates the ability to be able to protect from the perspective of security because we're moving things closer to the user. We're distributing things across multiple devices. and multiple architectures. So we need to remember that from a security perspective. Now let me keep going here because some of the important things to focus on related to obviously this edge network capability that we have today. First of all, there's some terminology that is specifically mentioned in the exam outline. And I think I've already referred to ingress monitoring, for example. So this simply means that as things are coming in, let's say to our network, for example, We want to be able to monitor it and make decisions on it so that we can make decisions related to whether we allow it in or not. And this is traditionally what we've done through firewalls. A firewall is able to look at incoming traffic, apply certain rules and policies to be able to decide whether we allow that traffic or not. So ingress monitoring means traffic that's coming into the network itself. But earlier, I stressed the importance of us being able to look at outgoing traffic as well. Traditionally, companies and organizations have always been focused on what's coming into a network because obviously we want to protect against attacks and other things that are coming in. to the network itself. That's always been the focus. But obviously, looking at what's going out of your network is also just as important. In fact, in many cases, it may be more important to look at what's going out for things like data exfiltration or data loss prevention. We want to make sure that sensitive data is not going outside of our networks and segments based on its value. So it's really important. And plus also, If there are denial of service attacks that are originating from inside of our network, we want to monitor outgoing traffic to make sure that it's legitimate so that we can stop denial of service from going out for example we'll talk more about that later on but obviously the theme here is that it's important to be able to monitor incoming traffic but also outgoing traffic right now it's something else that has become really popular over the last number of years is what's called peering. And simple definition of peering is what you see here, direct interconnection of separate networks. So imagine two networks. that want to exchange information. The traditional way of doing this would have been to have that traffic be carried across those two networks by, for example, providers and others like third parties that you would have had to pay for, for example. The concept of peering is that those two networks can just directly connect to each other and allow the exchange of traffic to happen between them without going through a third party, without involving any third parties that you typically have to pay for that traffic to go through. So the idea is the two networks. can directly connect to each other by using obviously network technologies that are available to us. So the traditional way of having those two networks connect to each other and exchange traffic would have been through for example network operators that can carry that traffic between those two networks. The idea in peering is that there are no third parties. The two networks can directly connect to each other. at no additional costs for mutually beneficial reasons. That's the idea behind pairing. All right. So the idea is one network connects to another through direct ways. And so what you end up having here is a business relationship between two or more networks to allow direct exchange of IP traffic in a mutually beneficial way. All right. And so if you think about what we just talked about in the last section, which was for example edge networking and edge technologies, because we move those edge technologies closer to the users, we can have those edge technologies connect directly to other direct, for example, edge technologies that exist in another network without having that traffic traditionally have to go through the cloud or the internet to get to that other network. We can have those devices like, for example, edge routers that exist at those locations to directly connect to each other using technologies like BGP, for example, which stands for Border Gateway Protocol. that, for example, routers can understand and use to be able to connect those edge routers directly to each other so that we have a direct connection. And that's what peering is all about. All right. So the idea behind that. is that you've got direct connections between networks through really co-location facilities. I mean, there's different ways to be able to satisfy this need of peering And the idea behind it is obviously to save money so that you're not involving, for example, you know, a third party to be able to carry that traffic across traditionally the Internet or providers, for example. The two networks can connect to each other, especially if they're co-located in a data center or in some location where it's very easy to connect, for example, routers together and switches together or even fiber optic cabling together. So that's one way of satisfying the need for peering. The other way is to use what are called virtual private interconnections, right? These are called PNIs, private network interconnections, where we use, for example, virtual private networks to be able to do that between the two networks themselves. And, you know, the typical way of doing this these days is to use what are called IXPs. And these stand for Internet Exchange Points or companies that have set up Internet Exchange Points to be able to allow peering to happen. and what they do is they house necessary hardware for example to be able to allow interconnections between networks and all these companies or networks need to have are again routers that can use BGP protocol, border gateway protocols, to be able to allow those connections to happen. So BGP is typically what's used to allow those connections to happen. And so, you know, really the connection here between what we called earlier edge networking and edge computing and peering, they are really things that are really good together because they allow these technologies to happen. and be able to allow for example edge computing right because it allows fast speeds high throughputs low prices They're really made possible through pairing capabilities, right? Because now we have the ability to be able to connect networks together directly without having to go through third parties and carriers, for example, to carry that information between networks. We can connect them together. to be able to provide even faster speeds without involving third parties, which reduces costs, of course. OK, we just talked about edge networks and edge computing, edge technologies and the concept of peering. One thing I forgot to mention about peering is that it really requires a couple of things. First of all, you need to get what's called a public autonomous system number. And that is assigned through regional intranet registry companies. So you need that number first. It's called a autonomous system number. And then the only other thing that is required for peering is an edge router for that BGP protocol, border gateway protocol, to be able to configure the interconnections and along with the public block of IP addresses that obviously the company has as well. That's what peering requires. It's become really popular, saves money, just simple connections between networks, bypassing any third parties that you would have to pay for carrying that traffic across the internet or other architectures, for example, to allow the inter-exchange of information between networks. They can connect together. using, for example, direct connections or edge routers that support BGP and those connections. Now, logical segmentation is something that we're going to talk about at this point. Again, this whole section is focused on how we protect our network. And really it was titled, for example, how we use partitioning and segmentation to be able to either allow or deny anything that's coming in or leaving, but not just networks, but parts of networks that we call segments. or even assets within those segments is really what logical segmentation is focused on. If you look at the history of how we've protected our networks, we really started with hardware firewalls that used to sit at your perimeters of your networks right and basically what we did is we you know thought of the entire network as one big asset that we tried to protect and therefore we put a firewall at your perimeter and that firewall made decisions as to what we allowed to come in and what we allowed to go out. Then the next evolution of really segmentation was to divide your network into different segments and at each segment, we had hardware firewalls as well. And then we started to use, the key word here is logical, we started to figure out that we can use software to act as firewalls, for example. And we put firewalls that were really software constructs to be able to make decisions at each of those segments to either allow in or out of those segments. And what we've got now is really what's called micro segmentation, where we've got not only segments inside of your networks, but we've got assets that are within those segments. And these could be any type of assets. They could be containers or they could be applications or they could be specific assets. We have the technology now to be able to use logical ways to make decisions as to what we allow in and out of specific assets, very granular assets. And so the advent of technology and the ability to be able to make more decisions granular security decisions is really where we're at with logical segmentation. We can divide your network and its contents and the architectures within into all kinds of little pieces, containers, if you will, and be able to make decisions at each of those levels. That's what micro segmentation is really all about. And of course, what you're going to see here as technologies that we can use to be able to make decisions. They're all going to contain the word virtual because virtualization is really what's given rise to the ability to do this granular security at very specific granular levels. It's really what's allowed us to implement zero trust capabilities where we trust nothing and verify everything through very granular security controls and in policy enforcement, for example. So take that concept and apply it to your network and anything that is very granular within your network and being able to make decisions at that very detailed, low-level, granular level. level is really what logical segmentation and micro segmentation is all about. In fact, I think you're going to see a definition of that. But a lot of these terms that you see here may appear on the exam. And they're used to make decisions, obviously, but they all contain the word virtual. And a lot of these we're going to talk about as we keep going through this presentation and this course. But you've all heard of virtual lands, for example. We've always had the ability to create virtual lands by using switching technology. But again, now we can take that same functionality and move it to software and be able to create, for example, virtual, segments or virtual local area networks you know by using software-defined networking and other things that obviously give us that ability we don't have to use physical switches anymore to be able to make the same sort of functionality and address the same sort of functionality that we needed in those switches for we can do things at the software level and a virtualization abstraction layers to be able to do all of this all right and you all know about virtual private networks as well we're going to talk about those later on but obviously they allow us to establish secure connections between, again, not just networks, but also segments within networks or assets within those networks by creating virtual connections that use really tunneling and encryption. to be able to create those very secure connections between entities across untrusted networks or untrusted segments of networks. And with edge computing and everything else that technology has given us today as far as advancements are concerned. And so just at a high level, what you typically see here is the way that we typically protect things. And what this is showing for us is the traditional way of how we've protected networks, right? So if you look at this, you've got what's called your private network here, where all of your valuable assets and services may reside, right? But obviously to be able to be in touch with your clients and customers and other third parties that you value, we have to have connections to them, typically through the internet and other untrusted networks. And so you want to be able to provide what are called your services and your products to your stakeholders that you have relationships with. And so being able to allow them to come right into your private network is not a good idea. So at a very high level, what we have to be able to do is enforce policies and rules in what this really shows us, for example. So the idea is those entrances and exits that we talked about before. You want to be able to allow traffic to come in, but only if it satisfies your controls and the rules that you've put in. So the idea behind what network people refer to as choke points, these are your entrances and exits into your network where you wanna be able to enforce rules. And the reason we call them choke points is because that's where we choke the traffic so that we look at all of it to be able to make decisions. This is where you would have all of those capabilities like firewalls. and intrusion detection systems and intrusion prevention systems, and also the ability to be able to correct anything that looks suspicious, for example, right? So the idea behind this is that You want to be able to look at incoming traffic, but also outgoing traffic to be able to make the right decisions. And this is where those partitioning technologies and segmentation technologies that we just talked about would be placed, but also within your networks to be able to make those decisions at a very granular level. All right. So this is typically at a high level what most companies and organizations networks look like. But this also shows us the idea of a choke point where you look at that traffic and be able to make decisions as to what you allow in and what you allow out out of your internal network. For example, it's referred to as a choke point where again, you would have those complete controls where you would be able to, for example, make preventive, detective and corrective capabilities. Now, obviously, there are technologies and architectures that need to be here to be able to do that. And so this also shows something that is specifically mentioned in the exam line, and that is a boundary router, which explains exactly what this is. It's a router that exists at the boundary of your networks. All right, so this is that boundary router that has capabilities to be able to take that traffic that is coming in from the outside or leaving, for example. it's that perimeter that you know we talked about earlier that is really the last point of defense anything inside of this is what you should be able to control anything that's outside of it you won't be able to control. So this is where that entrance and that exit really exists. And there are certain devices like boundary routers that need to be placed there. So that's the idea of that. And of course, you've all heard these letters before, DMZ, which obviously stands for Demilitarized Zone. But the idea of a DMZ in network terminology is that it's a sub network that is really not part of your internal network. It's not really part of the outside Internet, for example. It sits in between, but it is a sub network that you can control. All right. And what you do here is you place services and products and capabilities in here that need to be accessed from the outside internet, for example. So examples could be things like your web applications that are hosting products and services that your customers and others that you have relationships with would want to be able to access from the outside. So let's just use an example here. Let's say you want your clients and customers to be able to pay you for products. That requires some really nice applications that can make it easy for your clients and customers to be able to order those products and pay you for them. That requires some really nice applications to make it easy for them to do that. It would not make sense to have those applications running on the inside of your network, because then you would allow traffic to come right into your internal network. to be able to allow outside customers and other entities to be able to interface with those applications that would be running on the inside of your network. That would put everything else at risk inside of your private network. And so what we do is we place those really nice applications in a sub network that again is not part of your internal network, it sits in between, right? But you can control obviously what's in here. And so any traffic that's coming in from the outside will be channeled into your DMZ so that people that are ordering your products and paying for them are channeled into here and they can access those really nice applications that make it easy for them to order your products and pay you for them. right and of course other things that you would have in here you know maybe servers and mail servers and ftp servers anything that needs to be accessed from the outside right is channeled into here so that ultimately you're protecting all of your valuable resources and assets that are inside of your network all right so that's the idea behind a DMZ and the services that we place in here now obviously because this allows traffic to come in from the outside, untrusted internet, for example. Anything that is in that DMZ needs to be protected. It needs to be hardened. It needs to be strengthened. It needs to withstand attacks, for example. That's the concept of what we call bastion. In fact, if you look at the word bastion, I think its origin is from obviously Latin, but a bastion means a fortress. something that has been readied for an attack, something that has been strengthened to try and withstand an attack. So the idea, anything that you have in here, whether it's servers or routers or applications, have been strengthened and hardened to withstand attacks. That's why we call them, for example, bastion hosts or bastion routers or bastion applications. They've been strengthened so that they can withstand attacks. We've talked about the concept of hardening before, especially in domain number three. Hardening means if there are certain services that we don't need, we turn them off. Services that we do need, we protect as much as possible. And then we patch and we upgrade to the latest services. versions because typically they're more secure than earlier versions and so on. And we have increased monitoring capabilities and so on. All of that is what makes anything in the DMZ. Again, bastion, because we want to be able to protect the value of what's in here because we're allowing traffic to come in from the outside network, which puts all of these resources and assets that you have in here at risk. So they need to be strengthened. That's the concept of what we call bastion host or bastion in general, for example. It's been strengthened, it's been hardened, and so on. Thank you.

LESSON 204: Lesson 398
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Password vaults, you may well have used password vaults. They're often built into web browsers nowadays. A lot of us have them installed on our home computers, things like Dashlane, LastPass. They're used in corporate environments all the time. So what are password vaults? They are application that's used for storing passwords. They're often referred to as password managers, by the way. So here's the whole idea behind this, right? We as humans, or most of us are not very good at remembering a whole lot of passwords, let alone a whole lot of really complex, good passwords. If you force someone to choose a different, like, you know, have passwords for 20 different systems, pretty much guaranteed they're gonna use the same password on every single one of those 20 systems or just change like a number or something. And that's obviously a big security weakness, right? Because if one system is compromised and the attacker manages to figure out the user's credentials on one system, it's highly likely that the user has used the same credentials, right? The same email address and password on a bunch of other systems. So the whole fundamental idea behind password vaults is this is a tool that's meant to store passwords, to remember passwords for us. And that way, the whole reason you would want to use one of these things is that you can then store a unique, really long 20, 30 character complex password for every single different system. And it's the password manager that's going to remember those passwords and not you, the user. So that's the huge idea behind password vaults. And they're very beneficial and they can certainly help from a security perspective to make it so that you don't have to remember a whole bunch of complex passwords and you are able to use a different password on every system. they can also have some benefits from in a corporate perspective if you're in the difficult situation of having like older legacy systems that you can't have like say unique admin accounts on so there's like one shared admin account You can use password vaults to sort of like check out the admin password or share it across multiple employees. Not great, but it's workable. But like with any security tool, right? There's also major downsides. and one of the major downsides of a password vault is it's a massive single point of failure if your password vault is compromised now of course the attacker has access to everything so you want to be really careful about using these things and the master password the master password is the password that you use to unlock your password vault and get access to all your passwords contained within You really want to try and make sure that's a very secure password. And best practice would also be use some sort of multi-factor authentication to unlock the password vault. But yeah, that's the idea. Password vaults.

============================================================
LESSON 205: Lesson 399
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Passwordless authentication. It's exactly what it sounds like. Passwordless authentication is authenticating a user without using a password. So really what passwordless authentication is, is just using the other two factors of authentication to authenticate the user, to verify the user's identity. So authentication by ownership or authentication by characteristic. Authentication by ownership would be examples of having one-time passwords or a YubiKey that you have to have in your possession. That would be a way of authenticating a user without a password. Or biometrics. Authentication by biometrics through say biometrics like fingerprints or retinal scanners. Or authentication through behavioral characteristics like keystroke dynamics. Examples of authentication by characters are things like gate dynamics, how you walk, how you type, how you talk. So how you sign your name. So those would all be authentication by Characteristic, specifically behavioral characteristics. And these are all examples of passwordless authentication. And there's some big benefits of doing this of course, right? It's definitely considered to be more secure. One of the real challenges with passwords is if someone falls victim to say a phishing attack. where they are sent an email telling them that, oh, you know, your bank has decided to give you a $50 free dollars or whatever. Sign into your bank here to claim your $50 free dollars. That's a stupid example, but If a user falls victim to a phishing attack and they enter their username and password on what they think is their real banking website, or what they think is some trusted website, but it's actually the attacker's site, and the attacker has the user's username and password, they can easily now log in as them. That's the problem with passwords, right? If the attacker knows it, they can easily, or use that password to log in as the real user. But the same is not true with authentication by ownership or characteristic. Let's take authentication by ownership with one-time passwords. Those one-time passwords, as the name implies, one time, they're only good once. So even if the attacker captures a user's one-time password, it's going to change. There's going to be a new one-time password in 30 or 60 seconds. And the same thing with biometric data. It's a lot harder to capture and just store and reuse. So passwordless authentication can definitely provide increased security. Users may enjoy it because they don't have to remember these horrible, complex passwords. And as I mentioned, it can definitely provide for reduced phishing. There are, of course, downsides as well, though, right? If someone forgets their phone and they don't have their one-time password generator or their YubiKey, they're locked out. There's also a lot of concerns around biometrics, especially physiological biometrics. the secrecy of someone's biometric data. And if that data is exposed, it's a lot more consequential to the user. So there's some biometric concerns. And these passwordless systems tend to be more expensive. It's not all sunshine, happy days, and rainbows. There's some downsides as well. But overall, it's often a good idea. And that's why you see a lot of organizations looking at ways of authenticating users without using a password.

LESSON 206: Lesson 400
Source: network-intercept-vtt

All right, we just talked about role-based access, and here's just a quick bit of terminology you need to know, the difference between roles and groups. So, what are roles? Roles are what we just talked about, right? A role is where you, the way role-based access works is you create a role, and it's the role that defines what the access is. It's function centric, right? So you create a role for the payroll manager level one, something like that. So a role is a set of permissions that correspond to a job function. And this is slightly and subtly different than groups. Groups are just collections of users. So you put a bunch of users together, and then you say, OK, we'll give these users some access. So this is subtle, but the difference between roles and groups is that a role is very specific. And I think it's summarized well down here. Roles are function centric. So a role says payroll manager level one gets this access. Payroll manager level two gets this access. So you create roles and the roles define the access. And an employee can have one or more different roles assigned to them. Groups is where you group different users together. And these user groups get some sort of access. But just remember this really subtle difference. Roles are function centric, whereas groups are user centric. Probably not super important though for the exam, but just in case, that's the difference between roles and groups.

LESSON 207: Lesson 401
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

There's just a little bit of terminology that you need to know related to access policy enforcement. So access policy enforcement is exactly what it sounds like. These are the systems that we can use to enforce the access policies that we've come up with. So when we've decided this user should get access to what, how do we actually enforce that? How do we make sure that this user only gets access to what they're supposed to? And here's the two little bits of terminology that you need to know about PDPs versus PEPs. What the heck are these? The names are actually pretty self-explanatory. So a policy decision point, This is the system that's actually making the decisions as to what a user should get access to. And it would be basing these decisions based on things like need to know, least privilege, what the owner has decided a user should get access to. And the PDP, the policy decision point, is the component that's actually making the decisions, which is different from the PEP, which is, again, pretty self-explanatory, the policy enforcement point. This is the system that's actually enforcing the decisions. So whatever the PDP has decided, if the PDP says, yes, this user should be allowed in, then the policy enforcement point will say, yeah, go ahead. You're allowed in. Or if the policy decision point says, this user is not allowed in, then the policy enforcement point would prevent that user from accessing a system. So these can be combined into one system or they can be separate. You can have like a central decision point and then you can have distributed enforcement points around your organization. There's lots of different ways to architect this, but just there you go. That's PDPs versus PEPs.

LESSON 208: Lesson 402
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Service accounts. Service accounts are accounts that are used by something other than a human. So service accounts are used by systems, batch jobs, things like that. Think if there's a web server that needs to access a backend database. that web server should have its own service account into the database server. Or if there's a batch job that needs to run and store some data on an FTP server, that batch job should probably have a service account on that FTP server. So service accounts are simply accounts that are used by applications, systems, or something like that, specifically not by humans. And service account management is obviously managing these service accounts, managing the creation process. So when are these accounts required to be used and making sure that, you know, if there's a web server accessing a backend database, What are the absolute minimum number of permissions that the service account needs to allow the web server to query the database? you know, to gather the information that needs, but nothing more. Like should the service account have the ability to delete tables, drop the entire database? Probably not. The service account should probably just be allowed to update or read records, maybe delete records, things like that. So as part of the creation process, you want to make sure that service accounts only have the permissions they need. And as part of overall account management, you want to make sure that you have an inventory of all of these accounts that, as I mentioned, the permissions are appropriate, minimum needed. that there's some process in place to periodically review these accounts and review the permissions. There's a process in place for emergencies. What if a system is compromised or a service account is compromised? How can you go about changing that service account credential? All of this falls under service account management, maintaining these service accounts.

LESSON 211: Lesson 405
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

In relation to vulnerability management and penetration testing, you may have come across these terms, red team, blue team, and purple team. What is this? So red team, these are your simulated attackers. So in a good sized organization, you might have a group of a team of people that are focused on essentially being the simulated attackers in the organization. So the red team is there to be the threat. The red team is doing offensive security. They're doing things like ethical hacking and penetration testing, and they're doing things like simulated spear phishing attacks, you know, internal phishing campaigns. maybe baiting attacks and they're often sort of keeping an eye on what are the latest greatest threats that are out there and thinking about how they can be applied to the organization so essentially the red team is focused on being the threat The blue team, this is your defenders. The blue team is the folks that are focused on stopping the threat. So the blue team are running the security operations. They're monitoring systems and monitoring log files, SIM systems, alerts that are being generated, handling incidents when they happen. right? The blue team is your defenders and they're trying to stop the threat. So that's, if you've ever heard these terms, red team, blue team, that's the difference, right? Red team is your simulated attackers And blue team are the defenders. And by the way, a bit of trivia behind this, right? Where do these terms come from? They're actually from the military. I think it was the US military. They play war games, right? One of the best ways of sort of practicing is to play war games. And so, of course, the blue team, that's the good guys, right? The Americans. And then the red team is, you know, the evil Soviets. So that's where the terms come from. Blue team is the Americans. They're the good guys. And then the red team is the evil Soviet threat. So that's where these terms come from. Now, you may have also heard of purple team, and this is actually a really kind of misnamed thing, because purple team is not actually usually a separate team. Instead, what purple is, is getting your red team and blue teams to work together, to collaborate. Like, yes, it's good to have different teams focused on different things and even to have a little bit of friendly competition. But you don't want it to get to the point where, you know, these teams just do not talk to each other and they don't play nicely. You want them to collaborate. You want the red team to be saying, you know, hey, through our threat intelligence and through what we were finding here, we're finding these issues. and telling the blue team folks so that they can go, okay, good. Now that we know these issues exist, we can go and mitigate those vulnerabilities. We can do something about it. So that's what purple is all about. Getting your blue teams, you know, like, yes, you want some friendly competition. but you also want your red team and your blue team to work together collaboratively, to learn from each other, to share information. And that's what PRPL is all about.

Wouldn't it be cool if we had some sort of way of, especially in a large organization, keeping track of hundreds of thousands of different systems across the environment and making sure that none of those systems have vulnerabilities and that they're all configured correctly. Wouldn't it be cool if we had a way of somehow automating all of this? Because this is not an easy thing to do. So it'd be pretty awesome if we could somehow automate all of this. Enter SCAP, the Security Content Automation Protocol. This is exactly what SCAP is meant to do. So SCAP is a whole set of specifications, interoperable specifications that are meant to work together to help organizations automate their vulnerability management. and policy compliance, basically making sure that systems remain in compliance with policies, remain appropriately configured. So how does SCAP do this? Well, the way it does this is through a whole set of interoperable specifications. And I want to be clear here, you absolutely do not need to memorize all these names for the exam. The point here is, this is actually really complex. So SCAP is a whole initiative being driven by NIST, the National Institute of Standards Technologies, and a bunch of other really large organizations and government agencies in the US. They're all working together to try and create a bunch of specifications for how you could automatically maintain an inventory of all of your assets and then do automatic sort of assessment scans to make sure that those systems remain in compliance with their appropriate configuration to identify if those systems have vulnerabilities, to understand what the baseline configuration should be for a system, etc. So that's the idea of SCAP. It's a series of interoperable specifications that are meant to automate the whole vulnerability management process within an organization. which is subtly different from the next thing I'm gonna talk about, which is known as breach attack simulations. So here's what breach attack simulations is all about. Actually, let me take a step back here. Here was pretty common practice in a lot of organizations, not even that long ago. A lot of organizations would only do vulnerability scans like say once a quarter. I worked with a ton of organizations that would do this. And the reason they would do a vulnerability scan once a quarter is because that's what the PCI DSS standard mandated that they had to do. They had to do quarter vulnerability scans. So that's exactly what they would do. They would run a vulnerability scan every quarter. They'd find a whole bunch of stuff. They'd do a bunch of remediation work over a week or two. They might run one more scan just to show that those issues that were identified had been remediated. And then they wouldn't run another scan for another month or two. And is there a chance, a chance that a new vulnerability might be discovered in that intervening month or two between when they're doing scans? Of course. And that was the problem, right? it's probably not a good idea nowadays with all these different threats out there to just be doing a scan once a quarter. And so this is the whole idea behind breach attack simulation. The idea here is there are tools that are being developed. This is kind of cutting edge stuff, but there are tools now that will essentially automate the penetration testing and sort of automate the process of simulating attackers, of trying to continuously, constantly try and break into your organization to look to see where there's vulnerabilities so they can then be remediated. So breach attack simulation is basically automated the penetration testing process and doing it essentially continuously, constantly. So here's the subtle difference between SCAP and breach attack simulation. So as I said, right, SCAP is a whole extremely complicated suite of standards for automating vulnerability management and monitoring systems to make sure that they maintain compliance. Whereas breach attack simulation is a tool that you would buy. And that tool is just basically meant to be constantly scanning and trying to penetrate and test your systems to look for network vulnerabilities, system vulnerabilities, etc. So that's the subtle difference between these two. One final little bit of terminology I'll talk here about is compliance checks. Compliance checks are about reviewing the controls that an organization have in place and making sure they meet requirements. And there's a few different reasons why organizations will do this. Let's go back to PCI as an example. When would be a good time for an organization to take a look at their controls and decide, hey, if we're about to be PCI audited, do we have the right controls in place so that we're going to hopefully pass this PCI audit? When's the right time to be doing that? A day before the QSA shows up to audit? No. The ideal time that an organization should be checking to see, hey, do we have the right controls in place so that when we get PCI audited, we're not going to have a whole bunch of major deficiencies. When that check should be done is probably 18 to 24 months before the QSA shows up. And this is where you can use compliance checks. If you have to, if an organization has to be PCI compliant, the organization can say, OK, well, here's the PCI controls, all thousand PCI controls. And now let's look at the controls that we have in place operating within our organization. And you can see, are there any gaps? Are there any controls that, you know, they might either there's no control or there's a control in place, but it doesn't provide sufficient assurance or we do have everything we need. a compliance check is where you're looking for this. And you obviously, like in the case of PCI, where you're going to be audited, you want to do this compliance check probably 18 to 24 months ahead of when the PCI auditor shows up. Because that way, any deficiencies that are identified, you've got a year to six months to remediate those deficiencies, get controls in place, and then you can make sure those controls are operating effectively for a year. Because when the QSA shows up, they're often going to look back on the controls that have been operating over the previous year or so. This is a perfect example of when you'd want to do compliance checks. If you have a compliance requirement, check that you're going to be making sure that you're in compliance with whatever that contractual regulatory framework is before the auditor shows up. Another really good use of compliance checks is just to see if an organization is in compliance with industry best practices. So an organization might have sort of organically grown their own security controls, developed their own security controls. And the organization might say, like, hey, I wonder how we align to sort of an industry best practice, like, say, ISO 27001. So this is where you can do a compliance check as well. You can basically, and this often is always well referred to as a gap assessment. You'll take ISO 27001, you'll take the controls that the organization has in place, and you just look to see if there's any gaps. It's very similar to a compliance check, same sort of idea. So there you go. That's compliance checks.

RAID, redundant array of independent disks. RAID is all about using multiple hard drives in a system to achieve one or both of two different things, greater speed or greater redundancy or both. So that's fundamentally what RAID is all about. Redundant array of independent disks is all about using, instead of a single hard drive, let's use multiple hard drives in a system. And by using multiple hard drives, we can achieve either greater speed, greater redundancy, or both. Now, there's all sorts of different types of RAID, different flavors of RAID out there, but there's fundamentally only three that you need to know about for the exam. RAID 0, RAID 1, and RAID 5. And in just a moment here, I'll show you how they work, but let's just start with some naming, because you need to remember this. So RAID 0 is often referred to as striping. And I'll show you what striping is here in just a second. RAID 1 is known as mirroring. And then RAID 5 uses magical parity protection data. I'll show you what this magical parity protection data is soon. So just for now, remember these names. RAID 0, striping. RAID 1, mirroring. And RAID 5, parity protection. OK, let's start with RAID 0. And let me explain this diagram here, what this is trying to show you. First thing here is we have what's called the RAID controller. This is typically hardware. It can be software, but for much better performance, you want this to be an actual hardware device. And actually, I have one right here. Here's an actual RAID controller. This plugs into a PCI slot, and then there's four SATA connections on the back here, so you can connect four hard drives to it. This is a really cheap RAID controller, but it gives you an idea of what they look like. This is what a hardware RAID controller would look like. This one just died in one of my servers recently, so I had it sitting here. OK, now here's why you need a RAID controller. Because like I said, the whole point of RAID is to split data, to store data on multiple hard drives. The operating system doesn't want to get into that complexity. The operating system basically just wants to say, hey, store this file for me, or hey, I want this file back. doesn't want to get into the complexity of somehow storing data across multiple disks. So that's where the RAID controller comes in. The RAID controller acts as an intermediary between the operating system and the actual hard drives. And now let's start with something important. In RAID 0, you require a minimum, minimum of two hard drives. You can have more, but in order to implement RAID 0, you need a minimum of two hard drives. And now let's look at how it works. So first thing that'll happen here is a file will come in from the operating system. The operating system is saying, hey, store this for me. Now in a RAID 0 array, what the operating system is going to do is what's called striping. Striping is where the RAID controller will split the file into two or more pieces. Since we have two hard drives here, and by the way, that's what disk 0 and disk 1 are here. These are our two hard drives. Since we have two hard drives here, the RAID controller is going to split the file into two equal sized pieces of data. And now the RAID controller can write those two chunks of data simultaneously in parallel to the two different hard drives. What's the benefit of this? Why are we doing this? Speed, performance, right? Because by the RAID controller striping, splitting the file into two pieces and then being able to write those two pieces simultaneously in parallel, we've effectively doubled our read write performance. Add another hard drive, you've tripled your read-write performance. Add another hard drive, you've quadrupled your read-write performance. So remember this. RAID 0 is all about speed, performance. This is so that you can read and write data a lot more quickly. There is, however, a massive downside. What would happen if we lost disk 0 here? And by lost, I mean if it failed and the data on this drive was unrecoverable. So if we lost disk zero, what would the consequence of that be? How much of our data would we lost? Well, technically, you would have lost half the data. But good luck opening a file that you have half of, right? So... Truly, if you lose either one of these hard drives, you've effectively lost all of your data. And this is something important to point out, because I know a lot of people, when they hear RAID, they think, oh, that makes your data more redundant. Not necessarily, right? A RAID 0 array actually makes it much more likely that you're going to lose all your data, right? Two hard drives, it's twice as likely you're going to lose all your data. Add another hard drive, it's three times as likely, right? So just remember this, RAID 0 is all about speed. It gives you a really nice performance boost, but it actually makes it much more likely that you're going to lose all of your data. So you're not going to want to use a RAID 0 array for some really critical data. So RAID 0, all about speed. And as more files come in, those files will be striped and written across these two or more hard drives. OK, so that's RAID 0. Let's now look at RAID 1 mirroring. And as you can probably guess by the name, you can figure out what the RAID controller is going to be doing here. By the way, RAID 1 mirroring requires same setup. You have a RAID controller and a minimum of two hard drives. Again, you can have more, but you require a minimum of two hard drives. so what happens in a raid one array well file comes in from the operating system and as the name implies mirroring what the raid controller is going to do is make two complete copies of the entire file it's going to mirror the file and then it's going to write these two entire copies simultaneously to two different hard drives What was the benefit of this? Well, it didn't really give us any speed improvement, right? Because we're still having to write the entire file to a hard drive. We happen to be doing it two at the same time, but we still have to write the entire file, so... Doesn't give us any speed performance writing, and it doesn't really give us any performance reading. So what's the benefit of this? And it's pretty obvious here, right? If we lose disk 0, no problem, right? We've still got another complete copy of the file here on disk 1. So RAID 1 is not about performance. It's about data redundancy. If you have some super critical data that you just cannot lose, you could have a RAID 1 array with 10 hard drives in it, and you could lose nine of those hard drives, and you'd still have your data. So you can go pretty crazy with this if you really want to, but RAID 1 mirroring is all about data redundancy, not about performance. Now, I kind of lied when I said that you only need to know about three different types of raid. There's actually another one that you need to know about. It's known as raid 10 or raid 1 plus 0. And it's exactly what it sounds like. Raid 10 or raid 1 plus 0 is just raid 1 and raid 0 combined. So RAID 10 requires a minimum of four hard drives. And by the way, the reason I keep emphasizing this is this is a really good exam question. What's the minimum number of hard drives required for RAID 10 or RAID 5 or RAID 1? So I'm going to highlight it here, and then I'll show you a nice summary table at the end. So RAID 10 requires a minimum of four hard drives. And the reason you need a minimum of four hard drives is because RAID 10 is both mirroring and striping. So what the RAID controller will do when a file comes in is the RAID controller will first mirror the file. So now we've got two complete copies of the file and the RAID controller will now stripe these files, split them in half because we've got four hard drives here. We'll split into four chunks. So now we've got four chunks of data that can be written to the four different hard drives simultaneously. Did this give us a performance boost? Yes, right? Because we were able to stripe the file, split it in half, and then write those two halves simultaneously, we've effectively doubled our read write performance. So that's awesome. We get a really nice speed improvement here. And we also have data redundancy. We can lose a hard drive. So if we lose disk 0 here, no big deal, right? We've got an exact copy of that data over here on disk 2. Or if we lose disk 1, no big deal, right? We've got an exact copy of that data over here on disk 3. So a RAID 10 array can suffer the loss of any one hard drive. Any one hard drive. Can it suffer the loss of any two hard drives? No, not any two. You could suffer. Like if it was, if it was, if you were lucky and you lost disc zero and disc one, you'd be fine, right? Because you still have the other half of the file over here on two and three. But what would happen if you lost zero and two? Well, you're toast, right? Because now you've lost both copies of this original chunk of the data. Or if you lost both disk one and disk three, you're toast. You've lost everything. So RAID 10 can only suffer the loss of any one hard drive. And I highlight this because at the very end, we're going to talk about a type of RAID that can suffer the loss of any two hard drives. We'll get there. So just remember, RAID 10 can only suffer the loss of any one hard drive. Okay, now let's talk about the third major type of RAID that you need to know about, which is known as RAID 5. And RAID 5 uses magical parity protection data. And I'll show you why it's magical in just a second here. So you can kind of think of RAID 5 as like the Goldilocks of RAID. It's like the happy medium. It gives you a nice performance boost. It gives you data redundancy. And it doesn't require as many hard drives. That's the big downside with RAID 10. It requires a lot of hard drives, minimum of four. But you can have way more than that in a RAID 10 array. RAID 5 is like the happy medium. It gives you speed, gives you performance, and it doesn't require as many hard drives. You require a minimum of three. So here's how a RAID 5 controller works. Here's what a RAID 5 controller does with the data. So the first thing the RAID controller does is striping. It splits the file in half. But now, here's where the magical parity protection data comes in. The next thing that the RAID controller does is it calculates what's known as some parity data. And the mathematical function that it uses here is known as the exclusive OR function. Oh, and my light board isn't turned on, so you can't see it at all. There we go. Exclusive OR. So the mathematical function that is being used here by the RAID controller to calculate this magical parity data is known as the exclusive OR function. And this parity data is going to be the same size as one of the chunks. So let's say if we take a step back here. If this was a two megabyte file, when we stripe the file, split it in half, we're going to have a one megabyte chunk here. We're going to have another one megabyte chunk here. And the magical parity protection data is going to also be one megabyte. It's going to be the same size as one half of it, like one of the chunks. So what we end up with here is three chunks of data that are all equally sized. Three equal sized chunks of data that we can then write simultaneously to three different hard drives. So first of all, do we get a performance boost? Yes, right? Because we've striped, right? The RAID controller has striped the file. We're able to write this file, these two halves of the file simultaneously to do two different hard drives. Now, it's not quite as fast as, say, like simple RAID 0, because the RAID controller did have to take some time to calculate this parity data. So that does slow things down a little bit, but it's still faster than a single hard drive by itself. So we get some nice performance boost from this. And now, how do we get data redundancy? Well, if we lose the parity data here, who cares, right? We've still got the two original chunks of data. So we obviously don't care about losing the parity data. But here's where the magical parity data protection comes in. This is really cool. So what's going to happen if we lose disk 0 here, right? That's bad. This is one of the original chunks of data. Using the remaining original chunk of data here on disk one and the magical parity protection data here on disk two, we can actually reconstruct, recalculate the original lost chunk that would have been on disk zero. So if this hard drive fails, we replace it with a new empty hard drive. And then this takes a while, but the RAID controller can actually use the remaining original chunk of data on disk one here and the magical parody production data to reconstruct this lost chunk of data. Super cool. And it doesn't just work for rate disk 0. This is why the parity protection data is so magical. What would happen if we lost disk 1 here? Well, we can recover from it. We still have one remaining chunk of original data here on disk 0. And using the magical parity protection data, we can reconstruct the lost chunk on disk 1. So in other words, you can lose either one of the original chunks of data and using the other remaining original chunk and the magical parody production data, you can recalculate the lost chunk of data. So that's why I like to refer to it as magical parody production data, because it's kind of cool. You can lose either one of the original chunks and you can recalculate that lost chunk of data using the remaining original chunk and the magical parity protection data. And this is why RAID 5 gives you redundancy, because you can lose any one of these hard drives and you can still recover. And by the way, this is beyond the scope of the exam, but typically you don't have just one hard drive dedicated to all of your RAID data. It's typically sort of a round robin function, but it varies by RAID type and implementation. But that's RAID 5. And here's where I definitely lie even more about just needing to know three types of RAID. There's actually one more type of RAID that you need to know about, but you actually know exactly how it works. It's known as RAID 6. What RAID 6 is, is basically RAID 5, except instead of calculating one chunk of pair D data, the RAID controller actually calculates two separate chunks of pair D data. And so what RAID 6 requires is a minimum, minimum of four hard drives. Because in a RAID 6 array, you're going to have the original, one half of the original file, the other half of the original file, and then parity chunk, parity chunk. So RAID 6 requires a minimum of four hard drives. But here's the really, really cool thing that it provides. It provides double parity protection, which means... And here's the really key thing to remember for the exam. RAID 6 can handle loss of any two hard drives. You can lose, for instance, both original chunks of data. And you can actually recalculate those two original chunks using the two chunks of parity data. Or you could lose both chunks of parity data. Or you could lose one original and one parity data. You can recover from all these situations. So the critical thing you need to remember about RAID 6... is that it provides double parity protection, which means that a RAID 6 array can suffer the loss of any two original hard drives. Any two. Doesn't matter which ones. RAID 6 can suffer the loss of any two hard drives. Here's a handy dandy little summary here. So key things to remember, RAID 0 does not provide any data redundancy at all. In fact, RAID 0 makes it much more likely that you're gonna lose all of your data. And the critical thing to remember about RAID 6 is it provides double parity. So RAID 6 can suffer the loss of any two hard drives. And then as you can see here, with these giant numbers, we're really highlighting the minimum number of hard drives that you need here. So RAID 0 and RAID 1 each require a minimum of two hard drives. RAID 10 and RAID 6 require a minimum of four hard drives, and RAID 5 requires a minimum of three. So remember those minimum drive numbers. Okay, one more little concept to cover here. This actually doesn't have anything specifically to do with RAID. So CRC, cyclical redundancy checks. This is a way of really quickly checking for accidental changes to data typically implemented at a hardware level. So the reason we're kind of talking about this here is we've just talked about hard drives. They're part of a rate array. CRC checks are often used in hard drives. What will happen is that as a hard drive is writing data to the platter, the hard drive will basically read it back to itself really quickly to make sure that it didn't make any writing errors. Or when it's reading data, it'll basically read the data twice to make sure it didn't make any read errors. So you can use CRC checks on hard drives to quickly check for accidental changes to data. But it's not just on hard drives. CRC checks are often used in RAM. So when you're reading, when a system is reading or writing data to RAM. CRC checks can be done really quickly. Typically, again, implemented at a hardware level to check for accidental changes to data. And CRC checks are even often used in network communications. So when a system is turning ones and zeros into plus or minus five voltages on a copper wire, CRC checks can be used to see if there's any, again, any accidental changes to data when it's being written across the network. Just remember this, CRC's cyclical redundancy checks are a way of really quickly checking for accidental changes to data, a bit flip or something like that.
