Okay, let's now move up to talking about how we can suffer the loss of an entire data center and still be able to recover from that. And that's where we're now gonna talk about different recovery site strategies. What these are all about is what do you do if your entire data center fails? How are you gonna recover from that? And we're going to talk through a bunch of different options here. And the basic difference between all of them is as you're willing to pay more money, as the costs go up, the recovery time goes down, right? So if you're only willing to pay a very tiny bit of money for like, say a cold site, your recovery time is probably going to be weeks. And as you pay more and more and more money, moving up to all the way up to say like a redundant or mere site, your recovery time goes down to seconds, but your costs go way up. So that's the fundamental difference between all of these. And I'm going to explain them all in detail here. But before I get into explaining the difference between a cold site, a hot site, a warm site, et cetera, let me first define these different ways that we're going to evaluate. These are sort of the different capabilities that these recovery sites provide. So let's start with recovery time. Recovery time is the approximate amount of time that it's going to take to recover a certain type of site and put a star in your workbook beside this one, pay attention to this one. You should remember these recovery times. This is a really common exam question might ask you approximately how long does it take to recover a cold site or a warm site? Remember these approximate recovery times. Cost is just relative cost, not very important. Okay, now here's what infrastructure and HVAC means. Infrastructure HVAC basically means just the empty shell of a building. So imagine an empty warehouse with nothing in it. And the only thing you have is some power coming into the building, some utilities, power, network, things like that. And you probably have an HVAC unit up on the roof. But otherwise, it is a completely empty warehouse. That's what infrastructure HVAC means, an empty warehouse with nothing in it. Basic equipment is the cheap equipment. Cheap equipment is things like the actual server racks, not the servers themselves, just the racks that they go in and the power cables and the network cables and things like that. So basic equipment is the cheap equipment. You've put all the racks in place, you've run power cables to the racks, network cables to the racks, et cetera, but you're still missing the expensive equipment, which is the computer hardware. So computer hardware is exactly what it sounds like. It's the expensive equipment. It's the actual physical servers, firewalls, things like that. Computer hardware is the expensive equipment. And data is data and people are people. Probably don't need to define those ones. Okay, now, given those definitions, let's now look at what the different recovery sites provide. And we'll start with a cold site. All a cold site provides is an empty warehouse with nothing in it. Just the basic infrastructure and HVAC. So imagine you need to recover an entire data center and you're starting with just an empty warehouse with absolutely nothing in it. How long is it going to take you to get a data center up and running in there? Weeks. It's going to take you weeks. So remember that. The approximate recovery time for a cold site. Because all it provides is an empty building with nothing in it. The recovery time is weeks. Okay, let's now move up to a warm site. A warm site is the basic shell of a building and the cheap equipment. You've got all the server racks sitting there, you've run all the power cables, et cetera. And the way that most organizations will set this up with a warm site is they'll essentially have a whole shopping list of all the expensive equipment they need sitting almost like in a shopping cart, just ready for them to hit the buy now button. And if an emergency ever does occur and they need to get their warm site up and running, then they'll hit the buy now button. There's actually companies, suppliers that specialize in this. They maintain an inventory of systems. And if a company ever has an emergency and they need to get their warm site up and running, the company will have sort of basically a shopping list all ready to go of like, here we go, here's all the equipment we need. The supplier will overnight send all that equipment, you know, overnight shipping via FedEx or UPS or something to the customer. So yeah, this is gonna cost a lot of money, but what's gonna happen is right, disaster occurs, The company says, ah, we're going to activate our warm site. So they put in a call to their supplier. The supplier overnight ships all the equipment they need. So it's all there the next morning. All the racks and power cables are already all set up. So all the recovery team has to do is basically unbox all the servers, stick them into the racks to connect them to power and network. recover the data and you're back up and running in a matter of days. That's a warm site. Let's now move all the way up to a hot site. And a hot site means that you have all of the equipment. You've got the empty shell of a warehouse. You've got all the basic equipment and you've got all the expensive equipment. So remember that a hot site means that all of the equipment that you need is sitting there ready to go. A hot site provides all the equipment. But this is where we have to kind of, there's actually various different ways that hot sites can be run. And there's huge cost implications involved in this and differences in recovery times. And that's why it's anywhere between seconds and hours. That's a big difference, right? A few seconds to a few hours. Why the difference? Well, it's about whether you're actually staffing and running your hot site 24 by 7 or not. So the cheaper type of hot site is where the hot site has all the equipment sitting there ready to go, but it's all powered down. All the equipment is just sitting there turned off. None of the lights are on. There's no people there. you know, backups are being stored there. And if a disaster ever does occur, the recovery team is gonna have to rush out to the site, power everything on, recover all the data to the systems, and you're gonna be back up and running in a matter of hours in that case. The other extreme of a hotsite is this is a fully operational data center, staffed, powered on, running 24 by 7. So in this option, in this option of the hotsite where it's staffed running 24 by 7, What you're probably doing is some sort of real time replication from a primary site to your hot site of critical data. And so if your primary site ever fails, you can get your hot site up and running in a matter of seconds because it's a fully operational data center staff 24 by seven. You're doing real time data replication. And so if the primary fails, you can get your house site up and running in a matter of seconds. So that's why there's this variability from a few seconds to a few hours. It all depends on whether your hot site is being staffed, run 24 by 7 or not. And that's obviously a huge cost implication from people's time, electricity, etc. Okay, now just to go a little deeper into hot sites, there's actually two different types of hot sites that you need to know about. The first we'll talk about here is a mobile site. A mobile site is a hot site on wheels. Typically what organizations do here is they'll get a shipping container and then they'll stuff a whole bunch of servers inside the shipping container. So the shipping container is the data center basically. And then what makes it a mobile site, of course, is that you can take that shipping container and you can put it on the back of a truck and you can drive it to wherever you need it. A perfect example of this, in the US, there's an organization called FEMA, which I don't remember what it stands for. You can Google it. FEMA is the federal agency that responds to natural disasters. So when Florida or Texas get hit with a massive tornado and there's a bunch of recovery actions that have to occur, There's a bunch of coordination that has to happen, and FEMA will handle that coordination. And the way they'll do that is they have mobile hot sites. They have hot sites on wheels that they can drive to wherever there's a disaster and coordinate the disaster. So that's an example of a mobile hot site, basically just a hot site on wheels. And then, this is the most expensive option. A mere site or a redundant site. That's the type of hot site that I was talking about that's staffed and operating... 24 by seven. A mirror site is specifically what a mirror site is. It's the type of hot site that you're operating staffing 24 by seven. And so a mirror site has all, you know, it's not only warehouse, not only basic equipment, not only all the hardware. So you've got all the hardware you need. you're doing real time data replication and you're staffing this mirror site, this hot site 24 by seven. And so your recovery time is seconds, maybe even near instantaneously for some really critical applications if you're doing like absolute real time data replication. Out of all these options here, the Mir site is obviously by far the most expensive. It's the type of hot site that's staffed, running, operating 24 by 7. Very expensive option. But there's actually one more even expensive option. It's known as multiple processing sites. And I'll talk about them in just a minute here. But there you go. That's the basic differences that you should remember between cold, warm, and hot sites. Remember the approximate recovery times. And just remember mobile and mirror sites are two different examples of hot sites. and I'll get to what multiple processing sites are in just a second. Here's this term that I've introduced earlier and I'll now talk about in a little more detail, geographically remote or geographical disparity. What this is, geographical disparity, it's not any set distance. It's not 10 kilometers or 50 miles or whatever. What it means is that it's far enough away that whatever disaster befalls the primary site isn't going to take out your recovery site or your backups or whatever. So geographically remote or geographical disparity simply means that it's far enough away that whatever disaster, flood, fire, earthquake, power outage, war, whatever, whatever took out your primary site, isn't going to take out your recovery site. And so that's why it's not any set distance. So for instance, I live on Vancouver Island, just over here. Vancouver Island is massive. You can drive many, many, many, many hours north. A long, long way. But what would not be geographically remote would be to have a data center on the southern tip of the island where I am and then a data center on like the northern tip of the island. Like, yeah, that's a huge distance apart. But both data centers would be on the same island. And this is a very geographically active area. At some point, there's going to be a massive earthquake. The whole island is going to sink into the Pacific. Or there's going to be a massive tsunami. And that massive tsunami, massive earthquake is going to take out everything on the island. geographically remote would not be anywhere else on Vancouver Island. It would be on the other side of the Coast Mountains or on the other side of the Rocky Mountains. That would be geographically remote for where I am. So that's why the distance varies. So if you had a data center here, geographically remote might be on the other side of the Rocky Mountains or maybe over on the East Coast or Europe or something like that. That would be geographically remote. And that now leads us to talking about multiple processing sites. Multiple processing sites is where you have multiple geographically remote data centers that are all working together to support a workload. This is even more expensive than mirror sites. Because in mirror sites, you have a primary and then you have your recovery mirror site. In multiple processing sites, you have two, usually three geographically remote data centers that are all working together. why would you wanna do this? Because this is really expensive. Well, it's if the risk of downtime or outage was super high and the cost would be astronomical. So the perfect example of this, and this is organizations that do this, is credit card processing companies. Like imagine, how much would it cost Visa if their credit card processing capability went down on Black Friday. Honestly, I don't know what the numbers are, but it would be a pretty astronomical number, right? It would cost Visa many, many, many millions of dollars per second, right? If their credit card processing capability went down on, say, Black Friday. And so this is where they run multiple processing sites. And the way this typically works, and this is how the credit card companies do it. When you go to pay for something with your credit card, that transaction actually goes through two, usually three different data centers at the exact same time, multiple processing. So the same transaction will go through, say, all three data centers. And all three of those data centers are geographically remote from each other. And the reason that the credit card companies will usually use three data centers is there's another benefit you can get here, and that's data integrity. So if you run the same transaction through three data centers, you can compare the results. And if two data centers get one answer and another one gets a different one, something you want to look at. And of course, if one of those data centers goes down, zero impact, right? Because the same transaction is going through one or maybe even two other data centers. If you had three multiple processing sites, you could lose two entire data centers and you'd still be up and running. So extremely expensive solution. But... it might be cost justified. And for credit card companies, it is. A few more little things that we have to talk about here related to recovery sites. You may come across this terminology on the exam, internal versus external. So you might see an internal hot site or an external hot site or an internal warm site or an external warm site. All this is getting at is who owns the recovery site. So if it's Acme Corporation and they own their own cold site, that would be considered to be an internal cold site. Internal means it's owned and operated by the organization. There's also company service providers that provide recovery sites as a service. Perfect example of one of these companies is called SunGuard. So you can pay SunGuard a certain amount of money every month and they will maintain cold sites or warm sites available such that you know if you ever have a disaster they'll say okay yeah no problem we've got this building over here you can go and you recover your systems there so that would be an external cold site or an external warm site because it's provided by a service provider Another thing related to these recovery sites is what are known as reciprocal agreements. Reciprocal agreements is where essentially two organizations agree that, okay, if one of us goes down, so let's say we've got organizations A and B here, Organization A would say, OK, well, if our data center ever goes down, organization B will say, yeah, no problem. We've got some spare capacity in our data center, so come over here and you can recover your systems in our data center, and vise versa. If B goes down, Company A will say, yeah, no problem, right? We've got some spare capacity over in our data center here. You can come and recover your systems in our data center. So that would be a reciprocal agreement. Two organizations agreeing that if either one has an outage, the other can recover their systems. Reciprocal agreements are extremely rare in the commercial space. Like did you know that Coke and Pepsi have a reciprocal agreement? Of course not, right? They hate each other. It would never happen. So reciprocal agreements really don't happen in the commercial space. Where you much more commonly see reciprocal agreements is in, say, government agencies. So for example, where I live in British Columbia, the Ministry of Education has a reciprocal agreement with the Ministry of Finance, right? These are two government ministries that are both there to serve the people. They have two different data centers. And so if the Ministry of Education ever goes down, the Ministry of Finance says, yeah, you know, you can come and recover some of your systems in our data center or vice versa. that's where you'll tend to see reciprocal agreements is in between say government agencies. Another little bit of information here related to these different recovery sites, resource capacity agreements. Like let's say you have a cold site or a warm site or even a hot site. You wanna make sure ahead of time that your network service provider is gonna be able to provide enough network bandwidth. or the power company is going to be able to provide enough power to actually power up your data center. Or if you've got a fuel company that's going to be coming on site to provide fuel to your recovery site for the backup generators, you want to make sure that they can provide enough fuel in a timely manner. So this is where resource capacity agreements come in. pre-arranged agreements with vendors to ensure that whatever resources you need in the event of a disaster, that service provider, that vendor is going to be able to provide sufficient resources. So that's resource capacity agreements. All right, so here's that question again of which of these is best, which is better, like a cold site or a warm site or a redundant site or a multiple processing sites. And the answer is, as I said before, It depends. There is no best here. It all depends on things like your recovery point objective, how much data the organization and the owner is willing to lose as a measure of time, And the RTO, how quickly do you need to be able to recover things? So if the owner says, you know what? I'm fine with it taking a few weeks to get our data center back up and running. go ahead, use the cheapest option, use a cold site. Whereas if the owner says, I can only, I can't have my data center down more than two seconds, you're gonna have to pay for a way more expensive option, like say a redundant site. Or if the owner says, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, you probably have to use some sort of like mere backup solution or clustering or redundancy. So none of these is best. It depends on whatever best fits the needs of the organization. What's the RPO? What's the RTO? How much is the organization willing to pay? Final little thing I'll cover here is if you ever come across these terms like resiliency, high availability, quality of service, fault tolerance, how do we achieve all these things? Through all the techniques that we've just talked about through hot spares, through RAID 6 arrays, through redundant databases or clustering or multiple processing sites or whatever. So the way that you achieve these things, high availability, quality of service, fault tolerance, et cetera, is using all the different recovery techniques that we've just spent the last good chunk of time talking through here.

So we just walked through all of these different key measurements of time, RPO, RTO, WRT and MTD, right? These ones, we just walked through all of these. Now I can tell you from experience that many people struggle with understanding the difference between these, especially RPO and RTO. Myself included. It's a very subtle difference between these. And I recently came across something that I think really helps solidify, at least for me, so I hope it does for you. It really helps solidify the difference between RPO and RTO. So I wanted to add this in here and show you this. RPO and RTO are like asking these questions. So RPO is like asking, how old can the restored data be? Can it be a few seconds old, a few minutes old, a few hours old, et cetera. So if the business owner says to you, if my system fails, and when you recover my system, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, then would it be, like if your sort of recovery solution was that you were doing a nightly backup every 24 hours, are you going to be able to meet the RPO, right? Because if the owner says, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, you're essentially saying your RPO is two seconds, right? Because the RPO is a measurement of time. So if the owner says, my RPO is two seconds, if you're doing a nightly backup every 24 hours, are you going to be able to meet this RPO No, right? Because you could potentially lose up to 24 hours worth of data if you're only backing up every night. So if the owner says, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, then you're going to have to shift to a much more expensive, probably some sort of mere real-time streaming mirror backup or something like that, or a real-time replication of the data to a secondary database or something like that. So just remember, a really good way of remembering RPO is asking this question, how old can the restored data be? And then RTO is asking this question, how fast does the data need to be restored? Like, am I okay if it takes two hours to restore the data? Or like the owner should say this, right? Is the owner saying, you know what? I'm okay if it takes two hours. Or you know what? I'm okay if it takes two days to restore the data. Again, this is going to drive different recovery solutions. if the owner says i need that data to be restored within one minute you're gonna have to implement probably some sort of like active active solution where you know the redundant you know there's like a redundant system and it comes on you or maybe a good example would be like a hot spare we'll talk did we talk about these yeah we did we talked about hot spares earlier in domain seven So this would require, say, like a hot spare so that if the primary something failed, you had a hot spare there could automatically come online and you could get the system restored, the data back up, whatever, within, say, two seconds. So that's what Archie was asking. How fast does it have to be restored? The other thing I'll say to these is these have huge cost implications. The more stringent these requires are, and by stringent we mean less time. So if the owner says, I need this up in seconds, or I need to only lose seconds worth of data, that's a more stringent requirement, that's a higher requirement, that's gonna cost more money. So if the owner is willing to make their requirement less stringent, if they're able to essentially lower their requirement, if they're able to say, you know what? You can restore my data in 10 hours or three days. They're actually lowering these RPO or RTRO numbers. That's actually going to help to save costs. Because by making the requirement less stringent, you can implement cheaper recovery solutions. If the requirement is more stringent, in other words, less time, then you have to implement much more expensive solutions. Getting owners to basically lower these RPO or RTO numbers can help save a lot of money in the types of recovery solutions that you need to implement. So there we go. I hope this helps you. How old does the restored data need to be? How fast does it need to be restored?

An important part of business continuity management and more specifically creating your BCP and especially your DRP plans is thinking about what are your external dependencies. In other words, As part of your plans on how you're going to recover after some disaster, you have to think about who do you rely upon? Which vendors, suppliers, contractors, etc. What are your external dependencies? And how are you going to make sure that those providers, vendors, whomever, are going to actually do what you need them to do in the event of a disaster? Let me give you a really good example. Let's say part of your disaster recovery plan is that you have, in order to restore the power to your data center, you have backup generators. and those backup generators burn diesel fuel, so you have diesel tanks on site. And one of your external dependencies related to diesel generators and diesel tanks is your fuel provider, the company that comes and delivers more fuel. Because let's say as part of planning for disaster, like maybe your data center is in Texas or Florida where they get hit by hurricanes. If you get hit by some massive hurricane, it's not uncommon that the power might be out for, say, a week. And you need to plan for this, because your fuel tank might only provide 24 hours worth of diesel fuel for your generator. So your external dependency, right? Like if you need to be able to keep your data center running off of your backup generator for seven days, you're gonna need to get seven fuel deliveries, right? One every single day. So this is your external dependency. You're relying upon this fuel supplier to come and actually deliver fuel to fill up your diesel tank to keep your data center running while the power is out after a hurricane is hit. And the reason I brought up this example is that not too many years ago, when Texas got hit by a massive hurricane, There was a number of companies that ran into this exact situation where they were running their facilities off of backup power. They had contracts in place with fuels providers to come and deliver more fuel to keep them up and running. But the fuel providers didn't deliver the fuel. And the reason the fuel providers didn't provide the fuel is because the state came in and said, no, you know, the most important thing for us in this disaster is to keep the hospitals up and running, the police stations up and running, the fire, the emergency services up and running. And so there was basically a declaration that said any of the sort of fuel suppliers, you're going to be doesn't matter what contracts you have in place, you're delivering fuel to these government facility, you know, to these hospitals and things like that to keep them up and running. And so these companies, despite the fact that they had contracts in place, it didn't matter. That fuel had been absconded with and put to better use probably. So I'm aware of one client I worked with in Texas that after this occurred, What they did for any future hurricanes is when they got service contracts in place to get fuel delivered, they actually went out of state. So they went to a fuel provider out of Georgia. And that way, if Texas gets hit by a hurricane, and the state says, nope, you know, all the fuel is going to emergency services. Well, the company could still get their fuel from an out of state provider, right? Because Texas might say, OK, all the fuel is going to these emergency services, but you know, if it's fuel coming from Georgia, that can still be much more expensive trucking it all the way in. But at least the data center will still be running. And so you have to have to think about this. So this is the whole idea of external dependencies. Think about what external dependencies you have for fuel, for water, cooling water, for power, for network connectivity, et cetera. And make sure you've clearly understood what these dependencies are. I mean, here's what you need to do, right? You must assess these external dependencies and have strategies in place to mitigate potential disruptions. Like for example, getting your fuel from out of state. So that's the idea of external dependencies and why it's important to analyze, think about these and put mitigating controls in place.

LESSON 217: Lesson 412

A disaster has occurred and now you're beginning to restore systems. Which systems are you gonna restore first? This is an important question that you're gonna have to contemplate, right? If you're in the midst of a disaster or something cataclysmic has happened, like your whole primary data center has burned to the ground, you're going to have to make decisions as to which systems you restore first, because you cannot restore absolutely everything at the same time. So how are you going to determine what you're going to restore first? In what order? And so the first thing here is you're obviously gonna restore your most critical systems first. And that makes sense, right? Because what is business continuity management focus on overall? The most critical and essential functions. So obviously you're gonna restore your most critical systems first. but it's not quite that simple. We also have to think about dependencies. So what this means here, right? Is that like, let's say our most critical system is the finance system. Well, maybe the finance system requires some backend database. And the only way to log into the finance system is if Active Directory is up and running. And so now in order to restore the finance system, what we first have to bring up is probably the database and part of the network and maybe Active Directory so we can log in. And so this is where dependency charts come in. So yes, you want to restore your most critical system first. But you also have to think about what dependencies do the most critical systems have. And this is where you can start creating what are known as dependency charts or dependency mappings. I'm going to show you a really simple example here. This can obviously get horrendously complicated in a large, complex organization. But here's the basic idea. Let's say our most critical system is Deskcert.com. Well, destert.com, the website, might require that a load balancer be up and running. And that load balancer might require that there is a cluster of web servers up and running. And those web servers require a back end database. This is what a dependency chart or dependency mapping looks like. You know, this needs that, which needs this, which needs that. And so in order to get Deathcert.com up and running, our most critical system, the first thing we might need to get up is our database. Once the database is up, then we can bring up the web servers. Once those are up, then we can bring up the load balancer. And then destruct.com will be up. So this is often used as part of disaster recovery planning to figure out what systems are you going to restore in what order. Your most critical systems first, but what do they depend on? So you know the answer to this question, right? What systems operations should be moved first to the DR site? So if your primary data center fails, what are you gonna move first to your disaster recovery site? Obviously your most critical systems. What about this question though? Let's say that your primary data center has burned to the ground. You've moved all your most critical systems over to your secondary, your recovery site, and you've now rebuilt your primary data center. And now you're starting to migrate systems from the recovery site back to the newly rebuilt primary site. In this situation, what are you going to move first from your recovery site back to your rebuilt primary site? What are you going to move first? And the answer is your least critical systems. because if you've just rebuilt a whole data center there will be bugs there will be issues and you do not want to discover those issues with your most critical systems because you're just going to cause another major disaster so when you start migrating things back to say the rebuilt primary site what you want to migrate first is your least critical things discover the bugs, find the issues with those least critical things, and build back on up to your most critical things.

So these are focused obviously on software development processes. The activities on a lower maturity level are typically easier to execute. and require less formalization than the ones on a higher maturity level so the idea is you can measure your maturity level in each of these 15 security practices so that you ultimately can get to level three of each of these ones to get ultimately better Security practices, as you can see here, have activities grouped into logical flows and divided into two streams. That's what these little stream colors that you see here. represent streams, cover different aspects of a practice, and have their own objectives, aligning and linking the activities in the practice over the different maturity levels. And for each security practice, this model defines three maturity levels as we said each level has a successfully more sophisticated objective with specific activities and more strict success metrics so one of the things that this also emphasizes are metrics that you can provide obviously to management to be able to understand Thank you. how mature you are, not only overall, for example, in your software development, but in each of these security practices that you see here that are typically very important, right? I mean, look, you look at certain activities here that focus on things like secure deployment and testing and management and so on, all important things that you would address security in any sort of important corporate governance activity, including software development and how it relates to obviously these very important business functions that you see up here like governance, design, implementation and so on. So a really good way to relate software development to business activities and how security fits in is what this provides.

LESSON 221: Lesson 416
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

We just went through an overview of the risk management process, which is three major steps, right? Asset valuation, risk analysis, and treatment. And here is where we're gonna talk about asset valuation in just a little bit more detail. The reason this is gonna be such a short video is because asset valuation is a very simple process, conceptually. The whole point of asset valuation, right, is that you wanna come up with a big list of all the assets from across the organization And then you want to rank those assets from which are of greatest value on down to least. And this is really helpful for overall risk management because you want to focus most of your time on the most valuable assets. You're not going to spend two days evaluating the risks associated with a paperclip, right? It'd be kind of pointless. But if there's some really high value system, you want to invest a lot of effort into understanding the risks associated with that system. So when you have a big list of assets from all across your organization, you need to know which assets are of greater or lesser value. And that's the whole point of the asset valuation process, trying to rank the assets so you can rank them from greatest on down to least value. There are two major ways that we can perform this asset valuation, qualitative and quantitative analysis. Now, without question, the absolute most desirable way that we would want to do asset valuation is quantitative analysis, where we assign specifically, where we're assigning a monetary value to each asset. That would, without question, be the best way of doing this, where we could say, okay, our most valuable asset is worth $27 billion, and the next one's $22 billion, and this one's $138,000, and this one's $62,558, et cetera. That would be the best way of ranking all the assets by being able to assign an accurate monetary value to them. However, you're not going to be able to do that the vast majority of the time. Because in any organization, a huge number of the assets are nigh impossible to assign a reasonably accurate value to. Like, what's a company's reputation worth? For certain companies, their reputation is worth an immense amount. But can you put an exact dollar value on it? No. Or you look at some system and you're like, wow, that is a critical system, right? The data in that system is worth a fortune. Can you put an exact dollar value on it? No. And so this is true for the majority of assets in the organization. You are not going to be able to assign a reasonably accurate monetary value to them. So despite the fact that we like to use quantitative analysis everywhere and assign a monetary value to every asset, we just can't. There's some assets that you can do it for, but not that many. And that's why we're forced to use qualitative analysis the vast majority of the time. And fundamentally, first line here says, right, qualitative analysis is a relative ranking system. It's where you come up with some criteria. You know, you can still use numbers here. One, two, three, four, five. Or maybe you use names like this is valuable, super valuable, really super valuable, whatever. You come up with some categorization system and then you put assets into those categories and you basically are just ranking assets relative to each other. You're looking at two assets and you're saying, oh, is this one more valuable than that one? and you rank them accordingly. That's what qualitative analysis is. And this is, you know, you can do this without having the exact monetary value. You can use other criteria, like what if this system was unavailable? What if this data was exposed? What would the impact be to the business if this system was unavailable, et cetera? You can ask these sorts of questions, compare systems, and therefore rank your assets qualitatively as to which of the greatest value on down to the least. So conceptually, a very simple process can take a while. Just one final reminder, we would prefer to do quantitative everywhere. We're forced to use qualitative most of the time. Thank you.

Okay, so we've already talked about a whole bunch of different risk management frameworks, things like NIST 800-37, etc. Let's now talk about a few major security control frameworks that you need to know about. That's the major topic here of Section 3.3. We're going to talk through a bunch of different security control frameworks that you need to know about. So high level, what do security control frameworks provide? I mean, it goes back to who creates these frameworks? Experts. Frameworks are generally created by experts in an industry. And so if you want to think about how can you better protect your systems, often a really good way to do that is to go and look at a framework. Like if someone said, okay, you're now in charge of security at some company, a bad thing to do would probably be to take out a piece of paper and just try and make up your own entire security program. If you've never done it before, that's probably not going to go very well. A much better way of doing this would be to say, OK, well, other people have figured this. They've solved this problem. They've created security programs. Let's learn from what they did. And those experts have often compiled their collective wisdom in frameworks. So you could use something like the ISO 27001 framework or NIST 800-53. to help you figure out how to create security in your organization. So what do frameworks provide? Comprehensive guidance. They do a really good job of showing you, here's what you need to put in place to allow you to achieve your organizational goals and objectives. It's worth mentioning another major reason that security control frameworks are often of importance to an organization is you may have a contractual or regulatory requirement to be in compliance with one of these. One we're gonna talk about in just a second here is PCI, the Payment Card Industry DSS standard. If a company is processing credit cards from Visa, MasterCard, et cetera, they're gonna have to be PCI compliant. If you're in critical infrastructure, you might have to be NIRCSIP compliant. If you're in the healthcare industry, you might have to be HIPAA compliant, et cetera. So the point is these frameworks provide a really good job at sort of guiding, and they're also often a contractual or regulatory requirement to be in compliance with these things. So that's why companies care so much about security control frameworks. They're useful and they have to be in compliance. Okay, so let's start going through these frameworks. And before I start getting into any of the specifics of the frameworks, one more high-level thing here. You do not need to be an expert on any of these frameworks. You don't need to know any of them in any sort of detail. Really what you need to know is what do these different frameworks focus on? What are they particularly useful for? Because different frameworks are focused on different things and they're useful for different reasons. So you might get questions on the exam asking you, you know, where would you use COBIT versus FedRAMP or HIPAA? So what I'm going to do is just give you a high level overview, really high level of what each of these frameworks focus on. Let's start with COBIT. Do you happen to recall which organization is behind the creation of the COBIT framework? It's ISACA. And if you've heard of ISACA, ISACA is an organization made up of IT auditors, primarily IT auditors. So COBIT is a framework created by IT auditors. So what is COBIT particularly useful for? IT audit assurance work, because it's been written by IT auditors. The controls in COBIT are written in such a way that they're very easy to test. They're very easy to audit against. And so, in fact, one of the really common usages of COBIT controls is if there is some sort of, say, third-party audit being conducted where a service provider might be having their controls tested by an independent auditor, so the independent auditor could create a SOC 2 Type 2 report. We'll talk about those later. if the auditor was creating this SOC 2 Type 2 report to give to the service provider. So the service provider could then give that report out to their customers. Often the controls that are used in those audits are COBIT controls. Because they're written in such a way, you know, the access management controls, the backup controls, the network security controls. They're written in such a way that they're very easy to audit against. So that's one usage of COBIT. ITIL, if you've ever come across this framework, it does a really good job of defining all the major processes that you'd expect to have in place in a well-run security function. So basically anything with the word process in it. So your onboarding process, your access management process, your change management process, configuration management process, your backup and recovery processes. ITIL does a good job of defining all of these major processes. NIST, specifically NIST 800-53, which is the Cybersecurity Control Framework. It's a very useful framework because it does a good job of defining a broad set of security and privacy controls that an organization should have in place. And NIST is a US government entity. So NIST is primarily used by US government agencies. And a lot of US government agencies will have a requirement to show that they're in compliance with NIST 800-53, that they have the right controls in place to be compliant with it. PCI, I mentioned this in the intro, the payment card industry DSS standard. All you really need to know here, right? If a company is processing credit cards, they need to be PCI compliant. As a quick aside, have you ever had the great pleasure of being involved in a PCI audit? It's a complete nightmare of a framework. If you've never had the pleasure of being involved in a PCI audit, there's over 1,000 controls in the framework. There is zero concept of risk. So you spend an enormous amount of time testing controls that make no sense at all for a particular organization and not enough time testing controls that are important. So it's a very challenging framework to test against. I'm clearly not a big fan of it, but there you go. I mean, it's a good idea that the credit card companies have put this in place. The framework is just not super well defined or well created. All right. Enough ranting about PCI. HIPAA. The Health Information Privacy and Accountability Act, I think is what it stands for. By the way, you don't have to memorize all these acronyms for the exam. If HIPAA is ever used on the exam, or any of these acronyms are used on the exam, they'll be spelled out. So what is HIPAA used for? Well, again, this is another. In fact, all of the bottom ones here, they're all US specific frameworks. HIPAA is specific to the US. Any organization that is collecting, processing, using, that works in the healthcare industry and they're dealing with obviously sensitive patient data, they have to be HIPAA compliant. So HIPAA is a framework about protecting, safeguarding medical information. And of course, that medical information is stored on computer systems. So HIPAA contains quite a few security controls to ensure that healthcare data is appropriately protected. SOX is a really interesting one to have in this list, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. It is in no way, shape, or form a security control framework. In fact, if you know about the origins of SOX, It came about as a direct result of the wild fraud being committed by a particular organization as part of the dot-com boom. If you were around at that time and paying attention, the company I'm talking about is Enron. There was a few other companies, but Enron was the poster child of this. So if you haven't heard about this before, Enron, in collusion with their auditors, Arthur Anderson, were committing vast amounts of fraud. And this was discovered and Enron was sued into oblivion. It was a whole mess. And what was particularly irking for investors and shareholders and so forth was that The way the laws were back then, you could sue Enron, the corporate entity, but you could not sue, you could not make the senior executives, the CEO, the CFO, the people that were actually committing the fraud, you couldn't make them personally liable because they could hide behind the corporate firewall. So you could sue Enron, which went bankrupt, but you couldn't make these individuals accountable, really. And that, frankly, pissed a lot of people off. So the U.S. Congress acted. They enacted the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. And I'm way oversimplifying this here, but... Basically, one of the provisions of SOX requires that the senior executives of publicly traded companies in the US, it requires these senior executives, like the CEO, the CFO, to sign off on the financial results that are being produced. And if it is found, that those senior executives willfully, intentionally were committing fraud. Then, and here's the key piece, they could be personally liable. And this was a tectonic shift in the way that liability worked in the US. And this obviously freaked a lot of senior executives out. They're like, wait, by signing off on this, if there's like problems in this and, you know, if I'm found out that I was committing fraud, I can be personally liable. You can take away all my millions of or billions or whatever I'm being paid. That freaked a lot of them out. And That's actually why we're talking about it here, because Sarbanes-Oxley has really helped to promote the tone from the top that we want, where we want senior executives invested in security. We want them to care about security. And Sox really helped about that because of course, senior executives realize the numbers that they have to sign off on, those numbers are coming out of computer systems. So those computer systems better have confidentiality, integrity, etc. And so SOC's not a security control framework, but it has helped to promote this tone from the top to ensure that senior executives are actually invested in having good security controls within their organization. So it's kind of an interesting one. FedRAMP. This one's easy to remember. Any US federal government agency, Fed, any US federal government agency that wants to use cloud services, those cloud services have to be FedRAMP compliant. So FedRAMP is a security control framework specific to cloud security controls. And it's actually even a whole certification framework as well attached to it. But The simple thing you have to remember for the exam is that FedRAMP is a security control framework specific to cloud security controls. And any US federal government agency that wants to use cloud services, those cloud services have to be FedRAMP compliant. And then the final one here, FISMA. This is, again, another U.S. government agency standard. And it's a requirement that certain U.S. government agencies are in compliance with FSMA. It's another security control framework. And it's actually a requirement for some U.S. government agencies. You don't need to know which ones. But it's a requirement for some U.S. government agencies to be compliant with FSMA. So it's just another security control framework. OK, so that's the ones that you need to know just a tiny bit about. There is one security control framework that you need to know a bit more about, and that is ISO 27001. And the reason you need to know more about ISO is that if you were to survey the planet asking, and trying to figure out what is the most commonly used security control framework, I think you'd find the answer is this, ISO 27001. Less so here in Canada and in the US. It's still used very commonly, but not quite as commonly. Europe, other parts of the world, it's much more pervasively used. So that's why we're gonna talk about it in a bit more detail because it is such a commonly used security control framework. So first of all, here is the official definition. ISO 27001 provides best practice recommendations for an ISMS. What the heck is an ISMS? An Information Security Management System. What is that? It's basically this, it's a security function. So what ISO 27001 provides is the best practice recommendations for a security department, a security function. So if you were to think about it, what would you expect to have in place in a well-run security function? Well, it would start with having the right governance in place. to ensure that the security function was aligned with overall corporate goals and objectives. So making sure the security function had the right policies, procedures, et cetera. You'd then, run security function, then you then want to make sure that there's a good onboarding process, a good access management process, a good asset management process, configuration and change control processes, network security controls. backup and recovery controls, cryptographic controls, a compliance function, et cetera. So if you were to step back and think about what would a well-run security function look like, that's what ISO 27001 provides. That's what an ISMS is. An information security management system is a security function. There's a companion document to ISO 27001 that you need to know about, which is ISO 27002. So here's the relationship. ISO 27001 is a much shorter document because it basically just covers the controls. ISO 27002 is a much longer document, because what ISO 27002 provides is all of the implementation guidance for the controls in 27001. Let's say you look at a control in 27001, and you're like, how would I actually implement multi-factor authentication? You could look at 27002 and you would find that there's a ton of detail in there about here's exactly how you would do that. So 27002 provides a bunch of detailed implementation guidance for how to implement the controls in 27001. And that leads to a good exam question. Which of these could an organization be certified against? And the answer is, The reason being, an auditor can come in and audit an organization against a control framework, against a set of controls. You can't be audited against a guidance document. And actually, pay attention here. This is actually very common. Again, not so much here in Canada and the US, but certainly in Europe and other parts of the world. It's very common for service providers to be ISO 27001 audited and certified. And service providers use that as a means of demonstrating that they have good security controls in place. If a customer is going to a service provider and saying, hey, Do you have good security controls? The service provider can hand them their ISO 27001 certification and say, yeah, we do. We passed. We've been ISO 27001 certified. That indicates that we have good security controls in place. So remember that. You can be certified. An organization can be certified against ISO 27001. One more little tiny tidbit here related to ISO 27001. If you were to ask the question, where in the ISO 27001 document are the controls actually listed out? The answer is in Annex A, Appendix A. So if you were to look in the ISO 27001 document, where you would find all of the, there's like, these are the 14 domains and there's about 140 odd controls. If you were to look where they actually list it out, it's in Annex A. By the way, I would not suggest you need to memorize all 14 domains here, and you certainly do not need to memorize the 140-odd controls, but... Just remember, ISO 27001, one of the most commonly used security control frameworks in the world, defines the best practice recommendations. And if you look in the control document and ask where are the controls, they're listed in an annex A. One final little thing I'm going to cover here. This is not on the exam, but I think this is just useful information to think about. If you look at all these different security control frameworks, and of course, I don't have all of them listed here. If I were to list out more, you'd see even more overlapping. The point I'm trying to show you with this little diagram here is that all of these different security control frameworks, they're not mutually exclusive. It's not like ISO 27001 just covers this stuff, and then COBIT just covers completely different controls over here. That's not at all the case. These different security control frameworks have lots of overlap between them. And the reason I mention this is that a lot of especially larger organizations, they often have to be in compliance with multiple different frameworks. And those frameworks are going to overlap. And this can create quite the compliance burden. If an organization were to sort of be audited externally or even do their internal audits against all of these, like if they were basically to audit every single framework separately, like go through all the controls that they needed to be in compliance with ISO, and then all the controls they needed to be in compliance with COVID, and all the controls they needed to be in ITIL, they'd end up retesting the same stuff over and over again. Like access management is a perfect example. All of these control frameworks cover access management. And so if you have to be in compliance with like four or five different frameworks, they're all going to look at access management controls. And so to significantly reduce the audit burden, A good practice is what's often referred to as control rationalization. This is just a fancy way of saying, look at the different control frameworks you have to be in compliance with and identify the overlapping controls. And the whole sort of Holy grail that you want to aim for here is this whole idea of test once report many. So in other words, test your access management controls just a single time. Go through, test all these different controls once, and then use the results of that testing for your ISO compliance, for your PCI compliance, for your HIPAA compliance, whatever it might be. That's the ultimate goal. I'm not going to suggest it's easy to get there. A lot of people do this as a full time job. I used to do a lot of this back when I worked at Deloitte. We did a lot of consulting around this. It's not easy to do this, but it is worth figuring out taking the time to do this because it can significantly reduce a company's compliance burden how much time they're getting spending getting tested and audited every year so just wanted to point that final little thing out there okay there we go that's security control frameworks

OK, I wanted to add one little video here to give you a good example of a question on what we just talked about, the whole reference monitor concept, where the whole idea of a reference monitor concept, it's all about a subject wanting to access an object. And if we want to have security, we can't just let any subject access any object and do whatever they want. We have to have some form of mediation in between them. And that mediation has to be based on a set of rules. And we need to log and monitor all of this. That's the reference monitor concept. And of course, the implementation of the RMC is always known as a security kernel. And here's the three properties. And all this is contained within the TCB, along with many other things. We just covered this, right? Security kernel, RMC, TCB. You might get questions on these and they're all very similar. LESSON 224: Lesson 419

OK, I just gave you a fairly high level overview of virtual machines and hypervisors. I want to go into a little more detail on these things. I want to use my light board here and draw some stuff for you. because there's some terminology that I'm gonna highlight here that's important to know for the exam. So, right, the whole idea here of like, this is all based high level on the concept of abstraction. Abstraction is the idea of hiding the underlying complexity. An example of abstraction is virtualization. Virtualization is where you're creating a virtual version of something. And a perfect example of that is hypervisors and virtual machines. So let's go through that. I'm going to cover some of the stuff I covered in the previous video, but I'll do a longer-windowed explanation here. Let's start at the hardware level. So this is going to be our computer system. This is going to be the hardware of our computer system here. So this will be like the CPU and RAM and the network interface card and a bunch of other stuff as well. But we've got the hardware. And in a typical computer system, the major piece of software that will then be running on that underlying hardware is, of course, the operating system. And let's say we have the Windows operating system installed here. So we'll have Windows. And then on top of Windows, you can then run processes, programs, applications, like, say, I don't know, Zoom. So this is a very basic generic view of a computer system. You've got your underlying hardware, you've got your operating system, and then you've got your application running on top of it here. Now let's say for some reason, you know what? We don't want to just run Zoom here. We also want to be able to run this program called Sketch. If you've never come across Sketch, it's a user interface design tool. and rather problematically, Sketch does not run on Windows. It only runs on Mac OS. So what we might wanna do here, if we wanna be able to run Sketch, we're gonna need to also install Mac OS on our computer system here. And now the question is, is this going to work? If we try and install Windows, one operating system, and Mac OS, another operating system, on the same underlying computer system, and then we power that computer system on, Are both of these operating systems going to happily run side by side? Will they both happily run the way I've drawn this? And the answer is unequivocally, no. Why not? Well, because one of the major jobs of an operating system is to have total and complete control of all the underlying hardware. And so if you have two operating systems that are both trying to have total and complete control of all the underlying hardware, this will never work. These two operating systems will just completely conflict with each other. The system would never boot up. It would never work. You cannot do this. And yet we do this all the time. We have multiple operating systems running on one underlying physical server or computer system all the time. How? The answer is we have to create a layer of abstraction, a layer of virtualization between the operating systems and the underlying hardware. So what we do here, is we add an additional piece of software. And that's important to highlight here, right? This is a layer of software. We're not adding any new hardware here. So what we're gonna do is we're gonna add an additional piece of software here. And on top of this piece of software, We're then going to run our two operating systems. So here's we're going to have Windows and we're going to have Mac OS here. And then of course, on top of Windows, we can run our Zoom application. And then on top of Mac OS, we can run Sketch, like that. By introducing this additional layer of software here, we can now run these two operating systems simultaneously. So what magical piece of software allows us to do this? It's called the hypervisor. So the hypervisor is a piece of software that allows us to run multiple operating systems simultaneously. How? Well, the way I like to describe this rather comically is the hypervisor is essentially a giant liar. Because what's the hypervisor saying to Windows? It's saying, hey, Windows, you're the greatest operating system ever. You're in total and complete control of all the underlying hardware. And so Windows is like, yeah. I'm the greatest operating system ever. I'm in total and complete control. And so Windows happily runs because it thinks it's in total and complete control of all the underlying hardware, but it's not. What's actually happening here is the hypervisor is, the technical term here is it's performing emulation. Emulation is creating a virtual version of, say, a piece of hardware. So what the hypervisor is doing is it's emulating the CPU. It's emulating RAM. It's emulating the network interface card. In other words, it's creating a virtual CPU. virtual RAM, virtual NIC. And so what Windows is actually controlling here is not the underlying hardware. Instead, what Windows is controlling is the virtual CPU. and the virtual RAM, and then virtual network interface card. But Windows doesn't know that. For all Windows is concerned, it thinks it's controlling real hardware, but it's not. It's actually just controlling virtual hardware that's being emulated by the hypervisor. And so that's why Windows happily runs, because it thinks it's in total and complete control of all the underlying hardware, but it's not. And of course, it's the same story for macOS, right? The hypervisor is lying to macOS as well. It's like the hypervisor saying, hey, macOS, you're the greatest operating system ever. You're in total and complete control. And so macOS is like, yeah. But of course, what's happening here again is that Mac OS is not controlling the true underlying hardware. It's just controlling virtual versions of it. So this allows us to run multiple operating systems simultaneously. So now let's get into a ton of terminology here. So When you have an operating system and some applications running on top of a hypervisor, you call this here, this piece right here, you call this a virtual machine. Some other names for VMs that you might come across. Another common name for a virtual machine is an instance. or sometimes also referred to as a guest. So just remember that. Two commonly used synonymous terms for a virtual machine is instance or guest. So VM, instance, guest, these terms are all pervasively used in the industry to refer to this, right? An operating system with some applications running on top of it, running atop a hypervisor. And of course, how many virtual machines do we have here? We got VM1 and then this here is VM2. Virtual machine number two. So we have two virtual machines running here. An interesting question to contemplate is, to what degree can this hypervisor see what these virtual machines are up to? To what degree can this hypervisor see what they're doing? And the answer is, the hypervisor essentially has total and complete visibility into whatever these VMs are doing. Because think about it, right? Anytime this virtual machine sends any command to the CPU, any command to RAM, any data to RAM, any data across the network, any data to the hard drive, when this virtual machine does anything All of that is going through the virtual hardware that the hypervisor is emulating. So the hypervisor has basically total and complete visibility into what these VMs are doing. Here's why I'm pointing this out. Another less commonly used name for a hypervisor is actually a VM... The reason VM monitors, because if you think about it, to what degree can this hypervisor see what these VMs are doing? completely the hypervisor is able to monitor what the vms are up to so i point this out because this terminology is sometimes on the exam and it's not as commonly used in the industry so just remember another name for the hypervisor is VM monitor, okay? More terminology. Each virtual machine, right? Which is an operating system with some applications running on top of it. Each virtual machine is typically going to expect to have its own one or more of its own hard drives. So attached to each virtual machine is typically going to be one or more virtual hard drives. Here's another bit of terminology you might come across. In this context, another name for a virtual hard drive is also a volume. That term gets used for other things in other places. But in this context related to virtual machines, The term volume refers to a virtual hard drive. So remember that. Another name for a virtual hard drive is a volume. Okay. or terminology. Let's say for some reason, let's say I wanna create 10 identical virtual machines. Like let's say I wanna create a cluster of 10 Apache web servers. The inefficient way of doing this would be to create each one manually. So I'd create a new virtual machine. I'd install some flavor of Linux. I'd install the Apache web server. I'd update everything, set up the right user access controls. configure it properly, blah, blah, blah. It's going to take me a good chunk of time, a few hours probably, to do this, to set up one virtual machine. Now, the inefficient way of doing this would just be to do the same thing over and over again. If we want 10 virtual machines, you could just do that process nine more times. That's going to take you probably a few days, or at least a few hours. So that is not an efficient way of doing this. This is where images come in. An image is essentially a, listen carefully here, an image is essentially a pre-built ready to be deployed virtual machine. So the idea here is you take the time to essentially create the perfect virtual machine. You set up the operating system with all the right patches and configure it correctly. You install the Apache web server. You set all of that up correctly. You set up all the right user credentials, whatever you need to do. to properly configure, lock down, harden, and create the perfect ready to be deployed virtual machine. That's what an image is. Companies will typically store these images in repositories. And by the way, when you have an image that's like the perfect image ready to be deployed, you'll often hear the term like that's your golden image. The golden image is like the perfect one that's ready to be put into production. So you'll have a repository storing a bunch of these different golden images. And now, if I wanted to create 10 identical virtual machines, all I have to do is I have to take the time to create this image. So that's still going to take a bit of time. But now that I have this image ready, It's incredibly easy to stand up 10 virtual machines. I basically just copy this image 10 times, which creates 10 virtual machines, power them all on, and boom. Once I've got the image made, within a few seconds, I can have a whole cluster of identical virtual machines running on, say, a bunch of different hypervisors across my environment. And companies will maintain repositories of these different images. So if someone ever says, oh, I need a web server, or I need this type of server, there you go. Just pull the image out of the repository. You can deploy it in seconds. So that's an image, a pre-built, ready to be deployed virtual machine. One final bit of terminology here. This is going back to, again, what visibility does the hypervisor have in the VM? Total visibility. So one of the cool things that the hypervisor can do is create what's called a snapshot. When I think snapshot, I always think Polaroid cameras. If you ever played with a Polaroid camera, right? You take a picture of something, instantly the film would roll out. You just have to kind of like shake it for a few seconds and then boom, you'd have a picture. It was like an instant picture of something. That's what a snapshot is. It's like a Polaroid picture. A snapshot is, listen carefully here, a snapshot is a moment in time capture of a VM. And snapshots are highly configurable. And what you can do is you can configure a snapshot to capture every bit of data related to a virtual machine at a moment in time. Any of the data that's in the virtual CPU's cache and registers, any of the data that's in the virtual RAM, any of the data that's on the virtual volumes, hard drives, literally any bit of data related to a virtual machine, a snapshot can be configured to capture all of that at a moment in time. So a snapshot is a moment in time capture of a running virtual machine. Snapshots can be super useful for a bunch of different reasons. One useful, one use of a snapshot is let's say you're about to patch this VM. You of course should have done a bunch of testing ahead of time and you should be as sure as possible that when you patch this VM, you're not going to break anything, but you know, stuff still happens. So what you could do is right before you patch this VM, you could take a snapshot and store that snapshot. You can then patch the VM. And if you've discovered like, uh-oh, the patch has broken this VM, what you can do is basically just restore that snapshot on the hypervisor, press play again, and that virtual machine will resume from right where it was when you took the snapshot. So it can be useful for patching. Another really useful, really great use of snapshots is in forensic investigations. If you discover this VM has been compromised, you can take a snapshot of the VM, which is a moment in time capture, right? potentially everything related to the VM. As long as the snapshot is taken at the right time, it can be extremely useful for forensic purposes. So that leads to one more question that you might see on the exam. Let's say you wanted to perform a forensic investigation of this virtual machine. What would be most useful to get your hands on? The image, the snapshot, or the volume? And the answer is the snapshot, because the snapshot captures everything related to this virtual machine at a moment in time, including, as long as it's configured correctly, including everything in the volume. So the snapshot would be the most useful thing to get your hands on if you wanted to perform a forensic analysis. Let's say the snapshot wasn't available though. What would be the next most useful thing? The image or the volume? And the answer is the volume. Why? Well, the volume, I mean, think about, this is not quite correct, but think about it this way. The volume is like the hard, like let's say this is a physical server. The volume is the hard drive. And of course, the hard drive contains all the data from the running system. And it's not quite correct, but you could kind of think of the image as being like the Windows install CD, right? Like the Windows installation CD It's not totally useless for forensics purposes, but pretty close, right? Because an image is not going to tell you anything about an actual running system. Because an image is a pre-built, ready to be deployed system. So the only thing the image would tell you is, let's say this VM got hacked, you might look at your image and go, oh, OK, this image has this vulnerability in it. So that's probably how this VM got hacked. But you wouldn't know for sure. A volume, that's the hard drive from the VM, it would contain log file data, all sorts of other useful stuff that you could look at for forensic purposes. So if you had to choose between image and volume, For forensic purposes, volume is way more useful. Snapshot is the most useful. OK, there you go. That's some deeper terminology about hypervisors and VMs. We're going to cover them again just briefly when we talk about cloud computing. And I'll go into a little more detail on the different types of hypervisors. But I thought this would be useful just to go into a little more detail of some of the terminology here around virtual machines.

Okay, I just talked about hardening at a high level. I want to go into just a bit more detail about what hardening is all about and why it's so useful and how we use it and et cetera, so forth. So as I mentioned, fundamentally, hardening is all about locking down a system. Because when you go and buy some new application, some new server, operating system, whatever, firewall, let's pick on a firewall. When you go and buy a new firewall, does it come from the manufacturer all locked down, hardened, and super secure? No. Why not? Well, because when you get some brand new application or hardware, you need to be able to log into it for the first time. So there has to be a default username and password. And on the firewall, a bunch of the ports have to be open so you can remotely log into it. And it has to allow any IP address to log into it. When you buy something from a manufacturer, in almost all cases, it's going to come from the manufacturer in a very insecure state. And this is, of course, where hardening comes in. Before you put anything into production, you need to go through a process to lock that thing down, harden it before you put it into production. So that's what I want to talk about in just a little bit more detail here. Hardening is a very simple process. Hardening is just where you essentially go through a checklist of things that you need to do to lock something down to harden it. This list is obviously going to vary depending on what it is, whether it's a Cisco firewall, or a Palo Alto firewall, or the Windows operating system, or the Unix operating system, or whatever. The list is going to vary, but it's going to be stuff like, you know, change the default username and password, install the most recent patches, close these ports, disable that, etc. So fundamentally, hardening is just following a checklist and doing all the things on the checklist to lock something down. where do you get this checklist well one possible source is from the manufacturer themselves so windows microsoft for instance develops hardening and provides hardening guides for their windows operating systems cisco provides hardening guides for a bunch of their different network devices so sometimes you can get a hardening guide from the manufacturer themselves There's also a big open source repository. The CIS, the Center for Internet Security, maintains a big open source database of hardening guides. So you might be able to get one of these things from a manufacturer. You might be able to get it from an open source repository like CIS. Or you might just have to create one from scratch. No matter where you get a hardening guide from, especially if you get it from CIS or the manufacturer, you're going to have to tailor the hardening guide to your exact environment and exactly how you're using that server, application, whatever. Because obviously the way that you harden a system that's being used, like if you're using a web server in this environment with this data in it versus a web server here with this data in it, they're going to need different rules. They're going to need different controls in place to lock them down for different usages of them. So you're going to have to create a hardening guide for each type of system, each business use case for a system. And then, like I said, hardening is actually a very simple process. When you acquire something new, you just, as part of hardening it, you just go through that checklist. Disable the unnecessary services, enable a host-based firewall, whatever. You typically want two people involved in this process. You want the system administrator, the system administrator or the person that's setting the sync thing up, they'll go through and they'll harden it. And maybe they've even created some scripts to automate this. You know, people, system administrators, we're fallible, so are scripts. There might be a mistake in them. So you typically want a second person involved. So after the system administrator has hardened the system, you then usually want a second person, often from the compliance function, that will double check to make sure, okay, were all the steps followed? Has this system been properly hardened? before it goes into production. So that's why I usually want two people involved. The person actually doing the hardening, and then often someone from the compliance function just doing a final check to say, did we get everything? Or did we miss something? You can then put now this hardened, locked down device, you can now put it into production. But you're not done with hardening once something's in production. You need to do ongoing monitoring to make sure the system remains hardened for a couple of major reasons. One, you might update your hardening guide. Over time, you might realize, oh, we need to install a new patch, or we need to change this setting, or we need to add a new service account, or whatever. or disable some service account. So over time, your hardening guide will change, will evolve. And so obviously, any new systems you're going to deploy, you want to make sure that they're deployed according to your updated hardening guide. But you've also got to monitor and make sure that any of your systems that are already in production are appropriately updated to maintain compliance with the hardening guide. So that's one reason to do monitoring, to make sure that if you update your hardening guide, you update any of your systems in production. Another reason is that you want to monitor to make sure a system remains in compliance with the hardening guide is that a change might be made to a system in production that takes it out of compliance with the hardening guide. And you want to detect that. Was it one of your own system administrators that did this? Or was it maybe an attacker installing some sort of backdoor that opened up a port on the system? So by doing this ongoing monitoring to make sure a system remains in compliance, it might help you detect if something untoward has happened with your system. So that's why you need to do ongoing monitoring. to make sure that systems that are in production are appropriately hardened. And by the way, all of this fits within overall configuration management. Because of course, what is configuration management? Making sure the systems are properly configured before they go into production and then remain appropriately configured while they're in production. So hardening very much fits within overall configuration management.
