LESSON 226: Lesson 421
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Code signing. So we just talked about digital signatures. Code signing is simply the use of digital signatures to verify some code, to make sure you know who some code came from, and to make sure that that code wasn't changed in transit. The perfect example I always like to think about is, let's say that my iPhone has told me that there is a software update available from Apple. If I'm about to install a software update on my iPhone, I'd really like to make sure that that update actually came from Apple and that that software update wasn't modified in transit. to make, you know, someone included some backdoor or something like that or vulnerability in the software. So how could I check that? How could I make sure that this code actually came from Apple and no one modified in transit? And the answer is code signing. And all code signing is, is basically creating a digital signature for some code. So here's exactly how your iPhone does it, or your Android phone does exactly this. You've actually probably noticed your phone doing this. If your phone says, hey, there's an update available, and you say, install. or download and install, or whatever the button says. You may have noticed it takes a few minutes to download, and then once it's downloaded, it doesn't install right away. For a few minutes, it actually usually takes a little while, it says, Verifying. If you ever wondered what exactly is your phone doing when it's verifying, the answer is it's looking at the digital signature that's attached to the software update. So here's how this works. When Apple creates a new software update, they will generate a digital signature for that software update. Remember how you create a digital signature is the sender hashes the data that they want to send. So they would hash the code, the software update. The sender would then encrypt that hash value with the sender's private key. and that would create a digital signature. So Apple would hash their software update and then they would encrypt that hash value with their Apple's private key. And so the software update you download comes with a digital signature attached to it. And what your phone's doing when it's verifying The first thing your phone is doing is it's decrypting the digital signature attached to the software update. And if you have an iPhone, it's gonna be decrypting that digital signature with Apple's public key now if the digital signature decrypts with Apple's public key who is the only organization in the world that should have created that digital signature Apple with their private key. So this first step here, right, the fact that the digital signature decrypts with Apple's public key, that proves authenticity. You now at least know, OK, this digital signature has to have come from Apple because they're the only ones that could have created it with their private key. Okay, now, once your phone has decrypted the digital signature, contained within that digital signature is gonna be the hash value that Apple created. And now the final step that your phone is gonna do is your phone is going to hash the software update that it received, generating a new hash value. And your phone is then gonna compare the hash value that it just generated from the hash value that was contained within Apple's digital signature. And if those hash values match, that proves integrity, it proves that your phone has received exactly the same software that Apple sent. So this is code signing. It's simply the use of digital signatures in signing code. So you can verify the integrity and the authenticity of some code. So that's the whole idea of code signing. It's just the process of creating a digital signature, right? You take some code, you hash that code, which of course produces a fixed length message digest, a hash value. And then you encrypt the sender, in this case, Apple. Apple would encrypt that hash value with Apple's, the sender's, private key. And that's created a digital signature, which you attach to the code. So Apple does this. Android does this. Microsoft does this. So it's used all the time in software updates. There's other uses for this as well. Let's say you're a company with a bunch of software developers and you've got software developers uploading code to a central repository, like, say, a GitHub repository. If some new code is being added to the repository, would you like to make sure that code actually came from one of your developers and that code wasn't modified in transit? You can do that with digital signatures. You can sign the code. So your developer, before they submit their code to the repository, your developer can, on their system, and their development, their IDE, their development environment can do this for them. When they go to upload some code, the development environment will generate a new digital signature for that code that's being uploaded. And then that way, say, GitHub can say, OK, yeah, did this code actually come from a known developer, one of your company's developers? And was this code modified in transit? Does it have integrity? So this is the idea of code signing. It's simply the application of digital signatures to signing code to verify the integrity and authenticity of code. So that's code signing. And here's the definition.

LESSON 227: Lesson 422
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Okay, let's talk about dictionary attacks and then rainbow tables and then how we defeat rainbow tables with salts and peppers and so forth. But let's start here. Let's start with dictionary attacks. The whole idea of a dictionary attack is you want to try and find someone's password as efficiently as possible. The simplest, not the most efficient, but the simplest way of finding someone's password is just a simple brute force attack. You just try letter A and then B and then C and D, et cetera, et cetera. You just keep going through the entire character set until you find someone's password. But as long as they have a sufficiently long password, that's gonna take a very long time. Now what attackers realized long ago is that if you know, for instance, that someone speaks English, the probability of their password being an English word is super high. And so this is where the term dictionary attack came from. What attackers did many, many years ago is they would figure out, okay, what language does someone speak? And if they found out that they spoke Spanish, they would take a Spanish dictionary and then they would just iterate through all the words in the Spanish dictionary. And you were much more likely to find the user's password very quickly there. And of course, attackers did the usual substitutions. Like, I don't know about you, but to make my password more secure, I replace all the E's with like threes. I replace the O's with zeros, you know, leet speak, right? We all do this. We probably see eyes with like ones or something or whatever. We all do this to make our passwords more secure, right? Attackers are aware of this. So you do that in a dictionary attack as well. So that's where the name came from. The attackers used to literally use dictionaries to try and figure out someone's password. Not anymore though. What a dictionary attack looks like nowadays is over the last few decades, literally billions with a B, billions of users' credentials have been exposed, billions of usernames and passwords. And so nowadays, what attackers do when they're doing a dictionary attack is they have huge databases, files available to them, of the most common passwords in the world. Because what's happened, in all these billions of credentials that are being exposed, people have compiled lists of the most common passwords in the world. And so if you're an attacker, you get this list of the most common passwords in the world. And then if you want to try and figure out someone's password, just iterate through that list until you find their password. And you have a much higher chance of finding their password by iterating through that list of the most common passwords in the world. So that's what a modern dictionary attack looks like. attackers have compiled these lists of the most common passwords in the world, and then they just iterate through the list, right? Start with 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and then password. And they just go down through the list of the most common passwords in the world. You're much more likely to find someone's password. So that's what a modern dictionary attack looks like. Okay, now let's get to talking about rainbow tables. So let's take a couple steps back here to explain all this. Imagine this, I am creating a new application and I need my users to be able to log into my application. So I'm gonna have to create a database of usernames and passwords. So let's go ahead and do that. So this will be my user table here. I'm gonna have for each user, I'm gonna have their name in here And I'm also going to store their password. So let's put their password in here too. I'm going to add an entry for myself in here. So my username is Rob. And being the imminent security professional than I am. Imminent? Imminent? I don't even know what the word shit is. I'm clearly not that. Being the security professional that I am, I'm going to use this super secure password. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. So there we go. There's my super secure password that I've stored the database. And of course, you're going to have a bunch of other users in here with a bunch more passwords. First question, is this a good way to store users' passwords in plain text? Of course, the answer is no. You never want to do this. You never want to store your users' passwords in plain text. Because if any attacker ever breaks into this database, they instantly have all of your users' passwords. Or if you have a malicious insider, and they're trolling around through some database they're not supposed to be in. Suddenly, they have every user's password. So you never, ever, ever want to store your user's passwords in plain text. So how should you store users' passwords? Well, there's a couple of things you could do, right? One thing you could do is you could encrypt the user's password. So you could take 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and you could run it through, I don't know, the AES algorithm with 256-bit key, and then just store the ciphertext. That would certainly be more secure, but it's not considered to be the best way of storing users' passwords. And here's why. Encryption is... two way, right? If you're just storing ciphertext here, if the attacker ever got their hands on the encryption key, they can easily decrypt all of your user's passwords. So it's not best practice to store the user's passwords as ciphertext. Instead, we want to use something that's one way, that only works in one direction, hashing. So the secure way of storing users' passwords is to not store the password itself, but the hash of the password. So let's say that we run my super secure password, one, two, three, four, five, six, through the MD5 hashing algorithm. And the hash value of that is, I don't know, XR75. It's obviously not gonna be that, but let's just pretend. So what we've done is we've taken my password, one, two, three, four, five, six, we've run it through the MD5 hashing algorithm, and now we're storing that hash value in the database, my hashed password. And this is way better, right? Because hashing is one way. Even if an attacker breaks into our password database here, all they're gonna have is a bunch of hash values, and they're gonna be like, ah! My dastardly plan has been foiled. I can't figure out the user's passwords because they're all hashed. So this seems brilliant. And this is how a ton of systems store users' credentials as hash values. They're passwords. They store them hashed. A ton of systems do this. Here's the problem. As we just discussed with dictionary attacks, what attackers have their hands on nowadays is giant lists of the most common passwords in the world. So they'll have a giant data set that will list out a bunch of passwords. And as I said, the most common password in the world is 123456. And then the past, you know, this data set is just gonna contain a whole bunch more, you know, depending on how large the dataset is, hundreds of thousands, millions, hundreds of millions, billions more passwords. Okay, so the attackers have these lists of the most common passwords in the world. Here's what they've done. They have taken these most common passwords and they've run them through the most common hashing algorithms, like say SHA-1 and say MD-5 and others. So they've taken 123456 and run it through SHA-1, and maybe that's Q79L. And they've run it through MD5. And maybe that's XR75, et cetera, et cetera. And they'll just keep doing this for all the various passwords. Now, technically what I've drawn here is actually called a hash table, but for purposes of the CISSP exam, you can call this a rainbow table. It's technically not a rainbow table, they're a little more complicated, but for purposes of this high-level management level exam, you can call this a rainbow table. Now you can probably spot why this rainbow table here might be kind of useful for the attacker, right? So they break into a company's password database and they go, ah, drat, the user's passwords are all hashed. Then I say, OK, well, no problem. I've got a giant rainbow table. I'm going to take this hash value, and I'm going to do a lookup in my rainbow table. And by the way, this could be a database, and these could be indexed fields. So you're doing a query, you're doing a search on an index field in a database. to look for this hash value. And aha, within milliseconds, the attacker said, aha, we've got a matching hash value, XR75. And they know right away the user's password, 123456. So this is a major issue. By the way, these rainbow tables are extremely easy to get your hands on. They're on any torrent site, all that sort of stuff. I've got lots of these on my computer system. They're not hard to get your hands on. So any reasonably skilled attacker is going to have a pretty good rainbow table. And if your users are using crappy passwords like 123456, guaranteed that crappy password, the hash value of that crappy password is going to be in a rainbow table. And this is a massive problem because now, near instantaneously, the attacker has the user's password, 123456. So we need to solve for this problem. How do we as the defenders defeat rainbow tables? Because this is a major issue. The way that we do this is with what's called salting. So here's what a salt is. A salt, quite literally, is just a random number, a big random number. That's all a salt is. It's just a big random number. So here's what we're gonna do. For each user in our user database here, we're going to create a big random number for them. So for me, my big random number might be 0 2 m K or something like that. It's going to be a big random number, big random value. And you create a different random value, a different salt value for each user in the database. So that's all salt is. It's just a random number. And it's a unique random number for each user in the database. Now, here's where the salt comes in. When you're storing the user's password in the password database, you're not just taking the path. Like, cause remember what my password was, was 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. And we talked about, right? If you hash 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 with say MD5, it's going to be XR 75, which is going to be in every rainbow table that's ever been created. So how do we solve for this? Even if the user has a garbage password, like one, two, three, four, five, six. Well, what we do is before we store the, before we hash the user's password, we first look up and we say, okay, this is Rob and Rob salt value is zero two M. Okay, so we append this nice, long, random number to the user's password, and then we hash all of this. So we run this through a hashing algorithm. And when we run this through a hashing algorithm, what it might end up being is, I don't know, I'm terrible at randomness. RWAK4, something like that. Whatever it is. But the very important point here is it's a different hash value. It's no longer XR75. And the reason my crappy password no longer hashes to XR75 is because we've appended this really nice, big, long, random value onto the end of my password. And we end up with this new hash value. Now, the very important question is, is this new hash value, RWAK4, is that likely to be in a rainbow table? And the answer is no. Provided that this salt value here is a nice big random value, it essentially means that it's extraordinarily unlikely that... this hash value is going to be a rainbow table. These rainbow tables cannot cover the entire key space of every hash, hashing algorithm, right? They're huge. So rainbow tables are not, you know, cannot, They can't cover every single possible hash value. So as long as you've appended on a nice, big, long, random number, and you're using a good hashing algorithm, not like MB5, but you're using like SHA-512 or 256, there's basically zero chance of this hash value being in a rainbow table. Here's the takeaway. The way that we should be storing users password is we should be storing a salted hashed password because salting defeats rainbow tables. A couple of things I want to point out here. Salts are typically stored in plain text. You don't actually care if the attacker can figure out a user salt value. Why? Well, even if an attacker can figure out a user salt value, what it means is they would have to generate basically a brand new rainbow table for each individual user, right? Because each individual user is going to have their own salt value. So the attacker would have to take the salt value, append it to a password, and regenerate an entire rainbow table. These rainbow tables can take an enormous, like months, maybe even years of computational power to generate. And so if you force an attacker to have to generate an entire new rainbow table for every single user in the database, it completely defeats rainbow tables. The attackers just straight back to brute forcing. So that's why you don't actually care if the attacker gets the salt value because it forces them to generate a brand new rainbow table or just basically it forces them back to brute forcing for each individual user. There's another thing I'll point out here. You may have heard of a pepper. Here's the difference. Salts are more secure because a salt is a unique value for each individual user. Whereas a pepper is more like a system setting. where there'd be a pepper, I don't know, 0007212 or something like that. That might be the pepper. And that pepper will be applied to every single user's password before hashing it. So like a pepper is more like a system setting and it's the same pepper for every user. And that's obviously why peppers are a bit less secure. Salts are more secure because salts are unique to each individual user. There's one more little tiny thing I'll add in here. Not on the exam, but just an interesting bit of trivia. Often, the algorithm that's used for the hashing here is an algorithm called bcrypt. If you've never come across a bcrypt before, it is a hashing algorithm that is intentionally, intentionally super inefficient. Like, orders of magnitude less efficient than SHA-1 or MD5. Why would you intentionally want to use a super intentionally super inefficient hacking algorithm? Well, because if you're just hashing a single user's password and storing that in the database, even if your hashing algorithm is like two or three orders of magnitude less efficient, that's still not that much computational power. It doesn't have a huge impact on authenticating users and stuff like that. It's not going to really slow your system down when you're just authenticating a few users. But now if you're the attacker and you need to generate a rainbow table using bcrypt, which is, and bcrypt by the way is configurable. You can configure essentially how inefficient, how many orders of magnitude less efficient bcrypt is. If it's like, if you've configured it to be like 10 orders of magnitude less efficient, That means it's going to take 10 orders of magnitude more effort to generate your rainbow table. These things already take a ton of computational power to produce. If you've made it 10 orders of magnitude more difficult to generate this rainbow table, you've basically made it impossible for the attacker to do that. That's why you might actually want to use an extremely inefficient hashing algorithm here, like bcrypt. So that's kind of a cool idea. So there you go. That is rainbow tables. And if you wait for me here to erase my eight feet of glass here, I'll just show you a couple of quick slides. It'll summarize all of this. It takes a while to erase all this glass. All right, there we go. Good enough. Okay, so what's a rainbow table? Technically what I showed you is a hash table, right? But it's a pre-computed table of hash values for the most common. You take the most common passwords in the world. By the way, here's what a real rainbow table, real hash table looks like. You take the most common passwords in the world and then you run them through the most common hashing algorithms. And these are the real hash values, by the way, for MD5, for SHA1. These are the real hash values for these passwords. And a rainbow table, of course, would be hundreds of millions of passwords, et cetera. and we have to defeat this. So how do we defeat this? Salting. Salting is just a nice long random value. So let's take the password 123456, right? The most common password in the world. The MD5 of that is F447B2A0, et cetera. This hash value is going to be in every rainbow table that's ever been created. And that's a problem. So if your user is using a garbage password like 123456, it's going to be very easy for an attacker to figure out that password using a rainbow table. So how do we defeat this? Well, we add a really nice, big, long, random number onto the password. Like here, we've added on A8FF250, et cetera. We've added on this salt value. And now we hash. And we end up, of course, with a totally different hash value, BA0B05CF, et cetera. is this hash value likely to be in a rainbow table? No. So that's how hashing defeats rainbow tables.

Okay, let's continue our discussion of some of the controls that we can use in physical security. And we're going to continue on here next by talking about CCTV, closed circuit television, camera systems. Now, we used to go into a bunch of detail on different transmission media and camera quality, things like that, but I don't think you'll be tested on that anymore. What you might be tested on, and a good thing that you should know about, is what type of control is a camera system? Is it a detective control? Is it a deterrent control? Is it a sort of an audit control? What type of control is a camera system? The answer is all of those, right? Camera provides all those capabilities. Camera systems are absolutely good deterrents. Cameras are obviously good detective control. They even, to a sense, can kind of provide like an audit capability. You can see who came and went when. So cameras are all of those things. So here's a couple of things I would keep in mind for the exam. Number one. You might be given a question that asks you, which of the following types of controls is a camera system primarily? Deterrent, detective, corrective, et cetera. If you're forced to choose one thing above all else, the answer is detective. Above all else, camera systems are primarily a detective control. So remember that, above all else, camera systems are primarily detective controls. But like many controls, they can serve multiple purposes. So camera systems are absolutely a good deterrent. In fact, you've all seen this, right? You walk into a sketchy gas station corner store or something, right? And there's often like a big camera, right? When you walk in with a screen that says, you know, you are being recorded. They want you to know that you're on camera. They want you to know that you're being recorded because they're trying to deter you from stealing stuff. So cameras are absolutely a good deterrent, and they're often used for that exact purpose. They're put up in super visual places to deter people from committing crimes, doing things that they shouldn't do. They also are quite useful from an audit perspective. Like if you want to sort of see who came and went when. Maybe someone went into your store with an empty backpack and then they came out with a big bulging backpack. They probably stole your stuff. So they can be used sort of for audit purposes as well. So the reason I'm pointing this out is that you might see another type of question which asks you, you know, which are the following types of controls as a camera system. And you're given a number of choices. In that case, remember that cameras are absolutely a good deterrent. They're also a good detective control. They're also a good audit type control. So that's camera systems. Let's now talk about these passive infrared devices. And I gotta be careful how I say infrared. It's actually infrared with three syllables. And I know that because I recorded a mind map video and I put it up on YouTube and some kind soul on YouTube took a large portion of their day to write me like a huge explanation on why infrared is actually three syllables they included the latin derivation everything else so i don't want to piss that person off on youtube so Infrared, passive infrared devices. So what are passive infrared devices? They're motion sensors. We've all seen these. You walk into an office building, there's this little kind of boxy thing in the corner with this little kind of like Perspex lens on it. And when you move around, the little LED light turns on. passive infrared devices are motion sensors that's what we use them for sensing motion how do they sense motion well we are warm-blooded mammals and because we're warm-blooded mammals typically you know for a room like i'm in right now which is not that warm I'm radiating infrared light. The heat from my skin is radiating outward. I'm radiating infrared light. Now we obviously can't see that with our eyes, but... passive infrared devices are basically really low resolution cameras. And they're taking a picture of the room every few milliseconds. And then they're just comparing frame to frame. And if you're moving through the room, then the infrared light signature in the room is going to change from frame to frame. And that's how a passive infrared device can detect motion. It's taking a picture, basically a really low resolution picture of the infrared light in a room. And if we as warm-blooded mammals or other things are moving around, the infrared light signature in the room is going to change. So that's how they detect motion. Which leads to one other important point about these things. They need to be able to recalibrate themselves as the ambient air temperature changes. And the perfect example I always think of this is John and I were teaching a class in Phoenix, Arizona many years ago. i remember it was in the summertime and if you've ever been to phoenix arizona in the summertime i think it's about the same temperature as the surface of the sun it's insanely hot and i remember we were at this client's campus and we were taking a lunch break and we were on this outdoor patio and there was actually a little passive infrared device out on the lunch patio and i remember looking at it thinking Huh. Because normally, how would one of these things sense us moving around in the room? Well, they would actually be looking for an increase in the amount of infrared light in a room, right? Because our bodies are giving off heat. But in Phoenix, Arizona in the summertime, the ambient air temperature is hotter than your skin temperature. And so what that passive infrared device was looking for on that outdoor patio was if I walked by it, it wasn't actually looking for an increase in the amount of infrared light, it was actually looking for a decrease. of me walking by so the point is these things this kind of an extreme example but these things need to be able to recalibrate themselves as the ambient air temperature changes because the um you know the infrared light signature in the room is going to be changing so just remember that they need to recalibrate themselves as the ambient air temperature changes Okay. Lighting. Lighting is actually a really good physical security control for a few reasons. Lighting is really important for the safety of people. If you have like an employee parking lot and people are leaving their offices after dark and going out into the employee parking lot, If you don't have a well-lit parking lot, there's a much higher chance that someone's going to fall and hurt themselves. Also, lighting is a very good deterrent. It's well proven that a well-lit area is much less susceptible to crime. So again, this is good for the safety of people. If you have a well-lit parking lot, there's much less of a chance that one of your employees is going to get mugged or something. Lighting is really good for the safety of people. It's also a really good deterrent, as I mentioned. If you have a well-lit area all around your building, it's way less likely that someone's going to come up and vandalize something or try and steal something. So lighting is a good deterrent. It also, to a degree, helps with detection. Like, yes, there are camera systems that will work with infrared light. They can work at nighttime. But if you've ever looked at sort of an infrared view from your camera system, the image is always a lot more grainy, you don't have any colour, it's all black and white. It's a lot harder to see what's going on. If you have a well-lit area, your cameras can stay in sort of daytime mode, and they can be recording higher resolution, colour images, things like that. And if you have guards that are making their rounds and things, they can much more easily see if someone's doing something they're not supposed to. So lighting even helps from a detection perspective. Makes it so you can get better resolution, better images from your cameras, and your guards and things like that can see better. So that's actually why lighting is a really important physical security control. Good for the safety of people. Good deterrent. Helps with detection. He'll talk about card readers and badges. There's a subtle difference between these that you need to know. Typically conflate these two. Here's why. I'll show you what the definitions are. So let's start with a badge. A badge is just a name and a photo printed on a card. There's no electronics involved here. It's just a name and a photo printed on a card. And the way that a badge is meant to work is if you're trying to go into a secure building, you would show your badge to the guard. The guard would look at the picture on the badge, look at you, and try and decide are you the same person or not, and decide whether they're going to let you in. So a badge is just a name and a picture printed on a card. A card reader is an actual electronic system. It might be an old school swipe card, so it might be like a magnetic swipe card that you have to slide through your lock, or it could be a more modern wireless type card, could even be a smart card where you just tap, you know, just put the card near the reader and it'll let you through. The point is the card reader is some sort of electronic system where there's a unique number on the card that's being read or there's a smart chip on the card that's being challenged and responded to. and that's what's letting you through. So just remember card readers are actual electronic systems. Now the reason that we tend to conflate badges and card readers is because a lot of you probably have a single card that is both a card reader and A badge. Back when I worked at Deloitte and with lots of clients that we had, they would give me a single card. That card would be the electronic card. And of course, what would be printed on that electronic card? My name and my picture. So it was both a card reader and a badge. Okay, let's talk about doors. Doors are obviously extremely important from a physical security perspective. If you wanna control who can get where in your building, you need doors. So here's a few things you have to think about related to doors. First thing is what's the door made out of? All right, if you want a super secure door, you're not just gonna build it out of balsa wood. You're gonna build, you know, this is why like bank doors, vault doors, right? They're not built out of wood. They're built out of like steel or something, right? The example I always think of when I think about this is, have you watched The Shining? So Shining is this great movie with Jack Nicholson in it. And there's this great scene where Jack Nicholson is outside the door. And then he like smashes through this door in a matter of seconds. And he's like, here's Johnny. It's a super creepy scene. So I was thinking about that scene. If you don't want Jack Nicholson smashing his way into your nightmares, into your home, you want a door that's securely made. You want a door securely constructed, right? big steel door or something like that so obviously what the door is made of has a huge impact on the security of the door same with the door frame right there's no point in having a super secure steel door inside of a rotten wooden frame so you have to think about what the door frame is made of Last important thing to think about is where are the hinges located? I'll give you a perfect example of this. Just recently, we have a little ski condo that we go up to every weekend, my wife and our two daughters. And just a couple of weekends ago, we went up to our ski condo and we have a little locker inside of our ski condo where we keep all of our ski clothes and our helmets and our goggles and all this other stuff. And in order to go skiing, we absolutely need to get into this little locked closet, this little owner's closet. Well, when we went up there, we realized we just put new locks on the owner's locker and we realized that we'd left the key back at our house, which is like a four hour drive away. And so we were feeling pretty bummed about this, right? We've just driven four hours to get up there Friday night. It was snowing, so the next morning there was going to be beautiful snow and it was like, oh man, you know, like I didn't want to drive eight hours through the middle of the night to go and get the key. So we're like, what are we gonna do? And I looked at the door and I realized, aha, like this is just a cheap, normal residential door. And the door swung outwards towards where I was standing. And so that meant the hinges were on the outside. and you're probably, if you've got a nefarious nature, you know where I'm going here. If the door swings out there and you can see the hinges, a lot of doors, you can very easily take the door right off the frame just by removing the pins from the hinge. So I did that. Luckily I had a screwdriver. I just used a screwdriver and just banged the pins out took two pins out of the hinges, just took the entire door off the frame, didn't matter that it was locked, and we were able to get into our owner's locker and we were able to ski. So, got to think about this, right? If you want to build a more secure facility, you've got to think about where the hinges are. You can get more secure hinges where the pin is actually like welded in place, you can get even more secure hinges where they are like built into the door frame itself and there are certain types of doors that have locking mechanisms and that even if you pop the pins out the door is still not going to come out of the frame so depending on how secure of a facility you need to make there's many many increasing levels of more secure hinges and more secure ways of doing this but the point is you want to build a very secure facility you've got to think about this stuff what's the door made out of what's the door frame made out of where are the hinges located etc Last little piece I'm gonna cover here related to doors is man traps, which by the way, super sexist term. We shouldn't be calling these man traps, right? Women can use these things. They should be called like people traps, right? So as an industry, we should be calling these people traps, not man traps. Okay, but for the exam, you do need to know the term man trap. So what is a man trap? A man trap is a type of door that's specifically designed to prevent the social engineering attack known as tailgating or piggybacking. So remember, what is tailgating or piggybacking? This is where an unauthorized person follows an authorized person through the door, right? This happens all the time. And the reason this is a social engineering attack, right? It relies on human kindness, right? If you see, if you sense someone is, if you're like, if you're walking, if I'm the employee, And I have the badge, not the badge, the card reader to get through the door. If I sense that someone is behind me, my natural instinct when I'm walking up to a door and unlocking the door is to hold the door open for the person that's behind me and say, you know, please, please, after you. And this is why tailgating and piggybacking works because... Out of human kindness, we often want to hold the door open for someone. Well, you may not want that. You might want to prevent that from happening in a really secure facility. And this is where man traps come in. Man traps are specifically designed to prevent tailgating or piggybacking. There's two major styles of man traps. One is what's shown here, which is a turn style. This is often like a glass cylinder. And the way this works is you walk up to the big glass cylinder, you unlock it in some way, usually using your employee badge or something, and the glass cylinder will sort of rotate sufficiently to allow you into the cylinder. And now you're standing inside a glass cylinder. And it's not very big. So you're going to notice if someone else is in there with you. The glass cylinder rotates, so you're now locked inside the glass cylinder. Then you usually have to go through a second factor of authentication, maybe like a fingerprint scanner or an iris scanner or something like that. And as long as you are authenticated through that second factor, the glass cylinder will rotate the rest of the way to let you into the secure facility. And often these types of turnstiles, they'll have pressure sensors on the floor so they can detect if two people are standing inside because they're measuring the weight. Although you do have to be careful with these. These are often banned in places like Europe where there's actually privacy controls and you're not supposed to monitor your employees weight. So that's their personal information. So you have to be careful about that. But yeah, that's one style of man trap where it's a turnstile type system. Turnstiles don't work though in say like a data standard where you need to be bringing carts of equipment in. You can't get them through that little glass cylinder. And this is where you'll encounter the second type of man trap where there's a set of two doors and a little room in between them. So the way this works is you'll authenticate to the first door. The first door will open allowing you to get into this little tiny room. The trap. And the way this works is you cannot open the second door until the first door closes behind you. So now you're standing in a little room and you're going to notice that someone else is in that room with you. And often security guards will have a camera facing into that room and they'll make sure if someone's going through the man trap that only one person is going through at a time. So first door opens, you get into the man trap. First door has to close behind you. You then usually perform a second factor of authentication to open the next door and then you can proceed on. And the whole idea here, right, is you're getting trapped in this little room. First door has to close before you can open the second door and you're gonna notice that someone else is in there with you. So that's a set of, you know, sort of double doors. And the advantage of these is that little room is big enough to bring a cart of equipment and new servers or whatever. So that's the style of door that you'll typically see in data centers. You'll often see both. You'll see the turnstile man trap for letting employees in and out quickly. And you'll see the double doors to bring equipment in and out of the data center. So there you go. That is doors. Bye.

Privacy impact assessments. PIAs, privacy impact assessments, the whole point of these is to basically do a little bit of an assessment at the start of a new project, the implementation of a new system, to ask some questions to ask what sort of data is being collected here is any of this data personal data and if some of it is personal data where's the data being stored who's using the data who's the data being shared with how long is it being retained for You know, what different systems is this being transferred into? What laws are applicable here? Is this European Union citizen data? Is this US healthcare data and therefore HIPAA applies? So the whole point of the PIA is to essentially ask these questions. And so that's what PIAs are. Privacy Impact Assessments are a process for identifying and understanding what personal data is or is not being collected so that you can then understand the risks that might be associated with that. and then therefore identify what would be the mitigating controls to mitigate those risks. So PIAs are a very good practice and a lot of organizations conduct these. And it very much kind of falls under this idea of baking privacy in. It's just like security. We're gonna talk about this a lot throughout the course, but when you're thinking about like, when should security controls be considered and built into the system? It's not five minutes before the system goes live, right? Security should be baked into the system from the start. So should privacy. Privacy should be baked into a system from the start. And that starts with doing a PIA. So right at the very beginning of the project, asking these questions. What data is this system storing? What data is going to be used in this project? and seeing if any of it's personal data. And if it is, then you have to understand the risks associated with it and how you're going to mitigate those risks. So that's the purpose of PIAs, privacy impact assessments. And this next slide here, don't memorize this for the exam, but this gives you a good idea of the typical elements that are involved in a PIA. This is going to vary by organization, but Typically, these are the types of things you're doing. Data flow analysis, so you're understanding what data is being brought in, what systems is being transferred to, where is it being stored. Once you understand what the data is, you can see what legal implications there might be, right? If this is European Union citizen data, GDPR. If it's US health care data, HIPAA. If it's Canadian citizens' personal data, PIPEDA or PIPEDA. So different laws are going to apply depending on what personal data is being collected in what jurisdiction, how it's being used, et cetera. Which is going to lead to where you're going to want to think about what are the risks? What if this data was exposed? What if it's used inappropriately? All these sorts of things. Once you've identified the risks, you can then think about what could you do to mitigate those risks? Like maybe we just shouldn't collect this data. Or maybe we should... encrypt it with a 256-bit key, or maybe we should delete it after only 30 days. You can think about what strategies you can put in place to try and mitigate the risks. Definitely wanna document all of this, document the data flow diagrams, what legal compliance had been identified, et cetera. And as the system or the project goes through its implementation, you wanna keep up with it and make sure that the mitigating strategies, controls that have been identified have actually been implemented correctly. They're doing what they're supposed to be doing. And you want to make sure the system is being put into production correctly. And then you should do ongoing monitoring to review because things can change, right? New laws can come into effect. California not too long ago implemented a new privacy law very much modeled after GDPR. That has a big impact from a privacy perspective. You may need to update essentially your PIA for certain systems as privacy laws change, things like that. Maybe you start sharing data with a new stakeholder, he might need to kind of go back to the PIA and review things. So these are typically the steps involved in a PIA process.

Let's spend a few minutes here talking about different backup storage strategies. Backup storage strategies is really about where organizations determine where the recovery data is going to be stored, how many copies they're going to keep, so that if something bad does happen, they're able to actually recover their data. A good example of this that a lot of organizations will use, which you don't need to memorize this, but this is sort of a rule that a lot of organizations will use. It's called the 3-2-1 rule, right? Three copies of critical files, two backups on different media. So different storage media in case there's obviously an issue with your tape drive system or something. And one backup stored offsite. This is not a universal rule. This is going to vary for organization, how critical their data is. And it's also going to depend on the data. You're not going to treat all data the same across the organization. This is where your data classification comes in, determining how valuable the data is, which is then going to drive what your backup and storage strategies are for how many copies you need to keep of critical data on how many different types of media and how many different geographically remote locations. Another aspect of data storage strategy locations is the actual location where you're storing the data. So let's go through a couple of options here. So onsite, on premise, this is where the organization is storing the backup data locally. And this has the advantage of being able to quickly recover the data onto, say, the primary system. But it has the major downside with on-site storage is that if your primary data center burns to the ground, your primary system burns to the ground, and your backups are only on-site backups, you're going to lose everything. So you typically do not want to have only on-site backups. You might use on-site backups if you need to recover some data, like if you've got a database and you need to recover it quickly to a secondary database that's on-site, you might keep an on-site copy for sort of quick recovery of data. But you don't typically want to just keep your storage copy on-site. You typically want to copy also of the backup data offsite. And there's a term that I'll introduce here and I'll talk about more later as well, and that's geographically remote. So when we're talking about offsite backups, what you typically want is you want your offsite backup to be geographically remote. And as I'll talk about more later, geographically remote is not any set distance. It's just far enough away that whatever disaster has befallen your primary site or system, it doesn't take out your offsite backup. So that's a really good idea with offsite backups, have them geographically remote at somewhere far enough away that if your primary site is taken out, it's not going to take out your backups. And what a lot of organizations are moving towards nowadays is using the cloud as their backup, because this can be a very cost-effective solution rather than having to have like a separate physical location with tape libraries and people there, you know, switching out tapes, you know, at night to be able to handle the different backups, et cetera, or have really expensive automated tape libraries. you could just back up your data in the cloud. And with the major cloud providers providing all sorts of different availability zones around the planet, you can store your data at geographically remote locations around the world and even use different major cloud service providers in case one service provider even goes down entirely. There's a bunch of advantages of cloud backups. It's highly scalable, so you can store a little bit or a ton of data very easily, can provide very high availability, and it's pay as you go. So if you, for some reason, don't need to be backing up some data for a while, you're not going to be paying anything. If you do need to store a bunch of backups for a while, you're only going to pay for what you use. It's one of the reasons a lot of organizations are moving towards using the cloud as their backup storage location.

Let's talk about some innovative ways that the industry has come up with to promote the perfect development of software that also includes security as part of that, right? We've been talking at length how security needs to be involved in the process of software development. right from the start and throughout, and certainly throughout the decades that we have been producing code and writing applications and programs, for example, we've come up with innovative ways to try and speed up the applications development process and yet still produce quality code. Of course, what we've been emphasizing forever is that security needs to be part of that process to begin with. There are some newer platforms and methodologies that obviously allow that to happen. In fact, in some cases, force security to be involved so that as you know, we've been saying this many times too, the best type of security is always what you design into something based on requirements. That is not something that can be done after the fact. It needs to be done as part of the entire process itself. All right, so let's look at this. And of course, if you look at the exam outline, it'll specifically mention these words here, integrated product team. And if you think about each of these words, it tells you exactly what this is all about, right? Because the traditional way of creating applications and architectures has always been, for example, to understand the requirements and then we have development teams that build whatever it is that we need. And then once we've built what we think what we wanted in the first place, we put it into production and we start using that system or application. At that point, development is basically finished. Then the operations people take over because we're operating in production, for example. Then of course, quality assurance people. get involved in both of these from the start and throughout. And years ago, somebody realized that, wait a minute, we don't need to separate these processes because if we involve everyone, from the start and throughout then we can all collaborate better we can better align the product to goals and objectives of the organization itself and so there's value obviously in involving operations as part of the development and development as part of the operations And so we look at initiatives like this, not as a phased approach to building something, but collaborating and involving everyone right from the start and throughout. has advantages where we can communicate better we can achieve better collaboration between all team members and of course we can better align with goals and objectives of the organization itself now even though know the exam outline and the industry refers to this as integrated product teams you may know it as this right a lot of people call it devops although i think those two words don't do justice to certainly what security has to focus on. In fact, I've seen countless presentations of where people have talked about this concept here to management and stakeholders and they call it DevOps, but we forget about the most important component of all of this that is basically not even shown here. And that is the security component of this, right? Because when you think about it, if you're going to have integrated product teams that collaborate better and work together better and achieve alignment with goals and objectives better, you need to involve security as well. And in fact, look, you can't do development properly without including security right from the start and throughout. And you can't do operations properly without involving security from the start and throughout. And in fact, quality assurance has to focus on those aspects of things that are focused on security as well. So this is more appropriately called, some people will call it DevSecOps. And I think the reason they say SecSecond is because then it's connected to both development and operations if it sits in the middle. Although I've heard a lot of people argue, that it should be called SecDevOps because that emphasizes the most important piece of all of this, which is security. And in fact, I've seen presentations where they talk about this concept of DevOps and integrated product teams. And they use a similar diagram to what you see here. LESSON 233: Lesson 428
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

the cyber kill chain or the cyber attack chain. This is actually a concept that came from the military and military exercises. The basic idea is this. Imagine there's a tank here and there's an enemy tank on the hill over there. This tank here that wants to kill that tank over there There's a whole series of steps that have to occur in order for this tank to kill the one over there. First of all, this tank has to be able to spot, see the tank over there. Then it has to be able to rotate its turret. And it has to be able to set its gun at the right elevation. I know nothing about tanks. Then it has to be able to load some ammunition, hit the fire button. the point is there's a whole series of steps that need to occur in order for this tank to be able to kill the tank over there and that's where the term kill chain idea comes from the idea is is that in order to achieve an objective there's a whole chain of events that have to happen and if i'm the tank over here on the hill and i don't want to get shot and killed then what I want to do is think about how can I break the chain? How can I make it so I'm not spotted by the enemy tank? How can I make it so that the tank can't rotate its turret or load ammunition or whatever? So it's a concept that came from the military. Lockheed Martin took this idea of this chain of events that have to occur that must to achieve some objective. Lockheed Martin took this objective and they applied it to cybersecurity. And of course they called it the cyber kill chain or the cyber attack chain. And it's exact same underlying idea, which is, If a hacker wants to break in and do nefarious things inside of your organization, there's a whole series, there's a whole chain of events that have to occur. And so what we wanna be doing as defenders is we wanna think about what are all the links in this chain? And how can we try and break one of those links? Because we can break a link in the chain, the attack can't proceed, and we're not going to get hacked. We're not going to get compromised, which is obviously desirable. So here's what you need to know. You need to know the seven links in this chain. You need to know the order of those links. And you need to know roughly what's happening at each link. So let me walk you through all of that in detail now. And we'll start high level with just the seven links in the chain. And then in a second here, I'll show you, or in a minute, I'll show you a detailed slide with detailed information on each of the seven steps. All right, so here's the first one. Step one in the Cyberkill chain is reconnaissance. Step two is weaponization. Three, there we go, is delivery. Four is exploit. Five is installation. Six is command and control. And seven is actions. So again, I'll show you a detailed slide and I'll walk you through what each of these steps mean in just a moment here. But just staying at the high level. Remember, you need to know each of the seven links in the chain. You need to know the names of the links. And you need to understand, again, what's the whole purpose of this? This is a model that we can use as defenders to think about how can we try and protect our environment. Because if we can try and break a link in the chain, for instance, if we can prevent some malicious software from exploiting our systems, not being able to install and run on our systems, then we can break this link in the chain. And if we can break this link in the chain, the attack's not going to be able to proceed any further. The attacker's not going to be able to get into our environment and perform nefarious actions. So that's the whole purpose of this model. Think about how we as the defenders can break each link in the chain. And for the exploit, for instance, here, Typically, or one of the major ways that we can try and prevent malware from being able to exploit our systems is by patching our systems. So if we can patch our systems as quickly as possible in a timely manner, then we can try and break this link in the chain. And of course, the other thing we want to be thinking about this is we want to try and break the link as early as possible. It's probably not possible for you to entirely prevent the attackers from knowing you even exist. But, you know, if you could, that'd be great. And of course, you know, because the earlier you can break the link in the chain, the less far the attackers are going to get into their attack. And that's obviously desirable. So that's the whole idea here. And here's the details. Here's the seven links in the Cyberkill chain. Step one, as I said, or link one is reconnaissance. And this is where the attacker is researching the target organization. And specifically, one of the main things they're trying to do is figure out what systems are running in this environment and what are potential vulnerabilities in those systems that could be exploited. So this is happening during the reconnaissance. This is what's happening during reconnaissance. The attacker is reconnoitering, figuring out what systems are running, what vulnerabilities exist in the target environment. Step two, weaponization. This is where the attacker is creating some custom malware that will be able to exploit the vulnerabilities that have been identified. Step three, delivery. This is where the attacker is trying to think about how can they get the malware into the target environment. Can they get it in through a spear phishing email or a whaling email or smishing? Or maybe they could do a baiting attack by sprinkling USB sticks through the company employee parking lot and having an employee pick one up and walk that USB stick through all the layers of physical security and plug into their system. That's the point of delivery. The attacker is trying to figure out how can they get the malware into the target environment. Step four, exploit. This is where the malware is actually going to be triggered and will run in the environment, exploit some vulnerability in the system. Step five, installation. As soon as the attacker has managed to get a foothold in the target environment, they're going to want to make sure they can maintain that persistence. And that's typically by installing some sort of backdoor, which is going to make it so that even if the company patches their systems, the attackers still have a way in that can maintain that persistence. which is then going to allow them to maintain a persistent connection and start to send command and controls into the target environment, which will then, of course, allow them to perform nefarious actions, ransomware attacks, exfiltrate sensitive data, perform denial of service attacks, whatever they want to do. So... One final reminder, know all seven links in the chain, know the order of them and know roughly what's happening each link. And the whole point of this is we as defenders should be thinking through how can we break each link in this chain and how can we break the chain as early as possible to better defend our systems.

Super quick little clarification on a bit of terminology here. We just talked about industrial control systems, ICSs. You might also come across the term OT on the exam, operational technology. What's the difference? Operational technology is a broader term that refers to any hardware software technology used to monitor and control physical processes, devices, including industrial control systems. So operational technology is a broader term contained within operational technology is industrial control systems. So ICS is a subset, is part of OT. And industrial control systems obviously specifically focus on the systems that are used to control industrial processes, water treatment plants, robots in a manufacturing facility. pumps and valves in a nuclear power plant. So just remember OT is the broader term and ICS is a subset of that.
