LESSON 235: Lesson 430
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

I want to talk in a little more detail now about input validation and a couple of different major ways that we can do input validation. Allow lists versus deny lists and whether we should do it client side versus server side. So let's talk through that because some useful stuff to know for the exam. So let's start with allow list versus deny list. This is two different methods that we can use to perform input validation, to validate user input and make sure that this is stuff that we want to pass on to our SQL database or store somewhere or something like that. So let's start with a deny list. A deny list is the idea that you explicitly deny certain things. So let's say, let's say we're worried about someone performing a cross-side scripting attack on our server. So in our deny list here, right? We could like deny, say the script tag. I'm oversimplifying here, but so you get the idea. We could basically say, we're going to deny the script tag. And so that would mean if anyone ever tried to send some input to our server that contained a script tag, the server would say, not allowed. And it would just either reject all that input or strip out the script tag. And at first blush, this seems brilliant, right? Because if no one, you know, if no one's allowed to submit the script tag, you can't get any JavaScript through or anything like that. And to be clear, denialists have their use, but they're not considered to be the most secure way of doing this for forming input validation. And here's why. Attackers are incredibly good attackers are incredibly creative people and a lot of them have all the time in the world to try every single possible combination and So it's not impossible for us as defenders to think about every single thing that we should explicitly deny Like let me give you one just stupid example. Let's say our denialist is case-sensitive All the attacker has to do to get through our deny list is they could just change the S on script to be an uppercase S and boom, there we go. They just got through our deny list by capitalizing one of the letters in the script. So that's the problem with denialists because what we're doing in a denialist is we're explicitly denying certain things. And attackers are gonna find ways around that. And we as defenders just can't think of every possible thing that we should deny. There are use cases for this, but it's not considered to be as secure. Because a denialist means you're denying certain things and you're letting everything else through. Allow lists are the opposite. In an allow list, you're explicitly allowing certain things through and everything else is denied. So for example, here's what a really secure allow list filter would look like. You might allow through just alphanumeric. So like A through Z lowercase, A through Z uppercase, and maybe 0 through 9. So you might just let through alphanumeric. Now, is anyone getting a SQL injection attack through or cross-scripting attack through this allow list filter? No, like almost no chance, right? Because the attacker cannot use a single special character here. No braces, no apostrophe, no quotes, like nothing. All they can use is uppercase A, you know, upper through lowercase, A through Z and zero through nine, just alphanumeric. That's all you're allowing through and everything else is denied. So, this would be a very restrictive list of what's allowed through. And if you're an attacker, you're not getting an attack through this. So, that's why allow lists are considered to be more secure because you're only explicitly allowing certain things through and everything else is denied. Obviously, on the exam, if you had to pick between allow list and deny list, which is more secure? Allow list. By the way, In the industry, these terms historically have often been, you'll hear the term whitelist referred to this, and this will be a blacklist. There's not great connotations attached to those words. These are much better words that we should be using, allow list and deny list. They're clear on what exactly is happening and they're just better words to be using. But the reason I point out whitelist, blacklist is you still may see that terminology on the exams. In case you come across it, yes, an allow list is the same thing as a whitelist. And Denialist is the same thing as a blacklist, but use these terms. They're better. Okay. Now, the other thing I want to talk about here is where should this input validation be done? Client side or server side? Which is more secure? And the answer is unequivocally server-side. Server-side is way more secure. Let me explain. There's nothing wrong with input validation on the client side for usability purposes. Let's say you're asking someone, like you have an online form on your website, and you're asking someone to submit their email address. You can have really simple JavaScript code that will validate, hey, is this a valid email address, right? Because every email address follows a very standard format. Some characters, an at symbol, some more characters, a period, some more characters, right? Every email address follows that very simple. format and so you can very quickly do some validation to make sure hey is this a valid email address so there's nothing wrong with client-side validation from his usability perspective but it is useless useless from a security perspective Why? Well, because it's incredibly easy to bypass. Most client-side validation will be say JavaScript in a web browser. If I'm the attacker, I can trivially easily bypass your client-side input validation by just going into settings on my browser and just disabling JavaScript. If I disable JavaScript on my browser, Boom, all the client side validation is just gone. All right, you know, and if it's even a more skilled attacker, they can just completely bypass all of your client side code and just figure out what are the get and post requests to send data directly to your server and just completely bypass anything on the client side. So just to reiterate, client-side validation from a security perspective is useless. What you always, always, always, always want to do is do input validation on the server side. It always needs to be the server that's performing the input validation. So just to sum this up, If you've got a question asking you which is the most secure way of performing input validation, you want allow list, server side.

============================================================
LESSON 236: Lesson 431
Source: network-intercept-vtt
============================================================

Congratulations, you have made it through all the fundamentals of cryptography. We've talked about how you can achieve all five services of cryptography, confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation of both origin and delivery and access control. We've talked about symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, digital certificates, digital signatures, hashing, all of this. congratulations you've made it through all of that and what i'm now going to show you is one really cool slide that puts all of that together this one really cool slide shows us where do you use digital certificates versus digital signatures why do we sometimes use symmetric photography versus asymmetric where do we use hashing and why how and where do we achieve all five services so this one slide is going to put all of that together. It's a really cool slide. I love it. If you're watching this for the very first time, I have a really important suggestion, okay? Don't copy anything down. Just look up to what I'm saying here, pay attention, don't copy anything down. I'll tell you why at the end. Okay, so let's get into it. The one slide that puts everything together. Okay, so here's what's going to happen, okay? Alice wants to send a very large file to Bob. And in sending that very large file to Bob, Alice wants to achieve all five services of cryptography. Confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation of both origin delivery and access control so let's see how she does that let's look at all the steps that Alice has to go through and Bob have to go through to achieve this now Before Alice can start sending anything, the large file to Bob, the first thing that Alice and Bob need a copy of is they need a copy of each other's public keys. But remember, you should never, ever, ever just send your public key to someone because somebody could intercept it and replace it with their own and you wouldn't know. So instead, what Alice and Bob are gonna send to each other is their digital certificates, right? Because that's the main purpose of digital certificates. It's a way of securely getting your public key to someone and for them to be able to verify they've received your public key. So Alice and Bob are gonna send each other their digital certificates. Now, let's start with Alice. When Alice gets Bob's certificate, the first thing she needs to do is she needs to decrypt it. Because this digital certificate is encrypted, and it was encrypted with the CA's private key. So therefore, it's going to decrypt with the CA's public key, which Alice has built into her system. Now, when Alice decrypts Bob's certificate, she's going to see that it says, aha, this certificate belongs to Bob and contained within it is going to be a copy of Bob's public key. So the whole purpose of this is this has allowed Alice to get a copy of Bob's public key and verify that she got Bob's public key and not some sketchy other person's public key. And Bob, of course, has done the same thing over here. He's decrypted Alice's digital certificate, confirmed that it said it belongs to Alice, and now Bob has verified that he has a copy of Alice's public key. And this is super important. Alice and Bob need to know with certainty that they have a copy of each other's public keys. I'll show you why in a minute. Here is the very large file that Owls wants to send over to Bob. And in sending this file, Alice wants to achieve confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, etc. So how is she going to do that? Let's start with the confidentiality. How is she going to achieve confidentiality? How is she going to make sure that no one can read this file in transit? Well, she's going to have to encrypt it. Now, because this is a large file, what is the only type of cryptography that's fast enough to encrypt this large file quickly and efficiently? Symmetric cryptography. So Alice is going to have to encrypt with a symmetric algorithm here, like say AES. And if Alice is encrypting with a symmetric algorithm, she's going to need to use a symmetric key. But now Alice has a problem, right? It's the Achilles heel of symmetric cryptography, symmetric key distribution. How is she gonna get this symmetric key over to Bob in such a way that no one else can intercept that key and get a copy of it? She could of course use out-of-band key distribution. She could like carrier pigeon it to Bob or something, but that's not very efficient. Here's a way that Alice can securely get the symmetric key to Bob, how she can solve the symmetric key distribution. And it's using, of course, asymmetric cryptography. That's what we use asymmetric cryptography for all the time to solve the symmetric key distribution problem. So here's what Alice is going to do. She's going to encrypt this symmetric key in such a way that the only person in the world that can decrypt it is Bob. So how does she do that? Well, she encrypts with Bob's public key. So here, right here, this is why Alice needed to know that she had a copy of Bob's public key. Cause she's going to encrypt this symmetric key using Bob's public key as the encryption key. And that's going to generate a little bit of ciphertext. And just a second here, like, Why could Alice use asymmetric cryptography here where here she had to use symmetric? And the answer is right here, right? She was encrypting a very large file. So she needed something that was fast and efficient. Here, she's only encrypting a Symmetra key. Symmetra keys are minuscule, right? A Symmetra key is like 128 bits, maybe 256 bits. A Symmetra key is a few hundred bits. And with modern computer systems, a few hundred bits is a completely inconsequential amount of data. It's tiny. And so even though asymmetric cryptography is really inefficient, it doesn't matter because all we're doing is encrypting a tiny little symmetric key. So that's why Alice can use asymmetric cryptography here. Okay, so she's encrypted with Bob's public key, and she now sends that ciphertext across the internet. Anyone can intercept this ciphertext, but the only person in the world that's gonna be able to decrypt it is Bob with his private key. And so now Bob has a copy of the symmetric key. And the whole point of this swim lane here was to solve the symmetric key distribution problem, to get a copy of this symmetric key over to Bob securely. Okay, now before Alice sends the ciphertext of the file over to Bob, there's one more thing that she wants to do. In sending this file, she also wants to achieve integrity, authenticity, and non-reputation. As soon as you hear those things, right, integrity, authenticity, non-reputation, you should think digital signatures. So the next thing Alice is going to have to do is she's going to have to create a digital signature for this file. Recall, there's really only two easy steps required to create a digital signature. Step one, hash the data. So Alice is going to run the data through a hashing algorithm, which is going to produce a fixed length message digest, a hash value. And then the second step required to create a digital signature is the sender encrypts the hash value with the sender's which key? Private key. So Alice is going to encrypt this hash value with her private key. And that's going to create a digital signature. So there's now two separate pieces of ciphertext that Alice is sending over to Bob. The first piece of ciphertext is the giant encrypted file. And the second piece of ciphertext is this tiny little digital signature here. It's just a tiny little bit of ciphertext that Alice could append to the file. So Alice sends these two pieces of ciphertext over to Bob. Now Bob decides that the first thing that he wants to decrypt is the actual file. So now the question is, what key does Bob need to use to decrypt this file? Well, remember, this file, because it's a very large file, right? The only type of cryptography that was fast enough to perform the encryption was a symmetric algorithm. with this Symmetra key. So what key is gonna be used to decrypt? The same Symmetra key, right? This is why we needed to solve, this is why Alice needed to solve the Symmetra key distribution problem. In order to decrypt the large file, Bob's gonna need this Symmetra key. Okay, so Bob decrypts the file. Now, a really good question to contemplate at this point is right here where Bob has decrypted the file, which of the five services have been achieved at this point? Remember the five services are confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation and access control. So which of the five services have been achieved at this point? There's only two. Which two? The answers are, first of all, confidentiality, right? We've achieved confidentiality because the file was sent encrypted. I know some of you are thinking integrity, authenticity, or non-repudiation. Wrong! Those only come with the digital signature. So it's not integrity, it's not authenticity, it's not non-repudiation. Those all come from the digital signature. So the only one that's left is access control. And there is actually a form of access control here, right? Because Alice is able to control who she sends the ciphertext to and who she sends the symmetric key to. So because Alice has control over who she sends those two pieces to, she has a degree of control over who can decrypt the file. That's a simple form of access control. So at this stage right here, where Bob has decrypted the file, the only two services that have been achieved are confidentiality and access control. Next thing. Right. And now let's move on to the integrity, the authenticity and non-repudiation. Those come from the digital signature. So the next thing that Bob has to do is Bob has to decrypt this digital signature. With what key? Well, this digital signature was encrypted with Alice's private key. Therefore, it will decrypt with Alice's public key. So Bob is going to decrypt Alice's digital signature with Alice's public key. Now, this is really important. Pay attention here. The fact that Alice's digital signature decrypts with Alice's public key means that the only person in the world that could have created this digital signature is Alice. And that proves authenticity. This digital signature has to have come from Alice, right? Because she's the only one that could have created it with her private key. If it decrypted with her public key, it had to have been created with her private key. So right here, just the fact that this digital signature decrypts with Alice's public key, that proves authenticity. but we don't yet have integrity and non-repudiation. Those come in a minute here. Okay, so Bob decrypts Alice's digital signature with Alice's public key, proving authenticity. Contained within Alice's digital signature is the hash value that Alice generated. These are the same hash value. Now this hash value by itself proves nothing. So the next thing that Bob has to do is Bob has to hash the file that he received, generating his own new hash value. He'll now compare the hash value that he just generated to the hash value that he got from Alice's digital signature. And if these hash values match, that proves integrity. That's what hashing proves, integrity. So if these hash values match, it means that Bob has received exactly the same file that Alice sent. And now that we have authenticity and integrity, this is where we now achieve non-repudiation of origin. Alice cannot deny she sent this file because Bob can say, look, I know this digital signature came from you, authenticity. And I know this is exactly the same file that you sent. Integrity. So when you have authenticity and integrity, that's where you get non-repudiation. You can only have non-repudiation if you have both of those. Integrity and authenticity. Okay. Now there's only one final piece missing here, and that's non-repetition of delivery. Alice wants to know without a doubt that Bob actually got this file. So how do we achieve non-repudiation of delivery? Well, whenever you hear non-repudiation, you should think digital signatures. So what's Bob gonna have to do? He's gonna have to send his own digital signature back to Alice. Remember, there's just two easy steps required to create a digital signature. Step one, hash the data. Bob's already done that, right? He's got the hash value right here. So all Bob has to do is take this hash value. And then step two of creating a digital signature is the sender encrypts with the senders. private key so bob will encrypt that hash value with his private key generating his own digital signature which he's going to send back to alice And now Alice will go to decrypt Bob's digital signature using Bob's public key. And if this digital signature decrypts with Bob's public key, who is the only person in the world that could have created the digital signature? Bob with his private key. So that proves authenticity. And then contained of course, within Bob's digital signature is the hash value that Bob created over here. And the final thing that Alice is gonna do is she's gonna compare the hash value that she generated with the hash value that she got from Bob's digital signature. And if those hash values match that proves integrity. Alice now knows that Bob has received exactly the same file that she sent and she knows Bob received it right because of the authenticity from the digital signature. So now that we have authenticity and integrity, that's where we now finally achieve non-repudiation of Bob cannot deny receiving this file because Alice says, look, I know you received it because you sent back, you know, contained within your digital signature that you sent back to me was this hash value. And the only way that you'd have this hash value is if you'd received this file. So that's non-repudiation of delivery. Bob can't deny that he received the file. There you go, that's the amazing Alice and Bob diagram. Something important I'll point out here, okay? There is no way that Alice and Bob are doing all this manually. Can you imagine at your company if you said, okay, now, if you put out a very simple 70-page memo saying, okay, from now on, everyone, when you want to send someone an email, I need you to just go ahead and open up the command prompt, pull up your private key, Go ahead and run the email that you want to send through the AES algorithm. Make sure you use a 256-bit key. And then hash that using, I don't know, a SHA-256. Take that hash value, encrypt it with your private... Can you imagine if users had to do all this manually? It would never work. So the only way that all this works is if Alice and Bob both have a crypto system installed on their computers. And the crypto system will take care of all of these steps automatically. A perfect example of a crypto system is a piece of software called PGP. It stands for Pretty Good Privacy. PGP is a free open source crypto system that anyone can install on their computer. So Alice and Bob could both install PGP. And one of the cool things about PGP is it even integrates with Outlook. So you can just go into Outlook and hit send on an email. And if you've configured PGP to do this, PGP will take care of encrypting the email. PGP will take care of generating the digital signature to attach the email. PGP will take care of all of that. And then as long as Bob has PGP on his system, it'll take care of decrypting the email and decrypting the digital signature and all this other stuff and sending back basically a read receipt. So I just wanted to highlight that. Alice and Bob are not doing this manually, right? It would not work. They need a crypto system. And those crypto systems have to be configured in the same way, right? Because if Alice encrypted with AES and then Bob tried to decrypt with triple DES, it wouldn't work. Or if Alice hashed with MD5 and then Bob hashed with SHA512, it wouldn't work. So their crypto systems also have to be configured the same way. And by the way, like when a message is sent, it will contain metadata as to which algorithms are used in what way. But the point is, they both need crypto systems. They both have to be configured in the same way. Okay, one final piece here. I told you at the beginning, if you're watching this video for the first time, don't copy anything down. Here's why. Pick up your pencil now, or this evening, or at some point. Try and redraw this diagram from memory. See if you can remember all the sort of major pieces here. So here's what I would suggest in writing down. Write down that Alice wants to send a large vial to Bob and achieve all five services, right? Confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation of both origin and delivery, and access control. Write that down and then see if you can redraw this Alice and Bob diagram and understand where you're using digital signatures versus digital certificates, where we're using symmetric versus asymmetric cryptography, et cetera. You will never have to draw a diagram like this on the exam. But if you can redraw this from memory and understand what's happening where, it's a really good test for yourself of do you understand the fundamentals of cryptography and how all these various pieces that we've talked about work together. So I would recommend that you do that. And if you get stuck, of course, you can come back and watch this video or look at the diagrams in our book or whatever. All the best in your drawing. Have fun with some crayons.

We just talked about ports and the importance of hardening, for example. Port numbers is how we reference services and we define that services are actually applications that have been written to provide a specific service. So that's how we reference things at the transport layer through port numbers. Now these services that port numbers correspond to have been around forever. So we know which ones have vulnerabilities and so on. So this is why hardening becomes such an important thing. Here's a perfect example. I think in that last video, we did say that you don't need to memorize 65,000 odd port numbers and the services that they correspond to. That's not what makes you a competent security professional. But here's what you do need to remember, right? There are certain port numbers that equate to specific services that we know have exploits and vulnerabilities associated with them. So these are some of the ones that are most well known. You know that port 80 equates to, for example, the HTTP service, which is really the language of the web, but it does not have any sort of inherent security built in. So if you want security for HTTP traffic, it doesn't natively have it. You have to tunnel that traffic across something that can give it the security that it is lacking. And that's why we use, for example, SSL and TLS and HTTPS, for example, right? Now, some of these other ones, like for example, if you ask network people, you know, what's the most dangerous service that exists out there? A lot of them might say things like Telnet. Telnet has a lot of vulnerabilities and exploits associated with it. The idea behind hardening is that if you don't need some of these services, why would you let them run? and allow them through things like your firewall, for example. On certain hosts, obviously, it makes good sense that if you don't need those services, you turn them off so that exploits associated with them cannot be taken advantage of. The idea behind some of these, is that hardening from a network security perspective means that if you understand that some of these are exploitable, if you don't need those services, you turn them off. If you need them, then you try and go out there and find more secure versions of them. So obviously the industry has understood the importance of these things. You can't just turn off every service because then your network isn't going to function. So some of these are obviously very important to some of the services and what's going on across your network. So if you need a service, then you try and go find a more secure version of it. And here, for example, is a perfect example. FTP is port 21. It natively doesn't have any security built in. But the industry has come up with something that has more security built in for that specific service called FTP. And very appropriately, it is called SFTP. the S obviously standing for secure FTP. Now obviously we would mandate from a security perspective that we use the more secure version of it. Now certain things you can't just add security, like for example Telnet. There's no such thing as secure Telnet. But what you can do is you can tunnel your Telnet traffic across something that can give it the security that it may be lacking. So for example, we can tunnel Telnet maybe across a SSH tunnel to be able to provide things like authentication and encryption and multiplexing capabilities and some of those things. What we need to remember about this is that hardening is the concept of If we don't need a service, we turn it off. If we need it, we try and find a more secure version of it or implement the more secure version of it by tunneling it across something that can give it the security that it lacks. And then of course, hardening also means using the latest versions of things because they've been patched for known vulnerabilities and we do more monitoring and so on. But that's the idea behind some of these and the concept of hardening.

All right. You might see something familiar. I'm finally wearing the same sweatshirt that Rob usually wears. So we look like a team finally. Not the exact same one. Let's get that clear. And as part of network security, every organization has to protect against network attacks. That's what this section will deal with. Now, you know, we could spend weeks talking about how to protect against specific network attacks that exists out there, right? And there are many and so on, so that's not really the focus of the CISSP exam. Obviously, they could ask you about specific attacks and so on, but as usual, let's frame our minds on what we know is specifically mentioned in the exam outline, where the questions ultimately come from. and also what we know people are seeing, right? The first thing that we have to focus on from a network security perspective and therefore protecting against network attacks is that if we understand the phases of a successful attack then the whole focus of preventing against network attacks is to make each of these phases impossible or more difficult for an attacker to actually achieve. And you'll remember earlier in this course, we talked about something called the cyber kill chain. Right, something that the industry has been focusing on for a number of years now. And that is you wanna break that chain so that successful attacks cannot be completed. It's the same concept here. In fact, when you look at these phases here, reconnaissance, enumeration, vulnerability analysis, and exploitation. These are the phases that any successful attack has to go through and they have to be completed in the order that you see here. So the idea is that if you know the successful phases that a successful attack needs to go through, the focus from a network security perspective is to make each of these more difficult or impossible for an attacker to complete. So for example, if you understand that the first phase in any successful attack is reconnaissance, then you want to make that phase as difficult as possible. You'll remember that this is where the attackers are looking for initial gathering of information relating to potential targets. They're looking for things like domain names and IP address ranges and so on. So the idea is you don't want to make this easy for any potential attacker. So you don't publish your domain names and IP address ranges. You don't want to make yourself an attractive target. All right, so making each of these more difficult is a good strategy in preventing successful attacks. The other thing here, like for example, second phase here is enumeration. This is where the attackers are looking for specific information. The idea here is, again, you want to be able to understand all of your architectures. and systems and make sure that, for example, we harden them to the point where anything that is not needed is not running. And one of the really good things that you can do here is you prevent people that work for your organization in providing that type of information out there. So you have to have policies that talk about how your people cannot post things on news groups. and forums and up on social media websites like LinkedIn, for example, the experience that they have in working with specific technologies inside of your organization. These are obviously, you know, places that attackers can look for. and understand exactly the technologies and the architectures that you may be using inside of your organization, which may allow them to take advantage of each of these phases here so that ultimately they can potentially conduct successful attacks. So the whole idea of this, and you don't have to memorize all of this and so on, but making each of these phases difficult and impossible should be the focus of network security in any organization. Now, something that the exam does require you to understand is the difference between passive and active attacks. Now, there's an easy way to understand the difference here. And that is to look at it from the perspective of detection. All right, because passive attacks are impossible or very difficult to detect. Whereas active attacks, are very easily detectable, right? So if the exam asks you questions, is this attack a example of a passive or active attack? Look at it from the perspective of the organization itself being able to detect that attack. If it is detectable, it is a active attack. If it is not detectable, then it is a passive type of an attack. Now again, we could spend weeks talking about different types of attacks here, but we're only gonna focus on the ones that we know are specifically mentioned. in the exam outline. So you'll see a few slides here coming up that we know people are seeing questions on from the perspective of understanding whether those attacks are passive or active. But before we get to that, You see another set of terms here, threat agents. We would have talked about threat agents back in domain number one, when we talked about risk management. We said a threat agent is something or someone that can introduce risks into a environment, right? So a perfect example of a threat agent is an attacker because that attacker can introduce certain exploits and vulnerabilities into an architecture that they may be able to take advantage of. So think about threat agent being someone or something that can introduce risks and threats into an environment such as Now let's go back to these concepts here. And again, let's look at these from the perspective of being able to detect. That's the easy way to understand. whether an attack is passive or active. So here's an example, eavesdropping, which is defined as picking up something that is not meant for you. So for example, you could be at a restaurant and you could be eavesdropping on someone else's conversation. You're not doing anything, you're not interrupting them, you're simply listening. In fact, the word passive here gives it away as to what kind of an attack this is. Specifically related to networks, if you have someone that is eavesdropping or picking up traffic that's going across the network that is not meant for them, that is something that is very difficult for an organization to pick up or detect. Therefore, Passively sniffing or eavesdropping on network traffic is considered to be a passive type of an attack. Now, I know some very technical people will argue with this. Right, but I'll explain it here. But some people say, well, in order to be able to pick up all traffic across a network, for example, you need to put your network card into promiscuous mode. And that is something that may be detectable by others. And you'd be absolutely correct in saying that. But the exam doesn't get to that level of detail. So if you're simply picking up information without changing it, or doing anything with it, you're simply eavesdropping on that traffic, then that is something that is very difficult or impossible to detect. Therefore, the exam considers that, just like the industry does, to be a passive type of an attack, right? Here's the next one. And I guess the word actively gives this one away as well. But let's say that an attacker is potentially looking for ports and services that may be running on certain architectures or hosts, for example, so that they may be able to understand certain exploits and vulnerabilities that they can take advantage of. that requires the attacker to be able to probe or send packets to that host, for example, which is something that the organization would be able to detect. And therefore, this is considered to be a active type of an attack. Because again, the attacker needs to send something to that host to be able to determine whether that port or service is actually running. So this is detectable from the perspective of the organization. It is an active type of an attack. In fact, here's a specific example of that. It's referred to as SYN scanning, S-Y-N scanning. This refers back to what we talked about earlier, and that is TCP connections. But let's understand the scenario. This is where an attacker sends a synchronized request to a host. at a specific port number, which as you know equates to services. Depending on the information that that attacker gets back, they will be able to determine whether that service or port is open And in fact, more than that, because depending on the response that they may get back, it may also tell them whether there's a stateful firewall that sits in between. All right, so this is very valuable information. that obviously attackers are looking for. But because they're sending packets to the host, we can detect this and therefore this would also be considered to be a active type of an attack. Depending on the information that obviously they get back, they know whether something is exploitable or not. And here's another one too. In fact, we referred to this earlier when we talked about TCP connections, right? We talked about how TCP connections start with that three-way handshake which starts with a synchronized request. And then we said something like, what if a host is receiving a whole bunch of synchronized requests, but they're coming faster than the host can actually handle? What happens is the in-process queue or connection queue will slowly fill up because you're receiving as a host, for example, you're receiving synchronized requests faster than you can handle so therefore your in process also referred to as the connection queue slowly fills up and when it does fill up the host is going to say oh i can't do this i need to shut down it's a denial of service attack which is very appropriately referred to as a sin flood attack And we said the best way to protect against that is to not let the host handle synchronized requests on its own. Let's create an application, which we can call a proxy, which can handle synchronized requests. And because it's an application, It'll never go down. It can be smart enough to understand that we're getting flooded by synchronized requests and we can just ignore them. A SYN proxy is what we can call that. But any denial of service attack, which is what this is an example of, is obviously something that is detectable because it obviously changes the state of, in this case, the target server, for example. And it requires, obviously, sending of packets to that server, which can be detectable. And therefore, it would also be considered to be an active type of an attack. So any denial of service attack is going to be detectable and therefore also considered to be an active type of an attack.

Spare parts. We're gonna spend a few minutes here talking about some different options that you can have for having spare parts. So to put this in context, what this is about is having spare parts available such that if a part fails in a system, you've got a spare to replace it with. And we're going to talk through some different options here, cold, warm, and hot spares, and what the differences are and what these provide. But to put this into context, imagine a server and the spare part that we're talking about is the power supply. So if the primary power supply fails, where is our spare part? And really what the differences break down to between all of these is that it's two things that we're gonna look at for each of them. If the primary part fails, is the system going down? So that's the first thing. If the primary part fails, is the system going down? And then the second difference we'll look at between these is how long does it take the system to get back up and running? So is the system going down and how long does it take to get back up and running? Let's now start with a cold spare. What a cold spare means is that it's a spare part on a shelf somewhere, maybe not even in the same building. That's what a cold spare means. Spare part on a shelf somewhere, maybe not even in the same building. So using the power supply as an example, if the only power supply in the system fails, is the system going down? Absolutely, right? Because the spare part is nowhere near. So the system is absolutely going down. How long is it going to take to recover the system? I mean, if you ever replaced a power supply, it takes a while. You've got to pull all the cabling out of the case, put everything back in, and it probably takes half an hour to replace a power supply. And if the power supply is in another building somewhere and you've got to get it shipped in, it could be anywhere from a few minutes to a few hours to maybe in a few days. The point is it's going to take a while. to get the system back up and running with a cold spare. So this is obviously the cheapest solution, but it has some pretty significant downsides. Now, listen really carefully here to the definition of a warm spare. It's subtle. A warm spare is a spare part installed in the system, but not, not powered on and ready to go. So a warm spare is a spare part installed in the system, but it's not, not powered on and ready to go. So if you're using a warm spare power supply and the primary fails, is the system going down? Yes, because the warm spare is not powered on. It's not ready to go. So yes, the system is going down. What's the recovery time? Maybe just a few seconds, maybe just a few minutes. Someone has to run over to the server, manually switch over to the alternate power supply, and then power the system back on. So that's a warm spare. Spare part installed in the system, but not powered on and ready to go. So the system is going down, but it's only going to take a few seconds or maybe a few minutes to bring back up. And you can probably guess what the definition is now of a hot spare. A hot spare is a spare part installed in the system and it is, it is powered on and ready to go. And so with a hot spare, if the primary power supply fails, is the system going down? No, because if you're using a hot spare setup, the server will have been designed to have essentially two power supplies in it that are powered on, ready to go at all times. There'll be the primary and say the secondary. And the server will normally be operating off the primary power supply. But if it were to ever fail, the system would just automatically fail over to the hot spare secondary power supply and the system would not go down. So obviously you don't have to think about what the recovery time would be. So that leads to a good question. Which of these is best? And the answer is, it depends. What does it depend on? The perfect number that it depends on is the RTO, the recovery time objective. How quickly do you need to bring the system back up? That's really what determines which of these is best. If a system owner says to you, you know what, it's fine if this system is down for like two days, just make sure it's cheap, cold spare, right? At the other extreme, if the owner of the system says, this system can never be down and I'm willing to pay a lot of money to make that happen, then a hot spare would be the best option. So that's why we have these different options. You're going to pick the one that best suits the needs of the owner of that system, best suits the needs of the organization. There is no best. Depends.

We just introduced the concepts of data remnants and data destruction. Let's get into a little more detail in relation to this. So as part of the data lifecycle, we obviously want to protect data as it goes through its lifecycle focused on its value. That's always what drives how we protect things. That includes data. But at the end of the data lifecycle is what we call the destruction phase. And that has to be done properly based on the value as well. So imagine something that has a lot of value but needs to be destroyed. It needs to be destroyed in such a way so that it cannot be reconstructed by anyone else, for example. The data destruction phase needs to be done properly, and that's what we're going to talk about here in this section. And data destruction methods and data remnants, these are terms that you need to remember, are related to each other. And so the way that we destroy data needs to be good enough in order to prevent data remnants, which is defined as data remaining on an object after you've tried to securely get rid of it. There's different methods that we can employ here to be able to securely get rid of data. First of all, a definition of what data remnants is, and if you focus on the word remnants, I think it tells you what we're talking about here. We want to prevent data remaining on an object based on its value. So there's different methods that we can use here. But here's a good industry definition data remaining after deletion or secure deletion, for example, based on the value. of that data. Now, I think we've already stressed this many times. Obviously the owners are always accountable for protecting the value of their assets. And so data owners are accountable for protecting the value of the data that they own on behalf of the organization. But that also includes the destruction right so if we're trying to prevent data remaining on an object so that it cannot be reconstructed after we've destroyed it owners are also accountable for that right so destruction methods is that something that ultimately the owners are accountable for And need to be supported by security and technology, of course, so that we can do it based on the value of the data at the end of its life cycle. All right. So this comes from the industry now. Again, it's not ISE 2 that just dreams up certain terminologies and so on. Anything that is addressed in the exam First of all, comes from the exam outline, but the exam outline is derived from the common body of knowledge of security. So it's the industry, for example, that's advocating the different methods here for data destruction. And we're going to try and make it as easy as possible here because that's what's required as far as the exam is concerned. There's really three categories of how we can prevent data remnants or ensure the secure destruction of data at the end of its life cycle. And these are the three categories that you see here. So we've ordered them by order of best to worst. So your first option, best option, if it's available, is destruction. And here's the perfect definition from an industry perspective, common body of knowledge perspective. of what destruction really means what you're trying to do here is you're destroying the media that that data is on because if you destroy the the media obviously you're making the media unusable but you're also destroying the data that was on that media This is your best option always if it's available. You're destroying, physically destroying the media so that anything that's on there from a data perspective is also destroyed and not available for reconstruction in any way. Now, if destruction is not an option, your next best option is what we call purging. And here's the industry definition that you should remember of purging. We're using some sort of method that will ensure destruction. that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means. So the industry does use the word cannot. So if destruction is not available, your next best option is to purge. And purge means exactly this. You're using some sort of technique that obviously will ensure that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means. Now, if purging is not an option, then the last option that's available for us is what we call clearing. And here's the industry definition of clearing. It's almost the same as purging, except instead of using the word cannot, we use the word may not. So we're using some sort of method that may not allow the reconstruction of that data so that doesn't sound very good and in fact it isn't now how does this relate to some of the methods that you may have heard of right because we've all heard of different ways to try and get rid of data and here are those categories that we just described at the bottom physical destruction of the media purging which again meant that we're using some sort of method that will ensure that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means And then, of course, the last option, which we said wasn't very good, is clearing where the data may or may not be reconstructed. All right, so here are some of those Methods that would fit in these categories. All right now obviously media destruction and doing things like incinerating or turning your hard drive into liquid by using high heat and You've heard of some of these too, shredding, disintegrating, drilling a hole through your hard drive, for example. These are all destruction methods. And as far as the exam is concerned, we need to be very careful here because a lot of us have a tendency to overthink and look at things too deeply. Because some of you may be looking at these methods that we've put in this category here, that the industry has put in these categories, in this category of destruction here. And you might be thinking, well, you know, turning something into liquid is better than, for example, drilling a hole through the hard drive. And you'd be absolutely correct in thinking that. But as far as the exam is concerned, no need to get to that level of detail. All right. Any destruction method is obviously fitting here in the destruction category. It's better than anything else. The exam is not going to ask you to understand the difference between drilling and incinerating and disintegrating and treading. As long as the method is destruction, it fits here. It's what we prefer. Obviously, if it's available, destruction methods is what we want because it makes the media unusable, but also gets rid of the data that was on that media. Now, if destruction is not an option, we said purging was next. But your next option here, as far as methods are concerned, specific methods, is what we call degaussing. And you may recognize this word. It's actually an older technology that was developed back in the mainframe days. Because if you think about what mainframe used, they used tapes. so this is still useful today because it works really still with any technology that uses magnetic technology to represent the zeros and the ones so we can use it for hard disk drives or any other media technology that uses magnetic technology to represent the zeros and the ones. All right, that's what degaussing is. And note where we've put it here, where the industry has put it. right it's right in between destruction and purging because typically by degaussing some sort of media like a tape for example you make that tape unusable, but you're not quite really destroying it by using a destruction method. That's why it sits in between destruction and purging, but that's a really good method still. All right, now the next one that we see here is encryption. Crypto erase or crypto shredding is what the industry refers this to as. And basically, there's a slide coming up that talks about this, but basically what crypto shredding or crypto erase is, is you encrypt your data and then listen to the words here because they are important. You securely and defensively destroy every copy of the key. In other words, you encrypt something and then destroy the keys so that that data cannot be decrypted because you've securely and defensively gotten rid of the keys. And so there's no way for that data to be reconstructed. That's why that fits. really in the purging category in here, and they've put it in between purging and clearing. All right. And here are some of the other options that you may have heard of, like overwriting and wiping and formatting even. and erasing deleting and so on these all fit in the clearing category which again is defined as may not allow that data to be reconstructed even overwriting which sometimes is the only option that you may have in certain architectures but again As far as we are concerned, and the exam is concerned, and the industry is concerned, any of these methods, including, for example, how many times you're going to overwrite your data, will always be dictated by the value of that data, obviously at the time of destruction, which is what we're talking about here. All right. And even formatting and low level formatting and so on. These are all clearing methods according to the industry. All right. So you may see some of the ones that you see up here. and where they actually fit as far as categories are concerned.

We just looked at some data destruction methods and how to prevent data remnants. Let's look at this in a little more detail. And one of the terms that typically comes up, certainly in the industry in relation to all of this, are these object reuse, object reuse. And I know the history of these terms here that you see from the NSA and also actually from NIST, the National Institute of Standards and Technologies. Basically, when they defined object reuse, if you looked at these two words, it tells you exactly what we're talking about here. We're talking about the reassignment of objects, specifically media. We're trying to reuse media. But what this means from a NIST perspective, and therefore what you might see on the exam, is that we are trying to reuse objects like media, for example, but while also ensuring that there is no residual data on that object because we're reusing it. So the idea is if that media contained something on it that was very sensitive or valuable, before we can reuse that media, that object, we have to securely get rid of what was on it so that there is no residual data that can be reconstructed. So when you see these two words, object reuse, it means really the secure reassignment of objects and media. And so typically the only way that you can get rid of residual data so that it cannot be reconstructed by any known means is to use overwriting techniques. Now I know we said that overwriting is really an example of clearing that data may not be reconstructed by any known means and certainly that's true as far as what the industry considers overwriting to be regardless of how many times you do it. Overwriting is what we're using in order to get rid of that residual data.
