As we've talked about, there are three major resources that we need access to do anything with in the cloud. Compute, network, and storage. And what we're going to talk through now in detail is compute. and there's three ways that we can access compute and recall the major way like with with all these cloud resources the way that we access these key cloud resources compute network and storage is almost always through some layer of virtualization And that's what we're going to talk about now. What are the three major methods that we can access compute through some form of virtualization? And this is where we're going to talk about virtual machines, briefly cover them again. We've talked about them in detail. We're going to talk about containers in detail. And then we're going to talk about serverless computing. So quick bit of review talking about virtual machines. So here's the definition, right? Instead of running directly on hardware code, For example, an operating system runs on top of an abstraction layer, more code. Of course, what do we call that more code? The hypervisor, right? So what is a virtual machine? It's an operating system running on top of a hypervisor. That's what a virtual machine is. And here is the image that you've seen before that I drew earlier as well. So here's our underlying hardware, the compute node. Running on top of that is the hypervisor providing that layer of abstraction, that layer of virtualization. And then you can see here, we've got how many VMs running? three VMs, VM1, VM2, and VM3 here. And just as remember as well that these might be referred synonymously as instances or even guests. Now, one thing we have to go into a little more detail here is about hypervisors. There's actually two different type of hypervisors that you need to know about, type 1 and type 2. A type one hypervisor is often referred to as a hardware hypervisor. And the reason it's called that, and by the way, both of these hypervisors do the same job. They allow you to run virtual machines on top of them. But the difference is essentially what does the hypervisor run on top of itself? So a Type 1 hardware hypervisor means that the hypervisor is running directly on top of the physical hardware. To be clear, it does not mean the hypervisor itself is hardware. Hypervisors are software. What a Type 1 hardware hypervisor means is that the hypervisor is running directly on top of the hardware. Whereas a type two operating system hypervisor, the reason it's called that is because the hypervisor is not running directly on top of the hardware. Instead, as you can see here, the hypervisor is running on top of an operating system. That's why they call it a type two operating system hypervisor, because the hypervisor is running on top of an operating system. So where do we use these two different types of hypervisors? Let's start with this. Which would be more secure? Type 1 or Type 2? And the answer is Type 1. Why? Why is a Type 1 hypervisor more secure? Well, Type 2 hypervisors are running on top of this operating system here. if this operating system had any vulnerabilities in it, if this operating system, for instance, was compromised, what impact would that have on the security of the hypervisor? The answer is it would be game over, right? The hypervisor is essentially an application running on top of an operating system. If the underlying operating system is compromised, anything running on top of that operating system is going to be compromised. Type 2 hypervisors are less secure because they rely on the security of the operating system. And operating systems are large, complex pieces of code. They have vulnerabilities in them. So it's inherently less secure to run a hypervisor on top of an operating system. Okay, so type one hypervisors are more secure, which is more efficient? Again, the answer is type one, for exactly the same reason, right? Like a type two hypervisor, in order for that hypervisor to be running, you also need an additional operating system running in a type two hypervisor. And operating systems are large, complex pieces of code that take a lot of system resources. So type one hypervisors, more secure, more efficient. So if you wanna run hypervisors at scale and run virtual machines at scale efficiently, what type of hypervisor are you gonna use? Like if you're a cloud service provider and you wanna run millions of customers' virtual machines, how are you gonna do that efficiently and securely? Almost always using Type 1 hypervisors. So what do we use Type 2 hypervisors for? For development, testing, things like that. For example, on my Windows laptop, I used to teach various network security classes, and in these classes we would sometimes do demos of like Kali Linux and other tools like that. And so I'd want to be able to easily run Kali without using a dedicated system just to run Kali Linux. I could run Kali Linux in VMware Workstation. VMware Workstation is a hypervisor that runs on top of the Windows operating system that will allow you to then run multiple different hypervisors on top of VMware Workstation, which is running on top of your operating system. So VMware Workstation is a perfect example of a Type 2 hypervisor. We use it for testing, demonstration purposes, or let's say you're developing some code and you want to run that code on a particular operating system on your development machine. Run it in a Type 2 hypervisor on top of a Type U hypervisor. So we use them for testing, development, that sort of thing. Another perfect example, if you've got a Mac and if you want to run a Windows application on a Mac, you can use this really cool piece of software called Parallels. Parallels is a Type 2 hypervisor. So that's what we use Type 2 to for testing, development, things like that. Okay, this is a bit of a review. I kind of talked about this earlier in the more detailed video on virtual machines and hypervisors. I was asking, if you wanted to conduct a forensic investigation on a virtual machine, what would it be most useful to get your hands on? The snapshot, the image, or the virtual disk, the volume? The snapshot would be the most useful to get your hands on because the snapshot is a moment in time capture of a running system and it can potentially capture everything, including the data that would be on the virtual disk, the volume. So snapshot would be the most useful to get your hands on if you want to conduct a forensic investigation. The next most useful would be the virtual disk, the volume. And then the least useful, not entirely useless, but close to it, would be the VM image itself. Okay, so that's virtual machines. Let's now talk about containers. Containers are pervasively used nowadays. They're an extremely efficient way to run code at scale. So we're gonna spend a few minutes here talking about containers, at least at a high level, how they work and why they're beneficial. And before we get into talking about actual containers in a computing perspective, let's first talk about what I'm showing a picture of here, which is intermodal shipping containers. Intermodal shipping containers have revolutionized how we move goods around the planet. It used to be if you wanted to manufacture something on one side of the planet and sell it to someone else on the other side of the planet, there was a huge amount of labor involved to get it, to get the goods there. used to be you'd have to drive up to a with a wagon or a truck to the factory and there'd be a bunch of labor involved to hand load everything into the back of the truck and then the truck would drive to a nearby warehouse at the port and again there'd be a bunch of labor involved to move everything out of the back of the truck and pile up in the warehouse. And then a ship would show up and there'd be a bunch more labor to carefully take everything out of the warehouse and load it carefully into the ship, try not to break anything. And then the ship would sail somewhere and then a bunch more labor to unload everything from the ship into a warehouse or onto a truck. The point is, at every stage of moving it between different modes of transport, between, you know, a truck and a boat and a train and another truck, there'd be a bunch of labor involved. Not true with shipping containers. Not really. Now, when you want to move goods around the planet, you take an empty container, you drive up to a factory. Yes, there's a bunch of labor involved to load the container. But then after that, it's pretty easy, right? Because that container rides on the back of the truck to the local port. Giant crane picks it up, puts it in a pile. A few days later, whenever the ship shows up, right? Another giant crane picks it off of the pile, puts it on a little truck. Another crane picks it up, puts it on the boat. I mean, and at each different movement here from the pile to the truck, to the ship, I mean, it's just a big crane picking it up. It takes seconds to move the container. Whereas if you were to move all the goods within the container by hand, it would take you hours. So you can just pick it up, put it on a ship. You can sail that ship around the world. Pick the container up, put it on the back of a train. Take the train halfway across the continent. Pick the container up, put it on a truck. So one of the beautiful things about shipping containers, and this is why they're called intermodal shipping containers, is this same container, you can put it on a ship, on a barge, on a truck, on a train, on any form of transport. The container doesn't care. So this is what's cool about containers, shipping containers. They, intermodal shipping containers. You can move goods so easily on the planet because the same container can go on all these different modes of transport. that's kind of where the name for containers came from from a computing perspective because the big idea of container one of the big ideas and containers from a computing perspective is you want to make your code highly portable so you want to write your code on say a mac laptop and then qa test it on a linux laptop and then put it into production on a Unix server, or a Windows server in the Azure cloud, or maybe move it over to the Alibaba cloud. And it wouldn't be cool if every time you move your code to a different place, you didn't have to refactor it. Well, you can do that with containers from a computing perspective. Containers from a computing perspective, the idea is basically you stuff your code inside of a container, and I'm oversimplifying here, but basically you stuff your code inside of a standardized container. And then as long as you're using a standardized container, like, say, a Docker container, Docker containers will run on a Linux laptop. And then you can take that container and run it on a Windows laptop. And then you can take it and run it in the Azure cloud. And it's the same container with the same code in it. You don't have to modify anything. So this is one of the beautiful things about containers is they're highly portable. You can run your code basically anywhere. as long as you're running, as long as you're using a standardized type of container. So that's one really big benefit of containers. They make your code highly portable, which is obviously extremely desirable, right? We've been trying to do this for decades. programming languages like Java and JavaScript have promised this and many others. But that's the idea of containers. They make your code highly portable. There's another big aspect of why containers are beneficial beyond portability. The other major aspect is efficiency. So here, let's take a step back for a second here. Let's say that we want to run a bunch of different servers. So we want to run a web server, and an FTP server, and a DNS server, and an Active Directory server, and a DHCP server. Let's say we want to run a bunch of different servers. An absolutely terrible idea would be to run all of those servers on a single server. So on a single server, you have your web server, your FTP server, your DNS server, your Active Directory server, your web server, your FTP. I'm probably repeating myself. You're running all these different servers. all these different application servers on top of a single physical server. Why would that be a terrible idea? Well, two major reasons. One relates to the attack surface, right? Every additional service that you have running on a server means you're increasing the attack surface. You're increasing the number of ways that someone could break into the system. If you're running like 20 different servers, your attack surface is gonna be colossal, right? You're gonna have so much code running on the system. So many different ports are gonna have to be open. The attack surface is gonna be just massive. So it's going to mean that this is going to end up being an extremely insecure server. And then add to that the consequences of this server being compromised are dire, right? Because if you've got 30 different things, 20 different things running on this one server and this server is compromised, it's now game over for your web server, your FTP server, your email server, your Active Directory server, your DHCP, DNS, et cetera, et cetera. Those would all be compromised if that one server is compromised. So the blast radius is enormous. So this is why you never want to do this, right? You never want to run a whole bunch of servers on a single physical server because it's going to make it extremely insecure and the consequences of compromise are going to be dire. So a much better way to do this is you, let's say we're doing this with virtual machines, you dedicate a virtual machine to each server. So this is your web server VM. This is your email server VM. This is your DNS server VM. This is your Active Directory server VM. This is your DHCP server VM. Each of these servers are running on their own dedicated VM. The benefit of doing this is now the attack surface for each individual server is way smaller, right? Because just one VM is just dedicated to being a web server, or the next VM is just dedicated to being an FTP server, or the next one's just dedicated to being a SNMP server. So it allows you to much, much more carefully lock down, harden each of these individual servers. And the consequences of one of them being compromised is not nearly as dire, right? Like, yeah, if your web server is compromised, that sucks. Your web server is compromised. But that shouldn't compromise a completely separate DNS VM running, you know, DNS server running on a different VM beside it, which shouldn't then also compromise the active directory, et cetera, et cetera. So... From a security perspective, what you want to do is you want to have each server running on its own dedicated virtual machine. So if you're running 20 servers, you're running 20 virtual machines. But here's where we run into a bit of an efficiency issue. Every time you spin up a new virtual machine, what massive piece of software are you also spinning up? an operating system, right? Because that's what a virtual machine is. A virtual machine is an operating system with some applications running on top of it. So if you're running 20 different virtual machines to run 20 different servers, you're running 20 different operating systems. And of course, operating systems are not small, lightweight pieces of code. It's going to require a lot of resources to run 20 operating systems. And this is where the second benefit of containers come in. multiple containers can share a single underlying operating system. So you can run 10 containers and those 10 containers can all share one underlying operating system. How is this possible? Well, I'll show you in a second. Basically, we move the layer of virtualization up a level. Like with virtual machines, we call it the hypervisor. I'll show you in a second. Now that we've kind of talked through this, you'll now understand this definition. Containers provide highly portable code execution environments, right? In other words, once you stuff your code inside of a container, you can run that container. As long as it's a standardized container, you can run it anywhere. So this is one big benefit of containers. It makes your code highly portable, very desirable. And containers are more efficient because multiple containers can share, can leverage the resources of one underlying operating system. So you can run 10 containers and all 10 containers can share one underlying operating system. So containers are, make your code highly portable, and they're considered to be more efficient than virtual machines. Now let's get to that point I was getting to, which is how is this possible? How can multiple containers share the same underlying operating system? Well, I think this picture helps. So here's a virtual machine that we've talked about, right? A virtual machine is you've got some hardware, You've got hypervisor running on top of the hardware. By the way, quick quiz, what type of hypervisor is this? Type 1, because it's running directly on top of the hardware. So this is a Type 1 hardware hypervisor. And as you can see here, we've got three virtual machines running. If we want to run three applications, we have to run three operating systems. And that requires a lot of resources. If we moved those applications into containers, you can see here we've got three applications running, but only one underlying operating system running. How is this possible? Well, it's because essentially we've moved the layer of virtualization up a level. In virtual machines, the layer of virtualization sits below the operating system. So the hypervisor is emulating hardware and the layer of virtualization here, right? Which is the hypervisor is sitting below the operating system. With containers though, the layer of virtualization is this containerization engine. And you can see the containerization engine is sitting above the operating system. So we've moved the layer of virtualization up above the operating system instead of below it. And so what the containerization engine is doing is essentially emulating the underlying operating system. So I want to be clear on this. Hypervisors and containerization engines do very different things, right? Hypervisors are emulating hardware. containerization engines are essentially emulating an operating system. So they're emulating very different things. But they do share the idea that they're both performing emulation, just at very different levels. And what this results in is that you can run multiple applications each in their own container, and those multiple containers can share one underlying operating system. That makes the containers more efficient. So that's the basic idea of containerization, at least high level for what you need to know for the exam. Okay, now, the next thing we're gonna talk about is we're gonna talk about serverless, and we're gonna talk about functions as a service. But before we can talk about serverless, we first have to spend a few minutes here talking about how we can go about architecting applications. So the first thing we're going to talk about here is monolithic applications. This is kind of more of a traditional way of architecting an application. What a monolithic application means is that all of the functionality for that application is all bundled together into like a single executable. And that's what this diagram is trying to show you here. Each of the different colored shapes here is meant to represent a different function. And so let's say that this is our finance system. As you can see here, we've got a bunch of functionality all built into one executable, and this is our finance system. So let's say this first row of functions here, this is related to user administration. So you can add a user. You can remove a user. You can authenticate a user. That's what these three functions would be. And then maybe the next row of functionality here is related to journal entry. So you've got a function for adding a journal entry, a function for deleting a journal entry, a function for modifying a journal entry. And then this next row of functionality here could be related to reporting. You can have a function for creating a report, a function for deleting a report, a function for running a report. So the point is, in a monolithic application, all of the functionality is bundled together into one executable file. There are benefits to this. This is efficient because all the functions are all together in one executable. They can all talk to each other very quickly, very efficiently. So monolithic applications tend to be very efficient. One significant issue that you're going to run into though with monolithic applications is scalability. Monolithic applications are typically only ever designed to run on a single server. So let's say we're running this monolithic application on a single physical server. You can only scale that physical server so much. You can only add so many CPU cores, so much RAM, so many network interface cards providing so much network bandwidth. There's limits to how much horsepower we can build into a single server. So if your company has grown from one finance person and now you have 500 finance people, You know, when you had one finance person running two or three reports a day, no problem, right? The server that the monolithic application was running on could handle that with no issue. But if you now have 500 finance people and they're running 2,000 or 3,000 reports a day, that one physical server probably can't handle that load. And you can only add so much hardware to a single physical server before you hit a ceiling. And then you've now run into a major bottleneck. You can't scale your monolithic application any further. So how has this been addressed? Well, this leads us to kind of the next major way of architecting applications, and that's what's referred to as microservices. And the really basic idea here is that you take all this exact same functionality, but now you break that functionality down into loosely coupled groupings of similar functionality. Each of these groupings of similar functions is called a microservice. And each microservice will typically run in its own container. So this here, right, this first one, this might be our microservice for user administration, right? Adding a user, deleting a user, authenticating a user. So those three functions are sitting here in this user administration microservice, which is running on its own container. And then we have our next microservice here for journal entry stuff running in its own container. And then we have another microservice here for reporting running in its own container. One of the benefits of this is from a scalability perspective. So let's say we've grown, now we need to run two or 3000 reports a day. Probably a single container by itself cannot handle that many, running that many reports a day. Well, no problem. Instead of just running a single container to then run reports, let's set up a whole cluster of containers. So we'll set up a cluster of 10 of these reporting containers and stick a load balancer in front of them. So now when a request comes in to run a report, the load balancer will just send that request to whichever container has capacity at the moment. So it becomes a lot easier to scale your application because you can run these microservices across multiple containers and you can even duplicate microservices and put like a load balancer in front of them and have automatic failovers and all sorts of stuff like that. So that's the idea of microservices. You're breaking your application down into loosely coupled groupings. And you can probably see where the next evolution of this. Instead of having all of our functions grouped into one executable file, microservices break them out and down into groups. The next evolution of this is where you break your application down into individual functions. So this box here, right? This is the function for authenticating a user. This is the function for adding a user. This is the function for removing a user. This is the function for adding a journal entry. The whole idea of where we get to talk to now about serverless is you're breaking your application down into individual functions. You then upload each of those individual functions essentially to the cloud. So each function is now sitting in the cloud waiting to be run. And by the way, The proper term for this is FAS, functions as a service, right? Because you've broken your application down into individual functions, and then you can run those functions in the cloud as a service. So you've broken your application down into individual functions. You upload those functions into the cloud. And now, whenever you want to, you can call that function and have it run. So if you want to authenticate a user, you just call the user authentication function. And the beauty of this is that it abstracts away any concept of infrastructure. It's like from the developer's perspective, you just focus on developing your function and you get to completely ignore any of the infrastructure that's necessary to run it. All of that is abstracted away. You just upload your function and then you can call that function as much or as little as you want. And the cloud provider will figure out how to do that. So if you want to call this function once a year or a million times a second, it's the same function. And that's super helpful from a development perspective, right? Now, of course, the cloud provider is going to have to run radically different back end systems to support that. If you're running a function once a year, that obviously doesn't require much infrastructure. If you're running a function a million times a second, that requires a ton of infrastructure. And the cloud provider's going to have to figure out how to do that. But as you as a developer, not your problem. You just upload the function. And there is beauty in that, right? It radically simplifies things from a development perspective. Because with monolithic applications, with microservices, you absolutely have to be concerned with the infrastructure. With microservices, you're running containers, you're load balancing, you're doing failovers. With microservices, you're still maintaining a lot of infrastructure. With monolithic applications, you're absolutely maintaining a ton of infrastructure, right? You're having to maintain this virtual machine or physical server or whatever it is. But with serverless, you don't have to do that. You don't have to think about the infrastructure. So that's one of the huge benefits of serverless is that it abstracts away all of its complexity of running and maintaining infrastructure. You just focus on running individual functions. By the way, a bit of distinction on these terms here. Specifically, what we're talking about here is functions as a service where you can upload a function and run it. which is a subset of overall serverless. This term serverless encompasses a lot of stuff, but the basic idea is serverless is all these different services that you can access in the cloud that let you run code, databases, things like that without ever having to think about the infrastructure. So like in Amazon's world, for instance, right? FAS is known as Lambda functions, where you can upload an individual function and then just run in the cloud. That's called a Lambda function. Or if you want to do user administration without having to code anything, you can use a Cognito database. You don't have to, sorry, I shouldn't say you don't have to, it's not that you don't have to code anything. You don't have to maintain the infrastructure. You can just use this service, service known as a Cognito user store. That's examples of serverless. So serverless is a broader term that encompasses serverless databases, serverless user authentication, functions as a service, et cetera. So serverless is a broader term. and what i've described here is functions as a service one important point i want to make here which of these is best because you hear a lot of hype in the industry now about you know oh serverless is like cloud 2.0 this is the this is the greatest thing ever since sliced bread right this is amazing everyone needs to move to serverless No, not necessarily. The true answer to which of these is best is none of them or any of them. It depends on what your needs are. Monolithic applications are almost always more efficient. And so in certain circumstances, if you just need to run something extremely efficiently, you don't have to worry about scalability, you want to keep things simple, monolithic application is probably the way to go. Microservices has its pros and cons. Serverless has its pros and cons. So none of these is best. You have to look at very carefully at what are your needs and which of these best suit your needs and pick accordingly. So I want to point that out, right? Because anytime there's any new technology, everyone's like, oh, wow, this is the most amazing thing. Everyone has to do this. Maybe. There are some cool benefits of this. But there's also still great benefits of using micros of using monolithic applications and microservices. So pick whatever best suits your needs. OK, here's some definitions. So microservices, right? This is where application functions are divided up into loosely coupled groupings. And then each service, each microservice runs in its own container. And those containers, those microservices talk back and forth to each other by APIs, application programming interfaces. So that's microservices. And then, of course, one more logical step brings us to serverless or functions as a service. where you've broken your application down into individual functions. You upload those functions to the cloud, and then the cloud provider takes care of all the infrastructure necessary to run that function for you. And you can run that function as much or as little as you desire, million times a second or once a year. It's the same function. the cloud service provider is entirely responsible for figuring out how to make that work. And one final piece I'll cover here, zero usage equals zero cost. This is cool. If you upload a function and you never call it, you don't pay anything. So one benefit of serverless or functions as a service can be that, let's say you have an application that no one's using on the weekends. If no one's calling the functions, you're not gonna have any costs over the weekend. Whereas with microservices and especially with monolithic applications, you're typically running infrastructure 24 by 7. So with microservices, you'd typically be running certain containers 24 by 7. you're paying for that, even if no one's using the application. And with a monolithic application, you're running a physical server or a VM, typically 24 by 7, whether or not someone's using the application or not. So there can be some ongoing costs there. Serverless could potentially be cheaper for certain use cases. It can also be way more expensive in certain use cases. So again, pick what best suits your needs.

LESSON 246: Lesson 441

============================================================

We've now talked about how we can suffer the loss of a part like a power supply or a CPU in a system and how we can use cold spares, hot spare, warm spares. We've talked about how we can suffer the loss of a hard drive using different types of RAID. let's now move up to talking about how can we suffer the loss of an entire server an entire computer system and that's where we're going to get into clustering and redundancy so clustering redundancy is all about using multiple systems together such that if one of those systems fails You're not gonna go down. The other systems can somehow take over. But there's an important difference between clustering and redundancy. So let's start with clustering. What clustering is all about is where you're using multiple systems working together. Key word here is together. Clustering is all about multiple systems working together to support a workload. Perfect example of clustering is say having a cluster of web servers sitting behind a load balancer. So if you've got some really busy website where no one single web server on its own could handle all of the traffic, you could set up a cluster of web servers where you have say 10 web servers all sitting behind a single load balancer. And as requests come into the load balancer, the load balancer is just going to divvy those requests up amongst the cluster. And this gives us redundancy, right? Because if one of the members of the cluster fails, if we go from 10 web servers down to nine, we're still up and running. So that's clustering and how it provides redundancy because you've got all these systems working together. If one or two or three of those systems fails, you should still be up and running provided you've got enough members in the cluster. This is subtly different from redundancy. In redundancy, you have one primary system and then one or more secondary systems. And the difference is here in redundancy, that one primary system is handling the entire workload all by itself. So where redundancy is very commonly being used is in databases. There's databases that support this now, but if you go back and not to even do that long ago, trying to set up a cluster of databases would have been a terrible idea. How do you maintain consistency across all those databases? So a much more common technique would be to use redundancy with databases. And in redundancy, you'd have one primary database and all the transactions would be handled by that one primary database. What you could be doing in the background is that as changes are made to the databases, to the primary database, those changes could be replicated to the one or more secondary databases. But other than being updated, those secondary databases aren't doing anything. No users are interacting with them. Those secondary databases are just keeping up to date, but otherwise just sitting there waiting should the primary ever fail. And if the primary ever fails, then the first secondary database will take over automatically. And if that ever failed, then the next redundant one would take over. So redundancy is where you have one primary system. and then one or more secondary systems that are there and available to take over should the primary ever fail. And this leads to a really good exam question. What's the difference between clustering and redundancy from a capacity perspective? So what's the difference between clustering and redundancy from a capacity perspective if one of the systems fails? And the answer is in clustering, you're gonna have a reduction in capacity. Because if you go from say 10 web servers and one of those web servers in the cluster fails, now you're only down to nine, you've just lost 10% of your capacity. So in clustering, if one of the members fails, you're gonna have a reduction in capacity. Whereas with redundancy, you'll typically architect it so that the primary system and the secondary system are just as powerful. So if the primary fails, secondary system can take over and the secondary can support 100% of the workload on its own because it's just as powerful a system as the primary. So in redundancy, you'll typically have no reduction in capacity if the primary system fails. Okay, there you go. That's clustering and redundancy.

declaring a disaster? This is actually a really interesting question to contemplate. How and when is an organization gonna decide to declare a disaster? So earlier here in domain seven, we talked about incident response and the whole point of having an incident response process is that you can detect if an incident has occurred and then you can do something about it. And so, honestly, the primary way where most bad things are first detected is through your incident response process. But here's where you're gonna have to make a decision. If something bad happens, and it's detected by your instant response process, you're gonna have to make a decision as to whether or not, okay, so this bad thing has just occurred. Are we just gonna activate our instant response and follow our instant response process? Or are you going to say, you know what? Nope. You know, this bad thing that's just happened. We're not going to treat it as an incident. And instead, we're going to declare a disaster and activate our BCP, our DRP plans. So in other words, an organization will have to make a decision. When something bad happens and they detect it through their incident response process, are they just going to continue following their incident response process? Or instead, are they going to declare a disaster and activate their BCP and DRP plans? So the question then becomes is, what's going to drive them to make this decision one way or the other? And I'll give you a giant hint. What's going to be the major driver behind them making the decision is one of the key measurements of time we just talked about. Which measurement of time do you think it is? This one, MTD, Maximum Tolerable Downtime. Or remember, it's also called MAD, Maximum Allowable Downtime. So this is the key measurement of time that we used by an organization to decide, are we gonna treat this as an incident or are we gonna declare a disaster? So here's how this typically works. So something bad happens and it's detected. And you may recall when we talked about instant response, the first thing that an organization is gonna do when something bad happens is they're gonna activate their instant response team, and that instant response team is gonna do an impact assessment. In other words, they're gonna figure out how big of a deal is this bad thing? And how long is it gonna take to recover? So let's say that something bad happens, the IR instant response team is activated. They perform a quick impact assessment and they determine, you know what? If we follow the incident response process, it's going to take us seven days to get things back to business as usual. So. Bad thing happens, instant response team is activated, they do an impact assessment and they determine it's gonna take seven days. If we follow our instant response process, it's gonna take us seven days to get back to business as usual. If the MTD for say that system is only 24 hours, Should you follow the instant response process? No, right, because you're gonna exceed your MTD. Remember, what does MTD mean? Maximum allowable, maximum tolerable downtime? It's the maximum amount of time that something can be down for before, you're no longer a business, right? Before the business is no longer going concern. And so this is why the MTD is the perfect number to use here. So if you determine following the incident response process is gonna take seven days to get back to business as usual, and your MTD is only 24 hours, then you cannot follow your incident response process, right? It's gonna take way too long. You're gonna be out of business before you can get things back up and running. So this is when you would say, nope, right? Because the MTD has been exceeded. We're not going to treat this as an incident. Instead, we're going to declare a disaster. And then you'll activate your relevant BCP and DRP plans. And the whole point of those plans is to make sure that certain critical business processes, certain critical systems are back up and running within the MTD. within say 24 hours in the example I just used there. So that's one of the major criteria that's used as to whether or not to declare a disaster. Like, are we going to treat this as an incident or are we going to declare a disaster? This is a really good thing to remember. If the MTD is going to be exceeded, you're going to declare a disaster. So this is one of the criteria that would be used as part of the declaration criteria. There might be other things that would factor in as well, but remember MTD. If you do decide to declare a disaster, there's lots of communication that's going to have to happen. You're going to need to have a really well-trained team to be able to respond to this. In a disaster, the emergency management team is typically made up of all sorts of stakeholders from all across the organization. Because again, if you're responding to a disaster, it needs to be all hands on deck. Everyone needs to be helping out because otherwise you may not be a business anymore. So your emergency management team is going to be typically made up of senior executives from across the organization, all sorts of relevant stakeholders from human resources, IT, Maybe even public relations. Why public relations? Because maybe CNN is on line three. Maybe your customers are screaming because they're unhappy. So PR may need to be involved. Legal is an interesting one here. Why is legal involved? Well, let me ask you this question. If an organization is, say, storing sensitive data on European Union citizens, so the organization needs to be GDPR compliant, when the organization is in the midst of responding to a disaster, do they get to just ignore their legal and contractual requirements? You know, if they have to be storing the data in a GDPR compliant way, do they get to just ignore that when they're responding to a disaster? No, absolutely not. So this is why legal might even be involved as well. So that when the organization is responding to whatever cataclysmic event is happening. the organization can make sure they're doing it in such a way that they remain in compliance with whatever their contractual or regulatory requirements are. So this is why emergency management teams are often made up by a bunch of different stakeholders. As I mentioned, there's all sorts of communications that are going to be happening internal to various business units, system owners, senior management, etc., and all sorts of communications externally to customers, investors, regulators, the media, etc., So there you go. That's some of the things to be thinking about as a part of declaring and responding to a disaster.
